text:
June 2026 was the second-warmest June globally.

Western Europe experienced its warmest June on record and Europe as a whole its second-warmest June, with an intense heatwave affecting much of the continent during the second half of June.

Sea surface temperature over the extra-polar ocean was the highest on record for June, with developing El Niño conditions in the equatorial Pacific.

The global average temperature for June 2026 was 16.54°C, 0.56°C above the 1991-2020 average for June and 1.39°C above the estimated 1850-1900 pre-industrial average for the month.

June 2026 was the second-warmest June on record globally, slightly warmer (+0.03°C) than the third-warmest June in 2023 and 0.12°C cooler than the warmest June in 2024.

The average global temperature for the last 12 months (July 2025 to June 2026) was 0.55°C above the 1991-2020 average, and 1.43°C above the 1850-1900 pre-industrial average.

Outside Europe (discussed below), temperature anomalies in June 2026 were much above average in central Asia and western Siberia, as well as in northern Canada and to the west of the Antarctic Peninsula.

Warmer-than-average conditions prevailed over much of Africa, Australia, and the western United States.

Colder-than-average conditions occurred across parts of East Asia, southeastern South America, and much of eastern Antarctica.

The average temperature over European land for June 2026 was 19.14°C, 1.78°C above the 1991-2020 average for June.

June 2026 was the second-warmest June for Europe in the ERA5 dataset, slightly cooler than the warmest June of 2019 (19.23°C, 1.87°C above average).

The relatively large temperature anomaly in June 2026 is consistent with the strong warming trend observed during this month since the 1980s. Warm anomalies have become particularly pronounced since 2019, following a period with more variable year-to-year conditions between the late 1990s and the late 2010s.

Eight out of the nine warmest Junes for Europe have occurred since 2019.

Monthly temperatures for June 2026 were above average across most of Europe, reflecting the effect of the intense heatwave that affected much of the continent during the second half of the month.

The largest temperature anomalies were observed in western Europe, the region most affected by the heatwave, with monthly values reaching 3-5°C above average across France, Germany, Spain, Italy, and the Benelux countries.

According to national reports, England and France experienced their warmest Junes on record while the UK as a whole as well as Germany and the Netherlands had their second-warmest Junes on record.

Many June records, and some all-time records, for daily maximum temperature were also set across Europe, including in Germany, Czechia, Poland and Hungary and the UK.

Cooler-than-average conditions were only observed in the far-eastern parts of the European domain in western Russia and Türkiye.

The heatwave that affected much of Europe during the second half of June contributed to making June 2026 the warmest June on record for western Europe, the region most strongly affected by the event.

The monthly average surface air temperature over the western Europe region (land areas within 11°W–15°E, 37°–55°N) was 20.74°C, 3.06°C above the 1991–2020 average for June, surpassing the previous record set in June 2025 (2.81°C above average). June 2003 follows closely behind as the third-warmest June for the region (2.75°C above average).

Like June 2026, June 2025 was marked by heatwave conditions across western Europe. Further details about the June 2025 heatwave can be found in this article.

June 2026 temperatures were ‘much above average’ – corresponding to values above the 90th percentile of the 1991–2020 distribution – across virtually all of western and central Europe, as well as in parts of southeastern and northern Europe.

June 2026 was also the warmest June since 1979 across much of western and northern France, parts of England and Wales, and central and northern Spain, according to ERA5 data.

The average sea surface temperature (SST) over the extra-polar ocean (60°S–60°N) for June 2026 was the highest on record for the month, at 20.86°C, yet only marginally higher than the previous June record in June 2024 (20.85°C).

This the first time in 2026 that the daily and monthly SSTs for 60°S–60°N have exceeded the corresponding levels of 2024 and reached record levels for the time of year.

From July onwards, the highest values date back to 2023, coinciding with the development of the strong 2023–2024 El Niño event and exceptionally high SSTs across much of the tropical and North Atlantic Ocean.

The high SSTs observed in June 2026 are driven partly by much warmer-than-average conditions across large parts of the tropical Pacific. See further discussion on this topic below.

In June 2026, SSTs continued to reach record highs for the month across a large swathe of the Pacific Ocean, stretching from the central equatorial Pacific to the western coast of Mexico. This pattern has been prominent since April, extending partly into the Niño 3.4 region used to monitor El Niño events, and has been associated with severe to extreme marine heatwave conditions..

SSTs across most of the equatorial Pacific, including the Niño 3.4 region, were much above average, reflecting the developing El Niño conditions. According to the World Meteorological Organization, the current El Niño event is forecast to strengthen rapidly over the coming months.

Many other ocean regions experienced much warmer-than-average conditions, often with pockets of record-high values. These included the northwestern North Pacific, large parts of the Southern Hemisphere ocean, the western North Atlantic, the Mediterranean Sea, as well as the Nordic Seas.

Global and European monthly surface air temperature anomalies (ERA5, degrees C vs 1991-2020) covering the full series from 1940-01 through 2026-06: <ts></ts>

In June 2026, Arctic sea ice extent ranked sixth-lowest for the month, with particularly low sea ice cover in the northern Barents Sea around Svalbard and Franz Josef Land.

Antarctic sea ice extent ranked sixth-lowest for June, with much below-average sea ice cover in the Bellingshausen Sea.

The average sea ice extent in the Arctic in June 2026 was 10.9 million km², 0.6 million km² or 5% below the 1991–2020 average for June.

This was the sixth-lowest June extent in the 48-year satellite record, but only 0.1 million km² smaller than the value recorded a year earlier, in June 2025, which ranked second lowest. In contrast, the lowest June extent, recorded in 2016, was 7% below average.

The June 2026 extent comes after seven consecutive months during which sea ice extent ranked among the four lowest on record for the respective month, from November 2025 to May 2026.

The daily Arctic sea ice extent continued its seasonal decline in June. It remained lower than the 2025 levels for the first half of the month, to grow and stay slightly above 2025 for the rest of June. On 30 June, it was 9.81 million km², 0.12 million km² higher than in 2025 which was the lowest on record for that date.

During the month, the daily extent ranked between 4 th and 12 th lowest for the time of year.

In June 2026, sea ice cover was most below average in the northern Barents Sea around Svalbard and Franz Josef Land, and north of the New Siberian Islands.

Below-average sea ice cover was also observed in parts of the Kara Sea, in the Greenland Sea, the northeastern Canadian sector, including the northern Hudson Bay, and in limited patches in the North Pacific sector.

Conversely, sea ice cover was above average in much of the Pacific sector, in the Laptev Sea, and albeit slightly, along Novaya Zemlya.

The average Antarctic sea i ce extent in June 2026 was 12.7 million km², which is 1.1 million km² or 8% below the 1991–2020 average for June.

This was the sixth-lowest June extent in the satellite record and the fourth-lowest June extent in the past four years. After reaching a record-low of 16.8% in 2023, June sea ice anomalies have gradually recovered since then, with the largest increase occurring between 2023 and 2024.

The June extent was relatively close to the values observed in several recent years: 9% below average in 2025, 8.7% below average in 2022 and 2019, and 7.6% below average in 2017.

The daily ice extent continued its seasonal growth in June.

Consistently below the median for the 1991-2020 reference period, it remained well above the record-low levels of 2023, and close to the values of 2025 throughout the month, with daily rankings ranging from 3 rd   to 9 th lowest for the time of year.

In June 2026, sea ice cover was most below average in the Bellingshausen Sea, as observed in April and May.

Sea ice cover was also below average along most the East Antarctic ice edge, particularly in the South Atlantic sector, east of the Weddell Sea and at the border of Indian and western Pacific Sector.

By contrast, sea ice cover was above average in the Amundsen Sea and eastern Ross Sea, and close to or slightly above average in the Weddell Sea.

The sea ice indicator page provides additional information about the long-term trends in both the Arctic and Antarctica.

Copernicus is the European Union's Earth Observation Programme, looking at our planet and its environment for the ultimate benefit of all European citizens.

Arctic and Antarctic June sea-ice extent anomalies (million sq km, vs 1991-2020) for each June through 2026: <ts></ts>

May 2026 was the second-warmest May globally.

Europe experienced its seventh-warmest May and its third warmest spring, with an unusually early and intense heatwave affecting western Europe towards the end of May.

Sea surface temperature over the extra-polar ocean was the second highest for May, with exceptionally high values in the tropical Pacific.

The global average temperature for May 2026 was 15.81°C, 0.55°C above the 1991-2020 average for May and 1.42°C above the estimated 1850-1900 pre-industrial average for the month.

May 2026 was the second-warmest May on record globally, slightly warmer (+0.02°C) than the third-warmest May in 2025 and 0.10°C cooler than the warmest May in 2024.

The average global temperature for the last 12 months (June 2025 to May 2026) was 0.55°C above the 1991-2020 average, and 1.43°C above the 1850-1900 pre-industrial average.

Outside Europe (discussed below), temperature anomalies in May 2026 showed contrasting conditions across North America: warmer than average in western and northern regions, and colder than average across eastern areas and Alaska.

Warmer-than-average conditions prevailed over central and eastern Asia, and most of Africa.

Colder-than-average conditions occurred across the Middle East, parts of Asia, much of Australia and Argentina, southwest Africa, and parts of eastern Antarctica.

The global-average temperature for the boreal spring (March to May 2026) was the third highest on record, at 0.53°C above the 1991-2020 average.

This was 0.15°C and 0.06°C cooler than the two warmest boreal springs in 2024 (0.68°C) and 2025 (0.59°C), respectively.

Outside Europe, temperatures in boreal spring were most above average across the United States, much of the Arctic (outside northern Canada), central Asia, the far east of Russia, and large parts of Antarctica.

Colder-than-average conditions prevailed over Alaska, northern Canada, the Middle East, Siberia, and northern Australia.

The average temperature over European land for May 2026 was 13.87°C, or 0.60°C above the 1991-2020 average for May, making it the seventh-warmest May for Europe in the ERA5 dataset.

The warmest May on record – by a significant margin – occurred in 2018. However, May temperatures have shown substantial year-to-year variability in recent years. Despite a clear warming trend since the 1980s, five of the last ten Mays were cooler than average.

The monthly average temperature for May 2026 masks a sharp contrast in conditions during the month, with a rapid transition from colder- to much-warmer-than-average over much of the continent around 20 May. See further discussion on this topic in our web article.

May temperatures were warmer than average across most of Europe, with the largest anomalies in northeastern and western Europe. According to national reports, France experienced its second-warmest May on record, and the UK its joint-third warmest.

Colder-than-average conditions prevailed in southeastern Europe, including Türkiye and regions surrounding the Black Sea.

During the second half of the month, large parts of western Europe experienced an exceptionally early and intense heatwave that broke multiple May temperature records, particularly in France and the UK. See further discussion on this topic in our web article on this event.

The average temperature over European land for spring (March to May 2026) was 1.13°C above the 1991-2020 average.

This was the third-warmest spring for Europe in the ERA5 dataset, only marginally cooler (0.01°C) than the second-warmest spring in 2014.

The warmest spring for Europe occurred in 2024, with a temperature anomaly of 1.50°C.

Spring temperature anomalies across Europe largely reflected the patterns observed in May.

Temperatures were above average over much of the continent, most notably across northern Fennoscandia and western Russia, as well as in western Europe, particularly France, the Iberian Peninsula and the UK.

According to available national reports, France and Norway experienced their warmest spring while the UK recorded its third warmest spring (warmest for England and Wales).

Temperatures were below average across Türkiye, the eastern Balkans and the Caucasus.

During May 2026, the average sea surface temperature (SST) over the extra-polar ocean (60°S–60°N) remained only slightly below the record levels reached in 2024, well above the values seen in all other years.

For May 2026 as a whole, the average SST for 60°S–60°N was the second-highest on record for the month, at 20.90°C, behind May 2024 (20.93°C).

The near-record SSTs are driven partly by much warmer-than-average conditions across large parts of the tropical Pacific. See further discussion on this topic below.

In May 2026, SSTs continued to reach record highs for the month across a broad region stretching from the central equatorial Pacific to the western coast of Mexico. This pattern, already present in April, expanded further and was associated with severe to extreme marine heatwave conditions.

SSTs across most of the equatorial Pacific, including the Niño 3.4 region used to monitor El Niño events, were above average, reflecting the ongoing transition El Niño conditions. According to the World Meteorological Organization, the event is now virtually certain to develop over the coming months.

Many other ocean regions experienced much warmer-than-average conditions, often with pockets of record-high values. These included the northwestern North Pacific, large parts of the Southern Hemisphere ocean, the western North Atlantic, the Mediterranean Sea, as well as the Nordic Seas.

The average SST over the broad tropical Pacific region (155°E–90°W, 30°S–30°N) has reached record highs since the second half of April. Values have been well above those observed in 2016 and 2024, both of which were marked by the final stages of strong El Niño events.

These record levels are occurring even before the onset of the El Niño event forecast for the second half of 2026. The development of El Niño could further increase temperatures across the tropical Pacific.

Global and European monthly surface air temperature anomalies (ERA5, degrees C vs 1991-2020) covering the full series from 1940-01 through 2026-05: <ts></ts>

In May 2026, Arctic sea ice extent ranked fourth lowest for the month, with particularly low sea ice cover in the northern Barents Sea and around Svalbard.

Antarctic sea ice extent ranked seventh lowest for May, with much below-average sea ice cover in the Bellingshausen Sea.

The average sea ice extent in the Arctic in May 2026 was 12.4 million km², 0.6 million km² or 4.4% below the 1991–2020 average for May.

This was the fourth lowest May extent in the 48-year satellite record, behind May 2018 (-5.1%), 2019 (-5.6%), and 2016 (-7.4%).

The May 2026 extent is notably lower than the values recorded in the last five years (2021–2025) and comes after six consecutive months during which sea ice extent ranked among the three lowest on record for the respective month, from November 2025 to April 2026.

The daily Arctic sea ice extent continued its seasonal decline in May, remaining lower than the 2025 levels throughout the month.

During the month, the daily extent ranked between third and sixth lowest for the time of year.

In May 2026, sea ice cover was most below average in the northern Barents Sea and between Svalbard and Franz Josef Land.

Below-average sea ice cover was also observed across most other sectors of the Arctic region, especially the Denmark Strait (northwest of Iceland), the northeastern Canadian sector, and the North Pacific sector.

The average Antarctic sea ice extent in May 2026 was 9.7 million km², which is 0.9 million km² or 8.7% below the 1991–2020 average for May.

This was the seventh-lowest May extent in the satellite record, relatively close to the values observed in several recent years: 8.1% below average in 2022, 8.5% below average in 2024, and 9.4% below average in 2025.

The year 2023 was a notable exception, with a May extent 17% below average, the lowest value recorded for the month.

The daily ice extent continues its seasonal growth in May.

It closely tracked the 2025 values throughout the month, with daily rankings ranging from 6 th to 13 th lowest for the time of year.

In May 2026, sea ice cover was most below average in the Bellingshausen Sea, which remained mostly ice-free, continuing the conditions observed in April.

Sea ice cover was also below average along most of the ice edge along the East Antarctic coast, particularly in the South Atlantic sector, east of the Weddell Sea.

By contrast, sea ice cover was above average in the Amundsen Sea, and close to or slightly above average in the Weddell Sea.

The sea ice indicator page provides additional information about the long-term trends in both the Arctic and Antarctica.

Copernicus is the European Union's Earth Observation Programme, looking at our planet and its environment for the ultimate benefit of all European citizens.

Arctic and Antarctic May sea-ice extent anomalies (million sq km, vs 1991-2020) for each May through 2026: <ts></ts>

April 2026 was the joint third-warmest April globally, with an average surface air temperature of 14.89°C, 0.52°C above the 1991-2020 average for April, according to the ERA5 dataset. The warmest April on record was in 2024 and the second warmest in 2025.

April 2026 was 1.43°C above the estimated 1850-1900 average used to define the pre-industrial level.

The average temperature over European land for April 2026 was the tenth warmest at 8.88°C, 0.50°C above the 1991-2020 average for April. The warmest April on record was in 2018.

Temperature anomalies across Europe were marked by a sharp contrast, with much-warmer-than-average conditions over southwestern Europe and colder-than-average conditions over much of eastern Europe.

The average sea surface temperature (SST) for April 2026 over 60°S–60°N was the second-highest value on record for the month, at 21.00°C. The warmest April on record was in 2024 during the last El Niño event.

SSTs reached record highs for the month across a broad region from the central equatorial Pacific to the western coast of the United States and Mexico, corresponding to ‘strong’ marine heatwave conditions.

The global average temperature for April 2026 was 14.89°C, 0.52°C above the 1991-2020 average for April and 1.43°C above the estimated 1850-1900 pre-industrial average for the month.

April 2026 was the joint third warmest April on record globally, tied with April 2016 and April 2020 to within differences of less than 0.01°C. The warmest April occurred in 2024 and the second warmest in 2025.

This follows fifth-warmest rankings in December 2025, January, and February, and fourth warmest in March. The higher ranking for April reflects, at least partly, increased temperature anomalies over the ocean, in particular warmer sea surface temperatures in the Pacific.

The average global temperature for the last 12 months (May 2025 to April 2026) was 0.54°C above the 1991-2020 average, and 1.42°C above the 1850-1900 pre-industrial average.

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies, but the relatively dense observational coverage of the continent reduces uncertainty.

The European-average temperature for April 2026 was 8.88°C, or 0.50°C above the 1991-2020 average for April.

April 2026 ranked as the 10th warmest April in the ERA5 dataset.

The nine warmer Aprils all occurred between 1999 and 2025, with the warmest in 2018, at 1.92°C above average.

Temperature anomalies across Europe were marked by sharp regional contrasts, with much-warmer-than-average conditions over southwestern Europe (Iberian Peninsula, western and southern France, Italy) and colder-than-average conditions over much of eastern Europe.

This contrast can be linked to the atmospheric circulation, with a persistent area of high pressure over western Europe and low pressure over eastern Europe. These conditions favoured sunny and dry weather in the west and advection of colder air from the north into central and eastern Europe.

Toward the end of the month, a deep low-pressure system over the eastern North Atlantic also helped steer hot air from north Africa toward southwestern Europe.

Parts of northern Spain and southern France experienced their warmest April since at least 1979, according to ERA5 data. This warmth was reflected in national data: Spain recorded its warmest April since 1962, according to AEMET, and France its third-warmest April since 1900, on a par with April 2020, according to Météo France.

Near-average conditions prevailed in a diagonal band from the Benelux countries to western Türkiye.

Warmer-than-average conditions were most pronounced over the European Arctic sector (northern Barents Sea, eastern Greenland), over the United States, central Asia, western Australia, parts of East Antarctica, and much of the Weddell Sea.

Temperature anomalies across North America showed a marked contrast, with warmer-than-average conditions over the United States, particularly in the east, and colder-than-average conditions over most of Canada, especially in the Canadian Arctic Archipelago.

Colder-than-average conditions also prevailed across the Saharan region in Africa, the Middle East, parts of eastern Russia, northern Australia, and parts of western Antarctica.

Elsewhere, mixed conditions were observed across South America and Africa.

During April, the average sea surface temperature (SST) for 60°S–60°N inched closer to the record levels observed in 2024, remaining well above values seen in all other years, including 2023 and 2025.

By 30 April, the 2026 value was just 0.02°C below the 2024 level for the same date, according to ERA5 data.

For April as a whole, the SST for 60°S–60°N was 21.00°C, the second highest on record for the month, behind April 2024 (21.04°C).

April 2024 coincided with the end of the 2023–2024 El Niño event. In contrast, El Niño conditions are expected to develop in the coming months, according to the World Meteorological Organization, potentially pushing 2026 SSTs above 2024 levels later this year.

In the equatorial Pacific, most of the area with near-average or cooler-than-average SSTs observed in March has been replaced with above-average SSTs, reflecting the ongoing transition from ENSO-neutral to El Niño conditions.

Many other areas across the oceans experienced much warmer-than-average conditions, often with pockets of record-high values, including the northwestern North Pacific, large parts of the Southern Hemisphere oceans, the western North Atlantic, the Mediterranean Sea, as well as the Nordic Seas.

Global and European monthly surface air temperature anomalies (ERA5, degrees C vs 1991-2020) covering the full series from 1940-01 through 2026-04: <ts></ts>

In the Arctic, the average sea ice extent in April was about 5% below average, ranking second-lowest for the month, slightly less than the April record set in 2019 (6% below average).

Regionally, sea ice cover was most below average in the Sea of Okhotsk, in the northern Barents Sea and the Svalbard region, similar to March.

In the Antarctic, the monthly sea ice extent was about 10% below the April average, ranking 11th lowest for the month, and close to values observed in the past two years.

Sea ice cover was much below average in the Bellingshausen Sea, which remained virtually ice-free, while it was above average in the neighbouring Amundsen Sea.

3. Sea ice concentration anomalies across the Arctic

The average sea ice extent in the Arctic in April 2026 was 13.6 million km², 0.7 million km² or 4.8% below the 1991–2020 average for April.

This was the second lowest April extent in the 48-year satellite record, behind April 2019 (5.7% below average).

Since November 2025, Arctic sea ice extent has consistently ranked among the three lowest on record for each month, ranging from second-lowest in November and March to lowest in December, and third-lowest in January and February.

After reaching its winter maximum in early March, the daily Arctic sea ice extent began its seasonal decline with the onset of the melt season.

For most of the month, the daily extent remained lower than the 2025 levels and ranked among the two lowest for the time of year, alongside 2019.

In April 2026, sea ice cover was most below average in the Sea of Okhotsk (northwestern Pacific), as well as in the Barents Sea and around Svalbard, as already observed in March.

Conditions were more mixed in the Greenland Sea, the Bering Sea, and the Labrador Sea.

Sea ice extent in the Sea of Okhotsk has been at record low levels for the time of year in March and April according to data from OSI SAF, contributing to the overall low Arctic sea ice extent.

The low ice conditions in this region may be linked to large positive sea surface temperature anomalies in the northwestern Pacific, which became increasingly prominent during March and April.

The average Antarctic sea ice extent in April 2026 was 6.5 million km², which is 0.7 million km² or 9.7% below the 1991–2020 average for April.

This ranks as the 11th lowest April extent in the satellite record and is very close to the values observed in the past two years: 9.3% below average in 2024 and 10.1% below average in 2025.

The lowest April extent on record occurred in 2017 (20.8% below average), followed by 1980 (20.1% below average).

The daily ice extent continues its seasonal growth in April, following the summer minimum reached in February.

It closely tracked 2025 values throughout the month, ranking between 8th and 16th lowest for the time of year.

In April 2026, sea ice cover was most below average in the Bellingshausen Sea, which remained virtually ice-free.

By contrast, sea ice cover was above average in the adjacent Amundsen Sea. This dipole in sea ice concentration anomalies is typically associated with strong cyclonic activity (below-average atmospheric pressure) in the West Antarctic sector of the Southern Ocean.

Elsewhere, sea ice cover was below average along much of the sea ice edge across the Southern Ocean, except for a patch of above-average cover in the northern Weddell Sea.

The sea ice indicator page provides additional information about the long-term trends in both the Arctic and Antarctica.

Copernicus is the European Union's Earth Observation Programme, looking at our planet and its environment for the ultimate benefit of all European citizens.

Arctic and Antarctic April sea-ice extent anomalies (million sq km, vs 1991-2020) for each April through 2026: <ts></ts>

March 2026 was the fourth-warmest March globally, with an average surface air temperature of 13.94°C, 0.53°C above the 1991-2020 average for March, according to the ERA5 dataset. The warmest March on record was in 2024.

March 2026 was 1.48°C above the estimated 1850-1900 average used to define the pre-industrial level.

The average temperature over European land for March 2026 was the second warmest at 5.88°C, 2.27°C above the 1991-2020 average for March. The warmest March on record was in 2025.

Almost the whole of Europe experienced warmer-than-average temperatures, with the most pronounced warm conditions occurring over northwest Russia, northern Fennoscandia, and the Baltic States. Slightly cooler-than-average conditions were seen over Türkiye, southern Europe, and most of Iceland.

Outside Europe, warmer-than-average temperatures were most pronounced across the United States – where a prolonged heatwave affected the western part of the country – as well as much of the Arctic, northeast Russia, and parts of Antarctica. In contrast, unusually cold conditions occurred across Alaska, most of Canada, southern Greenland, and northwest Siberia.

The average sea surface temperature (SST) for March 2026 over 60°S–60°N was 20.97°C, the second-highest value on record for the month. The warmest March on record was in 2024 during the last El Niño event.

A t ransition from neutral to El Niño conditions is forecast by many climate centres for the second half of the year.

The daily SST steadily increased throughout March and is approaching the record values set in 2024.

Averaging over 12-month periods smooths out shorter-term variations in regional- and global-average temperatures. Globally, the annual average for the latest 12-month period (April 2025 to March 2026) was 0.55°C above the 1991-2020 average, and 1.43°C above the estimated 1850-1900 average used to define the pre-industrial level.

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies, but the relatively dense observational coverage of the continent reduces uncertainty.

2.27°C above the 1991-2020 average for March, with an average absolute temperature of 5.88°C,

Second-warmest March in the ERA5 dataset, behind March 2025.

Near-average or slightly below-average temperatures were seen across Türkiye, southern Europe, including Sicily and eastern Spain, and most of Iceland.

Warmer-than-average conditions were most pronounced across the United States, much of the Arctic, and the northeast Russia. Antarctica was mostly above average too.

A prolonged and intense heatwave affected the western United States in mid-March, lasting around one to two weeks and breaking numerous March temperature records. In some areas, temperatures reached levels more typical of mid-summer.

Colder-than-average conditions occurred across Alaska, most of Canada, southern Greenland, and northwest Siberia.

Mixed conditions were seen across Africa and Australia.

The average sea surface temperature (SST) for 60°S–60°N remained at historically high levels throughout March, with a monthly mean of 20.97°C, 0.44°C above the 1991–2020 average.

This ranked as the second-highest value on record for the month, and 0.10°C below the March 2024 record set when there was an El Niño in progress.

The daily SST steadily increased throughout March and is approaching the record values set in 2024.

The SST patterns in March 2026 were relatively similar to those observed in February 2026.

SSTs were close to or below average in the central equatorial Pacific.

Above-average SSTs in the eastern equatorial Pacific reflect a t ransition to El Niño conditions that is forecast by many climate centres for the second half of the year.

Many areas in the subtropical and northeast North Atlantic as well as North and South Pacific had the warmest SSTs on record for March. The Mediterranean Sea generally saw warmer-than-average SSTs with some small record-high pockets.

Below-average SSTs were recorded in the central North Atlantic, the Bay of Bengal, the Indian Ocean, and to the north of Australia, for example, in the Timor Sea.

Global and European monthly surface air temperature anomalies (ERA5, degrees C vs 1991-2020) covering the full series from 1940-01 through 2026-03: <ts></ts>

In the Arctic, the average sea ice extent in March was 5.7% below average, the lowest on record for the month, but only marginally below the previous March record set in 2025 (5.6% below average).

The daily Arctic sea ice extent reached its joint lowest winter maximum, tied with the winter maximum recorded in March 2025.

Regionally, sea ice cover was most below average in northern Barents Sea and Svalbard region, and in the Sea of Okhotsk, both of which experienced much warmer-than-average conditions during the month.

In the Antarctic, the monthly sea ice extent was 10% below the March average, ranking outside the ten lowest for the month, coming after four years of much larger negative anomalies in March (20% to 33% below average).

2. Daily sea ice extent and annual maximum

3. Sea ice concentration anomalies across the Arctic

The average sea ice extent in the Arctic in March 2026 was 14.2 million km², 0.9 million km² or 5.7% below the 1991–2020 average for March.

This was the lowest March extent in the 48-year satellite record.

It was only marginally lower than the previous lowest March extent, in March 2025 (5.6% below average).

Since November 2025, Arctic sea ice extent has ranked among the three lowest on record for the corresponding month: second lowest in November, lowest in December, and third lowest in January and February.

The daily Arctic sea ice extent reached its winter maximum on 7 March, at 14.43 million km 2 according to data from EUMETSAT OSI SAF.

This is only marginally higher (0.01 million km 2 higher) than the lowest winter maximum of 14.42 million km2 recorded in March 2025.

Given this small difference, the 2026 value can be considered tied with 2025 for the lowest winter maximum in the satellite record.

The exact date, extent, and ranking of the winter maximum can vary slightly between datasets, particularly when values are so close.

The US National Snow and Ice Data Center reported a 2026 winter maximum marginally lower (0.01 million km 2 lower) than in 2025, also describing them as tied for the lowest.

During about half of the month (16 days), the daily extent ranked lowest for the corresponding calendar day.

In March 2026, sea ice cover was most below average in two opposite sectors of the Arctic: a region comprising the northern Barents Sea and the area north of Svalbard; and the Sea of Okhotsk, off the far-eastern coast of Russia.

Both regions experienced much above-average surface air temperatures during the month.

The Bering Sea was the main region of above-average sea ice cover.

The average Antarctic sea ice extent in March 2026 was 3.9 million km², 0.4 million km² or 10% below the 1991–2020 average for March, ranking 14th lowest for March in the satellite record

This contrasts with the larger negative March anomalies of 20% to 33% observed over the previous four years and follows a near-average extent in February 2026.

After reaching its summer minimum in February, the daily sea ice extent around Antarctica resumed its seasonal growth.

It remained very close to the 1991–2020 median until around 20 February, then stayed consistently below it for the rest of the month.

In March 2026, sea ice cover was below average in most sectors of the Southern Ocean except in the northern Weddell Sea and, to a lesser extent, the Amundsen Sea, where it was above average.

Above-average sea ice cover in the Weddell Sea was already observed in January and February. The monthly sea ice extent for this ocean sector was the highest for March in 11 years.

February 2026 was the fifth-warmest February globally, with an average surface air temperature of 13.26°C, 0.53°C above the 1991-2020 average for February, according to the ERA5 dataset. The warmest February on record was in 2024.

February 2026 was 1.49°C above the estimated 1850-1900 average used to define the pre-industrial level, according to the ERA5 dataset.

The average temperature over European land for February 2026 was one of the three coldest in the past 14 years at -0.07°C, 0.10°C below the 1991-2020 average for February.

There was a strong contrast in average temperature across Europe, with western, southern and southeast Europe experiencing above-average temperatures, while Fennoscandia, the Baltic States, and northwest Russia had cold conditions.

Outside Europe, warmer-than-average temperatures occurred across the United States, northeast Canada, the Middle East, central Asia, and east Antarctica; in contrast, cold conditions occurred across Alaska, northern Canada, Greenland, and northern Russia.

The global-average temperature for boreal winter (December 2025 to February 2026) was the fifth highest on record at 0.51°C above the 1991-2020 average.

For Europe, the past winter was one of the two coldest winters in the last 13 years at 0.09°C above the 1991-2020 average.

Boreal winter temperatures largely reflected the regional contrasts in temperature and precipitation anomalies experienced in February.

The average sea surface temperature (SST) for February 2026 over 60°S–60°N was 20.88°C, the joint second-highest value on record for the month (with February 2025), and 0.18°C below the January 2024 record.

There was a notable SST gradient between cold SSTs in the central and west North Atlantic and warm SSTs in the subtropical North Atlantic which likely favoured the development of storms that reached Europe.

0.27°C and 0.22°C cooler than the two warmest boreal winters in 2024 (0.78°C) and 2025 (0.73°C), respectively.

The European average temperature over land for boreal winter (December 2025 to February 2026) was 0.09°C above the 1991-2020 average. It was 2.75°C cooler than the warmest European winter of 2020 (2.84°C).

0.78°C higher than the 1991-2020 annual average

0.89°C cooler than the highest 12-month average on record for the continent (February 2024 to January 2025).

In February 2026, there was a strong contrast in average temperatures across Europe. Western, southern and southeast Europe and Türkiye experienced above average temperatures, with the largest anomalies seen over Türkiye. Conversely, there were widespread cold conditions across Fennoscandia, the Baltic States, and northwest Russia, making it the second month in a row that these regions had cold conditions.

Outside Europe, warmer-than-average temperatures were most pronounced across the United States, northeast Canada, the Middle East, central Asia, and east Antarctica. In contrast, cold conditions occurred across Alaska, northern Canada, Greenland, and northern Russia. Mixed conditions were experienced across Australia and Antarctica.

In Europe, temperatures for boreal winter reflected the patterns in February shown above. Temperatures were above the 1991-2020 average over western and southern areas, while temperatures were below average across Fennoscandia, the Baltic States, and northwest Russia. The temperature averaged over Arctic land areas was colder than in any month since February 2002.

Outside Europe, temperatures also mostly reflected those seen in February. Temperatures were most above average across the western United States, the Labrador Sea, the Arctic, central Asia, the far east of Russia, and east Antarctica. Conversely, Alaska, northern Canada, and Siberia had colder-than-average conditions.

The average sea surface temperature (SST) for 60°S–60°N remained at historically high levels throughout February, with a monthly mean of 20.88°C, 0.41°C above the 1991–2020 average. This ranked as the joint-second-highest value on record for the month (with February 2025), and 0.18°C below the January 2024 record when there was an El Niño in progress.

The SST patterns in February 2026 were relatively similar to those observed in January 2026. SSTs were close to or below average in the central equatorial Pacific, reflecting weakening or neutral La Niña conditions. A large region of the subtropical and northern North Atlantic had the warmest SSTs on record for the time of year, as did many parts of the North and South Pacific. The Mediterranean Sea generally saw warmer-than-average SSTs with some small record-high pockets. Below-average SSTs were recorded in the western and central North Atlantic, the Bay of Bengal, the Indian Ocean, and the Tasman Sea. Over the North Atlantic, the relatively pronounced SST gradient was a contributing factor to the active storm track this winter.

In the Arctic, the average sea ice extent in February was 5% below average, ranking third lowest on record for the month.

Regionally, sea ice cover was below average in the Labrador Sea, Baffin Bay and the Sea of Okhotsk. It was unusually high in the Greenland Sea, where sea ice extent reached a 22-year high for February.

In the Antarctic, the monthly sea ice extent was close to average for February, in sharp contrast to the much below-average extents (25% to 33% below average) observed over the past four years.

Daily Antarctic sea ice extent likely reached its summer minimum on 22 February. This minimum was near the middle of the range for the past 48 years, in contrast to the record or near-record lows of the previous four years. The lowest minimum occurred in 2023.

The average sea ice extent in the Arctic in February 2026 was 14.1 million km², 0.7 million km² or about 5% below the 1991–2020 average for February.

This was the third lowest February extent in the 48-year satellite record.

The lowest February extent was 8% below average in 2025.

Since November 2025, Arctic sea ice extent has ranked among the three lowest on record for the corresponding month: second lowest in November, lowest in December, and third lowest in January.

The daily Arctic sea ice extent typically reaches its winter maximum in March. In seven of the 48 years from 1979 to 2025, this maximum occurred in February.

During February, the daily extent ranked between second and seventh lowest for the time of year. On 28 February, the daily extent ranked second lowest for that date.

In February 2026, sea ice cover was most below average in the Labrador Sea and Baffin Bay, in the Sea of Okhotsk, and to a lesser extent, in the eastern Barents Sea.

Sea ice cover was above average in the Greenland Sea. Sea ice extent for this ocean sector was the highest for February in 22 years.

Above-average sea ice cover in the Greenland Sea often results from strong southward export of sea ice from the central Arctic Ocean through Fram Strait (between northeastern Greenland and Svalbard). This was the case in February 2026.

The average Antarctic sea ice extent in February 2026 was close to the 1991–2020 average for February, at 3.2 million km², only 0.1 million km² or about 3% below average.

This is in sharp contrast to the large negative anomalies of 25% to 33% observed in the past four years.

February is typically the month when Antarctic sea ice extent reaches its summer minimum.

The summer minimum for 2026 may have been reached on 22 February, at 2.86 million km².

If confirmed later in March, this minimum would rank 19th lowest in the 48-year satellite record, placing it close to the middle of the range for this period.

This low ranking contrasts sharply with the record or near-record minima of the past four years: third lowest in 2022, lowest in 2023, fifth lowest in 2024, and second lowest in 2025.

In February 2026, little sea ice remained around Antarctica (only 18% compared to the extent at the last annual maximum in September 2025).

The most prominent pattern was above-average sea ice cover in the northern Weddell Sea, a feature already seen in January. Sea ice extent for this ocean sector was the highest for February in 11 years.

Global temperature January 2026 was the fifth-warmest January globally, with an average surface air temperature of 12.95°C, 0.51°C above the 1991-2020 average for January, according to the ERA5 dataset.

In comparison to the warmest January on record in 2025, January 2026 was 0.28°C cooler.

January 2026 was 1.47°C above the estimated 1850-1900 average used to define the pre-industrial level, according to the ERA5 dataset.

Europe and other regions During the second half of January 2026, severe cold conditions affected large parts of the Northern Hemisphere, including North America, Europe and Siberia, mainly driven by a wavier-than-usual polar jet stream that allowed frigid Arctic air to spill into mid-latitudes.

The average temperature over European land for January 2026 was -2.34°C, 1.63°C below the 1991-2020 average for January, making it the coldest January since 2010.

Widespread cold conditions occurred across Fennoscandia, the Baltic States, eastern Europe, Siberia, and the central and eastern United States.

The largest warmer-than-average temperatures occurred across the Arctic, most notably in most of the Canadian Arctic Archipelago, Baffin Bay, Greenland, and the Russian Far East. Above-average temperatures were also recorded across southern South America, northern Africa, central Asia, and most of Australia and Antarctica.

Sea surface temperature The average sea surface temperature (SST) for January 2026 over 60°S–60°N was 20.68°C, the fourth-highest value on record for the month, 0.29°C below the January 2024 record.

A large region of the subtropical and the northeast North Atlantic, including the Norwegian Sea, had the warmest SSTs on record for the time of year.

Most of the North Pacific continued to experience much above-average SSTs. In contrast, SSTs were close to or below the 1991-2020 average in the central and eastern equatorial Pacific, reflecting weak La Niña conditions.

The most below-average SSTs were recorded in the Arabian Sea, the Indian Ocean, the central South Pacific Ocean, and in the Tasman Sea.

In January 2026, the most striking feature in Europe was the widespread cold conditions across Fennoscandia, the Baltic States, and eastern Europe. Across Lapland, temperatures below -30°C were commonplace, and Lappeenranta, southern Finland, had its coldest night this winter of -31.5°C. Monthly average temperatures were more than 10°C colder than normal in both southern and northern Norway, and the country as a whole experienced its 15 th coldest January in a record dating back to 1901. Estonia had one of its coldest Januarys in 25 years, and at the end of the month Latvia also had its largest cold spell since February 2012. In contrast, warmer-than-average conditions were found across most of Italy, in the Balkans, and in western Türkiye. Mixed or slightly below-average temperatures were experienced in many regions of western Europe. These conditions masked the underlying variability, for example, in the United Kingdom where there was a cold start to the month.

Outside Europe, the cold conditions across eastern Europe stretched eastward across most of Russia and Siberia, where temperatures dropped to around -40°C. Alaska also had a cold first half of the month, reflected in the monthly anomalies. There was a contrast between colder-than-average temperatures in the central and eastern United States – where bitterly cold conditions occurred towards the end of the month – and the western United States, such as Utah, and western Canada where warm conditions were experienced. The extreme cold conditions experienced across the central and eastern United States are not well captured by the monthly temperature anomalies, as much warmer-than-average conditions during the first half of January partly offset the severe cold in the second half. See the Highlight Box for further information.

Warmer-than-average temperatures were most pronounced across the Arctic, most notably across much of the Canadian Arctic Archipelago, Baffin Bay, Greenland, and the Russian Far East. Above-average temperatures were also recorded across southern South America, northern Africa, central Asia, and most of Australia. A large part of Australia had two heatwaves in January, with multiple locations having their highest maximum temperature ever recorded. For example, Renmark, South Australia had its hottest day on record at 49.6°C. The month as a whole was the fourth-warmest January in Australia since 1910. Antarctica was mostly warmer than average.

During the second half of January 2026, severe cold conditions affected large parts of the Northern Hemisphere, including North America, Europe and Siberia. This is illustrated in the figure above (left panel), which shows conditions on 24 January.

These cold outbreaks were mainly driven by short-term changes in large-scale atmospheric circulation patterns. The polar jet stream, a fast-flowing band of winds about 8–12 km above the surface that separates cold polar air from warmer air to the south, became wavier than usual. This allowed frigid Arctic air to spill into mid-latitudes instead of remaining confined near the pole, as shown in the right panel of the figure.

This circulation was associated with a weakened and distorted stratospheric polar vortex, a large region of very strong winds and extremely cold air much higher up in the atmosphere (about 15–50 km above the Arctic). Such conditions are known to increase the likelihood of a wavier jet stream below, although the strength and timing of this link can vary between events.

By reducing the temperature contrast between the Arctic and mid-latitudes, the rapid warming of the Arctic in recent decades may favour a wavier polar jet stream, increasing the likelihood of cold outbreaks. However, the extent of this influence remains an active area of scientific research. Furthermore, the cold episodes observed in January 2026 were linked to short-term changes in atmospheric circulation. Such regional temperature fluctuations can temporarily outweigh the long-term warming trend, but do not contradict our understanding of climate change.

The average sea surface temperature (SST) for 60°S–60°N remained at historically high levels throughout January, with a monthly mean of 20.68°C, 0.37°C above the 1991–2020 average. This ranked as the fourth-highest value on record for January, below those observed in 2024, 2025, and 2016.

The SST patterns in January 2026 were relatively similar to those observed in December 2025. SSTs were close to or below average in the central and eastern equatorial Pacific, reflecting weak La Niña conditions. A large region of the subtropical and the northeast North Atlantic had the warmest SSTs on record for the time of year. The majority of the northern Pacific continued to experience well-above-average SSTs. Other large regions with much-warmer-than-average to record-warm SSTs include part of the central South Atlantic, a region off the coast of East Antarctica, and the South Pacific to the north of New Zealand. The Mediterranean Sea, Gulf of Mexico and the Caribbean generally saw much warmer-than-average SSTs with some small record-high pockets.

Below-average SSTs were recorded in the Arabian Sea, the Bay of Bengal, the Indian Ocean, and the central South Pacific Ocean. Other cooler-than-average regions include parts of the North and South Atlantic Oceans, and areas in the Tasman Sea.

Highlights for January 2026 In the Arctic, the average sea ice extent in January was 6% below average, the third lowest on record for the month.

Regionally, sea ice concentrations were much below average in the northern Barents Sea, between Svalbard and Franz Josef Land, as well as in Baffin Bay and Labrador Sea, coinciding with much-above-average surface air temperatures in those regions.

In the Antarctic, the monthly sea ice extent was 8% below average, outside of the 10 lowest extents for the month.

Sea ice concentrations around Antarctica were above average in the Weddell Sea, but generally below average in other ocean sectors, particularly in the Bellingshausen Sea.

In the Arctic, the average sea ice extent in January 2026 was 13.2 million km², 0.8 million km² (about 5.5%) below the 1991–2020 average. This was the third lowest January extent in the 48-year satellite record, only slightly higher than the lowest values recorded in 2018 and 2025 (6% below average). Although all January extents since 2005 have been below average (except for a near-zero anomaly in 2009), they have shown marked year-to-year variability over this period. The last two Januarys have been notable for their unusually low extents for the month.

As Arctic sea ice extent is approaching its annual maximum, which typically occurs in March, it remained at historically low levels for the time of year throughout January. For more than half of the month, daily values ranked among the three lowest on record for the time of year (lowest on 3 days, second lowest on 12 days, and third lowest on 4 days) and remained close to the low levels observed in 2025. On 31 January, the daily extent was the fourth lowest for that calendar date, at 13.76 million km².

In January 2026, the pattern of Arctic sea ice concentration anomalies showed some similarities with that observed in December. Much lower-than-average sea-ice cover persisted in two main regions: the northern Barents Sea, including the area between Svalbard and Franz Josef Land; and in Baffin Bay and the Labrador Sea. All these regions also experienced much-above-average surface air temperatures during the month. Hudson Bay was fully ice-covered in January after a much-delayed freeze-up in December, while above-average ice cover developed in the Greenland Sea. In the Pacific sector, below-average ice cover prevailed in the Sea of Okhotsk and the western Bering Sea, whereas the eastern Bering Sea saw above-average ice cover.

The average Antarctic sea ice extent in January 2026 was 5.0 million km², 0.5 million km² (about 8%) below the 1991–2020 average for January, and slightly lower than in January 2025 (5% below average). The January 2026 extent ranked 18th lowest for the month and was closer to average than the much lower January extents observed in some years since 2017, including the record low of 31% below average in 2023. Antarctic sea ice extent in January has shown substantial year-to-year variability since the beginning of satellite observations in the late 1970s, more pronounced than in the Arctic.

As Antarctic sea ice extent is nearing its annual minimum, typically reached in February, it hovered very close to the median extent for 1991–2020 throughout the second half of the month. Values were also similar to those observed in January 2025, when the daily extent went on to reach its second-lowest annual minimum in February. At this time of year, when relatively little sea ice remains around Antarctica, the daily extent can change rapidly, particularly in response to shifts in wind patterns. On 31 January 2026, the daily extent was 3.85 million km², ranking 26th lowest for that calendar date.

In January 2026, the most prominent pattern was above-average sea ice cover in the Weddell Sea and below-average cover in the Bellingshausen Sea. In other sectors, concentration anomalies were often mixed, although below-average conditions generally prevailed.

The sea ice indicator page provides additional information about the long-term trends in both the Arctic and Antarctica.

Global temperature December 2025 was the fifth-warmest December globally, with an average surface air temperature of 13.15°C, 0.49°C above the 1991-2020 average for December, according to the ERA5 dataset.

December 2025 was 0.36°C cooler than the warmest December on record in 2023.

December 2025 was 1.42°C above the estimated 1850-1900 average used to define the pre-industrial level.

The year 2025 was 0.59°C above the 1991-2020 annual average, making it the third-warmest year on record, only 0.01°C cooler than 2023, the second-warmest year, and 0.13°C cooler than 2024, the warmest year on record.

The year 2025 was 1.47°C above the pre-industrial level, according to the ERA5 dataset.

Further results for 2025 are presented in our Global Climate Highlights 2025 report.

Europe and other regions The average temperature over European land for December 2025 was 2.68°C, 1.99°C above the 1991-2020 average for December, making it the joint-fourth-warmest December.

Most of Europe had warmer-than-average temperatures, with the most pronounced anomalies found over central Norway and Sweden and Iceland.

Only a few areas of the Iberian Peninsula had below-average temperatures.

The year 2025 was 1.17°C higher than the 1991-2020 annual average, making it the third-warmest year on record for Europe, only 0.02°C cooler than 2020, the second-warmest year, and 0.30°C cooler than 2024, the warmest year on record.

Outside Europe, a striking temperature contrast occurred across North America, with Canada experiencing extreme cold and the western contiguous United States having warmer-than-average conditions. Above-average temperatures were also recorded across Greenland, Baffin Bay, the European Arctic, most of Asia and eastern Antarctica.

Negative temperature anomalies occurred over northern Siberia, India and across southern and northwest Africa.

Sea surface temperature The average sea surface temperature (SST) for December 2025 over 60°S–60°N was 20.50°C, the fourth-highest value on record for the month, 0.33°C below the December 2023 record.

A large region of the subtropical and the northeast North Atlantic, including the Norwegian Sea, had the warmest SSTs on record for the time of year.

Most of the North Pacific continued to experience much above-average SSTs. In contrast, SSTs were close to or below the 1991-2020 average in the central and eastern equatorial Pacific, reflecting a transition from ENSO-neutral to weak La Niña conditions.

The most below-average SSTs were recorded in the Arabian Sea, the southwestern Indian Ocean, the central South Pacific Ocean, and in the Tasman Sea / Southern Ocean off the southeastern coast of Australia.

Six months in 2025 had a global temperature estimate above the 1.5°C pre-industrial levels. This means that 22 months in the ERA5 dataset have recorded temperatures above this 1.5°C level, with most occurring in a near-continuous streak between July 2023 and April 2025, and the earliest three were January to March 2016.

Given the small differences in global temperature anomalies relative to 1991–2020 among datasets, the months identified here as exceeding or not exceeding the 1.5°C threshold may differ in datasets other than ERA5. Furthermore, due to dataset differences, there is inherent uncertainty in the estimated temperature change from pre-industrial levels to the 1991–2020 average.

In December 2025, most of Europe had warmer-than-average temperatures. The most pronounced anomalies were seen over central Norway and Sweden and in surrounding regions in Fennoscandia. Iceland also had notable warm conditions, with Christmas Eve – at 19.8°C – being the warmest 24th December on record at a station in the east of the country. The European Arctic in and around Svalbard, eastern Europe, the Balkans and the Alps experienced pronounced above-average temperatures. The warm signal over the Alps was potentially related to below-average snow cover in the region. Mixed conditions occurred across the Iberian Peninsula, and near-average or slightly above-average temperatures were experienced in northern Fennoscandia, western Türkiye, as well as in France and Germany despite a cold spell at the end of the month.

Outside Europe, a striking temperature contrast occurred across North America. Canada experienced record cold, where notably Braeburn in the Yukon recorded -55.7°C on 23 December, the coldest December temperature in Canada since 1975. Alaska also had cold conditions, with some regions having their coldest month since January 2012. Conversely, the western contiguous United States had warmer-than-average conditions with some record temperatures observed. Above-average temperatures were also recorded across Greenland, Baffin Bay, and in the Russian Far East, including Chukotka. Most of Asia saw much-warmer-than-average temperatures, too. Other regions with warmer-than-average temperatures include north central Africa, western Australia, and east Antarctica. Conversely, a large part of Siberia had colder-than-average temperatures, as did India and across southern and northwest Africa.

The ERA5 data indicate large anomalies, positive and negative, over parts of the Himalayan range and the Tibetan Plateau. This area is notoriously challenging for ERA5, and these values should therefore be treated with caution.

Further results for 2025 are presented in our Global Climate Highlights 2025 report.

The average sea surface temperature (SST) for 60°S–60°N remained at historically high levels throughout December, with a monthly mean of 20.50°C, 0.33°C above the 1991–2020 average. This ranked as the fourth-highest value on record for December, below those observed in 2023, 2024, and 2015.

A large region of the subtropical and the northeast North Atlantic had the warmest SSTs on record for the time of year. The majority of the northern Pacific continued to experience well-above-average SSTs, although over a smaller area compared to that of November. Other large regions with much-warmer-than-average to record-warm SSTs include part of the central South Atlantic, a large region off the coast of East Antarctica, and the South Pacific off eastern Australia. The Mediterranean Sea, Gulf of Mexico and the Caribbean generally saw much warmer-than-average SSTs with some small record-high pockets.

The most below-average SSTs were recorded in the Arabian Sea, the southwestern Indian Ocean, and the central South Pacific Ocean. SSTs were close to or below average in the central and eastern equatorial Pacific, reflecting a transition from ENSO-neutral to weak La Niña conditions. Other cooler-than-average regions include parts of the North and South Atlantic Oceans, and areas in the Tasman Sea / Southern Ocean off the southeastern coast of Australia.

Highlights for December 2025 In the Arctic, the average sea ice extent in December was 9% below average, the lowest on record for the month. This marked the second consecutive year in which December sea ice extent reached record-low levels for the time of year.

Regionally, as in November, sea ice concentrations were much below average in the western Eurasian sector (around Svalbard, Franz Josef Land, and the Kara Sea) and in the northeastern Canadian sector, generally coinciding with much-above-average surface air temperatures in both regions.

In the Antarctic, the monthly sea ice extent was the seventh lowest for December, at 10% below average, in line with the December anomalies often observed since 2017.

Sea ice concentrations around Antarctica were most below average in the Bellingshausen Sea, above average in the Weddell Sea and generally mixed in the other ocean sectors.

In the Arctic, the average sea ice extent in December 2025 was 11.3 million km², 1.1 million km² (about 9%) below the 1991–2020 average. This was the lowest December extent in the 47-year satellite record, surpassing the previous record low for December of about 8% below average observed a year ago, in 2024. Before 2024, the lowest December extent dated back to 2016.

While December sea ice extent anomalies have been negative every year since then, those recorded between 2017 and 2023 were all smaller than 5% below average and did not show a clear trend. The larger negative anomalies observed in December over the past two years are therefore a more recent phenomenon. In both years, even though the annual minimum sea ice extent in September was not record-breaking, the extent later went on to reach record low values for the time of year during autumn and early winter.

As Arctic sea ice extent continued its seasonal growth, the record-low values for the time of year observed near the end of November persisted into December. The daily extent ranked lowest for the time of year during most of December (22 days), below the previous lowest values recorded a year earlier, in December 2024. On 31 December, the daily extent was the lowest on record for that date, at 12.45 million km².

In December 2025, the pattern of sea ice concentration anomalies across the Arctic showed similarities to the pattern observed in November, with much less sea ice cover in two main sectors: the western Eurasian sector, including the northern Barents Sea and the area to the north, between Svalbard and Franz Josef Land, as well as the western Kara Sea; and the northeastern Canadian sector, including all of Hudson Bay and part of Baffin Bay. Parts of these two regions saw much-above-average surface air temperatures in December. Sea ice concentrations were near average over the central Arctic Ocean and mixed and relatively small in the Pacific sector (Bering Sea and Sea of Okhotsk).

The average Antarctic sea ice extent in December 2025 was 9.9 million km², 1.1 million km² (about 10%) below the 1991–2020 average for December, making it the seventh-lowest December extent in the satellite record. The December 2025 anomaly is in line with the negative values around 8–10% below average often observed following the record-low December extent of 20% below average in 2016. The fact that the fourth- and fifth-lowest December extents date back to 1979 and 1982 also highlights the substantial interannual to decadal variability that has characterised Antarctic sea ice extent since the late 1970s.

As Antarctic sea ice extent continued to decrease towards its annual minimum, expected in February, it hovered between the second-lowest and tenth-lowest values for the time of year, closely tracking the levels observed in December 2024. On 31 December, it ranked seventh lowest, at 6.78 million km², virtually on a par with values observed in 2023.

In December 2025, sea ice concentration anomalies were mixed across most sectors of the Southern Ocean. Only a few anomalies stood out: above-average sea ice cover in the Weddell Sea and – on the opposite side of Antarctica – east of the Ross Sea (off the coast of Adélie Land); and much-below-average sea ice cover in the Bellingshausen Sea.

Global temperature November 2025 was the third-warmest November globally, with an average surface air temperature of 14.02°C, 0.65°C above the 1991-2020 average for November, according to the ERA5 dataset.

November 2025 was 0.20°C cooler than the warmest November on record, in 2023, and 0.08°C cooler than the second warmest, in 2024.

November 2025 was 1.54°C above the estimated 1850-1900 average used to define the pre-industrial level, the second month – after October 2025 – above 1.50°C since April 2025.

The global average temperature anomaly for January to November 2025 stands at 0.60°C above the 1991–2020 average, or 1.48°C above the 1850–1900 pre-industrial reference, the same anomalies recorded for the full year 2023, currently the second warmest year.

2025 is virtually certain to finish as the second- or third-warmest year on record, possibly tied with 2023, the current second-warmest year, and behind 2024, the warmest year on record.

While 2025 may not reach 1.5°C above the pre-industrial level, the average global temperature for 2023–2025 is likely to exceed 1.5°C, which would be the first three-year average to do so in the instrumental period, according to the ERA5 dataset.

Seasonal highlights The global-average temperature for the boreal autumn 2025 (September to November) was the third highest on record at 0.67°C above the 1991-2020 average, and cooler only than the boreal autumns of 2023 and 2024.

T emperatures were mostly above average across the world and especially in northern Canada, over the Arctic Ocean, and across Antarctica. Mixed conditions occurred across southern Asia. Notable cold anomalies were experienced in northeastern Russia.

Europe and other regions The average temperature over European land for November 2025 was 5.74°C, 1.38°C above the 1991-2020 average for November, making it the fifth-warmest November.

The most pronounced above-average air temperatures for Europe were recorded over eastern Europe, Russia, the Balkans, and Türkiye.

Below-average temperatures were mainly experienced over northern Sweden and Finland, Iceland, and parts of northern Italy and southern Germany.

A large region with pronounced negative temperature anomalies occurred over northern Siberia, covering much of northeastern Russia.

Seasonal highlights Europe had its fourth-warmest autumn season on record at 1.06°C above the 1991-2020 average.

Autumn temperatures were above average across eastern areas, especially Fennoscandia and Russia. Western and central Europe, except the Iberian Peninsula, mostly experienced near-average temperatures.

Sea surface temperature The average sea surface temperature (SST) for November 2025 over 60°S–60°N was 20.42°C, the fourth-highest value on record for the month, 0.29°C below the November 2023 record.

Most of the North Pacific continued to experience much above-average SSTs, with record highs in the west. In contrast, SSTs were close to or below the 1991-2020 average in the central and eastern equatorial Pacific, reflecting a transition from ENSO-neutral to weak La Niña conditions.

The Norwegian Sea saw much-above to record-breaking SSTs, as did the Coral Sea, off the eastern coast of Australia.

October and November 2025 end a short, five-month period that were less than 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels. November is thus the 28th month in the ERA5 data record over that level. The majority (21) of the months were recorded in a near-continuous streak between July 2023 and April 2025, and the earliest three were January to March 2016.

Given the small differences in global temperature anomalies relative to 1991–2020 among datasets, the months identified here as exceeding or not exceeding the 1.5°C threshold may differ in datasets other than ERA5. Furthermore, due to dataset differences, there is inherent uncertainty in the estimated temperature change from pre-industrial levels to the 1991–2020 average.

The global-average temperature for the boreal autumn 2025 (September to November) was the third highest on record at 0.67°C above the 1991-2020 average for these three months. This is 0.21°C and 0.08°C cooler than the two warmest boreal autumns in 2023 (0.88°C) and 2024 (0.75°C), respectively.

Averaged over the first eleven months of 2025 (January to November), the global surface air temperature anomaly stands at 0.60°C above the 1991–2020 average, equivalent to 1.48°C above the 1850–1900 pre-industrial reference. These values are identical to those recorded for the full year 2023, currently the second-warmest year. It is therefore virtually certain by now that 2025 will finish as the second- or third-warmest year on record, possibly tied with 2023, and behind 2024 (0.72°C above 1991–2020, 1.60°C above 1850–1900), the warmest year on record. While 2025 may not reach 1.5°C above the pre-industrial level, the average global temperature for 2023–2025 is likely to exceed 1.5°C. This would be the first three-year average to do so in the instrumental period, according to the ERA5 dataset.

Averaging over 12-month periods smooths out shorter-term variations in regional- and global-average temperatures.

The European average temperature over land for autumn 2025 (September to November) was 1.06°C above the 1991-2020 average. It is the fourth-warmest autumn season on record. It was 0.40°C cooler than the warmest European autumn of 2020 (1.46°C) and 0.37°C and 0.19°C cooler than the second and third warmest autumns in 2023 and 2024, respectively. It was also only 0.01°C warmer than autumn 2006.

1.12°C higher than the 1991-2020 annual average

0.55°C cooler than the highest 12-month average on record for the continent (February 2024 to January 2025)

In November 2025, the most pronounced warmer-than-average European temperatures were seen over eastern Europe, Russia, the Balkans, and Türkiye. Notably, Kyiv, Ukraine, had its fourth-warmest November on record. Above-average temperatures also occurred across northern France, most of the United Kingdom and Ireland, and across central Norway and Sweden. Note that the average temperature anomalies reported can sometimes mask the variability throughout the month, which was the case this month for western Europe, where a cold wave occurred around 21-22 November, for example in France and the United Kingdom. Below-average temperatures were mostly experienced over northern Sweden and Finland and Iceland. Parts of northern Italy and southern Germany also experienced below-average temperatures.

Outside Europe, the largest positive temperature anomalies were recorded over Nunavut in northern Canada and the Canadian archipelago, as well as over the western Eurasian sector of the Arctic, including the Kara and northern Barents Seas, where temperatures exceeded 7°C above average. These warm anomalies coincided with much-below-average sea ice cover. Most of East Antarctica saw much-warmer-than-average temperatures, with a large region seeing values more than 4°C above average. Other regions with warmer-than-average temperatures include most other areas of Canada, the central and western United States, northwestern Africa, parts of the central Asian republics and southeast Russia, northeast China, southern South America, and New Zealand. A very large region of below-average temperatures was seen across northern Siberia, covering much of northern Russia. Other large regions with below-average temperatures include parts of India, the southwest of the Arabian Peninsula, southern Australia, and southern Africa.

The ERA5 data indicate large anomalies, positive and negative, over parts of the Himalayan range and the Tibetan Plateau. The cold anomalies are likely related to unseasonably early and heavy snowfall over the area brought about by the remnants of cyclone Montha in early November. However, this area is notoriously challenging for ERA5, and these values should therefore be treated with caution.

In Europe, temperatures for the boreal autumn of 2025 were above the 1991-2020 average over eastern areas, especially Fennoscandia and Russia. Western and central Europe, except the Iberian Peninsula, mostly experienced near-average temperatures.

Outside Europe, temperatures were mostly above average across the world and especially in northern Canada, over the Arctic Ocean, and across Antarctica. Mixed conditions occurred across southern Asia. Notable cold anomalies were experienced in northeastern Russia.

The average sea surface temperature (SST) for 60°S–60°N remained at historically high levels throughout November, with a monthly mean of 20.42°C, 0.29°C above the 1991–2020 average. This ranked as the fourth highest value on record for November, below those observed in 2023, 2024, and 2015.

A majority of the northern Pacific continued to experience well-above-average SSTs over an area similar to that of October, with record highs this month particularly in the west. Other large regions with much-warmer-than-average to record-warm SSTs include off eastern Australia in the Coral Sea, part of the central South Atlantic, much of the Norwegian Sea, as well as a region off the coast of East Antarctica, in a sector marked by much-below-average sea ice cover. The Mediterranean Sea, Gulf of Mexico and the Caribbean generally saw much warmer-than-average SSTs with some small record-high pockets.

The most below-average SSTs were recorded in the Sea of Okhotsk, in the Arabian Sea, and in the western Indian Ocean. SSTs were close to or below average in the central and eastern equatorial Pacific, reflecting a transition from ENSO-neutral to weak La Niña conditions. Other cooler-than-average regions include parts of the North and South Atlantic Oceans, areas in the South Pacific and the Tasman Sea / Southern Ocean off the southeastern coast of Australia.

Highlights for November 2025 In the Arctic, the average sea ice extent in November was 12% below average, ranking as the second lowest for the month.

Regionally, sea ice concentrations were much below average in the western Eurasian sector (around Svalbard, Franz Josef Land, and the Kara Sea) and in the northeastern Canadian sector, coinciding with much-above-average surface air temperatures in both regions.

In the Antarctic, the monthly sea ice extent was the fourth lowest for November, at 7% below average.

Sea ice concentrations around Antarctica were most below average in the Bellingshausen Sea and the Indian Ocean sector, while above-average or mixed conditions prevailed elsewhere.

In the Arctic, the average sea ice extent in November 2025 was 9.1 million km², 1.2 million km² (about 12%) below the 1991–2020 average. This ranked as the second-lowest November extent in the 47-year satellite record, not far from the lowest value of -13% recorded in 2016. The November 2025 anomaly is also significantly larger in magnitude than the negative values observed in some of the most recent years (2 to 4% below average in 2021–2023).

While the Arctic sea ice cover continued its seasonal growth, this growth has been slower than average since the last week of October. The daily extent ranked second lowest for the time of year during most of November, slightly above the 2016 values, before becoming the lowest in the final days of the month. On 30 November, the daily extent ranked lowest for that date, at 9.74 million km².

In November 2025, sea ice concentrations were most below-average in two main parts of the Arctic: the western Eurasian sector, including areas around Svalbard and Franz Josef Land as well as the nearby Kara Sea; and the northeastern Canadian sector, including Baffin Bay and northern Hudson Bay. Both regions experienced much-above-average surface air temperatures in November, exceeding 7°C above average in some areas. In contrast, aside from a small area of above-average sea ice cover in the Chukchi Sea, most of the remainder of the Arctic saw near-average sea ice conditions during the month.

The average Antarctic sea ice extent in November 2025 was 15.1 million km², 1.2 million km² (about 7%) below the 1991–2020 average for November, making it the fourth-lowest November extent in the satellite record. The negative anomaly for November 2025 was not as large as those recorded in November 2023 (-9%) and 2024 (-10%). However, it fits within a pattern of relatively large negative November anomalies observed since 2016, with the notable exception of 2020 (+2%).

As the Antarctic sea ice melt season gets progressively under way, the daily extent hovered between the third- and fifth-lowest values for the time of year during November. By 30 November, it ranked fourth lowest, at 13.11 million km², virtually on a par with the values observed in 2023.

In November 2025, sea ice concentration anomalies around Antarctica showed a pattern similar to that observed in October: below-average concentrations in the Indian Ocean sector and in the Bellingshausen Sea, and above-average or mixed concentrations across other sectors.
