October 2025 was 0.16°C cooler than the warmest October on record, in 2023, and 0.11°C cooler than October 2024.

October 2025 was 1.55°C above the estimated 1850-1900 average used to define the pre-industrial level, the first month above 1.50°C since April 2025.

The 12-month average temperature from November 2024 to October 2025 was 0.62°C above the 1991-2020 average, and 1.50°C above the pre-industrial level.

2025 is virtually certain to finish as the second- or third-warmest year on record, possibly tied with 2023, the current second-warmest year, and behind 2024, the warmest year on record.

While 2025 may not reach 1.5°C above the pre-industrial level, the average global temperature for 2023–2025 is likely to exceed 1.5°C, which would be the first three-year average to do so in the instrumental period, according to the ERA5 dataset.

Europe and other regions The average temperature over European land for October 2025 was 10.19°C, 0.60°C above the 1991-2020 average for October, ranking outside the ten warmest on record.

The most pronounced above-average air temperatures for Europe were recorded over Fennoscandia and the southern Iberian Peninsula.

Below-average temperatures were mainly experienced over southeastern Europe.

A large region with pronounced negative temperature anomalies covered much of southern and eastern Russia and Mongolia, as well as the eastern parts of Kazakhstan and northernmost China.

Sea surface temperature The average sea surface temperature (SST) for October 2025 over 60°S–60°N was 20.54°C, the third-highest value on record for the month, 0.24°C below the October 2023 record and only 0.02°C above the fourth-ranked October, in 2015.

Most of the North Pacific continued to experience much above-average SSTs, with record highs in the west. In contrast, SSTs were close to or below the 1991-2020 average in the central and eastern equatorial Pacific, reflecting a transition from ENSO-neutral to weak La Niña conditions.

The majority (21) of such months were recorded in a near-continuous streak between July 2023 and April 2025, and the earliest three were January to March 2016.

Given the small differences in global temperature anomalies relative to 1991–2020 among datasets, the months identified here as exceeding or not exceeding the 1.5°C threshold may differ in datasets other than ERA5. 0.14°C below the record global-average temperature anomaly of 0.76°C above the 1991-2020 average registered for each of the three 12-month periods ending in June, July and August 2024.

With 10 months of data now available for 2025, it is possible to further refine the temperature outlook for the year as a whole, as well as its expected ranking. Averaged over January to October, the global temperature anomaly currently stands at 0.60°C above the 1991–2020 average, making 2025 the second-warmest year for this 10-month period, 0.12°C cooler than 2024 and 0.04°C warmer than 2023.

Our projection of the global temperature anomaly for the full year, based on a simple statistical model fitted to past ERA5 data, gives a 95% confidence interval of 0.57°C to 0.63°C above average. This would make 2025 the second- or third-warmest year, possibly tied with 2023 (0.60°C), which currently ranks second, and behind 2024 (0.72°C), the warmest year on record. The predicted range corresponds to 1.45–1.51°C above the 1850–1900 pre-industrial reference, with our model indicating less than a 10% chance of exceeding 1.5°C. However, with the global average temperature having reached 1.48°C in 2023 and 1.60°C in 2024 relative to the pre-industrial level, even the lower end of the predicted range for 2025 would result in a 3-year average above 1.5°C, according to the ERA5 dataset*. This would be the first time that this has occurred in the instrumental period.

Even though 2025 may end up as warm as 2023, the climate context of the two years differs. In particular, the recent onset of La Niña conditions is expected to have a moderating effect on global temperature anomalies towards the end of 2025, whereas the end of 2023 was marked by a strong El Niño event reaching its peak intensity, contributing to higher global temperature anomalies.

*Note that other global temperature datasets may give a 3-year average slightly below 1.5°C.

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies, but the relatively dense observational coverage of the continent reduces uncertainty.

In October 2025, warmer-than-average temperatures were seen over large parts of northern and western Europe. The most pronounced positive anomalies were recorded over northern Fennoscandia, as well as over the southern Iberian Peninsula. Below-average temperatures were mostly experienced over southeastern Europe. The largest anomalies were seen in a region stretching from the western Balkans, with Serbia and Bosnia experiencing heavy snowfall early in the month, into southern Poland and Ukraine. Iceland and parts of France also experienced below-average temperatures.

While sea surface temperatures (SSTs) continue to be very high, throughout the month of October daily temperatures were tracking close to those of October 2015, the fourth warmest month on record. The average SST for 60°S–60°N for October 2025 was 20.54°C, 0.34°C above the 1991-2020 average, placing it third highest on record for October, 0.24°C and 0.14°C lower than the values of 2023 and 2024, respectively, and just 0.02°C above October 2015.

Most of the northern Pacific continued to experience above-average SSTs over an area similar to that of September, with record highs this month particularly in the west, associated with marine heatwaves there. Other large regions with much-warmer-than-average to record-warm SSTs include the eastern Indian Ocean, with a marine heatwave southwest of Indonesia, much of the Arctic, as well as a region off the coast of East Antarctica, in a sector marked by much-below-average sea ice cover. The western equatorial and southern Pacific and the Gulf of Mexico and the Caribbean generally saw much warmer-than-average SSTs with some small record-high pockets.

The most below-average SSTs were recorded in the Sea of Okhotsk, in the western Indian Ocean and in the eastern Pacific, off the western coast of Mexico. SSTs were close to or below average in the central and eastern equatorial Pacific, reflecting a transition from ENSO-neutral to weak La Niña conditions.  Other cooler-than-average basins include part of the North Atlantic and the Ocean off the coast of Brazil.

Highlights for October 2025 In the Arctic, the average sea ice extent in October was 12% below average, ranking eighth lowest for the month.

Regionally, sea ice concentrations were most below average in the Eurasian sector of the Arctic Ocean, particularly north of Franz Josef Land and Severnaya Zemlya, coinciding with much-above-average surface air temperatures.

In the Antarctic region, the monthly sea ice extent was the third lowest for October, at 6% below average.

Sea ice concentrations around Antarctica were most below average in the Bellingshausen Sea and in the Indian Ocean sector, the latter coinciding with much-above-average surface air temperatures in the adjacent part of East Antarctica.

In the Arctic, the average sea ice extent in October 2025 was 6.8 million km², 1.0 million km² (about 12%) below the 1991–2020 average. This ranked as the eighth-lowest October extent in the 47-year satellite record, well above the record low observed in 2020 (27% below average). While the value for October 2025 fits within a clear pattern of below-average extents observed since 2007, these negative anomalies have shown significant year-to-year variability, without indicating a clear trend in the October extent over the past 18 years.

After reaching its annual minimum in September, the daily Arctic sea ice extent resumed its seasonal growth. While the September minimum was only the 14th-lowest in the satellite record, well above the minimum recorded in 2012, during the second half of October the daily extent was very close to the values recorded in 2012. By the end of the month, the daily extent ranked fifth-lowest for this time of year, at 8.15 million km².

In October 2025, sea ice concentrations were most below-average in the central Arctic Ocean north of Franz Josef Land and Severnaya Zemlya. This area also experienced much-above-average surface air temperatures in October. The Laptev Sea further east and the Canadian Archipelago also showed a combination of below-average sea ice cover and much-warmer-than-average conditions. In contrast, above-average sea ice concentrations prevailed in the East Siberian Sea.

The average Antarctic sea ice extent in October 2025 was 17.5 million km², 1.1 million km² (about 6%) below the 1991–2020 average for October, making it the third-lowest October extent in the satellite record. The negative anomaly for October 2025 was smaller than those recorded in 2023 (11% below average) and 2024 (8% below average) but still fits the pattern of larger negative anomalies observed since 2023.

After reaching its annual maximum in September, the daily Antarctic sea ice extent resumed its seasonal decrease, ranking third-lowest for the time of year throughout October, and remaining above the values recorded in 2023 and 2024.

In October 2025, sea ice concentrations around Antarctica showed marked regional contrasts. Two ocean sectors saw much-below-average sea ice cover: the Indian Ocean sector (20°–100°E) and the Bellingshausen Sea, west of the Antarctic Peninsula. In contrast, sea ice concentrations were above average – primarily along the sea ice edge – in the South Atlantic, the western Pacific, and the northern Amundsen Sea sectors. The large negative anomalies in the Indian Ocean sector coincided with a striking pattern of much-above-average surface air temperatures in the adjacent part of East Antarctica, as well as record-high sea surface temperatures for the time of year further north in the same ocean sector.

the fifth warmest on record for September, but only marginally different (within 0.04°C) from the fourth warmest in 2018 and the sixth warmest in 2011

1.28°C cooler than the warmest September, in 2023

In September 2025, warmer-than-average temperatures were seen over Iceland, central and eastern Spain, Fennoscandia and in most of the eastern half of Europe. The most pronounced anomalies were recorded over northern Fennoscandia, in a region stretching straight southward from Finland, over most of the Baltic states, Belarus and western Ukraine, as well as over parts of Croatia, Serbia, Montenegro and Albania. Ireland, the United Kingdom, France, northern Portugal and northwestern Spain experienced slightly cooler-than-average temperatures, with most locations seeing anomalies that were less than one degree below average.

Outside Europe, Canada, parts of Greenland, northwesternmost Siberia and adjacent coastal regions, and large parts of Antarctica had the most significant warmer-than-average surface air temperatures globally. Canada registered its highest temperature on record for a month of September, reaching 40.8°C in Ashcroft, British Columbia. Above-average temperatures were also seen over Iran, the mountainous regions of central Asia and western China, along the Himalayas and most of the Tibetan plateau, in a large region east of the Lake Baikal, as well as easternmost China and Japan that experienced its third-warmest September on record. South America generally experienced above-average temperatures, most pronounced over the Andes and in the central and southern parts of the continent. Only a few scattered regions recorded below-average values. Most of Africa saw warmer-than-average temperatures, in particular over the Democratic Republic of Congo, as well as over southern Africa south of Lake Malawi. A few regions across the continent saw cooler-than-average temperatures, the larger of which include over the Sahel and Egypt. The only large regions globally with pronounced negative temperature anomalies were northern central Siberia, western Australia and parts of eastern Antarctica.

High surface air temperatures over ocean areas were associated with continuing high 60°S–60°N sea-surface temperatures (SSTs). The average SST for September 2025 was 20.72°C, 0.37°C above the 1991-2020 average, and the third highest on record for September, 0.20°C and 0.11°C lower than the values of 2023 and 2024, respectively.

Most of the northern Pacific, affected by marine heatwave conditions, continued to experience above-average SSTs, over a larger area than compared to July and August, reaching record highs in many regions. The western equatorial and southern Pacific and the Indian Oceans were also mostly much warmer-than-average with record high pockets.

Much of the northeastern North Atlantic saw much higher-than-average to record-high SSTs; a large region from the Norwegian to the Kara Sea experienced a marine heatwave, peaking at mid-month. Across the Mediterranean, which also experienced marine heatwaves, SSTs were mostly much-above average, especially in the western and central parts of the basin. The Baltic and the western Black Sea were also warmer than average. SSTs remained higher than average in many other extratropical ocean regions.

Conversely, SSTs were close to or below the 1991-2020 average in the central and eastern equatorial Pacific, reflecting the ENSO-neutral conditions. Other cooler-than-average basins include part of the North Atlantic, and a region of the western Indian Ocean encompassing the Arabian Sea and extending southward.

Highlights for September 2025 In the Arctic, the daily sea ice extent reached its 14th-lowest annual minimum in the satellite record, while the monthly extent ranked 13th lowest, at 12% below average, well above the record low of -32% observed in September 2012.

Regionally, below-average sea ice concentrations were most pronounced north of Svalbard and Franz Josef Land and in the Beaufort Sea.

Around Antarctica, the daily sea ice extent reached its third-lowest annual maximum, while the monthly extent ranked fourth lowest, at about 5% below average, behind 2023 (–9%) and 2024 (–7%).

Below-average sea ice concentrations around Antarctica were most pronounced in the Bellingshausen Sea and in the Indian Ocean sector while near- or above-average concentrations prevailed elsewhere.

In September 2025, Arctic sea ice reached its minimum extent for the year, covering 5.2 million km² on average, 0.7 million km² (about 12%) less than the 1991–2020 average.

This ranked as the 13th-lowest September extent in the 47-year satellite record, well above the two lowest extents recorded in 2012 (32% below average) and 2020 (26% below average). Since 2007 – a year that marked a significant shift towards lower September sea ice extents in the Arctic – only six years out of 19 saw higher September extents than in 2025. Two of them (2021 and 2022) occurred relatively recently. Overall, the latest September value further illustrates the absence of a clear downward trend in Arctic sea ice extent since the late 2000s.

The daily Arctic sea ice extent reached its annual minimum on 7 September 2025, at 5.07 million km², making it the 14th lowest minimum in the satellite record (note the small difference with the 13th-lowest ranking for the monthly extent). The 2025 minimum occurred five days earlier than the median date for the annual minimum during 1991–2020. The sea ice extent remained close to the 2025 minimum for about a week before increasing again, making the timing broadly consistent with the typical period of the annual minimum. From mid-June until early August, the daily extent closely tracked the values observed in 2012, the year of the all-time record minimum. However, during the second half of August, the decline in 2025 was noticeably slower than in 2012 and more in line with the average rate observed during 1991–2020.

In September 2025, below-average sea ice concentrations were most pronounced in two sectors of the Arctic Ocean: the western Eurasian sector, extending from the northern Greenland Sea to the northern Kara Sea, with the largest negative anomalies north of Svalbard and Franz Josef Land; and the Beaufort Sea. In contrast, above-average concentrations prevailed in the eastern Eurasian sector, north of the East Siberian and Laptev Seas.

As Arctic sea ice reached its annual minimum extent, the sea ice cover around Antarctica reached its annual maximum in September 2025. The monthly average extent was 18.1 million km², or 0.9 million km² (about 4.7%) below the 1991–2020 average for September. This ranked as the fourth-lowest September extent in the satellite record, virtually tied with 1986 (the third lowest). The negative anomaly for September 2025 was smaller than in 2023 (9% below average) and 2024 (7% below average), the two years with the largest negative anomalies.

The daily Antarctic sea ice extent reached its highest value of the year, 18.28 million km², on 13 September 2025. This was the third-lowest annual maximum in the satellite record, behind 2023 (lowest) and 2024 (second lowest). Here again, note the small difference with the fourth-lowest ranking for the monthly extent. The 2025 maximum occurred seven days earlier than the median date for the annual maximum during 1991–2020. For context, over the past six years, the maximum was reached on the same or nearly the same day in 2022 (13 September) and 2023 (12 September), but more than two weeks later, on 29 September, in 2019, 2021, and 2024.

In September 2025, sea ice concentrations around Antarctic were most below-average in the northern Bellingshausen (east of the Antarctic Peninsula) and in the Indian Ocean section. In the other sectors, concentrations were generally near or above average.

The sea ice indicator page provides additional information about the long-term trends in both the Arctic and Antarctica.

Global temperature August 2025 was the third-warmest August globally, with an average ERA5 surface air temperature of 16.60°C, 0.49°C above the 1991-2020 average for August.

August 2025 was 0.22°C cooler than the two warmest Augusts on record, in 2023 and 2024.

August 2025 was 1.29°C above the estimated 1850-1900 average used to define the pre-industrial level.

The 12-month period of September 2024 – August 2025 was 0.64°C above the 1991-2020 average, and 1.52°C above the pre-industrial level.

Seasonal highlights The global-average temperature for the boreal summer 2025 (June to August) was the third highest on record at 0.47°C above the 1991-2020 average, and cooler only than the boreal summers of 2023 and 2024.

Temperatures around the world were mostly above average, especially in the northern hemisphere. The largest positive anomalies were recorded over Asia, while notable negative anomalies occurred over South America and Australia.

Europe and other regions The average temperature over European land for August 2025 was 19.46°C, 0.30°C above the 1991-2020 average for August, leaving the month outside the 10 warmest Augusts in the data record.

Western Europe experienced Europe’s most pronounced above-average air temperatures. The Iberian Peninsula and southwest France were particularly affected by heatwave conditions.

A large part of northern Europe, including Fennoscandia, the Baltic States, Belarus and Poland experienced cooler-than-average temperatures.

Outside Europe, temperatures were most above average across Siberia, parts of Antarctica, China, the Korean Peninsula, Japan and the Middle East.

Temperatures were most below average over most of Australia and mixed conditions occurred across North America, South America and South Africa.

Seasonal highlights Europe had its fourth-warmest summer season on record at 0.90°C above the 1991-2020 average.

Summer temperatures were above average across almost all of the European continent, the only exception being some parts of eastern Europe. They were most above average over western parts of Europe, southeast Europe and Türkiye.

Sea surface temperature The average sea surface temperature (SST) for August 2025 over 60°S–60°N was 20.82°C, the third-highest value on record for the month, 0.16°C below the August 2023 record.

Most of the North Pacific saw much above-average SSTs, with record highs reached in some areas. In contrast, SSTs were close to or below the 1991-2020 average in the central and eastern equatorial Pacific, reflecting the ENSO-neutral conditions.

Around Europe, there were still record-high SSTs across an area of the North Atlantic to the west of France and the United Kingdom. SSTs across the Mediterranean were mixed and much less extreme than in August 2024.

The global-average temperature for the boreal summer 2025 (June to August) was the third highest on record at 0.47°C above the 1991-2020 average for these three months. This is 0.22°C and 0.19°C cooler than the two warmest boreal summers in 2024 (0.69°C) and 2023 (0.66°C), respectively.

The European average temperature over land for summer 2025 (June to August) was 0.90°C above the 1991-2020 average. It is the fourth-warmest summer season on record. It was 0.65°C cooler than the warmest European summer of 2024 (1.55°C) and 0.44°C and 0.07°C cooler than the second and third warmest summers in 2022 and 2021, respectively. The season ranks fourth warmest even though August was outside the 10 warmest Augusts in part because of the warmest temperatures on record in western Europe in June and July.

1.16°C higher than the 1991-2020 annual average

0.51°C cooler than the highest 12-month average on record for the continent (February 2024 to January 2025)

In August 2025, western Europe had mostly warmer-than-average air temperatures. The Iberian Peninsula and southwest France had the most pronounced anomalies, largely due to a prolonged heatwave around the middle of the month. See the Highlight Box for further details. Türkiye, the Caucasus, and the Kola Peninsula (northwestern Russia) also saw warmer-than-average conditions. In contrast, air temperatures across a large part of northern Europe, including Fennoscandia, the Baltic States, and Poland, were predominantly below the 1991-2020 climatological values. Mixed conditions occurred across Italy.

August saw the third major heatwave of the 2025 summer in western Europe, following the two that occurred in quick succession from mid-June to early July. The August heatwave started in late July in Portugal, before spreading to Spain on 3 August and reaching France on 8 August. It lasted until 18 August, mainly affecting the two Iberian countries and southern France. During this period, daily maximum temperatures reached 40°C across much of the region. Highs of up to 45°C were recorded in Portugal and southern Spain, and up to 42°C in southwestern France.

According to the Spanish meteorological service AEMET, Spain experienced its most intense heatwave since at least 1975, lasting 16 days. In Portugal, the national weather service IPMA reported that one station (Guarda) in the northeast also recorded 16 consecutive days of heatwave conditions, the longest such period ever observed at a single station in that region since national records began in 1931. In France, Météo France reported that the heatwave lasted 11 days, making it the second-longest August heatwave after that of August 2003 (16 days), and one of the most intense in the south of the country, based on national records going back to 1947.

Data from ERA5 provide additional insight on the intensity and spatial extent of the event. They show average daily maximum temperatures during the 11 days from 8 to 18 August reached 34–36°C across most of the region, and up to 38–40°C in southern Spain. Average temperature anomalies (based on daily means) during the same period were more than 4°C above average in the northern Iberian Peninsula and more than 6°C above average in southwestern France.

The extreme temperatures recorded during the heatwave caused severe heat stress for exposed populations. This impact is measured using the ‘feel-like’ temperature, which takes into account not only the air temperature, but also humidity, solar radiation and wind speed to estimate how hot it actually feels to the human body. Thermal Trace, a new application recently released by C3S for monitoring thermal stress worldwide, indicates that much of the affected region experienced ‘very strong heat stress’* during the heatwave, while parts of northeastern and southern Spain and southern Portugal experienced ‘extreme heat stress’* on 11–12 and 16–17 August. Maximum feels-like temperatures reached up to 45°C in France, 47°C in Portugal, and 49°C in Spain.

*‘Very strong heat stress’' corresponds to feel-like temperatures between 38°C and 46°C; ‘extreme heat stress’ to feel-like temperatures of 46°C or higher.

Outside Europe, northern Siberia and parts of Antarctica had the most significant warmer-than-average temperatures globally. China, the Korean Peninsula, and Japan also experienced warm conditions, the latter recording its warmest temperature ever of 41.8°C on 5 August. Across the Middle East, above-average temperatures occurred, with Jordan and Israel having their hottest nights on record. Greenland also experienced above-average temperatures, resulting in a persistent melting event, and western Canada had widespread warmth, with Lytton, British Columbia recording the warmest Canadian temperature this year of 41.3°C. Mixed conditions occurred across South America, and Africa; and the United States had a split between warm conditions in the west and cool conditions in the east. Australia had mostly cooler-than-average temperatures; the state of Queensland recorded its coolest August since 2014.

In Europe, temperatures for the boreal summer of 2025 were above the 1991-2020 average over almost all of the continent, the only exception being some parts of eastern Europe. The United Kingdom and Ireland had their warmest summers on record, while Spain experienced one of the two warmest summers and France had its third-warmest summer on record.

Outside Europe, temperatures were mostly above average across the northern hemisphere and especially in Asia. China saw its warmest summer in a record since 1961; Japan had its warmest summer since records began in 1898; and South Korea had its warmest summer in a record beginning in 1973. Mixed conditions occurred across India and southern hemisphere land masses, including Antarctica. Notable cold anomalies were experienced in South America which related to record cold conditions recorded in Argentina and Chile in June.

High surface air temperatures over ocean areas were associated with continuing high 60°S–60°N sea-surface temperatures (SSTs). The average SST for August 2025 was 20.82°C, 0.40°C above the 1991-2020 average, and the third highest on record for August, 0.16°C and 0.09°C lower than the values of 2023 and 2024, respectively.

Most of the northern Pacific saw much above-average SSTs, reaching record highs in many areas, as did pockets of the western equatorial and southern Pacific and Indian Oceans. There were also warmer-than-average SSTs in the northeast and western North Atlantic with some regions having record values. SSTs were close to or below the 1991-2020 average in the central and eastern equatorial Pacific, reflecting the ENSO-neutral conditions. The SST remained warmer than average in many other extratropical ocean regions. SSTs across the Mediterranean were mixed and much less extreme than in August 2024.

Highlights for August 2025 As Arctic sea ice is approaching its annual minimum extent, expected in September, the average extent in August 2025 was 12% below the 1991–2020 average, the 8th-lowest August extent on record. This anomaly was significantly smaller than the record 22% below average of August 2012, the year with the all-time September minimum.

Regionally, below-average sea ice concentrations were most pronounced in the European Arctic sector, north of Svalbard and Franz Josef Land.

Antarctic sea ice extent was 7% below average, the third lowest on record for the month, a similar ranking to those seen in June and July.

Sea ice concentration anomalies around Antarctica continued to show alternating areas of above- and below-average concentrations, shaped by the positioning of high- and low-pressure systems around the continent.

In August 2025, the average Arctic sea ice extent was 5.9 million km², about 0.8 million km² (or 12%) below the 1991–2020 average for the month. This was the 8th-lowest August extent in the 47-year satellite record, only slightly above the August values recorded in 2016 and 2017 (7th and 6th lowest, respectively). The lowest extent for August (22% below average) occurred in 2012, ahead of the all-time record minimum in September that same year. While the latest August value fits the overall pattern of below-average values observed since 2005, there has been no clear trend in August sea ice extent since the late 2000s.

The daily Arctic sea ice extent started the month as the 5th-lowest on record, slightly below the levels recorded in 2012, the year of the all-time record minimum. In the following weeks, the decline toward the annual minimum (typically reached in September) was slower than in several recent years, including 2012, 2020, and 2024. By the end of August, the daily extent had reached 5.3 million km², ranking 11th-lowest and making it unlikely that a new record low will be set in September.

In August 2025, below-average sea ice concentrations prevailed across much of the Arctic Ocean. Anomalies were most pronounced in the Eurasian Arctic sector, north of Svalbard, Franz Josef Land, and Severnaya Zemlya, continuing the pattern already seen in July. In this region, the sea ice edge followed a characteristic near-straight line, often observed in previous years and corresponding to the edge of the continental shelf. Other areas with below-average sea ice cover included the region north of the New Siberian Islands (between the Laptev and East Siberian Seas), parts of the Beaufort Sea, the Canadian Archipelago, and patchy areas of the central Arctic Ocean. Only the eastern Beaufort Sea and the eastern East Siberian Sea recorded above-average sea ice cover.

In August 2025, the average Antarctic sea ice extent was 16.9 million km², which is 1.3 million km² (about 7%) below the 1991–2020 average for the month. This was the third-lowest August extent in the satellite record, a ranking similar to those seen in June and July. Although less extreme than the anomaly of 12% below average observed in 2023, the 2025 value continues the series of negative anomalies greater than 5% observed since 2023.

The daily Antarctic sea ice extent continued its seasonal increase toward the annual maximum, expected in September. It started the month as the 3rd-lowest on record, above the levels seen in 2023 and 2024, then briefly fell below 2024. By the end of August, it ranked 4th-lowest, at 17.8 million km², above 2001, 2023, and 2024.

In August 2025, the pattern of sea ice concentration anomalies around Antarctica continued to display the characteristic three negative ‘lobes’ (areas with below-average concentrations) seen in July: in the Bellingshausen Sea, in the Indian Ocean sector (20°–60°E), and in the western Pacific sector (120°–180°E) along the East Antarctic coast. Areas with above-average concentration were less prominent than in July, with the main one located in the northern Amundsen-Ross Sea sector. This distribution of sea ice concentration anomalies reflects the position of high- and low-pressure systems around the continent and their influence on surface winds.

The sea ice indicator page provides additional information about the long-term trends in both the Arctic and Antarctica.

Global temperature July 2025 was the third-warmest July globally, with an average ERA5 surface air temperature of 16.68°C, 0.45°C above the 1991-2020 average for July.

July 2025 was 0.27°C cooler than the record of July 2023, and 0.23°C cooler than July 2024, which was the second warmest.

July 2025 was 1.25°C above the estimated 1850-1900 average used to define the pre-industrial level. It was only the fourth month in the last 25 with a global temperature less than 1.5°C above the pre-industrial level.

The 12-month period of August 2024 – July 2025 was 0.65°C above the 1991-2020 average, and 1.53°C above the pre-industrial level.

Europe and other regions The average temperature over European land for July 2025 was 21.12°C, 1.30°C above the 1991-2020 average for July, making the month the fourth-warmest July in the record.

Fennoscandia experienced Europe’s most pronounced above-average air temperatures, with heatwave conditions especially affecting Sweden and Finland. Southeast Europe also faced heatwaves and wildfire activity, and a national record temperature of 50.5°C occurred in Türkiye.

Central Europe, western Russia and isolated parts of Spain experienced cooler-than-average temperatures.

Outside Europe, temperatures were most above average across the Himalayas, China and Japan.

Temperatures were most below average over parts of Antarctica, and were also below average over North America and South America, India, most of Australia, and pockets of Africa.

Sea surface temperature The average sea surface temperature (SST) for July 2025 over 60°S–60°N was 20.77°C, the third-highest value on record for the month, 0.12°C below the July 2023 record.

SSTs remained unusually high in many ocean basins and seas.

The Norwegian Sea, parts of the North Sea, and an area of the North Atlantic to the west of France and the United Kingdom had record-high SSTs in July. A broad area of the western North Atlantic and western North Pacific also experienced record-high SSTs.

0.45°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average for July with an absolute surface air temperature of 16.68°C.

The third-warmest July on record, 0.27°C cooler than the warmest July in 2023, and 0.23°C cooler than 2024, the second warmest.

1.25°C warmer than an estimate of the pre-industrial July average for 1850-1900.

July 2025 was only the fourth month in the last 25 months for which the global-average surface air temperature was not more than 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels. Prior to this 25-month period, only five months (January-March 2016 and January-February 2020) had previously been above the 1.5°C level. 14 of the last 25 months, from September 2023 to April 2024, and from October 2024 to March 2025, were substantially above 1.5°C, ranging from 1.58°C to 1.78°C. However, the values for May, June, August and September 2024, as well as those for July and August 2023 and April 2025, were very close to 1.5°C (between 1.50°C and 1.54°C). Given the small differences among datasets for global temperature anomalies relative to 1991-2020, the months highlighted here in the last 25 months may differ in datasets other than ERA5. Furthermore, there is inherent uncertainty arising from dataset differences in the estimates used for the monthly temperature changes from pre-industrial to 1991-2020 levels.

Averaging over 12-month periods smooths out shorter-term variations in regional- and global-average temperatures.

In July 2025, Fennoscandia was the area with the most pronounced warmer-than-average air temperatures. These conditions reflected a long heatwave in this part of Europe, as experienced in northern Norrland, Sweden, and Finland where there were 15 days of temperatures above 30°C. Temperatures above 30°C were also recorded for a record-setting 13 consecutive days at two stations in Norway. Southeast Europe and Türkiye also had extreme temperatures, with Silopi, Türkiye, recording a temperature of 50.5°C on 25 July, the first time a temperature above 50°C was observed in the country. Many wildfires occurred in North Macedonia, Albania, Croatia, Greece, and Türkiye. The United Kingdom had its fifth-warmest July on record, and Iceland and eastern Europe were generally warmer than average. In contrast, air temperatures in central Europe and western Russia were predominantly below the 1991-2020 climatological values, and mixed conditions were found across Spain.

Outside Europe, there was a mixture of warmer- and cooler-than-average conditions across global land masses. Asia saw the most significant warmer-than-average temperatures globally, with warm conditions across the Himalayas, China, South Korea and Japan, which recorded its warmest July in a record back to 1898. Saudi Arabia and north Africa generally had warm conditions, as did the eastern United States.  Greenland also experienced above-average temperatures and a protracted spell of extensive ice sheet melting. There was a band of warmer-than-average temperatures in the Andes mountains too. Conversely, there were cooler-than-average temperatures in North America in a line from Mexico to northern Canada, and in South America where notable cold conditions occurred in late June and early July. India was also mostly colder than average, as was Australia and pockets of Africa. Antarctica had a strong contrast between warmer- and cooler-than-average conditions.

High surface air temperatures over ocean areas were associated with continuing high 60°S–60°N sea-surface temperatures (SSTs), albeit not as high as in 2023 and 2024. The daily SST throughout July 2025 increased slightly but was below the values recorded in both 2023 and 2024. The average SST for July 2025 was 20.77°C, 0.39°C above the 1991-2020 average, and the third highest on record for July. The July 2025 value was 0.12°C lower than the monthly value for July 2023 and 0.11°C lower than the value for July 2024.

The Norwegian Sea, parts of the North Sea, and an area of the North Atlantic to the west of France and the United Kingdom had record-high SSTs in July which were related to the warmer-than-average air temperatures. Some parts of the western and central Mediterranean Sea also had record-high SSTs, as did a broad area of the western North Atlantic and western North Pacific. SSTs in the western equatorial Pacific to the northeast of Australia continued to be anomalously warm, while SSTs were close to or below the 1991-2020 average in the central and eastern equatorial Pacific, reflecting the ENSO-neutral conditions. The SST remained warmer than average in many other extratropical ocean regions.

Highlights for July 2025 Arctic sea ice extent was 10% below average, ranking joint second-lowest for July in the 47-year satellite record, virtually tied with 2012 and 2021.

Regionally, below-average sea ice concentrations were most pronounced in the Kara Sea and around Svalbard and Franz Josef Land.

Antarctic sea ice extent was 8% below average, ranking third lowest on record for the month, a less extreme anomaly than those seen in July 2023 (-15%) and 2024 (-11%).

Antarctic sea ice showed alternating areas of above- and below-average concentrations, shaped by the positioning of high- and low-pressure systems around the continent.

In July 2025, the average Arctic sea ice extent was 8.3 million km², about 0.9 million km² (10%) below the 1991–2020 average for the month. This was the second-lowest July extent in the 47-year satellite record, virtually tied with 2012 and 2021, and slightly below 2019. The anomaly for July 2025 was more negative than in the past three years, though not as low as the record low for July set in 2020 (14% below average). While July extents have remained consistently below average since 2005, they continue to show notable year-to-year variability.

Throughout July, the daily Arctic sea ice extent closely followed the values observed in 2012—the year of the lowest annual minimum—ending the month at 12.3 million km², the 8th  lowest on record for that date. However, it is too early to predict how low the September minimum will be this year, as atmospheric circulation patterns in August can significantly influence the pace of the seasonal decline.

In July 2025, sea ice concentrations were most below average in the Kara Sea, north of the Barents Sea around Svalbard and Franz Josef Land, and in the Greenland Sea. Below-average concentrations also prevailed in the northern Laptev Sea, in the broad Canadian sector (including Hudon Bay and Baffin Bay) and in the central Arctic Ocean. In contrast, the Beaufort Sea saw above-average sea ice cover.

In July 2025, the average Antarctic sea ice extent was 15.1 million km², which is 1.4 million km² (about 8%) below the 1991–2020 average for the month. This was the third-lowest July extent in the satellite record. Although less extreme than the July anomalies of 15% and 11% recorded in 2023 and 2024, respectively, the 2025 value continues the series of negative anomalies greater than 5% observed in July since 2022.

The daily Antarctic sea ice extent continued its seasonal increase throughout July, remaining consistently below the 1991–2020 median but above the levels seen in 2023 and 2024. On 31 July, the extent reached 16.2 million km²—the third lowest on record for that date, above 2023 (15.1 million km²) and 2024 (15.7 million km²).

In July 2025, sea ice concentration anomalies around Antarctica featured three positive ‘lobes’ (areas with above-average concentrations) in the Amundsen Sea, the Lazarev Sea (20°W–20°E), and along the 90°–120°E sector of East Antarctica; and three negative ‘lobes’ (below-average concentrations) in the northern Ross Sea, the Bellingshausen Sea, and the Indian Ocean sector. This arrangement—often described as a wavenumber-3 pattern—is relatively common in the region and was also seen in May 2025. It reflects the influence of high- and low-pressure systems surrounding the continent.

The sea ice indicator page provides additional information about the long-term trends in both the Arctic and Antarctica.

Global temperature June 2025 was the third-warmest June globally, with an average ERA5 surface air temperature of 16.46°C, 0.47°C above the 1991-2020 average for June.

June 2025 was 0.20°C cooler than the record June of 2024, and 0.06°C cooler than June 2023, which was the second warmest.

June 2025 was 1.30°C above the estimated 1850-1900 average used to define the pre-industrial level. It was only the third month in the last 24 with a global temperature less than 1.5°C above the pre-industrial level.

The 12-month period of July 2024 – June 2025 was 0.67°C above the 1991-2020 average, and 1.55°C above the pre-industrial level.

Europe and other regions The average temperature over European land for June 2025 was 18.46°C, 1.10°C above the 1991-2020 average for June, making the month the fifth-warmest June in the record.

Most of western and central Europe experienced warmer-than-average air temperatures in June 2025. Western Europe as a whole saw its warmest June on record, with an average temperature of 20.49°C, 2.81°C above the 1991–2020 average. It narrowly surpassed (by only 0.06°C) the previous June record set in 2003 (20.43°C).

Two major heatwaves in June and early July 2025 affected large parts of western and southern Europe. Much of the region saw feels-like temperatures exceeding 38°C, corresponding to ‘very strong heat stress’. Part of Portugal saw feels-like temperatures reach around 48°C or ‘extreme heat stress’.

Outside Europe, temperatures were most above average over the United States, northern Canada, central Asia, eastern Asia, and west Antarctica.

Temperatures were most below average over southern South America, with record cold conditions recorded in Argentina and Chile. India and east Antarctica also had below-average temperatures.

Sea surface temperature The average sea surface temperature (SST) for June 2025 over 60°S–60°N was 20.72°C, the third-highest value on record for the month, 0.13°C below the June 2024 record.

SSTs remained unusually high in many ocean basins and seas.

An exceptional marine heatwave developed in the western Mediterranean in June, leading to the highest daily SST ever recorded for the region as a whole in June (27.0°C), corresponding to the highest daily SST anomaly for any month (3.7°C above average).

June 2025 was only the third month in the last 24 months for which the global-average surface air temperature was not more than 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels. Prior to the last two-year period, only five months (January-March 2016 and January-February 2020) had previously been above the 1.5°C level. 14 of the last 24 months, from September 2023 to April 2024, and from October 2024 to March 2025, were substantially above 1.5°C, ranging from 1.58°C to 1.78°C. However, the values for May, June, August and September 2024, as well as those for July and August 2023 and April 2025, were very close to 1.5°C (between 1.50°C and 1.54°C). Given the small differences among datasets for global temperature anomalies relative to 1991-2020, the months highlighted here in the last 24 months may differ in datasets other than ERA5. Furthermore, there is inherent uncertainty arising from dataset differences in the estimates used for the monthly temperature changes from pre-industrial to 1991-2020 levels.

In June 2025, most of western and central Europe experienced warmer-than-average air temperatures. Many countries including Austria, France, Germany, Italy, Portugal, Spain, Switzerland and the United Kingdom had heatwave conditions in which some places in Portugal and Spain saw temperatures soar above 40°C. This is reflected in the June monthly average with France having its second-warmest June, Spain having its warmest June in 64 years and England having its warmest June in the record starting in 1884. Further analysis of the European heatwave events in June 2025 can be found in this web article. Southeast Europe was also affected by warmer-than-average temperatures. In contrast, air temperatures in Iceland, northern parts of Fennoscandia, western Russia, and the Caucasus were predominantly below the 1991-2020 climatological values.

Outside Europe, the United States was mostly warmer than average, following heatwave conditions across parts of the country. Northern Canada, central Asia and China were also warmer than average, and records were broken in the Jianghuai and Jiangnan regions of China. Japan had its warmest June on record too as did many cities in Korea. Across Africa, South America, and Australia there was a mix of warmer- and cooler-than-average conditions. Notable cold conditions occurred towards the end of the month across southern South America, including in Argentina, Chile, Paraguay, and Uruguay, where an anticyclonic circulation drew cold air out of Antarctica, leading to new record low temperatures being set in Argentina and Chile. India was also mostly colder than average. Antarctica had a strong contrast with warmer-than-average conditions in the west and cooler-than-average conditions in the east.

High surface air temperatures over ocean areas were associated with continuing high 60°S–60°N sea-surface temperatures (SSTs) for the time of year. Since March 2023 the daily SST has mostly been above all previous years. The daily SST throughout June 2025 rose slightly but was below the values recorded in both 2023 and 2024. The average SST for June 2025 was 20.72°C, 0.36°C above the 1991-2020 average, and the third highest on record for June. The June 2025 value was 0.13°C lower than the monthly value for June 2024 and 0.09°C lower than the value for June 2023.

The western and central Mediterranean Sea had record-high SSTs in June, as did a large part of the western North Atlantic and western North Pacific. An area of record-high SSTs in the northeast North Atlantic, as discussed in May’s bulletin, shrunk during June. SSTs in the western equatorial Pacific to the northeast of Australia continued to be anomalously warm, while SSTs were close to the 1991-2020 average in the central and eastern equatorial Pacific, reflecting the ENSO-neutral conditions. The SST remained warmer than average in many other extratropical ocean regions.

The European continent experienced two significant heatwaves in mid-June and late June-early July 2025. The first event peaked between 17 and 22 June, affecting large parts of western and southern Europe. The second heatwave occurred at the turn of the month, with even more extreme temperatures between 30 June and 2 July across the same region.

Highlights for June 2025 Arctic sea ice extent was 6% below average, the second lowest monthly extent for June in the 47-year satellite record. During the latter part of the month, the daily extent was consistently the lowest for the time of year.

Regionally, below-average sea ice concentrations were most pronounced along the northern coast of Eurasia (Barents, Kara, and Laptev Seas).

Antarctic sea ice extent was 9% below average, ranking as 3rd lowest value on record for the month.

Antarctic sea ice concentrations were dominated by below-average concentrations in the Bellinghausen Sea and eastern South Atlantic and average or above-average concentrations in the other ocean sectors.

The monthly average sea ice extent in the Arctic in June 2025 was 10.7 million km², 0.7 million km² (about 6%) below the 1991–2020 average for the month. This was the second-lowest extent for June in the 47-year satellite record, just slightly above (by 0.2 percentage points) the lowest value recorded in 2016. The June 2025 anomaly is notably more negative than in the past four years and follows several months in 2025 when Arctic sea ice was at record or near-record lows for the time of year (lowest extent in January, February, March, and second-lowest extent in May).

While continuing its seasonal decrease in June, the daily Arctic sea ice extent reached record lows for the season every day from 10 June onward. The month ending at a daily extent of 9.6 million km², the lowest on record for that date.

In June 2025, sea ice concentrations were most below average in the Eurasian sector of the Arctic Ocean, particularly between Svalbard and Franz Josef Land, and in the Barents, Kara, and Laptev Seas. They were also below average eastern Canadian sector, including in Hudson Bay and Baffin Bay. In contrast, concentrations were above average in the Beaufort and Chukchi Seas.

In June 2025, the average Antarctic sea ice extent was 12.6 million km², which is 1.3 million km² (about 9%) below the 1991–2020 average for the month. This was the third-lowest June extent in the satellite record, after 2023 and 2024, and close to the anomalies recorded in 2019 and 2022 (also around 9% below average). Although less extreme than the record anomaly of 17% below average in June 2023, the 2025 value continues the series of negative anomalies greater than 8% that have been observed each June since 2022.

Antarctic sea ice extent continued its seasonal increase throughout June, remaining consistently below the 1991–2020 median but generally above the levels observed in 2023 and 2024 levels. On 30 June, the daily extent was the third lowest on record for that date, at 14.0 million km², only higher than in 2023 (12.7 million km²) and 2024 (13.5 million km²).

In June 2025, sea ice concentration anomalies around Antarctica showed two prominent areas with below-average concentrations: the Bellingshausen Sea and the eastern South Atlantic (0°–45°E). Above-average concentrations prevailed in the northeastern Weddell Sea and northern Amundsen Sea. Concentrations were generally mixed or near-average in the other sectors.

Global temperature May 2025 was the second-warmest May globally, with an average ERA5 surface air temperature of 15.79°C, 0.53°C above the 1991-2020 average for May.

May 2025 was 0.12°C cooler than the record May of 2024, and 0.06°C warmer than the third warmest of 2020.

May 2025 was 1.40°C above the estimated 1850-1900 average used to define the pre-industrial level, interrupting an extended period of 21 months (out of 22) with a global-average temperature more than 1.5°C above the pre-industrial level.

The 12-month period of June 2024 – May 2025 was 0.69°C above the 1991-2020 average, and 1.57°C above the pre-industrial level.

Seasonal highlights The global-average temperature for the boreal spring 2025 (March to May) was the second highest on record at 0.59°C above the 1991-2020 average, and colder only than the boreal spring of 2024.

Temperatures were mostly above average, especially in the Northern Hemisphere. The largest positive anomalies were recorded over west central Asia, northeastern Russia, Greenland, and western Antarctica, while negative anomalies occurred over the Hudson Bay, southern and northeast Africa, India, northern parts of Australia, and eastern Antarctica.

Europe and other regions The average temperature over European land for May 2025 was 12.98°C, 0.29°C below the 1991-2020 average for May.

There was a notable contrast in surface air temperatures across Europe in May 2025, with below-average temperatures in eastern Europe from eastern Italy and the Balkans to Finland and above-average temperatures in western Europe.

Outside Europe, temperatures were most above average over western Antarctica, a large area of the Middle East and western Asia, northeastern Russia, and northern Canada. Temperatures were most below average over India, Alaska, southern Africa, and eastern Antarctica.

Seasonal highlights Europe had its fourth warmest spring season on record at 1.04°C above the 1991-2020 average.

European temperatures for the boreal spring of 2025 were above average over almost all of the continent, the only exception being some parts of Spain and isolated pockets in eastern Europe. They were most above average over eastern parts of Europe, over southern Norway and eastern Iceland.

Sea surface temperature The average sea surface temperature (SST) for May 2025 over 60°S–60°N was 20.79°C, the second-highest value on record for the month, 0.14°C below the May 2024 record.

SSTs remained unusually high in many ocean basins and seas. Among them, large areas in the northeast North Atlantic, which experienced a marine heatwave, had record-high SSTs for the month. Most of the Mediterranean Sea was much warmer than average.

Only five months (January-March 2016 and January-February 2020) had previously been above the 1.5°C level. 14 of the last 23 months, from September 2023 to April 2024, and from October 2024 to March 2025, were substantially above 1.5°C, ranging from 1.58°C to 1.78°C. However, the values for May, June, August and September 2024, as well as those for July and August 2023 and April 2025, were very close to 1.5°C (between 1.50°C and 1.54°C). Given the small differences among datasets for global temperature anomalies relative to 1991-2020, the months highlighted here in the last 23 months may differ in datasets other than ERA5. Furthermore, there is inherent uncertainty arising from dataset differences in the estimates used for the monthly temperature changes from pre-industrial to 1991-2020 levels.

The global-average temperature for the boreal spring 2025 (March to May) was the second highest on record at 0.59°C above the 1991-2020 average for these three months. This is 0.09°C cooler than the record set in boreal spring 2024 (0.68°C).

The European average temperature over land for spring 2025 (March to May) was 1.04°C above the 1991-2020 average. It is the fourth warmest spring season on record. It was 0.46°C cooler than the warmest European spring ending in May 2024 (1.50°C) and only 0.10°C and 0.04°C cooler than the second and third warmest springs in 2014 and 2007, respectively.

1.33°C higher than the 1991-2020 annual average

There was a notable contrast in surface air temperatures across Europe in May 2025. In eastern Europe from eastern Italy and the Balkans in the south to Finland in the north, temperatures were predominantly below the 1991-2020 climatological values. The largest cold anomalies were recorded to the northwest of the Black Sea centred over and near western Ukraine where they may cause issues for agriculture. Bulgaria also had one its three coldest Mays since 2005. There were also isolated pockets of cold anomalies in Spain and France. In contrast, western Europe was mostly warmer than average. Iceland had the largest warm anomaly due to an unusual heatwave in the middle of the month which led to a new national May temperature record of 26.6°C being set. The United Kingdom recorded its fifth-warmest May on record. Parts of Portugal, Spain, and southern France experienced record-breaking temperatures at the end of May, although these are not clearly seen in the monthly average map.

Outside Europe, other continents also experienced a mix of warmer- and colder-than-average temperatures. Temperatures were most above average over western Antarctica. A large area of the Middle East and western Asia had above-average temperatures, where for example, Iran and Pakistan, experienced extreme heatwaves. Northeastern Russia had an area of warmer-than-average conditions as did northern China and northern Canada. Conversely, the largest cold anomaly occurred over India which was related to the wetter-than-average conditions across the country. Other notable cold conditions were experienced over Alaska, southern Africa, and eastern Antarctica. Australia typified the mixture of conditions found in May, with warmer-than-average temperatures in southeastern regions, and cooler-than average temperatures in more northwestern areas.

In Europe, temperatures for the boreal spring of 2025 were above the 1991-2020 average over almost all of the continent, the only exception being some parts of Spain and isolated pockets in eastern Europe. They were most above average over eastern parts of Europe, southern Norway, eastern Iceland, and the United Kingdom, which had its warmest and sunniest spring since the record started in 1884.

Outside Europe, temperatures were mostly above average, especially in the Northern Hemisphere. The largest positive anomalies were recorded over west central Asia, northeastern Russia, and Greenland. Most of North America also had above-average temperatures. There were several regions with negative anomalies, including the Hudson Bay, southern and northeast Africa, India, and northern parts of Australia. South America was largely above average, although there were pockets of colder anomalies. The patterns across Antarctica reflected those seen in May.

In May 2025, a marine heatwave developed in the northeastern North Atlantic. At the peak of the event on 23 May, sea surface temperatures (SSTs) were more than 4°C above the 1991–2020 average in some areas off the coast of Ireland. On the same day, the average SST for the broader region reached 1.75°C above average. This was the highest value recorded outside of 2023. In June 2023, the region experienced the most intense marine heatwave ever observed there. At its peak, the average SST anomaly reached a record high of 2.31°C above average.

The 2025 heatwave came after several months (since January) of record-high SSTs for the time of year in this part of the North Atlantic. Prolonged anticyclonic conditions —marked by cloud-free skies, high solar radiation, and low wind speeds—likely contributed to the warming of surface ocean waters. Notably, the United Kingdom recorded its sunniest spring on record in 2025, reflecting the exceptional atmospheric conditions that extended over the nearby ocean.

High surface air temperatures over ocean areas were associated with continuing high 60°S–60°N sea-surface temperatures (SSTs) for the time of year. Since March 2023 the daily SST has mostly been above all previous years. By the end of May 2025, the daily SST dropped below the values recorded in both 2023 and 2024. The average SST for May 2025 was 20.79°C, 0.38°C above the 1991-2020 average, and the second highest on record for May. The May 2025 value was 0.14°C lower than the monthly value for May 2024 and 0.02°C higher than the value for May 2023.

Notably, the northeast North Atlantic had record-high SSTs, as discussed further in the Highlight Box. SSTs in the western equatorial Pacific to the northeast of Australia continue to be anomalously warm or at record levels, while SSTs were a little above or close to the 1991-2020 average in the central and eastern equatorial Pacific, reflecting the ENSO-neutral conditions. The SST remained the highest on record or much above average in many oceanic regions outside of the equatorial Pacific.  Large parts of the Mediterranean were much warmer than average.

Highlights for May 2025 Arctic sea ice extent was only 2% below average, the 9th lowest monthly extent for May in the 47-year satellite record.

Regionally, below-average sea ice concentrations were most pronounced along the northern coast of Eurasia (Barents, Kara, and Laptev Seas).

Antarctic sea ice extent was 9% below average, marking its 5th lowest value on record for the month.

Antarctic sea ice showed alternating areas of above- and below-average concentrations, shaped by the positioning of high- and low-pressure systems around the continent.

The monthly average sea ice extent in the Arctic in May 2025 was 12.7 million km², 0.3 million km² (about 2%) below the 1991–2020 average for the month. This ranks as the 9th lowest May extent in the 47-year satellite record. The May 2025 anomaly is notably smaller than the record low of 7% below average observed in 2016, and also lower than the 4 to 6% anomalies recorded in 2018–2020.

While continuing its seasonal decrease in May, the daily Arctic sea ice extent remained below its 1991–2020 median throughout the month, slightly lower than the values observed at the same time of year in 2024, but without reaching record-low values for the season. As of 31st May, the daily extent was 11.8 million km², ranking 5th lowest on record for that date.

In May 2025, sea ice concentrations were most below average in the Eurasian sector of the Arctic Ocean—between Svalbard and Franz Josef Land, and in the Barents, Kara, and Laptev Seas. In contrast, concentrations were above average in the Greenland Sea (for the third consecutive month) and, to a lesser extent, in the Bering and Beaufort Seas. Elsewhere, sea ice concentrations were generally mixed.

In May 2025, the average Antarctic sea ice extent was 9.6 million km², which is 1.0 million km² (about 9%) below the 1991–2020 average for the month, ranking as the fifth lowest May extent in the satellite record. This was close to the anomaly of 8% below average observed in May 2024. While significantly smaller than the record anomaly of 17% below average in May 2023, the May 2025 value is in line with the negative anomalies of 8% or more below average frequently observed since 2017.

Antarctic sea ice extent continued its seasonal increase throughout May, remaining consistently below the 1991–2020 median and closely tracking 2024 levels. It began the month as the 9th lowest on record but gradually approached the lowest values typically observed at this time of year. By 31 May, the daily extent was the second lowest on record for that date, at 10.8 million km², only higher than in 2023 (10.3 million km²).

In May 2025, sea ice concentration anomalies around Antarctica showed a mixed pattern. Above-average concentrations were observed in three regions: the Amundsen Sea, the eastern Weddell Sea, and the 90°–180°E sector along East Antarctica. These were interspersed with areas of below-average concentration in the northern Ross Sea, the Bellingshausen and western Weddell Seas, and the Indian Ocean sector (east of the Weddell Sea). This configuration—featuring three positive ‘lobes’ and three negative ‘lobes’—is often referred to as a wavenumber-3 pattern. It is relatively common around Antarctica and is associated with the positioning of high- and low-pressure systems surrounding the continent.

Global temperature April 2025 was the second-warmest April globally, with an average ERA5 surface air temperature of 14.96°C, 0.60°C above the 1991-2020 average for April.

April 2025 was 0.07°C cooler than the record April of 2024, and 0.07°C warmer than the third warmest of 2016.

April 2025 was 1.51°C above the estimated 1850-1900 average used to define the pre-industrial level and was the 21st month in the last 22 months for which the global-average surface air temperature was more than 1.5°C above the pre-industrial level.

The 12-month period of May 2024 – April 2025 was 0.70°C above the 1991-2020 average, and 1.58°C above the pre-industrial level.

Europe and other regions The average temperature over European land for April 2025 was 9.38°C, 1.01°C above the 1991-2020 average for April, making it the sixth-warmest April for Europe.

Temperatures were predominantly above average across Europe, with the largest warm anomalies recorded over eastern Europe, western Russia, Kazakhstan, and Norway, while colder-than-average temperatures occurred across Türkiye, eastern parts of Bulgaria and Romania, the Crimean Peninsula, and northern Fennoscandia.

Outside Europe, temperatures were most above average over the Russian Far East and in a large part of west-central Asia. They were also above average over most of North America part of Australia, and across the Antarctic Peninsula and West Antarctica.

Temperatures were most below average over southern South America, in eastern Canada in the Great Lakes region and over the Hudson Bay, across northeastern Greenland and Svalbard and over northern Australia and east Antarctica.

Sea surface temperature The average sea surface temperature (SST) for April 2025 over 60°S–60°N was 20.89°C, the second-highest value on record for the month, 0.15°C below the April 2024 record.

SSTs remained unusually high in many ocean basins and seas. Among them, large areas in the northeast North Atlantic continued to show record-high SSTs for the month. Most of the Mediterranean Sea was much warmer than average, but not as record-breaking as in March.

Surface air temperatures were predominantly above the 1991-2020 climatological values across Europe in April 2025. The largest warm anomalies were recorded over eastern Europe and western Russia and Kazakhstan. Norway also had notable warmer-than-average conditions. The United Kingdom recorded its third-warmest April on record, with the daily maximum temperatures being particularly above average – at 2.8°C – for the United Kingdom as a whole. Ireland also set its all-time record daily maximum temperature of 25.9°C for April. In contrast, Türkiye, eastern parts of Bulgaria and Romania, the Crimean Peninsula, northern Fennoscandia and northwest Russia had colder-than-average temperatures.

Outside Europe, temperatures were most above average over the Russian Far East and in a large part of west central Asia. This was reflected by the United Arab Emirates having its warmest April on record and an average daily high of 42.6°C and Pakistan having its second-warmest April in 65 years. China also recorded its second-warmest April since 1961. The United States, Mexico, and large parts of Canada experienced mostly above-average temperatures. Australia had mixed conditions, with warmer-than-average temperatures in southern and western regions, where Victoria had its warmest April since 1910, and cooler-than average temperatures in northern areas. Most of Africa generally had above-average temperatures, although there were pockets of below-average conditions. Cooler-than-average temperatures also occurred across southern South America, in eastern Canada in the Great Lakes region and over the Hudson Bay, and across northeastern Greenland and Svalbard. Antarctica saw above-average temperatures across the Antarctic Peninsula and West Antarctica while there were below-average temperatures in east Antarctica.

High surface air temperatures over ocean areas were associated with continuing high 60°S–60°N sea-surface temperatures (SSTs) for the time of year. Since March 2023 the daily SST has mostly been above all previous years. This continued throughout April 2025, when the daily SST was higher than all previous years except 2024. The average SST for April 2025 was 20.89°C, 0.39°C above the 1991-2020 average, and the second highest on record for April. The April 2025 value was 0.15°C lower than the monthly value for April 2024 and 0.03°C higher than the value for April 2023.

SSTs in the western equatorial Pacific to the northeast of Australia have become the warmest on record during April, while SSTs were a little above or close to the 1991-2020 average in the central and eastern equatorial Pacific, reflecting the ENSO-neutral conditions. The SST remained the highest on record or much above average in many oceanic regions outside of the equatorial Pacific. Notably, the northeast North Atlantic continues to be anomalously warm, with large areas exhibiting record-high SSTs. Large parts of the Mediterranean were much warmer than average, although the area with record-high temperatures was smaller than in March.

Highlights for April 2025 Arctic sea ice extent was 3% below average, the sixth lowest monthly extent for April in the 47-year satellite record, following four months with record low monthly values for the time of year.

Regionally, below-average sea ice concentrations were most pronounced in the Barents Sea and Sea of Okhotsk while above-average concentrations prevailed in the Greenland Sea.

Antarctic sea ice extent was 10% below average, making it the 10th lowest on record for the month.

Sea ice concentration anomalies around Antarctica were mixed.

The monthly average sea ice extent in the Arctic in April 2025 was 13.9 million km², 0.5 million km² (about 3%) below the 1991–2020 average for the month. This ranks as the sixth lowest April extent in the 47-year satellite record. This follows a series of four consecutive months (from December 2024 to March 2025) when the monthly Arctic sea ice extent reached record low values for the time of year. The lowest April extent was recorded in 2019, at 6% below average.

Following its annual maximum in March, daily Arctic sea ice extent began to decline with the onset of the melt season. Over the previous four months, the daily extent was often at record or near-record lows for the time of year, but in April it moved closer to the 1991–2020 average—especially in the second half of the month, when they were frequently almost identical to those recorded in 2024. As of 30 April, the extent stood at 13.6 million km², ranking 15th lowest for that date.

In April 2025, the central Arctic Ocean remained entirely ice-covered. The most notable sea ice concentration anomalies were found in the peripheral seas of the Atlantic and Pacific sectors. Concentrations were below average in the eastern and southern Barents Sea, the Sea of Okhotsk, parts of the Bering Sea, and, to a lesser extent, along the eastern coast of Canada. In contrast, a prominent area of above-average concentrations was observed in the Greenland Sea and around Svalbard—possibly linked to a persistent low-pressure system over the Barents Sea during April and the associated stronger sea ice drift toward the Greenland Sea. A smaller area of above-average concentrations was also present in the Bering Sea.

In April 2025, the average Antarctic sea ice extent was 6.5 million km², which is 0.7 million km² (about 10%) below the 1991–2020 average for the month, making it the 10th lowest April extent in the satellite record. This anomaly was close to that of April 2024 (-9%). While it continues the recent pattern of more frequent and pronounced negative April anomalies observed since 2017, it was not as extreme as the values recorded in April 2017 (-21%, lowest on record for April) and 2023 (-19%, third lowest).

Since reaching its second-lowest annual minimum on record in February, daily Antarctic sea ice extent has begun increasing again with the onset of the growth season, moving closer to the 1991–2020 average. Since mid-March, it has closely followed the 2024 levels, remaining well above the record lows observed in 2023. As of 30 April, the daily extent was 8.0 million km², ranking 9th lowest for that date.

In April 2025, sea ice concentration anomalies around Antarctica showed a mixed pattern. Two large patches of above-average concentrations were observed in the Weddell and Amundsen Seas, while below-average concentrations prevailed in the northern Ross Sea, the Bellingshausen Sea, and parts of the Atlantic sector, including the northern Weddell Sea and areas east of it.

The sea ice indicator page provides additional information about the long-term trends in both the Arctic and Antarctica.

Global temperature March 2025 was the second-warmest March globally, with an average ERA5 surface air temperature of 14.06°C, 0.65°C above the 1991-2020 average for March.

March 2025 was 0.08°C cooler than the record March of 2024, and only marginally warmer — by 0.02°C — than the third warmest of 2016.

March 2025 was 1.60°C above the estimated 1850-1900 average used to define the pre-industrial level and was the 20th month in the last 21 months for which the global-average surface air temperature was more than 1.5°C above the pre-industrial level.

The 12-month period of April 2024 – March 2025 was 0.71°C above the 1991-2020 average, and 1.59°C above the pre-industrial level.

Europe and other regions The average temperature over European land for March 2025 was 6.03°C, 2.41°C above the 1991-2020 average for March, making it the warmest March for Europe.

Temperatures were predominantly above average across Europe, with the largest warm anomalies recorded over eastern Europe and southwest Russia, although across the Iberian Peninsula, colder-than-average temperatures occurred.

Outside Europe, temperatures were most above average over large parts of the Arctic, in particular over the Canadian Archipelago and Baffin Bay. They were also above average over the United States, Mexico, parts of Asia, and Australia.

Temperatures were most below average over northern Canada, Hudson Bay, and eastern Russia, including the Kamchatka Peninsula.

Sea surface temperature The average sea surface temperature (SST) for March 2025 over 60°S–60°N was 20.96°C, the second-highest value on record for the month, 0.12°C below the March 2024 record.

SSTs remained unusually high in many ocean basins and seas. Some seas, such as the Mediterranean Sea and the northeast North Atlantic, saw larger record-breaking areas than last month.

2. The last 12 months – April 2024 to March 2025

However, the values for May, June, August and September 2024, as well as those for July and August 2023, were very close to 1.5°C (between 1.50°C and 1.54°C). Surface air temperatures were predominantly above the 1991-2020 climatological values across Europe in March 2025. The largest warm anomalies were recorded over eastern Europe and southwest Russia where records for the month were set, and earlier-than-average river ice breakups occurred in the Ural Mountains. Fennoscandia also had notable warmer-than-average conditions as did Türkiye. The United Kingdom recorded its sunniest March in the record since 1910. In contrast, the Iberian Peninsula had colder-than-average temperatures, relating to the wetter-than-average conditions across the region.

Outside Europe, temperatures were most above average over parts of the Arctic, in particular over the Canadian Archipelago and Baffin Bay. The United States and Mexico experienced mostly above-average temperatures. A large area of northern Africa had warmer-than-average conditions and parts of Asia recorded record heat too, including Beijing with its earliest 30°C day since 1959 and Tokyo with its warmest March day on record. Australia also had its warmest March in the record since 1910. New South Wales and Western Australia experienced heatwaves during the month.

Temperatures were most below average over northern Canada, Hudson Bay, and eastern Russia, including the Kamchatka Peninsula. They were also below average over central parts of Asia, the far northwest of Africa, for example in Morocco, part of southern Africa, and in other isolated pockets. Antarctica saw a mixed pattern of above and below average temperatures.

High surface air temperatures over ocean areas were associated with continuing high 60°S–60°N sea-surface temperatures (SSTs) for the time of year. Since March 2023 the daily SST has mostly been above all previous years. This continued throughout March 2025, when the daily SST was higher than all previous years except 2024. The average SST for March 2025 was 20.96°C, 0.42°C above the 1991-2020 average, and the second highest on record for March. The March 2025 value was 0.12°C lower than the monthly value for March 2024 and 0.06°C higher than the value for March 2016.

SSTs in the central equatorial Pacific were a little above or close to the 1991-2020 average during March 2025, while they were mostly above average in the eastern equatorial Pacific, suggesting a slight warming compared to February and potentially a move away from La Niña conditions. The SST remained the highest on record or much above average in many oceanic regions outside of the equatorial Pacific. Notably, the northeast North Atlantic has become more anomalously warm since February, while temperatures have become less anomalously warm to the east of Madagascar since February. Record-breaking warm temperatures cover a larger part of the Mediterranean Sea than in February.
