Global temperature February 2025 was the third warmest February globally, with an average ERA5 surface air temperature of 13.36°C, 0.63°C above the 1991-2020 average for February, and only marginally warmer, by 0.03°C, than the fourth warmest of 2020.

February 2025 was 1.59°C above the estimated 1850-1900 average used to define the pre-industrial level and was the 19th month in the last 20 months for which the global-average surface air temperature was more than 1.5°C above the pre-industrial level.

The global-average temperature for boreal winter 2025 (December 2024 to February 2025) was the second highest on record at 0.71°C above the 1991-2020 average for these three months, 0.05°C cooler than the record set for boreal winter 2024.

The 12-month period of March 2024 – February 2025 was 0.71°C above the 1991-2020 average, and 1.59°C above the pre-industrial level.

Europe and other regions The average temperature over European land for February 2025 was 0.44°C, 0.40°C above the 1991-2020 average for February, ranking it well outside the 10 warmest months of February for Europe.

European temperatures were most above average over northern Fennoscandia, Iceland and the Alps. A large region of negative anomalies was recorded for eastern Europe.

Outside Europe, temperatures were most above average over large parts of the Arctic. They were also above average over northern Chile and Argentina, western Australia and the southwestern United States and Mexico.

Temperatures were most notably below average over parts of the United States and Canada. Other regions with below-average temperatures include the regions adjacent to the Black, Caspian and eastern Mediterranean Seas, as well as in a large region in eastern Asia, covering parts of southern Russia, Mongolia, China and Japan.

The average temperature for European land for winter 2025 (December 2024 to February 2025) was the joint second highest on record for the season at 1.46°C above the 1991-2020 average, significantly cooler than the warmest European winter in 2020 (2.84°C).

Sea surface temperature The average sea surface temperature (SST) for February 2025 over 60°S–60°N was 20.88°C, the second-highest value on record for the month, 0.18°C below the February 2024 record.

SSTs remained unusually high in many ocean basins and seas, though the extent of these regions decreased compared to January, especially in the Southern Ocean and in the southern Atlantic. Some seas, such as the Gulf of Mexico and the Mediterranean Sea, on the contrary, saw larger record-breaking areas than last month.

The European average temperature over land for winter 2025 (December 2024 to February 2025) was 1.46°C above the 1991-2020 average. It is the joint second warmest winter season on record, with 2016 (less than 0.01°C difference) and not much warmer than the 4 th and 5 th warmest winters ending in February 2023 and 2024, both at 1.44°C. It was 1.38°C cooler than the warmest European winter ending in February 2020 (2.84°C).

Surface air temperature differences from 1991-2020 climatological values varied across Europe in February 2025. Warmer-than-average temperatures were recorded over northern and southwestern Europe, while a large region of negative anomalies was seen for eastern Europe. The largest warm anomalies were recorded over northern Fennoscandia, Iceland and the Alps. Early in February, three stations in northern Sweden had their warmest February temperatures on record and it was the second warmest February on record for Troms county in northern Norway.

Outside Europe, temperatures were most above average over large parts of the Arctic, in particular over the North Pole, the Canadian Archipelago and the western and central Siberian Arctic, stretching south towards Central Asia and south-eastwards to Sakhalin Island. During early February, temperatures over the Arctic were around 20°C above average, and high enough for sea ice to melt. Other noteworthy regions with much above-average temperatures include northern Chile and Argentina, western Australia and the southwestern United States and Mexico. Several of these regions experienced heatwaves during the month, including record highs in Los Angeles, California.

Temperatures were most widely below average over a large region around the western United States and Canada border. They were also below average over the regions adjacent to the Black, Caspian and eastern Mediterranean Seas, as well as in a large region in eastern Asia, covering parts of southern Russia, Mongolia, China and Japan. Additionally, all other continents saw a few regions of below average temperatures. Antarctica saw a mixed pattern of above and below average temperatures.

In Europe, temperatures for the boreal winter of 2025 were above the 1991-2020 average over almost all of the continent, the only exception being parts of Iceland and northernmost France. They were most above average over northeastern parts of Europe, over the Alps and around the Adriatic Sea.

Outside Europe, conditions were quite mixed, especially in the Northern Hemisphere. The largest positive anomalies were recorded over North American and Russian Arctic and subarctic land regions, specifically over Alaska, Quebec and central Siberia. Another region with much above-average temperatures was the eastern Himalayas and Tibetan Plateau. There were several regions with negative anomalies, including the central and eastern United States, a band stretching from the Arabian Peninsula over Iran into parts of Central Asia and northern China, as well as parts of the Indochinese Peninsula. South America, Africa, and Australasia generally saw above-average temperatures.

High surface air temperatures over ocean areas were associated with continuing high sea-surface temperatures (SSTs) for 60°S–60°N for the time of year. Since March 2023 the daily SST has been markedly above all previous years, except for a short period during mid-July 2024. This continued throughout February 2025, when the daily SST was higher than all previous years except 2024. The average SST for February 2025 was 20.88°C, 0.42°C above the 1991-2020 average, the second highest on record for February. The February 2025 value was 0.18°C lower than the monthly value for February 2024 and 0.10°C higher than the value for February 2016.

SSTs in the central equatorial Pacific were a little below or close to the 1991-2020 average during February 2025, while they were close to or a little above normal in the eastern equatorial Pacific, suggesting a further slowing or stalling of the move from El Niño towards La Niña conditions as already seen in January (see Highlight box). The SST remained the highest on record or much above average in many oceanic regions outside of the equatorial Pacific, however the extent of these regions decreased since January in almost all ocean basins. Temperatures have moved towards less unusually warm temperatures north of Australia, off the coast of western and southern Africa and in the Southern Ocean. On the contrary, warmer than average, or even record-breaking temperatures, cover a larger part of the Gulf of Mexico, the Mediterranean Sea and the ocean around the coasts of India than in January.

The El Niño Southern Oscillation (ENSO), a natural climate pattern that involves changes in sea surface temperature and winds in the tropical Pacific, known in its warm phase as El Niño and in its cool phase as La Niña, is one of the natural patterns of variability that influences global temperature. On top of the long-term increase in global temperature, driven by human-induced greenhouse gases, El Niño typically gives a temporary “boost” to global temperatures, while La Niña typically acts as a “brake”, both effects sometimes seen with a lag of several months.

El Niño conditions during 2023 and the beginning of 2024 were followed by a slow transition to more neutral conditions. During the last few months there has been some expectation of a possible transition to La Niña. As can be seen from the above animation, there was indeed the appearance of cold SST anomalies in the equatorial Pacific at the end of 2024 and in January 2025. However, since early January, a resurgence of warm anomalies has been observed over the eastern Pacific. A temporary “brake” on global temperature rise due to La Niña is therefore not currently in sight.

Highlights for February 2025 Arctic sea ice reached its lowest monthly extent for February, at 8% below average. This marks the third consecutive month in which the sea ice extent has set a record for the corresponding month.

It is important to note that the new record low for February is not an all-time minimum. Arctic sea ice extent is currently approaching its annual maximum, which typically occurs in March.

In the Arctic region, sea ice concentrations were below average in all ocean sectors outside the central Arctic Ocean.

Antarctic sea ice recorded its fourth lowest monthly extent for February, at 26% below average. The daily extent may have reached its annual minimum near the end of the month. If confirmed, it would be the second-lowest minimum extent

In the Antarctic region, sea ice concentrations were below average in most ocean sectors, with the exception of the eastern Weddell Sea.

In February, the monthly Arctic sea ice extent reached a record low for the time of year for the third consecutive month, following record lows in December and January. The average extent in February 2025 was 13.7 million km2, the lowest value for the month in the 47-year satellite data record, 0.8 million km2 (or about 8%) below the 1991–2020 average for February. The previous lowest February extent was in 2018 (-6%) and was followed by several years with relatively small negative anomalies. It is important to note that this record low for February is not an all-time minimum. Arctic sea ice extent is currently approaching its annual maximum, which typically occurs in March, and remains significantly higher than the levels observed the rest of the year. All-time record minima in the Arctic have always occurred in September, the month when sea ice extent reaches its annual minimum.

The daily Arctic sea ice extent decreased sharply at the end of January, dropping by about 0.3 million km² (roughly the size of Italy) in less than a week. Such a rapid decrease is unusual at this time of year, when sea ice is typically expanding towards its annual maximum. This drop coincided with the onset of a pronounced warm event over the Greenland Sea and Svalbard region (see discussion below). At the time, Arctic sea ice extent was already hovering near record-low levels for this time of year. Throughout February, the daily extent remained at its lowest values for the season. As of 28 February, the daily extent stood at 13.9 million km²—the lowest ever recorded for this date.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

In February 2025, sea ice concentrations in the central Arctic Ocean were close to average, with nearly the entire area covered with ice. Outside this region, sea ice concentrations were predominantly below average. This was particularly evident in the European Arctic sector (Barents Sea and north of Svalbard), the eastern Canadian sector (Gulf of St. Lawrence and Labrador Sea), and the Pacific sector (Bering Sea and Sea of Okhotsk). The negative sea ice concentration anomalies were associated with well above-average surface air temperatures across high northern latitudes in February.

The below-average concentrations in the Svalbard and Barents Sea sector can be partly linked to a pronounced warm event over the area early in the month. This event brought sustained, relatively warm northward winds, bringing above-freezing temperatures as far as the North Pole. The winds also caused strong sea ice drift toward the central Arctic Ocean, pushing the sea ice edge farther north. Later in the month, the wind pattern reversed and increased sea ice drift toward the Greenland Sea, along Greenland’s northeastern coast. This combination of events resulted in near or slightly above average sea ice cover in this sector for the month as a whole.

February is the month when Antarctic sea ice typically reaches its annual minimum extent. The average extent in February 2025 was 2.5 million km², which is 0.9 million km² (about 26%) below the 1991–2020 average for February. This anomaly is significantly larger in magnitude than in January, when the extent was just 5% below average. The February 2025 extent ranks as the fourth lowest in the satellite record, with an anomaly slightly smaller than that of February 2024, which was the third lowest at -28%. It continues a trend toward relatively large negative February anomalies—exceeding 20% below average—observed since 2017. The lowest February extent on record occurred in 2023, at 33% below average, which also marked the all-time minimum for Antarctic sea ice extent.

On 25 February, the daily extent reached its lowest value of 2025 so far, at 2.10 million km². However, it is still too early to confirm whether this will be the year’s minimum. If confirmed, it would be the second-lowest Antarctic sea ice minimum on record, slightly above the lowest value of 2.04 million km² recorded in February 2023. By 28 February, the daily extent stood at 2.21 million km², the lowest on record for that date, marginally below the 2023 level (data from the US National Snow and Ice Data Center suggest place 2023 slightly below 2025 for that date).

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

In February 2025, sea ice concentrations were mostly below average around Antarctica, consistent with the large negative anomaly in sea ice extent mentioned earlier. As Antarctic sea ice cover approaches its annual minimum, little sea ice remains, except in the western Weddell Sea. Well-below-average concentrations are evident across the rest of the Weddell Sea, as well as along the coast of West Antarctica and near Victoria Land (around 160°E). The only other area with above-average concentrations was a small region in the Amundsen-Ross Sea sector.

The sea ice indicator page provides additional information about the long-term trends in both the Arctic and Antarctica.

Global temperature January 2025 was the warmest January globally, with an average ERA5 surface air temperature of 13.23°C, 0.79°C above the 1991-2020 average for January.

January 2025 was 1.75°C above the pre-industrial level and was the 18th month in the last nineteen for which the global-average surface air temperature was more than 1.5°C above the pre-industrial level.

The February 2024 – January 2025 period was 0.73°C above the 1991-2020 average, and 1.61°C above the estimated 1850-1900 average used to define the pre-industrial level.

This twelve-month period was only 0.03°C below the record global-average temperature anomaly of 0.76°C recorded for each of the three 12-month periods ending in June, July and August 2024.

Europe and other regions The average temperature over European land for January 2025 was 1.80°C, 2.51°C above the 1991-2020 average for January, cooler only than January 2020 which was 2.64°C above average.

Outside Europe, temperatures were most above average over northeast and northwest Canada, Alaska, and Siberia. They were also above average over southern South America, Africa, and much of Australia and Antarctica.

Temperatures were most notably below average over the United States and the easternmost regions of Russia, Chukotka and Kamchatka. The Arabian Peninsula and mainland Southeast Asia also had below-average temperatures.

Sea surface temperature The average sea surface temperature (SST) for January 2025 over 60°S–60°N was 20.78°C, the second-highest value on record for the month, 0.19°C below the January 2024 record.

SSTs were below average over the central equatorial Pacific, but close to or above average over the eastern equatorial Pacific, suggesting a slowing or stalling of the move towards from El Niño towards La Niña conditions. SSTs remained unusually high in many other ocean basins and seas.

Surface air temperature differences from 1991-2020 climatological values varied across Europe in January 2025. Western Russia had the largest warm anomalies, with record daily temperatures registered. Fennoscandia, southern and eastern Europe, and Türkiye also experienced warmer-than-average temperatures. Notably, Valencia, Spain, had its warmest January temperature of 26.9°C in a 150-year record. In contrast, Iceland, the United Kingdom, Ireland, northern France, and an area of northern Fennoscandia had below-average temperatures.

Outside Europe, temperatures were most above average over northeast Canada, including Newfoundland, Labrador, Quebec, and Hudson Bay, where the concentration of sea ice was unusually low. They were also well above average across northwest Canada, Alaska, and Siberia. Most of Africa, southern South America, and Australia had warmer-than-average temperatures, with Australia having its second-warmest January in a record dating from 1910. Antarctica also experienced temperatures that were predominantly higher than average.

Temperatures were most widely below average over the United States and notably record-breaking temperatures and historic snowfall affected the southern states. They were also below average over Chukotka and Kamchatka, the easternmost regions of Russia, and across the Arabian Peninsula and mainland Southeast Asia, where Thailand experienced colder-than-average conditions.

High surface air temperatures over ocean areas were associated with continuing high sea-surface temperatures (SSTs) for 60°S–60°N for the time of year. In mid-July 2024, the daily SST fell below the values observed in 2023 after staying continuously at record levels for the first half of 2024. Both 2024 and 2023 remained markedly above all previous years. Throughout January 2025, the daily SST was higher than all previous years except 2024, although some days in January 2016 were within 0.05°C, which was associated with the strong El Niño in 2015. The average SST for January 2025 was 20.78°C, 0.47°C above the 1991-2020 average, the second highest on record for January. The January 2025 value was 0.19°C lower than the monthly value for January 2024 and 0.07°C higher than the value for January 2016.

The latest El Niño event was an important contributor to the high global SSTs observed over the last few months of 2023 and the first months of 2024. Although SSTs in the central equatorial Pacific were mainly below the 1991-2020 average during January 2025, they were close to or a little above normal in the eastern equatorial Pacific, suggesting a slowing or stalling of the move from El Niño towards La Niña conditions. Moreover, SSTs remained the highest on record or at least much above average in many oceanic regions outside of the equatorial Pacific. The map shows high January SSTs in large parts of the Atlantic and Indian Oceans, in parts of the Southern Ocean, and in various parts of the extratropical Pacific Ocean. This was also the case for the Caribbean Sea and parts of the Gulf of Mexico, the Mediterranean Sea and the Barents Sea. The western North Atlantic, eastern South Pacific Ocean, and parts of the South Atlantic and Indian Oceans had cooler-than-average SSTs.

Global temperature December 2024 was the second-warmest December globally, after December 2023, with an average ERA5 surface air temperature of 13.42°C, 0.76°C above the 1991-2020 average for December.

December 2024 was 1.69°C above the pre-industrial level and was the 17 th month in the last eighteen for which the global-average surface air temperature was more than 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels.

The global-average temperature for 2024 is 0.72°C higher than the 1991-2020 average, which is the highest annual average on record, and 0.12°C warmer than the average for 2023, the warmest year prior to 2024.

2024 is 1.6°C warmer than the pre-industrial level and the first year that has exceeded the threshold of 1.5°C, according to ERA5.

Further results for 2024 are presented in our Global Climate Highlights  for the year.

Europe and other regions The average temperature over European land for December 2024 was 2.05°C, 1.37°C above the 1991-2020 average for December, cooler than six other European Decembers since the year 2000. December 2019 is the warmest December on record, at 2.62°C above average.

The European average temperature for 2024 was 1.47°C above the 1991-2020 average for December. This makes 2024 the warmest year on record for Europe.

European temperatures were most above the 1991-2020 average over north-western Russia. They were above average over central, eastern and north-western parts of the continent, and around or a little below average over south-western parts.

Outside Europe, temperatures were most above average over western Siberia. They were also well above average over the rest of Siberia, almost all of North America, southern Africa, and much of Australia and Antarctica.

Temperatures were most notably below average over Greenland and much of central Asia and central-southern South America.

Sea surface temperature The average sea surface temperature (SST) for December 2024 over 60°S–60°N was 20.58°C, the second-highest value on record for the month, 0.21°C below the December 2023 record.

SSTs were below average over the central equatorial Pacific, but close to or above average over the eastern equatorial Pacific, suggesting a slowing or stalling of the move towards neutral or La Niña conditions. SSTs remained unusually high in many other ocean basins and seas.

December 2024 was the 17th month in an 18-month period for which the global-average surface air temperature was more than 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels. 11 of these 17 months, from September 2023 to April 2024, and from October to December 2024, were substantially above 1.5°C, ranging from 1.58°C to 1.78°C. However, the values for May, June, August and September 2024, as well as those for July and August 2023, were very close to 1.5°C (between 1.50°C and 1.54°C). For this reason, given also the small differences among datasets for global temperature anomalies relative to 1991-2020, the 17 months in an 18-month period highlighted here may differ in datasets other than ERA5. Furthermore, there is inherent uncertainty arising from dataset differences in the estimates used for the monthly temperature changes from pre-industrial to 1991-2020 levels.

Surface air temperature differences from 1991-2020 climatological values varied across Europe in December 2024. North-western Russia was unusually mild, with temperatures exceeding 4°C above normal in places. Temperatures were 2 to 3°C above average over much of the north-western, central and eastern regions of the continent. The United Kingdom, for example, experienced what was provisionally its fifth warmest December (and fourth warmest year) in records dating back to 1884, although for Ireland it was provisionally only the eleventh warmest December since 1900. Temperatures measured in southern Norway averaged up to 3 to 4°C above normal, but were up to around 1°C below normal in the far north of the country.

Outside Europe, temperatures were most above average over western Siberia, where they reached more than 10°C above the 1991-2020 average.  They were also well above average over the rest of Siberia and almost all of North America, where the largest anomaly was over Hudson Bay, which had an unusually low concentration of sea ice away from its western shoreline. From the southern hemisphere there were numerous reports of summer heatwave conditions from countries in southern Africa - Botswana, Malawi, Mozambique, Namibia, South Africa and Zimbabwe. Australia experienced its third warmest December (and second warmest year ) since records began in 1910.  Antarctica also experienced temperatures that were predominantly higher than average.

Temperatures were most notably below average over Greenland. They were also below average over much of central Asia and parts of central-southern South America and central-northern Africa.

High surface air temperatures over ocean areas were associated with continuing high sea-surface temperatures (SSTs) for 60°S–60°N for the time of year. In mid-July, the daily SST fell below the values observed in 2023 after staying continuously at record levels for the first half of 2024. Both 2024 and 2023 remained markedly above all previous years until December. SST was higher for some days in December 2015 than for those days in December 2024, and higher for January-March 2016 than for those months in 2023, associated with a strong El Niño in 2015/16 and neutral or La Niña conditions in December 2024 and January-March 2023. The average SST for December 2024 was 20.58°C, 0.40°C above the 1991-2020 average, the second highest on record for December. The December 2024 value was 0.21°C lower than the monthly value for December 2023 and just 0.03°C higher than the value for December 2015.

The latest El Niño event was an important contributor to the high global SSTs observed over the last few months of 2023 and the first months of 2024. Although SSTs in the central equatorial Pacific were mainly below the 1991-2020 average during December, they were close to or a little above normal in the eastern equatorial Pacific, suggesting a slowing or stalling of the move from El Niño towards neutral or La Niña conditions. Moreover, SSTs remained the highest on record or at least much above average in many oceanic regions outside of the equatorial Pacific. The map shows high December SSTs in large parts of the Atlantic and Indian Oceans, in parts of the Southern Ocean, and in various parts of the extratropical Pacific Ocean. This was also the case for the Caribbean Sea and parts of the Gulf of Mexico, the Mediterranean Sea and the Barents Sea. Only small areas of ocean were much cooler than average.

Arctic sea ice reached its lowest monthly extent for December, at 8% below average, surpassing the previous record from 2016 (-7%).

In the Arctic region, sea ice concentration anomalies were well below average in Hudson Bay, in the northern Barents Sea, and western Kara Sea.

Antarctic sea ice extent ranked 10th lowest for December, at 8% below average. This contrasts with the lowest November value, reached a month earlier.

In the Antarctic region, sea ice concentration anomalies in the Southern Ocean were dominated by below-average concentrations in the South Atlantic sector and above-average concentrations in the Weddell Sea and the broad West Antarctic sector.

In the Arctic, the monthly average sea ice extent in December 2024 was 11.5 million km 2, which is 1.0 million km 2 (or about 8%) below the 1991–2020 average for December. This represents the lowest extent for December in the 46-year satellite dataset, surpassing the previous record set in 2016 (-7%). This new record low contrasts with the relatively small negative anomalies observed in December over the past three years (2021–2023).

After reaching its annual minimum in September, the daily Arctic sea ice extent began to quickly grow again in October and November. This growth slowed down in early December, likely due in part to the delayed freeze-up of Hudson Bay. As a result, the daily extent remained at record-low levels for the time of year during most of December. By 31 December, it ranked second lowest for this date, at 12.52 million km 2, slightly higher than 2017 (12.51 million km 2 ).

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

In December 2024, most of the central Arctic Ocean was covered with sea ice and generally saw slightly above-average sea ice concentrations. In contrast, as in November, sea ice concentrations were below average in the northern Barents Sea, around Svalbard, and in the western Kara Sea. Well below-average concentrations also prevailed in Hudson Bay. These low sea-ice conditions coincided with well above average surface air temperatures in the area. The freeze-up of Hudson Bay was likely delayed by unusually warm waters, a result of much warmer-than-average conditions in northeastern Canada in November.

The average Antarctic sea ice extent in December 2024 was 10.1 million km 2, which is 0.9 million km 2 (or about 8%) below the 1991–2020 average for December. This is the 10th lowest extent for December within the 46-year satellite record. This contrasts with November, which saw a record-low extent for the month. While the 2024 anomaly was less pronounced than the anomalies recorded in 2016 (-20%, lowest) and 2023 (-15%, second lowest), it continues the series of relatively large negative anomalies observed since 2016. It is also close to the anomalies recorded in 2017–2019 and 2021.

As the austral summer melt season is underway, Antarctic sea ice extent is rapidly decreasing. After ranking second lowest for the time of year most of the time from July to November, the rate of seasonal decrease slowed down in December. By 31 December, at 7.5 million km 2, the daily extent was close to the median for 1991–2020 and ranked only 25th lowest for that date in the satellite record.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The pattern of sea ice concentration anomalies around Antarctica in December 2024 was more complex than in previous months and characterised by mixed anomalies in most sectors of the Southern Ocean. In general, concentrations were above average over the sea ice pack. Below-average concentrations were most prominent in the Atlantic sector, east of the Weddell Sea, as well as in the Bellingshausen Sea and in the northern Ross and Amundsen Seas. In contrast, above-average concentrations dominated in the Weddell Sea and in the Ross and Amundsen Seas.

The sea ice indicator page provides additional information about the long-term trends in both the Arctic and Antarctica.

Global temperature November 2024 was the second-warmest November globally, after November 2023, with an average ERA5 surface air temperature of 14.10°C, 0.73°C above the 1991-2020 average for November.

November 2024 was 1.62°C above the pre-industrial level and was the 16 th month in a 17-month period for which the global-average surface air temperature exceeded 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels.

The year-to-date (January to November 2024) global-average temperature anomaly is 0.72°C above the 1991-2020 average, which is the highest on record for this period and 0.14°C warmer than the same period in 2023. At this point, it is effectively certain that 2024 is going to be the warmest year on record and more than 1.5°C above the pre-industrial level according to ERA5.

The global-average temperature for boreal autumn (September to November) 2024 was the second highest on record at 0.75°C above the 1991-2020 average for these three months, 0.13°C cooler than the record set in September–November 2023.

Europe and other regions The average temperature over European land for November 2024 was 5.14°C, 0.78°C above the 1991-2020 average for November, leaving the month outside the top 10 warmest Novembers on record for Europe. November 2015 is the warmest November on record, at 1.74°C above average.

Temperatures were above average over northern Russia and across northeastern and southwestern Europe, and below average over southeastern Europe.

Outside Europe, temperatures were most above average over eastern Canada and the central and eastern USA, most of Mexico, Morocco, northwest Africa, China, Pakistan, most of Siberia, and Australia.

Temperatures were most notably below average across the western United States, parts of northern Africa, over far eastern Russia, and across most of Antarctica.

The average temperature for European land for autumn (September to November) 2024 was the third highest on record for the season at 1.25°C above the 1991-2020 average, 0.21°C cooler than the warmest European autumn in 2020.

Sea surface temperature The average sea surface temperature (SST) for November 2024 over 60°S–60°N was 20.58°C, the second-highest value on record for the month, and only 0.13°C below November 2023.

The equatorial eastern and central Pacific had below-average temperatures, indicating a move towards neutral or La Niña conditions, but SSTs across the ocean remained unusually high over many regions.

0.73°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average for November with an absolute surface air temperature of 14.10°C

1.62°C warmer than an estimate of the pre-industrial November average for 1850-1900.

November 2024 was the 16 th month in a 17-month period for which the global-average surface air temperature exceeded 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels. Ten of these 16 months, from September 2023 to April 2024, and October and November 2024, were substantially above 1.5°C, ranging from 1.58°C to 1.78°C. However, the values for May, June, August and September 2024, as well as those for July and August 2023, were very close to 1.5°C (between 1.50°C and 1.54°C). For this reason, given also the small differences among datasets for global temperature anomalies relative to 1991-2020, the 16 months in a 17-month period highlighted here may differ in datasets other than ERA5. Furthermore, there is inherent uncertainty arising from dataset differences in the estimates used for the monthly temperature changes from pre-industrial to 1991-2020 levels.

The global-average temperature for the boreal autumn 2024 (September to November) was the second-highest on record at 0.75°C above the 1991-2020 average for these three months. This is 0.13°C cooler than the record set in September–November 2023 (0.88°C).

Averaging over 12-month periods smooths out shorter-term variations in regional- and global-average temperatures.

Read more  about how the temperature change above the pre-industrial level is estimated and the differences between global temperature datasets.

The year-to-date global-average temperature anomaly (for January–November) is 0.72°C above the 1991-2020 average; this is 0.14°C warmer than for the same period in 2023, the current warmest calendar year. With just one month of the year left it is effectively certain that 2024 will be warmer than 2023, and thus the warmest calendar year on record. Furthermore, given that 2023 was already 1.48°C above the pre-industrial level according to ERA5, it also effectively certain that 2024 will be more than 1.5°C above this level. It is likely, though less certain, that the year will be close to 1.6°C above.

The six surface-temperature datasets examined in ESOTC 2023 show the calendar year 2023 to be the warmest year on record. These different datasets place the year at between 1.44°C to 1.48°C warmer than the 1850–1900 pre-industrial average. This is based on a single value of 0.88°C for the temperature increase from 1850–1900 to 1991–2020, as used routinely in this bulletin. The year-to-date temperatures available at the time of writing for datasets other than ERA5 are for fewer months than is the case for ERA5, but they indicate that it is likely that 2024 will be warmer than 2023 in all six datasets. Temperatures for 2024 are expected to lie within, or very close to, the range 1.5-1.6°C warmer than the 1850–1900 pre-industrial average.

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies, but the relatively dense observational coverage of the continent reduces uncertainty.

The European average temperature over land for autumn (September-November) 2024 was the third highest on record for the season, 1.25°C above the 1991-2020 average and 0.21°C cooler than the warmest European autumn in 2020 (1.46°C).

The warmest calendar year on record for Europe is 2020, which had a temperature 1.19°C higher than the 1991-2020 average.

In November 2024, varied surface air temperature conditions were experienced across Europe. Northern Russia experienced the warmest anomalies from the 1991-2020 climatological values, and record warmth was recorded at Kirkenes, Arctic Norway, on 8 November 2024. Denmark had its warmest November night too. Across southwestern parts of Europe, air temperatures were also above average, with extreme night-time temperatures recorded in France later in the month. In contrast, eastern and southern Europe and Türkiye had below-average temperatures, as did parts of Italy. Although Iceland experienced mixed conditions, it also set a record for the warmest November night anywhere above 60° latitude of 22.9°C.

Outside Europe, temperatures were well above average over much of the Siberian and Canadian Arctic and across north-eastern Canada and the central and eastern USA. Multiple daily temperature records were broken across Canada. Temperatures were also relatively high over most of Mexico, Morocco and northwest Africa, China, and Pakistan, which recorded its warmest November in its 64-year record. Above-average temperatures also predominated in the southern hemisphere, where Australia had its fifth-warmest November on record.

Conversely, below-average temperatures occurred most notably across the western United States, parts of northern Africa, over far eastern Russia, and across most of Antarctica.

In Europe, temperatures for the boreal autumn of 2024 were predominantly above the 1991-2020 average over almost all of the continent. They were most above average over eastern and northeastern parts of Europe and northern Russia. In contrast, Iceland was colder than average, as were pockets of southeastern Europe.

Outside Europe, seasonal-average temperatures were most above average over northern Canada, northern Siberia, central Asia, across the USA, and over the North Pacific Ocean and the Southern Ocean due south of South Africa. The season was colder than average over the Sahel and in isolated pockets of Africa, Asia, and South America. The Bellingshausen Sea also had below-average temperatures and there were below-average temperatures over the equatorial eastern Pacific.

High surface air temperatures over ocean areas were associated with continuing high sea-surface temperatures (SSTs) for 60°S–60°N for the time of year. In mid-July, the daily SST fell below the values observed in 2023 after staying continuously at record levels for the first half of 2024; however, both 2024 and 2023 are markedly above all previous years. The average SST for November 2024 was 20.58°C, 0.46°C above average, the second highest on record for November, and only 0.13°C lower than the monthly value for November 2023.

The recent El Niño event was an important contributor to the high global SSTs observed over the last few months of 2023 and the first months of 2024. Although SSTs in the eastern and central equatorial Pacific were mainly below the 1991-2020 average during November, reflecting the move from El Niño towards neutral or La Niña conditions, SSTs remained the highest on record in the western parts of the tropical Pacific. Moreover, high SSTs in oceanic regions outside of the equatorial Pacific are key contributors to the high global-average SST in November. The map shows that SSTs also reached record high values, or were at least much warmer than average for November, in large parts of the Atlantic and Indian Oceans, in parts of the Southern Ocean, and in various parts of the extratropical Pacific Ocean, most notably around and east of Japan and in the central northern pacific. This was also the case for parts of the Mediterranean Sea and the Barents Sea.

Global and European monthly surface air temperature anomalies (ERA5, degrees C vs 1991-2020) covering the full series from 1940-01 through 2024-11: <ts></ts>

Arctic sea ice reached its third lowest monthly extent for November, at 9% below average.

Sea ice concentration anomalies were well below average in the ocean sector surrounding Svalbard and Franz Josef Land.

Antarctic sea ice extent reached its lowest monthly value for November, at 10% below average, slightly surpassing the values from 2016 and 2023, and continuing a series of historically large negative anomalies observed throughout 2023 and 2024.

Sea ice concentration anomalies in the Southern Ocean were dominated by above-average concentrations in the Weddell Sea and broad West Antarctic sector, and below-average concentrations in the South Atlantic, Indian, and Ross Sea sectors.

In the Arctic, the monthly average sea ice extent in November 2024 was 9.4 million km 2, which is 0.9 million km 2 (or about 9%) below the 1991–2020 average for November. This marks the third-lowest extent for November in the 46-year satellite data record. The November 2024 anomaly is notably larger than in the previous three years (2 to 4% below average) and is comparable to the anomalies observed in 2019 (-7%) and 2020 (-10%). The lowest November extent occurred in 2016, at -13%.

After reaching its annual minimum in September, the daily Arctic sea ice extent began to grow again in October and November. However, it remained among the lowest on record for the time of year in the satellite record. This trend was particularly notable from mid-October onward, when daily extents dropped below the values recorded in 2012—the year when Arctic sea ice extent reached an all-time minimum in September. By 30 November, the daily extent ranked as the third lowest for this date, at 10.3 million km 2, slightly behind 2016 and 2006.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

In November 2024, most of the central Arctic Ocean was covered with sea ice. The most notable exception was the ocean sector surrounding Svalbard and Franz Josef Land, where sea ice concentrations were significantly below average and which remained largely ice-free. Below-average concentrations were also observed in the adjacent western Kara Sea. In contrast, concentrations were generally above average on the opposite side of the Arctic Ocean, particularly in the northern Chukchi Sea. Meanwhile, outside of the central Arctic Ocean, below-average concentrations dominated in the Canadian sector, including Hudson Bay and Baffin Bay). This pattern coincided with surface air temperatures that were well above average.

The average Antarctic sea ice extent in November 2024 was 14.7 million km 2, which is 1.6 million km 2 (or about 10%) below the 1991–2020 average for November. This is the lowest extent for November within the 46-year satellite record, slightly surpassing the previous lowest value of -9% recorded in November 2016 and 2023. With the notable exception of November 2020 (+2%), the November 2024 value marks the continuation of a series of relatively large negative anomalies observed in November since 2016. It also extends the series of historically low sea ice extents recorded throughout 2023 and 2024.

After reaching its annual maximum at the end of September, the daily Antarctic sea ice extent began decreasing again as the melt season progressed. It remained the second lowest throughout most of October and November, slightly exceeding the values from 2016 or 2023. By 30 November, at 13.0 million km 2, the daily extent ranked as the third lowest for that date, slightly above the values from 2016 and 2023.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The pattern of sea ice concentration anomalies around Antarctica in November 2024 was characterised by a marked contrast between above-average concentrations over the sea ice pack and below-average concentrations along and north of the sea ice edge. The Weddell Sea and the broad West Antarctic sector (Bellingshausen, Amundsen, and Ross Seas) were dominated by above-average concentrations. Below-average concentrations were concentrated in the South Atlantic and Indian Ocean sectors (30°W to 45°E) and the northern Ross Sea.

Arctic and Antarctic November sea-ice extent anomalies (million sq km, vs 1991-2020) for each November through 2024: <ts></ts>

Arctic sea ice reached its 4 th lowest monthly extent for October, at 19% below average.

Sea ice concentration anomalies were well below average in all peripheral seas of the Arctic Ocean, particularly in the Barents Sea, Canadian Archipelago, and north of Svalbard.

Antarctic sea ice extent was the second lowest for October, at 8% below average, behind October 2023 (-11%), continuing a series of large negative anomalies observed throughout 2023 and 2024.

Sea ice concentration anomalies in the Southern Ocean continued to be dominated by much below-average concentrations in the Indian Ocean sector, as they have been since July.

In the Arctic, the monthly average sea ice extent in October 2024 was 6.3 million km 2, 1.5 million km 2 (or about 19%) below the 1991–2020 average for October. This is the 4th lowest extent for October in the 46-year satellite data record, with an anomaly notably larger than in 2023 (12%) and close to those observed in 2007 (18%) and 2012 (19%). The lowest October extent occurred in 2020, at -27%.

After reaching its annual minimum extent in September, Arctic sea ice began growing again in October. However, this growth was relatively slow, slower for example than in 2023 or in 2012 (the year when Arctic sea ice extent reached an all-time minimum in September). On 31 October, the daily sea ice extent ranked third lowest for the time of year, at 8.0 million km 2, behind 2016 and 2020.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

In October 2024, sea ice concentrations were well below average in all peripheral seas surrounding the central Arctic Ocean. The negative anomalies were most pronounced in the Beaufort Sea and in the Canadian Archipelago as well as north of Svalbard and Franz Joseph Land. These negative anomalies were consistent with much above-average surface air temperatures in these parts of the Arctic. Below-average concentrations also prevailed along the Siberian coast (East Siberian, Laptev, and Kara Seas). Concentrations were near average within most of the central Arctic Ocean, with notable exceptions in the northern East Siberian and Kara Seas, where they were above average.

The average Antarctic sea ice extent in October 2024 was 17.1 million km 2, 1.5 million km 2 (or 8%) below the 1991–2020 average for October. This is the second-lowest extent for October within the 46-year satellite dataset, behind the record of -11% observed in October 2023. The large negative anomaly for October 2024 marks the continuation of historically low sea ice extents observed throughout 2023 and 2024.

After reaching its annual maximum at the end of September, the daily Antarctic sea ice extent began decreasing again as the melt season got underway. It remained the second lowest for the time of year during the first half of the month. By 31 October, at 16.38 million km 2, it was close to other low values observed in recent years, such as in 2016 and 2022, although not as low as in 2023.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The pattern of sea ice concentration anomalies around Antarctica in October 2024 was dominated by much below-average concentrations in the South Atlantic and most of the Indian Ocean sector (30°W–70°E), a persistent feature observed since July. Concentrations were also below average in the northern Ross Sea. In contrast, above-average concentrations prevailed in the northern Bellinghausen and Amundsen Seas and in the western Pacific sector.

July 2024 was the second-warmest July globally in the data record, with an average ERA5 surface air temperature of 16.91°C, 0.68°C above the 1991-2020 average for July, and only 0.04°C lower than the previous high set in July 2023.

This marks the end of a 13-month period when each month was the warmest in the ERA5 data record for the respective month of the year. While unusual, a similar length streak of monthly global temperature records happened previously in 2015/2016 during the last strong El Niño event.

Although July 2024 was not quite as warm as July 2023 on average, the Earth experienced its two hottest days in the ERA5 data record. The daily global-average temperature reached 17.16°C and 17.15°C in ERA5 on 22 and 23 July. Given the small difference, similar to the level of uncertainty in the ERA5 data, we cannot say which of the two days was the hottest with complete certainty.

According to ERA5 data, the month was 1.48°C above the estimated July average for 1850-1900, the designated pre-industrial reference period, marking the end of a series of 12 consecutive months at or above 1.5°C.

The global-average temperature for the past 12 months (August 2023 – July 2024) is 0.76°C above the 1991-2020 average and 1.64°C above the 1850-1900 pre-industrial average.

The year-to-date (January–July) global temperature anomaly for 2024 is 0.70°C above the 1991-2020 average, 0.27°C warmer than the same period in 2023. The average anomaly for the remaining months of this year would need to drop by at least 0.23°C for 2024 not to be warmer than 2023. This rarely happened in the entire ERA5 dataset, making it increasingly likely that 2024 is going to be the warmest year on record.

The average European temperature for July 2024 was 1.49°C above the 1991-2020 average for July, making the month the second warmest July on record for Europe after July 2010.

European temperatures were most above average over southern and eastern Europe, but near or below average over northwestern Europe.

Outside Europe, temperatures were most above average over the western United States and western Canada, most of Africa, the Middle East and Asia, and eastern Antarctica.

The equatorial Pacific had below-average temperatures, indicating a developing La Niña, but air temperatures over the ocean remained unusually high over many regions.

The sea surface temperature (SST) for July 2024 over 60°S–60°N was 20.88°C, the second-highest value on record for the month, and only 0.01°C below July 2023, which marks the end of a 15-month period when the SST had been the warmest in the ERA5 data record for the respective month of the year.

0.68°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average for July;

the second-warmest July on record, only 0.04°C cooler than July 2023, the warmest July;

1.48°C warmer than an estimate of the pre-industrial average for 1850-1900;

July 2024 was just under 1.5°C above the pre-industrial level and follows a 12-month period of global-average surface air temperatures at or beyond 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels. Eight of these 12 months, from September 2023 to April 2024, were substantially above 1.5°C, ranging from 1.58°C to 1.78°C. However, the values for May and June 2024, as well as those for July and August 2023, were very close to 1.5°C (between 1.50 and 1.53°C). For this reason, and given the small differences among global temperature datasets, the 12-month streak highlighted here may not be present in datasets other than ERA5. Furthermore, there is inherent uncertainty in estimating monthly temperature changes above the pre-industrial levels in ERA5.

Read more  about how the temperature change above the pre-industrial level is estimated and the differences between global temperature datasets.

Averaging over 12-month periods smooths out shorter-term variations in regional- and global-average temperatures.

only marginally colder than the previous 12-month period of July 2023 to June 2024, with a temperature 0.76°C above the 1991-2020 average, or an estimated 1.64°C above the 1850-1900 level;

much higher than the peak values of 0.46°C above average reached for 2015/16 and 2019/20.

For reference, the year 2023 is the warmest calendar year on record to date, with a temperature of 0.60°C above the 1991-2020 average.

The year-to-date global anomaly (for January–July) is 0.70°C above the 1991-2020 average, which is 0.27°C warmer than the same period in the previous warmest calendar year in 2023. Given the large positive global temperature anomalies observed in the latter part of 2023 and the transition to La Niña conditions expected in the coming months, the margin between 2024 and 2023 is expected to decrease by the end of the year. Nevertheless, an average anomaly of only 0.47°C or higher for the remaining months of this year would make 2024 warmer than 2023. In other words, the average anomaly for August–December 2024 would need to drop by at least 0.23°C for 2024 not to be warmer than 2023. A drop of this magnitude between January–July and August–December only happened once in the entire ERA5 dataset (in 1992). Therefore, it is increasingly likely that 2024 will be warmer than 2023, and thus the warmest year on record.

On 22 and 23 July 2024, the Earth experienced its two hottest days in recent history, according to ERA5 and other datasets. The daily global-average temperature reached 17.16°C on 22 July and 17.15°C on 23 July in ERA5, the highest values in the dataset, exceeding the previous records of 17.09°C, set just one day before on 21 July 2024, and 17.08°C, set a year earlier on 6 July 2023. However, the small temperature difference (0.01°C) between 22 and 23 July and the uncertainties inherent to ERA5 data mean that it is not possible to confirm with certainty which of the two days was the hottest. Further details are available in this web article.

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies, but the relatively dense observational coverage of the continent reduces uncertainty.

1.49°C higher than the 1991-2020 average for July;

the second-warmest July on record for Europe after July 2010, which was 1.73°C above the average, and only a marginal 0.07°C warmer than July 2021.

the highest on record for any period of 12 consecutive months.

For reference, the year 2020 is the warmest calendar year on record for Europe, with a temperature 1.19°C above the 1991-2020 average.

In July 2024, air temperatures were well above their 1991-2020 average over southern and eastern Europe. Heatwaves gripped many regions with temperatures exceeding 40°C in Italy and Greece and night-time temperatures in Athens were around 30°C, which substantially exceeded the criterion of 20°C for the definition of a tropical night. Bulgaria also recorded one of its warmest Julys – similar to 2012 – since 1930. Wildfires were widespread in the Balkans, notably in Albania and in Greece too. The northern part of Fennoscandia also experienced warmer-than-average temperatures. In contrast, many areas of northwestern Europe had near- or below-average temperatures, including northern France, Ireland, the United Kingdom, Denmark, and southern Norway and Sweden.

Outside Europe, eastern Antarctica had the largest warm anomalies globally. Temperatures were well above average over the western United States and over British Columbia and Alberta in Canada, where heatwaves caused multiple records to be broken, for example, in Palm Springs, California. Northwest Mexico was also warmer than average. The majority of Africa had warmer-than-average conditions, as did the Middle East and Asia, where India and Pakistan experienced heatwaves. Conversely, the most below-average temperatures occurred over western Antarctica. Alaska, the central United States, parts of southern South America and Australia. Pockets of Africa and Asia also had cooler-than-average temperatures.

below average over a few oceanic areas, particularly over part of the south-eastern Pacific Ocean and east of Greenland.

High surface air temperatures over ocean areas were associated with continuing record or near-record sea-surface temperatures (SSTs) for 60°S–60°N for the time of year. The daily SST fell below the values observed in 2023 (by up to 0.05°C on 31 July) after staying continuously at record levels since the beginning of 2024; note though that both 2024 and 2023 are markedly above all previous years. The average SST for July 2024 was 20.88°C, 0.50°C above average, the second highest on record for July, and only 0.01°C lower than the monthly value for July 2023. This marks the end of a 15-month period when the SST had been the warmest in the ERA5 data record for the respective month of the year.

The latest El Niño event has been an important contributor to the high global SSTs observed over the past year. Although SSTs in the eastern equatorial Pacific increasingly fell below the 1991-2020 average during July, indicative of an expected transition from El Niño to La Niña conditions, SSTs remained the highest on record in a sizeable part of the tropical western Pacific and to the west of Central America, as shown in the above map of anomalies and extremes. Moreover, high SSTs in oceanic regions outside of the equatorial Pacific are a key contributor to the record or near-record global-average SST in July. The map shows that SSTs also reached record high values for July in a substantial portion of the Atlantic Ocean (subtropical North Atlantic, extratropical South Atlantic, equatorial Atlantic, and the Caribbean Sea), in many parts of the Indian Ocean, in the Southern Ocean sector south of Africa, and in various parts of the extratropical Pacific Ocean. Record SSTs across the Caribbean Sea may have contributed to the intensification of Hurricane Beryl, a storm that was exceptional in reaching Category 5 as early in the year as 1 July.

Arctic sea ice extent was 7% below average, more below average than in 2022 and 2023 but not as much as the record -14% observed in 2020.

Sea ice concentration anomalies were below average across most of the Arctic Ocean, especially along the northern coast of Siberia.

Antarctic sea ice extent was 11% below average, the second-lowest extent for July in the satellite data record, behind the lowest July value of -15% observed in 2023.

Sea ice concentration anomalies in the Southern Ocean were dominated by much below average concentrations in the Indian Ocean sector.

Arctic sea ice extent remained relatively close to average in July 2024. The monthly average extent was 8.6 million km 2, only 0.6 million km 2 (or about 7%) below the 1991–2020 average for July. This is the 9th lowest extent for July in the satellite data record (note that data from the US National Snow and Ice Data Center place July 2024 as the 6th lowest). The anomaly for July 2024 is in line with anomalies of about 5–7% observed in 2010, 2013–2014, and 2016–2017. It is larger in magnitude than those recorded in 2022 and 2023 but smaller than the large anomalies observed in 2019–2021. The lowest extent for July occurred in 2020, at -14%.

The -7% anomaly for July as a whole masks a contrast in the evolution of the daily Arctic sea ice extent between the beginning and the end of the month. The daily sea ice extent started the month slightly above the median extent for 1991–2020. It then decreased rapidly to end the month markedly below the median, at levels virtually identical to those recorded in 2012.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

Sea ice concentration anomalies in July 2024 were dominated by below-average concentrations across most of the Arctic Ocean. This is a contrast with the mixed concentration anomalies observed in previous months. Concentrations were most below average along the Siberian coast (eastern Kara Sea, Laptev Sea, and western East Siberian Sea), in the northeast Atlantic sector (Greenland Sea and northern Barents Sea), in the Beaufort Sea, and in most of Hudson Bay. Only three areas saw noticeably above-average concentrations: the Eastern Kara Sea, the western Chukchi Sea, and along the western shore of Hudson Bay.

The average Antarctic sea ice extent for July 2024 was 14.6 million km 2, 1.8 million km 2 (or 11%) below the 1991–2020 average for July. This was the second-lowest extent for July within the 46-year satellite dataset, behind the record of -15% observed in July 2023. 2024 is the third year in a row characterised by a relatively large negative anomaly in July.

Throughout July, the daily Antarctic sea ice extent remained the second-lowest for the time of year, markedly below the values observed in all years prior to 2023. Around the middle of the month, it even came close to reaching the record-low values of 2023.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The pattern of sea ice concentration anomalies around Antarctica in July 2024 was dominated by much below-average concentrations in the Indian Ocean sector, east of the Weddell Sea. In this sector, the map of monthly average concentrations (left panel in the figure above) highlights a substantial area of ice-free ocean between the average ice edge for 1991–2020 and the sea ice cover observed in July 2024. The much-reduced sea ice cover in this area also led to much above-average surface air temperatures. Below-average sea ice concentrations also prevailed in other sectors of the Southern Ocean, such as the northern Ross and Amundsen Seas (90°W–180°W) and the western Pacific sector (120°E–180°E). In contrast, the northern Weddell Sea stood out with an area of well above-average sea ice concentrations.

Arctic and Antarctic July sea-ice extent anomalies (million sq km, vs 1991-2020) for each July through 2024: <ts></ts>

June 2024 was warmer globally than any previous June in the data record, with an average ERA5 surface air temperature of 16.66°C, 0.67°C above the 1991-2020 average for June and 0.14°C above the previous high set in June 2023. This is the thirteenth month in a row that is the warmest in the ERA5 data record for the respective month of the year. While unusual, a similar streak of monthly global temperature records happened previously in 2015/2016. According to ERA5 data, the month was 1.50°C above the estimated June average for 1850-1900, the designated pre-industrial reference period, making it the twelfth consecutive month to reach or break the 1.5°C threshold. The global-average temperature for the past 12 months (July 2023 – June 2024) is the highest on record, at 0.76°C above the 1991-2020 average and 1.64°C above the 1850-1900 pre-industrial average.

The average European temperature for June 2024 was 1.57°C above the 1991-2020 average for June, making the month the joint-second warmest June on record for Europe. European temperatures were most above average over southeast regions and Türkiye, but near or below average over western Europe, Iceland and northwestern Russia.

Outside Europe, temperatures were most above average over eastern Canada, the western United States and Mexico, Brazil, northern Siberia, the Middle East, northern Africa and western Antarctica. Temperatures were below average over the eastern equatorial Pacific, indicating a developing La Niña, but air temperatures over the ocean remained at an unusually high level over many regions.

The sea surface temperature (SST) averaged for June 2024 over 60°S–60°N was 20.85°C, the highest value on record for the month. This is the fifteenth month in a row that the SST has been the warmest in the ERA5 data record for the respective month of the year.

In June 2024, air temperatures were well above their 1991-2020 average over southern Italy, southeast Europe and Türkiye, reflecting the heatwaves that occurred in Cyprus, Greece and Türkiye. Temperatures over 40°C were recorded in many places and Athens had its hottest June in a data record going back to 1860, while Greece had its warmest June since 2010. Most of eastern Europe and Fennoscandia also experienced above-average temperatures. In contrast, many areas of western Europe had near- or below-average temperatures, including Portugal, Spain, Ireland and the United Kingdom; the average United Kingdom temperature did mask the variability within June. Iceland and a region of northwest Russia near the Kara Sea had below-average temperatures too.

Outside Europe, temperatures were above average over eastern Canada, the western United States and Mexico, and Brazil, where there were a large number of wildfires. Eastern India suffered heatwaves, as did Pakistan and Korea where the hottest June day was recorded. The majority of northern Siberia, the Middle East and northern Africa also experienced temperatures above average, as did western Antarctica. Conversely, below-average temperatures occurred over central Canada, in eastern Russia surrounding the Sea of Okhotsk, along the coastal region of northwest Africa, in Patagonia in Argentina, in the northern and eastern areas of Australia, and in parts of eastern Antarctica.

Air temperatures were higher than average over most of the Atlantic and Indian Oceans and much of the Pacific Ocean. Temperatures were below average over the eastern equatorial Pacific Ocean and several other relatively small areas of the world’s ocean, including the Bering Sea, and most extensively over the Southern Hemisphere in a region close to Chile and Argentina that extends into the sub-tropical eastern Pacific, and across the southern Indian Ocean.

The high marine air temperatures were associated with continuing record or near-record sea-surface temperatures (SSTs) for the time of year. The daily SST fell below the values observed in 2023 (but only by 0.01°C) after staying continuously at record levels since the beginning of the 2024. Despite not reaching record values at the end of June, the daily SST is virtually on par with the values from June 2023, both years being markedly above all previous years. The average for June 2024 of 0.50°C was below the 0.54°C recorded for March and April 2024, and above the previous highest June value of 0.46°C recorded for June 2023. June 2024 is the fifteenth month in a row that the average SST has been the warmest in the record for the respective month of the year. The area-averaged SST fell below corresponding 2023 values towards the end of the month, but remained much higher than in earlier years.

The latest El Niño event has been an important contributor to the high global SSTs observed over the past year. Although SSTs in the eastern equatorial Pacific increasingly fell below the 1991-2020 average during June, indicative of an expected transition from El Niño to La Niña conditions, SSTs remained the highest on record in a sizeable part of the tropical western Pacific and to the west of Central America, as shown in the above map of anomalies and extremes. Moreover, high SSTs in oceanic regions outside of the equatorial Pacific are a key contributor to the record or near-record global-average SST in June. The map shows that SSTs also reached record high values for June in a substantial portion of the Atlantic Ocean (subtropical North Atlantic, extratropical South Atlantic, equatorial Atlantic, and the Caribbean Sea), in many parts of the Indian Ocean, in the Southern Ocean sector south-west of South Africa, and in various parts of the extratropical Pacific Ocean. Record SSTs across the Caribbean Sea may have contributed to the intensification of Hurricane Beryl, a storm that was exceptional in reaching Category 5 as early in the year as 1 July.

0.67°C  warmer than the 1991-2020 average for June

the warmest June on record, 0.14°C warmer than June 2023, the previous warmest June

1.50°C warmer than an estimate of the pre-industrial average for 1850-1900

the thirteenth consecutive month for which the monthly temperature has been the warmest on record for the respective month of the year.

According to the ERA5 dataset, June 2024 was the twelfth consecutive month with a global average surface air temperature at or beyond 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels. Eight of these 12 months, from September 2023 to April 2024, were substantially above 1.5°C, ranging from 1.58 to 1.78°C. However, the values for May and June 2024, as well as those for July and August 2023, were very close to 1.5°C (between 1.50 and 1.53°C). For this reason, and given the small differences among global temperature datasets, the 12-month streak highlighted here may not be present in datasets other than ERA5. Furthermore, there is inherent uncertainty in estimating monthly temperature changes above the pre-industrial levels in ERA5 (see Relating daily and monthly anomalies to 1850-1900 reference values).

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for June 2024 was 1.57°C  higher than the 1991-2020 average, making the month the joint-second warmest June on record for Europe with June 2022, though only a marginal 0.06°C warmer than June 2021. June 2019 was the warmest European June, at 1.87°C above the average.

below average over a few oceanic areas, particularly over part of the south-eastern Pacific Ocean and east of Greenland.

Averaging over 12-month periods smooths out shorter-term variations in regional- and global-average temperatures. Globally, the last 12 months were warmer than any previous 12-month period, with a temperature 0.76°C  above the 1991-2020 average, much higher than the peak values of 0.46°C above average reached for 2015/16 and 2019/20. 2023 is the warmest calendar year on record, with a temperature of 0.60°C above the 1991-2020 average.

Using an annual-mean offset of 0.88°C (see side box), the average global temperature for the 12 months to June 2024 is estimated to be 1.63°C above the 1850-1900 level.

There is more variability in average European temperatures, but the relatively dense observational coverage of the continent reduces uncertainty. The average for this latest 12-month period is 1.37°C  above the 1991-2020 average, the highest on record for any period of 12 consecutive months. 2020 is the warmest calendar year on record for Europe, with a temperature 1.19°C above the 1991-2020 average.

From October 2021 an updated approach is used to relate recent global temperatures to 1850-1900, a period taken to approximate the pre-industrial level. Following this approach, the value 0.88°C should be added to annual values relative to the 1991-2020 reference period. This estimate derives from the approach used in the WMO statements on “The state of the global climate” from the Preliminary Statement for 2021 onwards, which builds on the methods outlined in the IPCC AR6 WG1 report. Read more in About the data.

There is general agreement among datasets that the period since 2015 is much warmer globally than any previous period, and that 2023 is the warmest year of all. There is also agreement that global temperature has risen at an average rate close to 0.2°C per decade since the late 1970s. There is nevertheless still some spread between the datasets for recent years, such as for 2022 and 2023, and the annual average temperature anomalies for these years from ERA5 are generally higher than those from the five other datasets considered. The differences range from 0.01 to 0.07°C for 2016-2023. The range is 0.00 to 0.06°C if air temperature over sea is replaced by sea-surface temperature for ERA5 and the other dataset for which sea-surface temperature was not used by design. The remaining differences depend partly on the extent to which datasets represent the relatively warm conditions that have predominated over the Arctic and Antarctic during these years. Differences elsewhere in estimates of sea-surface temperature and surface air temperature over land have been further factors. The surface air temperature analysis homepage explains more about the production and reliability of the values presented here.

Arctic sea ice extent was 3% below average, close to the values observed most years since 2010. Sea ice concentration anomalies were mixed across the Arctic Ocean, but mostly well below average in Hudson Bay.

Antarctic sea ice extent was 12% below average, the second-lowest extent for June in the satellite data record, behind the lowest June value of -16% observed in 2023. Sea ice concentrations were most below-average in the Ross-Amundsen Sea sector and east of the Weddell Sea.

Arctic sea ice extent remained relatively close to average in June 2024. The monthly average extent was 11.1 million km2, only 0.4 million km 2 (or about 3%) below the 1991–2020 average for June. This is the 12th lowest extent for June in the satellite data record. The anomaly for June 2024 is relatively close to the range of June anomalies frequently observed since 2010 (within 3–5% below average). The lowest extent for June occurred in 2016, at -6%.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

As the summer melt season progresses, areas of open water have started to appear in the peripheral seas of the Arctic Ocean. Sea ice concentration anomalies in June 2024 were mixed across the various ocean sectors. Concentrations were much below average in most of Hudson Bay, except for a band of above average concentrations along its western shore. Below-average concentrations prevailed in the Greenland Sea, contrasting with the above-average concentrations that persisted in this sector during most of 2023 and the beginning of 2024. Concentrations were also below average in the northern Barents Sea (south of Svalbard), eastern Kara Sea and eastern Beaufort Sea. On the other hand, concentrations were above average in the western Kara Sea and in the Chukchi Sea.

The average Antarctic sea ice extent for June 2024 was 12.2 million km2, 1.6 million km2 (or 12%)   below the 1991–2020 average for June. This was the second-lowest extent for June within the 46-year satellite dataset, behind the record of -16% observed in June 2023. 2024 is the third year in a row characterised by a large negative anomaly in June.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The pattern of sea ice concentration anomalies around Antarctica in June 2024 bears some resemblance to the pattern of anomalies observed in May, particularly in the South Pacific and South Atlantic sectors. Well below-average sea ice concentrations were prominent in the Amundsen Sea, north-eastern Ross, and east of the Weddell Sea from about 20°W to 100°E. Above-average concentrations prevailed on both sides of the Antarctic Peninsula (in the Bellingshausen Sea and northern Weddell Sea). Concentration anomalies were mixed in the Western Pacific sector (100°–180°E).
