Arctic and Antarctic June sea-ice extent anomalies (million sq km, vs 1991-2020) for each June through 2024: <ts></ts>

May 2024 was warmer globally than any previous May in the data record, with an average ERA5 surface air temperature of 15.91°C, 0.65°C above the 1991-2020 average for May and 0.19°C above the previous high set in May 2020. This is the twelfth month in a row that is the warmest in the ERA5 data record for the respective month of the year. While unusual, a similar streak of monthly global temperature records happened previously in 2015/2016. The month was 1.52°C above the estimated May average for 1850-1900, the designated pre-industrial reference period.

The global-average temperature for the past 12 months (June 2023 – May 2024) is the highest on record, at 0.75°C above the 1991-2020 average and 1.63°C above the 1850-1900 pre-industrial average. The average European temperature for May 2024 was 0.88°C above the 1991-2020 average for May, making the month the third warmest May on record for the continent. European temperatures were most above average over northern regions, but below average over Russia and parts of the south-west and south-east of the continent.

Outside Europe, temperatures were most above average over northern Canada, central America and parts of Africa, Asia and Antarctica. Temperatures were below average over the eastern equatorial Pacific, indicating a developing La Niña, but air temperatures over the ocean  remained at an unusually high level over many regions.

The sea surface temperature (SST) averaged for May 2024 over 60°S–60°N was 20.93°C, the highest value on record for the month. This is the fourteenth month in a row that the SST has been the warmest in the ERA5 data record for the respective month of the year.

The global average temperature for March-May 2024 was a record 0.68°C above the 1991-2020 average for these three months.

The European average temperature for spring (March-May) 2024 was the highest on record for the season, 1.50°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average for the season and 0.36°C warmer than the previous warmest European spring, in 2014. European temperatures were most above average over central and eastern regions of the continent, and over parts of Norway and Sweden.

May 2024 temperatures were well above their 1991-2020 average for the month over most of northern Europe, although below average over western Russia. They were closer to average over the south of the continent, with slightly below average values in several countries. The month was reported by the United Kingdom to be provisionally the warmest May in a national record dating back to 1884. It was close to the warmest May on record over Norway as a whole, second only to 2018. Much of Sweden also experienced its second warmest May. Exceptionally for May, Finland issued a heatwave warming in the final week of the month.

Outside Europe, temperatures were much above average over much of northern and eastern Canada, north-eastern Asia, Africa and Antarctica. Heatwave conditions were experienced in Mexico and in neighbouring Texas and Central America. Pre-monsoon temperatures in parts of Pakistan and northern India reached 50°C or more. High temperatures over southern Brazil early in the month along with colder conditions to the south have been cited as factors behind the extreme flooding  that occurred in the country’s southernmost state.

Conversely, below-average temperatures occurred over Greenland, western Russia, the north-west of North America, southern South America, and parts of Antarctica. Chile is reported to have experienced its coldest May in more than seventy years, while observational data from Argentina for the first 27 days of May pointed to the coldest May in a record dating back to 1961.

Air temperatures were higher than average over most of the Atlantic and Indian Oceans and much of the Pacific Ocean. Temperatures were below average over the eastern equatorial Pacific Ocean and several other relatively small areas of the world’s ocean, most extensively over the Southern Hemisphere, in a region close to Chile and Argentina that extends into the sub-tropical eastern Pacific, and in a band from Antarctica to New Zealand.

The high marine air temperatures were associated with continuing record sea-surface temperatures (SSTs) for the time of year. The daily SST averaged over 60°S–60°N (see our Climate Pulse web app) ranged in May 2024 from 0.50°C to 0.55°C above its 1991-2020 average for the month. The average for May 2024 of 0.53°C was only marginally below the 0.54°C recorded for March and April 2024, and well above the previous highest May value of 0.36°C recorded for May 2023. May 2024 is the fourteenth month in a row that the SST has been the warmest in the record for the respective month of the year.

The latest El Niño event has been an important contributor to the high global SSTs observed over the past year. Although SSTs in the eastern equatorial Pacific increasingly fell below the 1991-2020 average during May, indicative of an expected transition from El Niño to La Niña, SSTs remained the highest on record in a sizeable part of the tropical western Pacific and in the northern tropical eastern Pacific, as shown in the above map of anomalies and extremes. Moreover, high SSTs in oceanic regions outside of the equatorial Pacific are a key contributor to the current global SST records. The map shows that SSTs also reached record high values for May in a substantial portion of the Atlantic Ocean (subtropical North Atlantic, extratropical South Atlantic, equatorial Atlantic), in much of the Indian Ocean, in the Southern Ocean sector south-west of South Africa, and in various parts of the extratropical Pacific Ocean.

In the maps showing ‘anomalies and extremes’ for surface air temperature and sea surface temperature, 'Warmest' means the warmest within the 1979–2024 period, ‘Coolest’ means the coolest within the 1979–2024 period. Other categories refer to the temperature distribution during the 1991–2020 reference period:

‘Warmer than average’: warmer than the 66.6th percentile;

‘Near average’: between the 33.3th and 66.6th percentiles;

‘Cooler than average’: cooler than the 33.3th percentile;

‘Much cooler than average': cooler than the 10th percentile.

0.65°C  warmer than the 1991-2020 average for May

the warmest May on record, 0.19°C warmer than May 2020, the previous warmest May

1.52°C warmer than an estimate of the pre-industrial average for 1850-1900

the twelfth consecutive month for which the monthly temperature has been the warmest on record for the respective month of the year. Further discussion of this is given in the highlight box below.

The daily global temperature anomaly relative to 1991-2020 ranged from 0.56°C on 19 and 20 May to 0.85°C on 31 May (see Climate Pulse ).

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for May 2024 was 0.88°C  higher than the 1991-2020 average, making the month the third warmest May on record for Europe, though only a marginal 0.01°C warmer than two other Mays. May 2018 was the warmest European May, at 2.07°C above the average.

May 2024 marks the 12th consecutive month (since June 2023) for which the global average temperature reached a record value for the corresponding month, according to ERA5 data. The margin by which each of the last 12 months has been the warmest on record varies from 0.10°C for March 2024 to 0.50°C for September 2023, relative to previous records. These margins are larger than the typical differences among the various global temperature datasets for recent years. While unusual, a similar streak of monthly global temperature records (by margins of 0.08°C to 0.46°C) previously occurred in 2015/2016, lasting up to 15 months.

Following the streak of 12 monthly records, the global average temperature for June 2023 to May 2024 is also the highest on record, at 0.75°C above the 1991–2020 average or 1.63°C above the 1850–1900 pre-industrial average. Out of the 12 months, 11 (from July 2023 to May 2024) saw monthly global temperatures more than 1.5°C above the pre-industrial level, according to ERA5 data. While a few months in 2015, 2016, and 2020 also reached 1.5°C, the latest 11-month streak is the longest to date.

The past 12 months saw much above-average surface temperatures over large fractions of the continents and oceans, with exceptions in the south-eastern Pacific as well as parts of Antarctica. In particular, the 12-month average temperatures reached record values over many parts of the tropical oceans, including the western Pacific, Atlantic, and Indian Oceans.

Temperatures for the boreal spring of 2024 were predominantly above the 1991-2020 average over almost all of Europe. They were most above average over central and eastern regions of the continent, and over parts of Norway and Sweden.

Seasonal-average temperatures elsewhere were most above average over northern and eastern Canada, central America, much of Africa and parts of eastern Asia and Antarctica. The season was colder than average over the western USA, the Arctic Ocean, parts of northern and south-western Asia, much of Chile, Argentina and Australia, and parts of Antarctica.

The seasonal-average temperature anomalies relative to 1991-2020 were similar over ice-free seas to those seen for May 2024. The main change during the season was an increasing prominence of below-average temperatures over the equatorial eastern Pacific.

The global average temperature for March-May 2024 was a record 0.68°C  above the 1991-2020 average for these three months, but less extreme than the two preceding three-month periods.

The European average temperature for spring (March-May) 2024 was the highest on record for the season, 1.50°C  above the 1991-2020 average for the season and 0.36°C warmer than the previous warmest European spring, in 2014.

below average over a few oceanic areas, particularly over part of the south-eastern Pacific Ocean and east of Greenland.

Averaging over 12-month periods smooths out shorter-term variations in regional- and global-average temperatures. Globally, the last 12 months were warmer than any previous 12-month period, with a temperature 0.75°C  above the 1991-2020 average, much higher than the peak values of 0.46°C above average reached for 2015/16 and 2019/20. 2023 is the warmest calendar year on record, with a temperature of 0.60°C above the 1991-2020 average.

Using an annual-mean offset of 0.88°C (see side box), the average global temperature for the 12 months to April 2024 is estimated to be 1.63°C above the 1850-1900 level. The average over the 17 months from the beginning of 2023 is 1.53°C above the 1850-1900 level.

There is more variability in average European temperatures, but the relatively dense observational coverage of the continent reduces uncertainty. The average for this latest 12-month period is 1.30°C  above the 1991-2020 average, the highest on record for any period of 12 consecutive months. 2020 is the warmest calendar year on record for Europe, with a temperature 1.19°C above the 1991-2020 average.

From October 2021 an updated approach is used to relate recent global temperatures to 1850-1900, a period taken to approximate the pre-industrial level. Following this approach, the value 0.88°C should be added to annual values relative to the 1991-2020 reference period. This estimate derives from the approach used in the WMO statements on “The state of the global climate” from the Preliminary Statement for 2021 onwards, which builds on the methods outlined in the IPCC AR6 WG1 report. Read more in About the data.

There is general agreement among datasets that the period since 2015 is much warmer globally than any previous period, and that 2023 is the warmest year of all. There is also agreement that global temperature has risen at an average rate close to 0.2°C per decade since the late 1970s. There is nevertheless still some spread between the datasets for recent years, such as for 2022 and 2023, and the annual average temperature anomalies for these years from ERA5 are generally higher than those from the five other datasets considered. The differences range from 0.01 to 0.07°C for 2016-2023. The range is 0.00 to 0.06°C if air temperature over sea is replaced by sea-surface temperature for ERA5 and the other dataset for which sea-surface temperature was not used by design. The remaining differences depend partly on the extent to which datasets represent the relatively warm conditions that have predominated over the Arctic and Antarctic during these years. Differences elsewhere in estimates of sea-surface temperature and surface air temperature over land have been further factors. The surface air temperature analysis homepage explains more about the production and reliability of the values presented here.

Arctic sea ice extent was only slightly below average, as it was in May 2022 and 2023. Sea ice concentration anomalies were mixed across the Arctic Ocean, with a marked contrast across Hudson Bay.

Antarctic sea ice extent was 8% below average, the 6th lowest extent for May in the satellite data record, markedly smaller in magnitude than the record -17% observed in 2023. As in the past three months, sea ice concentrations were most below-average in the northern Weddell Sea and in the Ross-Amundsen Sea sector.

Arctic sea ice extent remained relatively close to average in May 2024. The monthly average extent was 12.9 million km2, only 0.1 million km 2 (or about 1%)  below the 1991–2020 average for May. This is the fourth consecutive year characterised by small negative sea ice extent anomalies (2% or less) in May. This follows a period with larger negative anomalies (4–7% below average) in 2016 and 2018–2020.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The pattern of sea ice concentration anomalies for May 2024 is characterised by a mix of above- and below-average concentrations across the different sectors of the Arctic Ocean. In the Greenland Sea, concentrations were above average only in the southern part of the sea, in contrast to the more widespread positive concentration anomalies observed in this sector since October. Above-average concentrations also prevailed in the northern Barents Sea and in the Bering Strait. Concentrations were below average (albeit often only slightly) in the Labrador Sea, northern Greenland Sea, parts of the Barents Sea, and northern Sea of Okhotsk. Hudson Bay saw a sharp contrast between well below-average concentrations in its western part and above-average concentrations along its eastern coast.

The average Antarctic sea ice extent for May 2024 was 9.7 million km2, 0.9 million km2 (or 8%)  below the 1991–2020 average for March. This was the sixth-lowest extent for May within the 46-year satellite dataset. While it was on par with the anomaly recorded in May 2022, it was much smaller in magnitude than the anomaly of -17% recorded a year earlier, in 2023, at the beginning of a 6-month stretch of record-low values for the time of year (until October 2023).

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The pattern of sea ice concentration anomalies around Antarctica in May 2024 was overall very similar to one observed in April. Below-average sea ice concentrations were most prominent in the north-eastern Weddell Sea and the northern Ross and Amundsen Seas. On the other hand, above-average concentrations prevailed in the Bellingshausen Sea and in the western Pacific sector (90°–180°E).

The sea ice analysis page provides additional information about the nature, production and reliability of the data and information presented here.

The sea ice indicator page provides additional information about the long-term trends in both the Arctic and Antarctica.

Arctic and Antarctic May sea-ice extent anomalies (million sq km, vs 1991-2020) for each May through 2024: <ts></ts>

April 2024 was warmer globally than any previous April in the data record, with an average ERA5 surface air temperature of 15.03°C, 0.67°C above the 1991-2020 average for April and 0.14°C above the previous high set in April 2016.   This is the eleventh month in a row that is the warmest in the ERA5 data record for the respective month of the year. While unusual, a similar streak of monthly global temperature records happened previously in 2015/2016. The month was 1.58°C warmer than an estimate of the April average for 1850-1900, the designated pre-industrial reference period. The global-average temperature for the past 12 months (May 2023 – April 2024) is the highest on record, at 0.73°C above the 1991-2020 average and 1.61°C above the 1850-1900 pre-industrial average. The average European temperature for April 2024 was 1.49°C above the 1991-2020 average for April, making the month the second warmest April on record for the continent. Temperatures were most above average in eastern European regions. Fennoscandia and Iceland experienced below-average temperatures. The mean temperature, however, masks the contrast between warmer and colder temperatures experienced at the start and latter part of April in western Europe.

Outside Europe, temperatures were most above average over northern and northeastern North America, Greenland, eastern Asia, northwest Middle East, parts of South America, and most of Africa. The El Niño in the eastern equatorial Pacific continued to weaken towards neutral conditions, but marine air temperatures in general remained at an unusually high level.

The global sea surface temperature (SST) averaged for April 2024 over 60°S–60°N was 21.04°C, the highest value on record for the month, marginally below the 21.07°C recorded for March 2024.This is the thirteenth month in a row that the SST has been the warmest in the ERA5 data record for the respective month of the year.

April 2024 saw temperatures at or above the 1991-2020 average across most of Europe. The largest anomalies occurred over western Russia, eastern Ukraine, and Türkiye; some areas had warm anomalies of around 7°C. Moscow broke a daily temperature record on 2 April – set in 1951 – when 23.2°C was measured. Southeast Europe and southern Italy also experienced above-average temperatures, as did the Iberian Peninsula where parts had days with maximum temperatures exceeding 30°C. In contrast, temperatures were below average across most of Fennoscandia. Notably, in Sweden, a temperature record from 1955 was broken when -32.2°C was measured at Västerbotten. Temperature anomalies were mixed over eastern France, western Germany, and Switzerland, where mild weather followed by frosts towards the end of the month caused significant risks to orchards and vineyards, a risk which was lessened by the lighting of torches to combat the low temperatures there.

Outside Europe, temperatures were much above average over northern and northeastern North America (similar anomalies occurred in northeast North America in March 2024 ), Greenland, and south and east Asia, the latter being affected by a  heatwave in many areas. Temperatures were above average over the northwestern Middle East, as exemplified by the 40.7°C recorded in Tel Aviv which broke a record set in 1939. Most of South America and Africa also experienced above-average temperatures with extreme heat conditions in the Sahel region early in the month. Northwest Africa, and especially Morocco, experienced warm anomalies which may relate to the ongoing drought there.

Conversely, below-average temperatures occurred across Australia, with April having the coldest national-average mean temperature since 2015. Parts of Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, Oman, Iran, and Pakistan also had below-average temperatures which may relate to the extreme rainfall events  experienced in some of these areas. Northern Siberia and southern South America also experienced below-average temperatures.

Air temperatures were above average over large parts of the ocean, although El Niño continued to weaken towards neutral conditions over the eastern equatorial Pacific. Higher-than-average temperatures persisted over the southern Indian Ocean and parts of the equatorial and southern Atlantic and Pacific Ocean basins. Air temperatures were at record levels for April over much of the tropics, the eastern North Atlantic and the North Pacific. Temperatures were below average over several mainly small areas of the world’s ocean, most extensively over much of the ice-covered Arctic Ocean, and in the Southern Hemisphere to the south and west of Chile, to the southeast of Brazil, to the east of South Africa, and in a band from Antarctica to Australia.

The high marine air temperatures were associated with continuing record sea-surface temperatures (SSTs) for the time of year. The daily SST averaged over 60°S–60°N in April 2024 (see our Climate Pulse web app) continued to be just a few hundredths of a degree below the new absolute high of 21.09°C reached at the end of February and in March. The average for April of 21.04°C was the highest for April and only marginally below the previous monthly high of 21.07°C recorded for March. This is the thirteenth month in a row that the SST has been the warmest in the record for the respective month of the year.

Although the latest El Niño event has been an important contributor to the high global SSTs observed over the past months, SSTs in the eastern equatorial Pacific fell below the 1991-2020 average in places during April, as in March. SSTs were nevertheless the highest on record over a sizeable part of the tropical western Pacific, as shown in the above map of anomalies and extremes. Moreover, high SSTs in oceanic regions outside of the equatorial Pacific are a key contributor to the current global SST records. The map also shows that SSTs reached record high values for April in a substantial portion of the Atlantic Ocean (subtropical North Atlantic, extratropical South Atlantic, equatorial Atlantic), in the western Indian Ocean, in the Southern Ocean sector south of South Africa, as well as in various parts of the extratropical Pacific Ocean.

Arctic sea ice extent was about 2% below average, a relatively small negative anomaly compared to the April anomalies recorded over the past 10 years. As in March, sea ice concentration anomalies were mixed across the Arctic Ocean. Concentrations remained above average in the Greenland Sea, a persistent feature since October.

Antarctic sea ice extent was 9% below average, the 10th lowest extent for April in the satellite data record, continuing a pattern of frequent large negative anomalies observed since 2017. As in February and March, sea ice concentrations were most below-average in the northern Weddell Sea and in the Ross-Amundsen Sea sector.

After reaching its annual maximum in March, Arctic sea ice extent has started decreasing with the sea ice melt season getting underway. The monthly average extent in April 2024 was 14.1 million km2, only 0.2 million km 2 (or about 2%)  below the 1991–2020 average for April. The anomaly for April 2024 continues a series of negative April anomalies that started in 2014. The latest value is however one of the smallest negative anomalies for the month recorded since 2014. The lowest sea ice extent for April occurred in 2019.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The map of sea ice concentration anomalies for April 2024 bears some resemblance to the map for March 2024, with a mix of above- and below-average concentrations across the different sectors of the Arctic Ocean. Concentrations remained above average in the Greenland Sea, a persistent feature observed since October and, before that, through most of 2023. Above-average concentrations also prevailed in the northern Barents Sea and in the northern Bering Sea. On the other hand, concentrations were below average in the western North Atlantic sector (Labrador Sea) – as they were in March – and in the southern Bering Sea. The Sea of Okhotsk also saw below-average concentrations, in contrast to well above-average concentrations in March.

The average Antarctic sea ice extent for April 2024 was 6.6 million km2, 0.7 million km2 (or 9%)   below the 1991–2020 average for April. This marks the tenth-lowest extent for April within the 46-year satellite dataset. The negative anomaly for April 2024 is part of a pattern of large negative anomalies observed since 2017, with exceptions in 2020 and 2021. The lowest extent for April occurred in 2017, at 21% below average.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The sea ice cover around Antarctica in April 2024 remained within the average ice edge for the 1991–2020 period. As in March, below-average sea ice concentrations were most prominent in the northern Weddell Sea and the Ross-Amundsen Sea sector. On the other hand, concentrations were above average in the Bellingshausen Sea and in the western Pacific sector (90°–180°E).

Arctic and Antarctic April sea-ice extent anomalies (million sq km, vs 1991-2020) for each April through 2024: <ts></ts>

March 2024 was warmer globally than any previous March in the data record, with an average ERA5 surface air temperature of 14.14°C, 0.73°C above the 1991-2020 average for March and 0.10°C above the previous high set in March 2016. This is the tenth month in a row that is the warmest on record for the respective month of the year. The month was 1.68°C warmer than an estimate of the March average for 1850-1900, the designated pre-industrial reference period. The global-average temperature for the past twelve months (April 2023 – March 2024) is the highest on record, at 0.70°C above the 1991-2020 average and 1.58°C above the 1850-1900 pre-industrial average.

The average European temperature for March 2024 was 2.12°C above the 1991-2020 average for March, making the month the second warmest March on record for the continent, only a marginal 0.02°C cooler than March 2014. Temperatures were most above average in central and eastern regions.

Outside Europe, temperatures were most above average over eastern North America, Greenland, eastern Russia, Central America, parts of South America, many parts of Africa, southern Australia and parts of Antarctica.

The El Niño continued to weaken in the eastern equatorial Pacific, but marine air temperatures in general remained at an unusually high level.

The global sea surface temperature averaged for March over 60°S–60°N was 21.07°C, the highest monthly value on record, marginally above the 21.06°C recorded for February.

March 2024 saw temperatures at or above the 1991-2020 average across the whole of Europe. As in February, the largest anomalies occurred over central and eastern regions of the continent.   Germany reported its warmest March since measurements began in 1881. The Netherlands likewise reported its warmest March in a record dating from 1901, with no frost day experienced for only the second time. Austria recorded its warmest March at lowland stations, though not at altitude. Late in the month, many countries further to the east, including Croatia, Latvia and Moldova, saw national records for high March temperatures being equalled or exceeded.

Outside Europe, temperatures were much above average over eastern North America (where the month had begun with record low levels of ice cover over the Great Lakes ), Greenland and eastern Russia. Temperatures were especially high over Central America (where drought continues to reduce shipping through the Panama Canal ) and parts of South America, particularly over Venezuela and a central region of the continent. Many parts of Africa also experienced exceptionally high temperatures. South Australia had its warmest March on record. Antarctic temperatures were relatively high over Marie Byrd Land and east of the Antarctic Peninsula.

Land regions with notably below-average temperatures include western Siberia, the western USA and central Canada, rain-affected western and northern Australia, southern Chile and Argentina, and parts of Antarctica.

Air temperatures were above average over large parts of the ocean, although El Niño conditions continued to weaken over the eastern equatorial Pacific. Higher-than-average temperatures persisted over the southern Indian Ocean and parts of the southern Atlantic and Pacific Ocean basins. Air temperatures were at record levels for March over much of the tropics, the eastern North Atlantic and the North Pacific. Temperatures were below average over several mainly small areas of the world’s ocean, most extensively over much of the ice-covered Arctic Ocean, and in the Southern Hemisphere to the south and west of Chile and in a band from Antarctica to New Zealand.

The high marine air temperatures were associated with continuing record sea-surface temperatures (SSTs). The daily SST averaged over 60°S–60°N (see our Climate Pulse web app) continued in March 2024 at or just a few hundredths of a degree below the new absolute high of 21.09°C reached at the end of February. Its average for March, 21.07°C, has become the highest monthly value on record, marginally exceeding the previous monthly high of 21.06°C recorded for February.

Although the current El Niño event has been an important contributor to the high global SSTs observed over the past months, SSTs in the eastern equatorial Pacific fell below the 1991-2020 average in places during March. SSTs were nevertheless the highest on record over a sizeable part of the tropical western Pacific. Moreover, high SSTs in ocean regions outside of the equatorial Pacific are a key contributor to the current global SST records. The above map of SST percentiles shows that SSTs reached record high values for March in a substantial portion of the Atlantic Ocean (subtropical North and South Atlantic, equatorial Atlantic), in the western Indian Ocean, in the Southern Ocean sector south of South Africa, as well as in various parts of the extratropical Pacific Ocean.

Global and European monthly surface air temperature anomalies (ERA5, degrees C vs 1991-2020) covering the full series from 1979-01 through 2024-03: <ts></ts>

Arctic sea ice extent reached its annual maximum in March, with a monthly value slightly below average, marking the highest March extent since 2013. As in January and February, sea ice concentration anomalies were mixed across the Arctic Ocean. Concentrations remained above average in the Greenland Sea, a persistent feature since October.

Antarctic sea ice extent was 20% below average, the sixth lowest extent for March in the satellite data record, continuing a series of large negative anomalies observed since 2017. As in February, sea ice concentrations were most below-average in the northern Weddell Sea and in the Ross-Amundsen Sea sector.

Arctic sea ice extent reached its annual maximum in March 2024, with a monthly average of 14.9 million km2. This was only about  0.1 million km2  (or 1%) below average. While being well below the values observed in the 1980s and 1990s, the March 2024 extent is well above the lowest March value of about 5% below average recorded in 2018 and marks the highest March extent since 2013.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

As in January and February, the map of sea ice concentration anomalies for March 2024 shows a mix of above- and below-average concentrations across the different sectors of the Arctic Ocean, consistent with the small anomaly for the total Arctic sea ice extent. Concentrations remained above average in the Greenland Sea, a feature that has persisted since October and, before that, through most of 2023. Above-average concentrations also prevailed in the Sea of Okhotsk and in the Bering Sea, in the northern Pacific sector. In contrast, sea ice concentrations were below average in the western North Atlantic sector (Labrador Sea, Gulf of St Lawrence) and in the eastern Barents Sea.

After reaching its annual minimum in February (the third lowest minimum on record), Antarctic sea ice resumed its seasonal growth around Antarctica. The average sea ice extent for March 2024 was 3.5 million km2, 0.9 million km2 (or 20%) below the 1991-2020 average for March. This is the sixth-lowest extent for March within the 46-year satellite dataset, close to the values recorded in 2006 (-21%) and 2019 (-22%). The below-average extent observed in March 2024 continues a series of large negative anomalies observed in March since 2017, with notable exceptions in 2020 and 2021. In 2023, after Antarctic sea ice extent reached a record low at its annual minimum in February, it increased very slowly during the freezing season, remaining at record low levels for the time of year from May to October. At this stage, it is uncertain what trajectory the sea ice extent is going to follow for the 2024 freezing season (March to September).

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The sea ice cover around Antarctica in March 2024 remained well within the average ice edge for the 1991–2020 period. Below-average sea ice concentrations were most prominent in two ocean sectors: the northern Weddell Sea and the Ross-Amundsen Sea sector – the same two sectors as in February. On the other hand, concentrations were above average in a few small areas: eastern Weddell Sea, eastern Amundsen Sea, western Ross Sea and off the coast of Victoria Land, northwest of the Ross Sea.

The sea ice analysis page provides additional information about the nature, production and reliability of the data and information presented here.

Arctic and Antarctic March sea-ice extent anomalies (million sq km, vs 1991-2020) for each March through 2024: <ts></ts>

February 2024 was the warmest February on record globally, with an average ERA5 surface air temperature of 13.54°C, 0.81°C above the 1991-2020 average for February and 0.12°C above the temperature of the previous warmest February, in 2016. This is the ninth month in a row that is the warmest on record for the respective month of the year. The month was 1.77°C warmer than an estimate of the February average for 1850-1900, the designated pre-industrial reference period. The global-average temperature for the past twelve months (March 2023 – February 2024) is the highest on record, at 0.68°C above the 1991-2020 average and 1.56°C above the 1850-1900 pre-industrial average. The daily global temperature was exceptionally high during the first half of the month, reaching 2°C above the 1850-1900 levels on four consecutive days (8–11 February).

European temperatures in February 2024 were 3.30°C above the 1991-2020 average for February, with much-above average temperatures experienced in central and eastern Europe. Outside Europe, temperatures were above average over northern Siberia, central and northwest North America, the majority of South America, across Africa, and in western Australia. The El Niño continued to weaken in the equatorial Pacific, but marine air temperatures in general remained at an unusually high level.

The average global sea surface temperature (SST) for February 2024 over 60°S–60°N was 21.06°C, the highest for any month in the dataset, above the previous record of August 2023 (20.98°C). Sea surface temperature is defined over the global extrapolar ocean, from 60°S to 60°N. This is used as a standard diagnostic for climate monitoring. The average daily SST reached a new absolute high of 21.09°C at the end of the month.

Boreal winter was the warmest globally at 0.78°C above the 1991-2020 average. European winter temperature was the second warmest on record, after the winter of 2019/2020, at 1.44°C above the 1991-2020 average.

February 2024 saw temperatures well above the 1991-2020 average across most of Europe. The largest anomalies occurred over eastern Europe, where, for example, Romania registered record highs and Lviv in western Ukraine recorded its highest February temperature of 17.8°C. In western Europe, England and Wales had their warmest February in a record dating back to 1884, and Switzerland also had its warmest February since records began in 1864. Germany experienced record-breaking temperatures too. In contrast, there were only a few areas where temperatures were colder than average. These included Iceland and western Russia; slightly below-average temperatures were recorded mostly along the border between Norway and Sweden.

Outside Europe, temperatures were much above average in northern Siberia and over the Barents Sea to the west of Novaya Zemlya which was linked to well below-average sea ice cover. Central and northwest North America experienced above-average temperatures, with the U.S. Midwest region particularly affected. On 26th February, 78 daily maximum temperature records were equalled or exceeded across the central United States; and highest February temperatures ever were observed in Omaha, Nebraska, Des Moines, Iowa, and Minneapolis, Minnesota. Above-average temperatures occurred over the majority of South America, which favoured high wildfire activity there, and across most of Africa, where the largest positive anomalies were found in the south of the continent. Australia reported its fourth-warmest February in a record dating back to 1910, and the state of Western Australia its second warmest.

Temperatures were below the 1991-2020 average over central Asia, including in the Xinjiang region of China where a record-breaking low temperature of –52.3°C was observed on 18th February 2024. The Kamchatka Peninsula in the far east of Russia also experienced below-average temperatures, as did most of Greenland, a part of northeast Australia, and pockets of northeast Africa and north - western North America.

Air temperatures were above average over large parts of the ocean, associated with the continuation of record sea-surface temperatures noted in the highlight box below, although El Niño conditions continued to weaken over the equatorial Pacific. Regions with higher-than-usual temperatures extended northward from the Antarctic to the southern Indian Ocean and parts of the southern Atlantic and Pacific Ocean basins. Air temperatures were at record levels over large areas and were generally higher than the 1991-2020 average over most of the tropics, the eastern North Atlantic and the North Pacific, to the east of Japan in particular. Temperatures were below average over several relatively small areas of the world’s oceans, particularly in the Southern Hemisphere, north of Japan, over the western North Atlantic and parts of the eastern North Pacific.

0.8°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average for February

the warmest February on record, 0.12°C warmer than February 2016, the previous warmest February

1.77°C warmer than an estimate of the pre-industrial average for 1850-1900.

the ninth consecutive month (since June 2023) for which the monthly temperature has been the warmest on record for the corresponding month of the year.

a month with a temperature anomaly higher than for any month before September 2023.

a month with the fifth-largest anomaly across all months, behind September (0.93°C), October, November, and December (all at 0.85°C) of 2023.

The daily global temperature was exceptionally high during the first half of the month, reaching 2°C above the 1850-1900 levels on four consecutive days (8–11 February). This is the second time that the 2°C mark was reached within the ERA5 dataset, the first time being on 17–18 November 2023. The high daily global temperatures were associated with simultaneous large warm anomalies over North America and Eurasia. To visualise these daily temperature anomalies, visit our Climate Pulse web app.

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for February 2024 was 3.30°C   higher than the 1991-2020 average. The month was 0.67°C colder over Europe than February 1990, and the second warmest February on record for Europe, although only an insignificant 0.01°C warmer than February 2020.

As reported in our Temperature Summary  for January 2024, daily global sea surface temperatures (SSTs) averaged over extra-polar waters (60°S–60°N) have reached new absolute records since 31st January, surpassing the previous record set in August 2023. SSTs were exceptionally high for the time of year through most of 2023 and the beginning of 2024. At the end of February, the daily average SST reached a new absolute high of 21.09°C. For more details, see the interactive daily SST charts in our  Climate Pulse web app.

The monthly average SST for February 2024 was 21.06°C, by far the highest on record for the month, 0.59°C above the 1991–2020 average for February. This is 0.27°C higher than the previous February record, set in 2016 (20.79°C). It is also the highest absolute value for all months, surpassing the previous record from August 2023 (20.98°C).

Although the current El Niño event has been an important contributor to the high global SSTs observed over the past few months, it is currently weakening and SSTs in the equatorial Pacific are lower than in February 1998 and February 2016. However, high SSTs in ocean regions outside of the equatorial Pacific are a key factor to the current global SST records. The map of SST percentiles above shows that SSTs reached record high values in a substantial portion of the Atlantic Ocean (subtropical North and South Atlantic, equatorial Atlantic), in large fractions of the Indian Ocean, in the Southern Ocean sector south of South Africa, as well as in various parts of the Pacific Ocean.

During the boreal winter of 2023/2024, there was a striking contrast across Europe. Above-average temperatures occurred over most of the continent, with the largest anomalies over the Alps and in southeast Europe. Conversely, below-average temperatures were experienced across the Nordic countries and northwest Russia.

Seasonal-average temperatures across North America reflected the pattern seen in February, with warm conditions seen especially in the U.S. Midwest and Canada. The season as a whole was also warmer than average over much of South America, Africa, the Middle East, and Australia. In contrast, the season was colder than average in the far east of Russia, including the Kamchatka Peninsula, across pockets of central Asia, and in most of Alaska.

The seasonal-average temperatures over sea are similar to the pattern of differences from 1991-2020 as already seen for February 2024. However, the seasonal average shows the El Niño conditions more pronounced, and the regions of cold anomalies in the northern, southern, and northwestern Pacific Ocean are weaker in the seasonal average.

The global-average temperature from December 2023 to February 2024 was the highest in the record at 0.78°C  above the 1991-2020 average for the season. This is 0.19°C warmer than the next warmest period from December 2015 to February 2016.

The average temperature for Europe from December 2023 to February 2024 was 1.44°C  above the 1991-2020 average for the season. This is the joint second warmest winter value on record for Europe. The winter of 2019/2020 was almost 1.4°C warmer. Winter 2023/24 was within 0.03°C of 2022/23, 2015/16, and 2006/7.

a little below average over several small areas of land, including parts of Australia, Antarctica, northwestern India, and western North America.

Averaging over twelve-month periods smooths out shorter-term variations in regional- and global-average temperatures. Globally, the last 12 months were warmer than any previous 12-month period, with a temperature 0.68°C  above the 1991-2020 average. The next warmest such period was the period of February 2023 to January 2024 at 0.64°C above average. 2023 is the warmest calendar year on record, with a temperature 0.60°C above the 1991-2020 average.

Using an annual-mean offset of 0.88°C (see side box), the average global temperature for the 12 months to February 2024 is estimated to be 1.56°C above the 1850-1900 level. The average over the fourteen months from the beginning of 2023 is 1.51°C above the 1850-1900 level.

There is more variability in average European temperatures, but relatively dense observational coverage of the continent reduces uncertainty. The average for this latest 12-month period is 1.01°C  above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 is the warmest calendar year on record for Europe, with a temperature 1.19°C above the 1991-2020 average.

From October 2021 an updated approach is used to relate recent global temperatures to 1850-1900, a period taken to approximate the pre-industrial level. Following this approach, the value 0.88°C should be added to annual values relative to the 1991-2020 reference period. This estimate derives from the approach used in the WMO statements on “The state of the global climate” from the Preliminary Statement for 2021 onwards, which builds on the methods outlined in the IPCC AR6 WG1 report. Read more in About the data.

There is general agreement among datasets that the period since 2015 is much warmer globally than any previous period. There is also agreement that global temperature has risen at an average rate close to 0.2°C per decade since the late 1970s. There is nevertheless still some spread between the datasets for recent years, such as for 2021 and 2022, and the annual average temperature anomalies for these years from ERA5 are generally higher than those from the five other datasets considered. The differences range from 0.01 to 0.07°C for 2016-2023. The range is 0.00 to 0.06°C if air temperature over sea is replaced by sea-surface temperature for ERA5 and the other dataset for which sea-surface temperature was not used by design. The remaining differences depend partly on the extent to which datasets represent the relatively warm conditions that have predominated over the Arctic and Antarctic during these years. Differences elsewhere in estimates of sea-surface temperature and surface air temperature over land have been further factors.

The surface air temperature analysis homepage explains more about the production and reliability of the values presented here, but has yet to be updated to include the new information on dataset spread mentioned above.

Arctic sea ice extent was 2% below average, not as low as in most recent years, in particular compared to the minimum February extent recorded in 2018 (6% below average). However, the February 2024 extent is well below the values observed in the 1980s and 1990s. Sea ice concentrations were markedly below average in the northern Barents Sea but remained above average in the nearby Greenland Sea, a persistent feature since October.

Antarctic sea ice reached its annual minimum monthly extent, the third lowest in the satellite data record at 28% below average, not far from the all-time minimum from February 2023 (-33%). Little sea ice remained around Antarctica, mainly in the Weddell Sea, with below-average sea ice concentrations most prominent in the northern Weddell Sea and in the Ross-Amundsen Sea sector.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in February 2024 was 14.6 million km2, only about 0.2 million km2 (or 2%) below average. This value ranks 16th lowest since the beginning of the satellite data record in 1979. Although it is well below the values observed in the 1980s and 1990s, it is clearly not as low as the lowest February value of about 6% below average recorded in 2018. The value for February 2024 is also virtually identical to the one recorded two years earlier, in February 2022.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

As in January, the map of sea ice concentration anomalies for February 2024 shows a mix of above- and below-average concentrations across the different sectors of the Arctic Ocean. Concentrations remained above average in the Greenland Sea, a feature that has persisted since October and through most of 2023. Above-average concentrations also occurred in the Sea of Okhotsk, in the northern Pacific. In contrast, sea ice concentrations were markedly below average in the northern Barents Sea and – to a lesser extent – in the western North Atlantic sector (Labrador Sea, Gulf of St Lawrence).

The Antarctic sea ice extent reached its annual minimum in February 2024, with a monthly average extent of 2.4 million km2, 0.9 million km2 (or 28%) below the 1991-2020 average  for February. This is the third-lowest extent for February within the 46-year satellite dataset, virtually tied with the second-lowest extent recorded in February 2017. The low sea ice extent observed in February 2024 continues a series of large negative anomalies observed since 2017 and is relatively close to the all-time minimum recorded a year earlier,  in February 2023 (33% below average). The evolution of the daily sea ice extent and its annual minimum are discussed in the box below.

On 20 February 2024, the total daily sea ice extent around Antarctica reached its annual minimum, at 2.22 million km2 according to data from EUMETSAT OSI SAF. This was the fourth lowest daily extent within the 1979–2024 satellite observations, behind 2017 (2.21 million km2), 2022 (2.19 million km2), and 2023 (2.04 million km2, record minimum). It is noteworthy that the difference between the fourth- and second-lowest values is very small (0.03 million km2 or about 1% of the total extent). At this point (early March), it is still too early to predict whether, during the upcoming growth season, the sea ice extent will reach such unprecedented low values for the time of year as those observed in 2023.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

As Antarctic sea ice reached its annual minimum extent, little sea ice remained around the continent. Most of it was concentrated in the Weddell Sea. The map of sea ice concentration anomalies for February 2024 shows two ocean sectors where below-average concentrations were most prominent: the northern Weddell Sea and the Ross-Amundsen Sea sector. Concentrations were above average only in a few limited areas: eastern Weddell Sea, eastern Amundsen Sea, and off the coast of Victoria Land, northwest of the Ross Sea.

January 2024 was the warmest January on record globally, with an average ERA5 surface air temperature of 13.14°C, 0.70°C above the 1991-2020 average for January and 0.12°C above the temperature of the previous warmest January, in 2020. The global temperature anomaly for January 2024 was lower than those of the last six months of 2023, but higher than any before July 2023. The month was 1.66°C warmer than an estimate of the January average for 1850-1900, the designated pre-industrial reference period. The global mean temperature for the past twelve months (Feb 2023 – Jan 2024) is the highest on record, at 0.64°C above the 1991-2020 average and 1.52°C above the 1850-1900 pre-industrial average. European temperatures varied in January 2024 from much below the 1991-2020 average over the Nordic countries to much above average over the south of the continent.

Outside Europe, temperatures were well above average over eastern Canada, north-western Africa, the Middle East and central Asia, and below average over western Canada, the central USA and most of eastern Siberia. The El Niño began to weaken in the equatorial Pacific, but marine air temperatures in general remained at an unusually high level.

The average global sea surface temperature (SST) for January over 60°S–60°N reached 20.97°C, a record for January, 0.26°C warmer than the previous warmest January, in 2016, and second highest value for any month in the ERA5 dataset, within 0.01°C of the record from August 2023 (20.98°C). Since 31 January, the daily SST for 60°S–60°N has reached new absolute records, surpassing the previous highest values from 23–24 August 2023.

There was a striking contrast in average January temperatures across Europe in 2024, and considerable variations during the month in some places. It was much colder than the 1991-2020 average over much of the north of the continent, but much warmer than average in the south, for the fourth month in a row. Temperatures below -43°C early in January in Lapland were the lowest January values in Sweden for 25 years, and for one of the stations concerned the lowest in a data record of more than one hundred years. Milder conditions later in the month led to less extreme averages: statistics for Finnish and Swedish stations show January 2024 to be below the 1991-2020 average, but less cold than either January 2016 or January 2021. Changes during the month were also notable for France and the UK, which recorded what is provisionally its highest every daily maximum temperature for January late in the month, despite average temperatures for the month that were close to normal. It was much warmer than average over southern Spain, with particularly high temperatures towards the end of the month. Temperatures also reached record highs at altitude over the Alps. 1

Outside Europe, temperatures were most above average over eastern Canada, north-western Africa, the Middle East and central Asia. The average observed temperature was more than 4°C higher than the 1981-2010 average for the second month in a row in Montreal, and close to 7°C above this average in northern Quebec. The warm conditions over north-western Africa were accompanied by little or no rainfall,  increasing concerns over drought. Heatwaves with record or close-to-record high temperatures occurred over South America in countries ranging from Colombia in the north to Chile and Argentina in the south.   Australia reported its third warmest January in a record dating back to 1910.

Temperatures were well below the 1991-2020 average over part of eastern Russia. The month was also colder than average for most of western Canada and the central USA, but here there were large swings in temperature, as a warm December gave way to record-breaking cold in the first half of January, followed by record-breaking warmth.

Air temperatures were above average over large parts of the ocean, associated with the continuation of record sea-surface temperatures noted in a highlight box in the October temperature bulletin, although El Niño conditions weakened a little over the equatorial Pacific. Regions with higher than usual temperatures extended northward from the Antarctic to the southern Indian Ocean and parts of the southern Atlantic and Pacific Ocean basins. Air temperatures were at record levels over large areas and were generally higher than the 1991-2020 average over most of the tropics, the eastern North Atlantic and the North Pacific, to the east of Japan in particular. Temperatures were below average over several relatively small areas of the world’s oceans, particularly in the Southern Hemisphere.

[1] The high average values shown in the map for January should be viewed with caution as the observations have large local variations that are not well resolved by the analysis.

0.70°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average for January

the warmest January on record, 0.12°C warmer than January 2020, the previous warmest January

1.66°C warmer than an estimate of the pre-industrial average for 1850-1900.

Each month since June 2023 has been the warmest on record for the respective month of the year.

As reported in the C3S Global Climate Highlights 2023, global sea surface temperatures (SSTs) averaged over extra-polar waters were persistently and unusually high through most of 2023, reaching record levels for the time of year, from April through December. Since the beginning of 2024, these SSTs have remained exceptionally high for the time of year. At 20.97°C, the average SST for January 2024 was by far the highest on record for the month, 0.66°C above the 1991–2020 average for January. This is 0.26°C higher than the previous January record, set in 2016 (20.71°C) and the second highest absolute value for all months, within 0.01°C of the highest value recorded in August 2023 (20.98°C). However, at a daily scale, the SST has reached new record values since 31st January, surpassing the previous record of 21.02°C from 23–24 August 2023. While the current El Niño event has been an important contributor to the high global SSTs over the past few months, high SSTs in ocean regions outside of the equatorial Pacific have also played an important role. The exceptional warmth of the oceans is a key factor behind the record global surface air temperatures for January.

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for January 2024 was 0.18°C  higher than the 1991-2020 average. The month was 2.467°C colder over Europe than January 2020, the warmest January in the European record, and colder than another nine Januaries in the period since 2000.

In the Global Climate Highlights for 2023, C3S reported that 2023 was 1.48°C warmer than the 1850-1900 average according to its ERA5 dataset. C3S also reported that it expected all six of the global temperature datasets routinely examined to place 2023 in the range of 1.4°C to 1.5°C above 1850-1900 once data were complete for the year. The 1850-1900 average is taken to be the pre-industrial level for the purpose of monitoring the climate as it approaches the global warming levels of 1.5°C and 2°C specified in the Paris Agreement.

The approach used by C3S to relate recent global temperatures to 1850-1900 values entails adding 0.88°C to annual values relative to the latest standard WMO reference period 1991-2020. This estimate derives from the approach currently used in the WMO reports on “The State of the Global Climate”, which in turn builds on the methods used for the Sixth Assessment report of the IPCC: “ Climate Change 2021: The Physical Science Basis ”. This approach results in a best estimate of 0.69°C for the increase in temperature from 1850-1900 to the reference period 1981-2010, based on the average of values from four datasets. Extending this to the 1991-2020 reference period gives a corresponding temperature increase of 0.88°C.

All six datasets are now complete for 2023, and according to the C3S calculations presented in the first data row of the table below, the range for the year is 1.43°C-1.48°C above the 1850-1900 average. The range narrows to 1.43°C-1.47°C if marine air temperature is replaced by sea-surface temperature for the reanalyses (ERA5 and JRA-3Q), to follow more closely what is done for the other four datasets.

C3S calculations using a 0.88°C offset of 1991-2020 from 1850-1900 for each dataset

Three of the datasets used routinely by C3S extend back to 1850. The producers of each of them ( Berkeley Earth, HadCRUT5 and NOAAGlobalTempv5 ) have reported temperature differences between 2023 and 1850-1900 that range from 1.35°C to 1.54°C, much larger than the range given by the C3S calculations. The entries in the second and third rows of the table indicate that the differences are due to each of the three producers using only their dataset to estimate the 1850-1900 level. Reconciliation of the results for Berkeley Earth also requires use of that producer’s values for global averages rather than those derived routinely by C3S using downloaded data on a regular 1°×1° grid. The two calculations of global averages differ appreciably when data coverage is poor in the early decades of the dataset, as data from regions with high uncertainties are masked in the gridded Berkeley Earth dataset but used by Berkeley Earth in its calculation of global means.

Large uncertainty in the temperature rise from 1850-1900 to the recent past does not necessarily mean that there is large uncertainty in how close the atmosphere is to reaching the temperature limits specified in the Paris Agreement. Further discussion of this point is given here.

a little below average over a several small areas of land, including parts of Australia, Antarctica, northwestern India, and western North America.

Averaging over twelve-month periods smooths out shorter-term variations in regional- and global-average temperatures. Globally, the last 12 months were warmer than any previous consecutive twelve months, with a temperature 0.64°C above the 1991-2020 average. The next warmest such period was the calendar year of 2023. 2023 is the warmest calendar year on record, with a temperature 0.60°C above the 1991-2020 average.

Using an annual-mean offset of 0.88°C (see box), the average global temperature for the 12 months to January 2024 is estimated to be 1.52°C above the 1850-1900 level. The average over the thirteen months from the beginning of 2023 is 1.50°C above the 1850-1900 level.

There is more variability in average European temperatures, but relatively dense observational coverage of the continent reduces uncertainty. The average for this latest 12-month period is 0.85°C  above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 is the warmest calendar year on record for Europe, with a temperature 1.19°C above the 1991-2020 average.

From October 2021 an updated approach is used to relate recent annual global temperatures to 1850-1900, a period taken to approximate the pre-industrial level. Following this approach, the value 0.88°C should be added to annual values relative to the 1991-2020 reference period. This estimate derives from the approach used in the WMO statements on “The state of the global climate” from the Preliminary Statement for 2021 onwards, which builds on the methods outlined in the IPCC AR6 WG1 report. Read more in About the data.

There is general agreement among datasets that the period since 2015 is much warmer globally than any previous period, and that 2023 is the warmest year of all. There is also agreement that global temperature has risen at an average rate close to 0.2°C per decade since the late 1970s. There is nevertheless still some spread between the datasets for recent years, such as for 2021 and 2022, and the annual average temperature anomalies for these years from ERA5 are generally higher than those from the five other datasets considered. The differences range from 0.01 to 0.07°C for 2016-2023. The range is 0.00 to 0.06°C if air temperature over sea is replaced by sea-surface temperature for ERA5 and the other dataset for which sea-surface temperature was not used by design. The remaining differences depend partly on the extent to which datasets represent the relatively warm conditions that have predominated over the Arctic and Antarctic during these years. Differences elsewhere in estimates of sea-surface temperature and surface air temperature over land have been further factors.

Arctic sea ice extent was close to average, and the highest since 2009. Sea ice concentrations were above average in the Greenland Sea (a persistent feature since October) and Sea of Okhotsk while below-average concentrations prevailed in the Labrador Sea.

Antarctic sea ice extent was the sixth lowest for January, at 18% below average, well above the lowest January value recorded in 2023 (-31%). Below-average sea ice concentrations prevailed mainly in the Ross and Amundsen Seas, northern Weddell Sea, and along the coast of East Antarctica.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in January 2024 was close to the 1991–2020 average for January, at 13.9 million km2, only about 0.1 million km2 (or 0.5%) below average. This is the highest extent (or smallest negative anomaly) for January observed since 2009. It is well above the lowest January value of about 6% below average recorded in 2018.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The map of sea ice concentration anomalies for January 2023 shows a mix of above- and below-average concentrations across the Arctic region, consistent with the near-average total extent for the region as a whole. Concentrations remained above average in the Greenland Sea, a feature that has been observed since October. Above-average concentrations also prevailed around Svalbard and Franz Joseph Land, in the northern Bering Sea and in the Sea of Okhotsk. However, sea ice concentrations were below average in the Labrador Sea, Gulf of St Lawrence, northern Barents Sea, and in the southern Bering Sea.

The average Antarctic sea ice extent for January 2024 was 4.5 million km2, 1.0 million km2 (or 18%) below the 1991-2020 average  for January. This is the sixth-lowest extent for January within the 46-year satellite dataset, part of a series of large negative anomalies observed since 2017. The lowest January extent occurred in January 2023, at 31% below average. At a daily scale (not shown here), Antarctic sea ice extent started January with values well above those observed in January 2023. This difference gradually reduced later on. By the end of January, the daily values were virtually identical to those observed in January 2023. Therefore, this leaves open the possibility of Antarctic sea ice reaching a new record-low extent in February, when the annual minimum is usually observed.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

At this time of year, as the sea ice melt season is well underway, the Antarctic sea ice cover is typically quite fragmented. This is reflected in the spatial distribution of sea ice concentration anomalies around the Antarctic continent in January 2024, which shows a mix of above-average and below-average values. Below-average concentrations were most prominent in the Ross and Amundsen, northern Weddell Sea, and along the coast of East Antarctica. Conversely, above-average concentrations prevailed in the inner Weddell Sea and (on the other side of Antarctica) off the coast of Victoria Land.

Arctic and Antarctic January sea-ice extent anomalies (million sq km, vs 1991-2020) for each January through 2024: <ts></ts>

December 2023 was the warmest December on record globally, with an average surface air temperature of 13.51°C, 0.85°C above the 1991-2020 average for December and 0.31°C above the temperature of the previous warmest Decembers, in 2015 and 2019. The global temperature anomaly for December 2023 was on a par with October and November 2023, and only lower than the September 2023 anomaly of 0.93°C. The month as a whole was about 1.78°C warmer than an estimate of the December average for 1850-1900, the designated pre-industrial reference period. The global mean temperature for 2023 is the highest on record, at 0.60°C above the 1991-2020 average and 1.48°C above the 1850-1900 pre-industrial average.

European temperatures varied in December 2023 from much below the 1991-2020 average over the Nordic countries to much above average over the far southeast of the continent.

Outside Europe, the warmest conditions occurred over North America, and in particular, across Canada. The El Niño event continued in the equatorial Pacific.

There was a striking contrast in temperatures across Europe in December. Warmer-than-average conditions compared with their 1991-2020 climatological averages were seen in a northwest-to-southeast band from the UK and Ireland – where the UK recorded its warmest Christmas Eve in 26 years – to Turkey and the Caucasus, where Armenia recorded its warmest December in 100 years. Record daily temperatures also occurred across Spain in mid-December, but this is not reflected in the map; mixed conditions were experienced across the Iberian Peninsula for December as a whole. Conversely, a large part of the Nordic countries had colder-than-average conditions for the third month in a row. In Sweden, some regions had daily average temperatures 10°C below average. These cold conditions also extended into northwest Russia and towards central Greenland.

Outside Europe, temperatures were most strikingly warmer than average across North America. The warm conditions were centred across Canada. For example, Thunder Bay in Ontario had its warmest December on record. The United States recorded many record temperatures especially in the Upper Midwest region, including cities such as Minneapolis and Milwaukee. Temperatures were above normal over most of South America, Africa, the Middle East, and across the Central Asian Republics. Warm conditions were also experienced in southeast Asia and in northwest Australia, where Marble Bar, Western Australia, recorded a temperature of 47.4°C.

Below-average temperatures occurred over eastern Asia and Siberia, a place where temperatures were as low as –50°C in early December and Beijing set a record for the number of hours of sub-zero temperatures. Only a few other regions had colder-than-average conditions and these include Alaska, Mexico, southeast South America, and the coasts of South Africa and South Australia.

Air temperatures were above average over large parts of the ocean, associated with the continuation of record sea-surface temperatures noted in a highlight box in the October temperature bulletin. El Niño conditions continued to develop over the equatorial Pacific. Regions with relatively mild temperatures extended northward from the Antarctic to the southern Indian Ocean and parts of the southern Atlantic and Pacific Ocean basins. Air temperatures were also higher than the 1991-2020 average over most of the tropics, the eastern North Atlantic and the North Pacific, and to the east of Japan in particular. Temperatures were below average in a band stretching to the west of southern South America and over several other relatively small areas of the world’s oceans.

0.85°C  warmer than the 1991-2020 average for December

the warmest December on record, 0.31°C warmer than Decembers 2015 and 2019, the joint second warmest Decembers

about 1.78°C warmer than an estimate of the pre-industrial average for 1850-1900.

Each month since June 2023 has been the warmest on record for the respective month of the year.

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for December 2023 was 0.97°C  higher than the 1991-2020 average. The month was 1.65°C colder over Europe than December 2019, the warmest December in the European record, and colder than six other Decembers in the period since 2000. It should be noted that the eastern boundary used for European averages is located at 40°E, which excludes the easternmost part of the continent, where temperatures were most above average in December 2023.

a little below average over several relatively small regions, including parts of Australia, Antarctica, northwestern India, and western North America.

Averaging over twelve-month periods smooths out shorter-term variations in regional- and global-average temperatures. Globally, the last 12 months were the warmest on record, with an average temperature 0.60°C above the 1991-2020 average. The next warmest such period was the twelve months to November 2023. This now makes 2023 the warmest calendar year on record surpassing 2016 with its temperature of 0.44°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 was on a par with 2016, being cooler by less than 0.01°C, well below the spread among multiple datasets.

Using the annual-mean offset of 0.88°C, the average global temperature for the 12 months to December 2023 is estimated to be 1.48°C above the 1850-1900 level. The way the average for the 1991-2020 reference period is related to that for 1850-1900 is outlined in the sidebar.

There is more variability in average European temperatures, but relatively dense observational coverage of the continent reduces uncertainty. The average for this latest 12-month period is  1.02°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 is the warmest calendar year on record for Europe, with a temperature 1.19°C above the 1991-2020 average.

From October 2021 an updated approach is used to relate recent annual global temperatures to 1850-1900, a period taken to approximate the pre-industrial level. Following this approach, the value 0.88°C should be added to annual values relative to the 1991-2020 reference period. This estimate derives from the approach used in the WMO statements on “The state of the global climate” from the Preliminary Statement for 2021 onwards, which builds on the methods outlined in the IPCC AR6 WG1 report. Read more in About the data.

There is general agreement among datasets that the period since 2015 is much warmer globally than any previous period. There is also agreement that global temperature has risen at an average rate close to 0.2°C per decade since the late 1970s. There is nevertheless still some spread between the datasets for recent years, such as for 2021 and 2022, and the annual average temperature anomalies for these years from ERA5 are generally higher than those from the five other datasets considered. The differences range from 0.01 to 0.07°C for 2016-2022. The range is 0.00 to 0.06°C if air temperature over sea is replaced by sea-surface temperature for ERA5 and the other dataset for which sea-surface temperature was not used by design. The remaining differences depend partly on the extent to which datasets represent the relatively warm conditions that have predominated over the Arctic and Antarctic during these years. Differences elsewhere in estimates of sea-surface temperature and surface air temperature over land have been further factors.

The surface air temperature analysis homepage explains more about the production and reliability of the values presented here, but has yet to be updated to include the new information on dataset spread mentioned above.

Arctic sea ice extent reached its 10 th lowest value for December, at 3% below average, well above the lowest December value recorded in 2016 (7% below average).  Sea ice concentrations were above average in the Greenland Sea (a persistent feature since October) while below-average concentrations prevailed in the northern Canadian region, coinciding with much warmer-than-average conditions.

Antarctic sea ice extent was the second lowest for December, at 15% below average, after reaching record-low values for the time of y ear by large margins for six consecutive months earlier in the year.  Below-average sea ice concentrations prevailed in large parts of the South Atlantic sector, while above-average concentrations occurred in the Weddell Sea and the Bellingshausen-Amundsen Sea sector.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in December 2023 was 12.1 million km 2, about 0.3 million km 2  (or 3%) below the 1991-2020 average  for December. This value ranks 10 th lowest in the satellite data record, well above the lowest December value of about 7% below average recorded in 2016.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The map of sea ice concentration anomalies for December 2023 shows near-average concentrations over most of the central Arctic Ocean along with above-average concentrations in the Greenland Sea (off the coast of northeastern Greenland), a feature that has persisted since  October. In contrast, below-average concentrations prevailed in the northern Canadian sector (Hudson Bay, Hudson Strait, and Labrador Sea), consistent with well above-average temperatures  the region in December. Below-average concentrations also occurred in the northern Barents Sea, eastern Kara Sea, and Chukchi Sea.

The average Antarctic sea ice extent for December 2023 was 9.3 million km 2,  1.7 million km 2   (or 15%) below the 1991-2020 average   for December. This is the second-lowest extent for December within the 45-year satellite dataset, with an anomaly notably smaller in magnitude than the lowest extent recorded in December 2016 (about 20% below average). The value and ranking of the sea ice extent for the month of December 2023 as a whole reflect a recent change in the behaviour of the sea ice extent at a daily time scale. After six consecutive months (May–October) at unprecedented low values for the time of year, the daily extent shifted to values closer to the 1991–2020 average around the middle of the month.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The spatial distribution of sea ice concentration anomalies in December 2023 shows a mix of below-average and above-average concentrations around the Antartic continent. Below-average concentrations were most prominent in the South Atlantic sector of the Southern Ocean (northern Weddell Sea and off the coast of Queen Maud Land in East Antarctica) as well as in the northern Amundsen Sea. Conversely, above-average concentrations prevailed in the Weddell Sea and the Bellingshausen-Amundsen Sea sector. Concentration anomalies were mixed in the Ross Sea.
