November 2023 was the warmest November on record globally, with an average surface air temperature of 14.22°C, 0.85°C above the 1991-2020 average for November and 0.32°C above the temperature of the previous warmest November, in 2020. The global temperature anomaly for November 2023 was on a par with October 2023, and only lower than the September 2023 anomaly of 0.93°C. The month as a whole was about 1.75°C warmer than an estimate of the November average for 1850-1900, the designated pre-industrial reference period.

For the calendar year to date, January to November, the global mean temperature for 2023 is the highest on record, 1.46°C above the 1850-1900 pre-industrial average, and 0.13°C higher than the eleven-month average for 2016, currently the warmest calendar year on record. European temperatures varied in November 2023 from much below the 1991-2020 average over central Norway and Sweden to much above average over the far southeast of the continent. The average sea surface temperature for November over 60°S–60°N was the highest on record for November at 0.25°C warmer than the second warmest November, in 2015. The El Niño event continued in the equatorial Pacific, with anomalies remaining lower than those reached at this time of year in the 2015 event.

The boreal autumn September–November 2023 was the warmest on record globally by a large margin, with an average temperature of 15.30°C, 0.88°C above average. The European-average temperature was 10.96°C, which at 1.43°C above average made boreal autumn 2023 the second warmest on record, just 0.03°C cooler than autumn 2020.

November temperatures differed considerably across Europe in 2023 compared with their 1991-2020 climatological averages, varying from substantially below average over parts of Norway to substantially above average over the Kalmykia region of Russia, to the north-west of the Caspian Sea.  Weather-station data for central Norway showed that temperatures in places were more than 7°C below their 1991-2020 averages and colder than in any year of the reference period. Central parts of Sweden had their coldest November since 2010. Central and southern Europe were mainly warmer than average for the month. Particularly high temperatures were reported from Spain in the west and Crete in the east.

Several regions outside Europe were significantly warmer than average. Temperatures were substantially above normal over the Central Asian Republics and northern parts of Siberia and North America. Almost all of Africa had above-average temperatures, especially over much of the north of the continent and extending over the Middle East, and also in the south, where  South Africa and neighbouring countries experienced heatwave conditions and record temperatures. Temperatures were much higher than average over northern and central parts of South America, particularly over parts of Brazil, Bolivia and Paraguay. The high temperatures in Brazil are coinciding with exceptionally dry conditions over the Amazon basin. Temperatures were also well above average over Western Australia and east of the Antarctic Peninsula.

Below-average temperatures occurred over a small fraction of the land surface.  In addition to Nordic Europe, it was colder than usual over part of Antarctica, southern Chile and Argentina, the Horn of Africa, south-eastern Canada, Mongolia and northern China, and a few smaller regions.

Air temperatures were above average over large parts of the ocean, associated with the continuation of record sea-surface temperatures noted in a highlight box in the October temperature bulletin. El Niño conditions continued to develop over the equatorial Pacific. Air temperatures were above average around much of Antarctica, where sea-ice cover continued to be lower than normal, though less extreme than earlier in the year. Regions with relatively mild temperatures extended northward from the Antarctic to the southern Indian Ocean and parts of the southern Atlantic and Pacific Ocean basins. Air temperatures were also higher than the 1991-2020 average over most of the tropics, the North Atlantic and the North Pacific, and to the east of Japan in particular. Temperatures were below average over much of the Antarctic coastline, in a band stretching to the west of southern South America, east of Australia and over several other relatively small areas. The band of cold temperatures stretching from Greenland to Svalbard is aligned with a region where sea-ice extent was larger than usual.

0.85°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average for November

the warmest November on record, 0.32°C warmer than November 2020, the second warmest November

jointly with October 2023, more anomalous in warmth than any single month of any year in the ERA5 dataset other than September 2023.

about 1.75°C warmer than an estimate of the pre-industrial average for 1850-1900.

Each month since June 2023 has been the warmest on record for the respective month of the year.

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for November 2023 was 0.48°C higher than the 1991-2020 average. The month was 1.26°C colder over Europe than November 2015, the warmest November in the European record, and colder than 14 other Novembers in the period since 1996. It should be noted that the eastern boundary used for European averages is located at 40°E, which excludes the easternmost part of the continent, where temperatures were most above average in November 2023.

Sea surface temperature averaged over extra-polar waters (60°S–60°N) has continued its usual seasonal evolution. It remains substantially higher for the time of year than in any previous year in the data record. At 20.71°C, the November 2023 average was by far the highest on record for the month, 0.58°C above the 1991–2020 average for November, and 0.25°C higher than the previous record from November 2015 (20.46°C).

Currently more than a third of days in 2023 at more than 1.5°C above preindustrial level

Early in June 2023, a preliminary calculation indicated that the global-mean surface air temperature had briefly reached more than 1.5°C above the designated 1850-1900 pre-industrial level, the first time this had occurred during boreal summer. The event was short-lived, as it was linked to weather variations in various parts of the world superimposed on longer-term warming trend and climatic variability. Nevertheless, as the above figure shows, by July and August the global temperature was continually close to or above the 1.5°C level, and from 6 September onwards it has been above the 1.5°C level every single day. The global temperature reached more than 2°C above the 1850-1900 level for two days in mid-September, the first time this has occurred in the ERA5 data record. This was associated with simultaneous peak values of the anomalies over North America, Asia, South America and Antarctica shown earlier for monthly means.

For the year to the end of November, all days have been above the 1°C level above preindustrial in 2023, again for the first time. 43% of days have been above the 1.5°C level. This compares with 21% of days for the whole of 2016, the previous warmest year.

If the anomaly relative to 1850-1900 were to have the same value (1.75°C) in December 2023 as in November 2023, the average temperature for 2023 will be 1.48°C above the preindustrial reference level.

Background discussion can be found in a web article published after the June event. The calculation of daily temperature anomalies relative to 1850-1900 has been refined since then. It is discussed in the About the Data section of this website.

Temperatures over land were predominantly above the 1991-2020 seasonal average for the boreal autumn of 2023. Over Europe, only the Nordic countries had below-average temperatures. Other colder-than-average regions were few and far between, among them southern Argentina and Chile, northern Australia and parts of Antarctica.

Regions where seasonal-mean temperatures were notably higher than average include northern Canada and Alaska, western and central Siberia, north-eastern Africa, neighbouring parts of Brazil, Bolivia and Paraguay, western Australia and Queen Maud Land in East Antarctica.

The seasonal-average temperatures over the ocean were mainly above average, showing much the same pattern of differences from 1991-2020 as already noted for November 2023.

The global-mean temperature for boreal autumn (September–November) 2023 was the warmest on record globally by a large margin. The average temperature of 15.30°C was 0.88°C above the 1991-2020 seasonal average, 0.43°C higher than that in 2020, the previous warmest boreal autumn.

The European-average temperature for boreal autumn was 10.96°C, 1.43°C above the 1991-2020 average. This is the second highest autumn value on record, only 0.03°C lower than the temperature recorded for the warmest European autumn, which also occurred in 2020.

a little below average over several relatively small regions, including parts of Australia, Antarctica and western North America.

Averaging over twelve-month periods smooths out shorter-term variations in regional- and global-average temperatures. Globally, the last 12 months were the warmest on record, with an average temperature 0.55°C above the 1991-2020 average. The next warmest such period was the twelve months to October 2023. The warmest calendar year is 2016, with a temperature 0.44°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 was on a par with 2016, being cooler by less than 0.01°C, well below the spread among multiple datasets. 2023 is virtually certain to be the warmest calendar year to date, with an anomaly likely to be higher than 0.55°C above the 1991-2020 average.

The average global temperature for the 12 months to November 2023 is estimated to be 1.43°C above the 1850-1900 level. The way the average for the 1991-2020 reference period is related to that for 1850-1900 is outlined in the sidebar.

Comparing the average temperature for the calendar year to date (January to November) with the temperatures for the same periods of past years, the global average for 2023 is by far the highest on record, with an anomaly of 0.58°C relative to 1991-2020. This year so far is 1.46°C higher than the designated 1850-1900 pre-industrial level, using the annual-mean offset of 0.88°C. The January-November average anomaly for 2016 was 0.45°C relative to 1991-2020. The December 2023 temperature would have to average more than 0.2°C below the 1850-1900 level for the year 2023 to be colder than 2016. It is thus virtually certain that 2023 will become the warmest calendar year on record.

There is more variability in average European temperatures, but relatively dense observational coverage of the continent reduces uncertainty. The average for this latest 12-month period is 1.01°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 is the warmest calendar year on record for Europe by a clear margin, with a temperature 1.19°C above the 1991-2020 average.

From October 2021 an updated approach is used to relate recent global temperatures to 1850-1900, a period taken to approximate the pre-industrial level. Following this approach, the value 0.88°C should be added to annual values relative to the 1991-2020 reference period. This estimate derives from the approach used in the WMO statements on “The state of the global climate” from the Preliminary Statement for 2021 onwards, which builds on the methods outlined in the IPCC AR6 WG1 report. Read more in About the data.

There is general agreement among datasets that the period since 2015 is much warmer globally than any previous period. There is also agreement that global temperature has risen at an average rate close to 0.2°C per decade since the late 1970s. There is nevertheless still some spread between the datasets for recent years, such as for 2021 and 2022, and the annual average temperature anomalies for these years from ERA5 are generally higher than those from the five other datasets considered. The differences range from 0.01 to 0.07°C for 2016-2022. The range is 0.00 to 0.06°C if air temperature over sea is replaced by sea-surface temperature for ERA5 and the other dataset for which sea-surface temperature was not used by design. The remaining differences depend partly on the extent to which datasets represent the relatively warm conditions that have predominated over the Arctic and Antarctic during these years. Differences elsewhere in estimates of sea-surface temperature and surface air temperature over land have been further factors.

Arctic sea ice extent reached its 8 th lowest value for November, at 4% below average, well above the lowest November value recorded in 2016 (13% below average). Most of the Arctic Ocean was dominated by above-average concentrations, most prominent in the Greenland Sea, while below-average concentrations prevailed in some peripheral seas (Chukchi, Kara, and Barents Seas, Baffin Bay).

Antarctic sea ice extent was the second lowest for November, at 9% below average, after reaching record-low values for the time of year by large margins for six consecutive months. While large areas of below-average sea ice concentrations persisted in the Atlantic sector and in the northern Ross-Amundsen Seas, above-average concentrations prevailed over a larger fraction of the sea ice pack than in previous months.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in November 2023 was 9.9 million km 2, about 0.4 million km 2 (or 4%) below the 1991-2020 average for November. This value ranks 8 th lowest in the satellite data record, well above the lowest November value of 13% below average recorded in 2016.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The map of sea ice concentration anomalies for November 2023 shows a dominance of above-average concentrations (albeit often only slightly above) over most of the central Arctic Ocean, along most the northern Siberian coast, and in the Greenland Sea, while below-average concentrations prevailed in some of the peripheral seas (Chukchi, western Kara, and northern Barents Seas, Baffin and Hudson Bays). Above-average sea ice conditions in the Greenland Sea were already seen in October but became more prominent in November and coincided with well below-average surface air temperature over the area.

The average Antarctic sea ice extent for November 2023 was 14.8 million km 2, 1.5 million km 2 (or 9%) below the 1991-2020 average for November, which is the second-lowest extent for November within the 45-year satellite dataset, close to the lowest extent recorded in November 2016 (also about 9% below average). This followed six consecutive months (May–October) during which the sea ice extent remained at record low levels for the time of year by substantial margins.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The spatial distribution of sea ice concentration anomalies around Antarctica in November 2023 continues to show large expanses of below-average concentrations north of the sea ice edge in the Atlantic sector and in parts of the Pacific sector in the northern Ross and Amundsen Seas. There is a greater dominance of above-average concentrations within the sea ice pack than in the previous months, especially in the Weddell Sea and around the coast of West Antarctica (Ross, Amundsen, and Bellingshausen Seas). As in previous months, sea ice concentrations were most above average in the Bellingshausen Sea. A mix of above- and below-average concentrations prevailed along most of the East Antarctic coast (from 30°E to 160°E).

October 2023 was the warmest October on record globally, with an average surface air temperature of 15.30°C, 0.85°C above the 1991-2020 average for October and 0.40°C above the temperature of the previous warmest October, in 2019. The global temperature anomaly for October 2023 was the second highest across all months in the ERA5 dataset, behind September 2023. The month as a whole was 1.7°C warmer than an estimate of the October average for 1850-1900, the designated pre-industrial reference period.

For the calendar year to date, January to October, the global mean temperature for 2023 is the highest on record, 1.43°C above the 1850-1900 pre-industrial average, and 0.10°C higher than the ten-month average for 2016, currently the warmest calendar year on record. For Europe, October 2023 was the fourth warmest October on record, 1.30°C higher than the 1991-2020 average. The average sea surface temperature for October over 60°S–60°N was 20.79°C, the highest on record for October. El Niño conditions continued to develop in the equatorial Pacific, although anomalies remain lower than those reached at this time of year during the development of the historically strong 1997 and 2015 events.

Southern and central latitudes of Europe were much warmer in October 2023 than the 1991-2020 climatological average. Several countries reported record high temperatures for October early in the month. Average temperatures for the month from ERA5 were the highest on record over Italy, part of the Balkans and the central Mediterranean Sea. Switzerland recorded its second warmest October since records began in 1864; only October 2022 was warmer. Conversely, temperatures over Iceland, Norway and countries around the Baltic Sea were below the 1991-2020 average. Weather-station data for Norway, for example, showed that the country overall had its coldest October since 2009 and the 26 th coldest October in a record dating back to 1900.

Substantial regions outside Europe were also significantly warmer than average. Temperatures were well above normal over most of Siberia and central Asia, and over northern and north-eastern parts of North America, where several Canadian cities, among them Ottawa, reported their hottest recorded October day early in the month. Almost all of Africa had above-average temperatures, especially over countries in the north-west of the continent, and over a region including parts of Ethiopia, Sudan and South Sudan. Temperatures were also much higher than average over northern and central parts of South America, including the drought-hit Amazon Basin, where low water levels and high water temperatures are suspected factors responsible for large fatalities  of fish and river dolphins. Temperatures were also well above average over Western Australia and a region in East Antarctica.

Below-average temperatures occurred only over a small fraction of the land surface. The largest such regions in addition to Scandinavia were over Antarctica and central longitudes of Australia.

Air temperatures were above average over large parts of the ocean, associated with the record sea-surface temperatures noted below in a highlight box. El Niño conditions continued to develop over the equatorial Pacific. Air temperatures were above average around much of Antarctica, where sea-ice cover continued to be far lower than normal. Regions with relatively mild temperatures extended northward from the Antarctic to the southern Indian Ocean and parts of the southern Atlantic and Pacific Ocean basins. Air temperatures were also higher than the 1991-2020 average over most of the tropics, the North Atlantic and the North Pacific. Temperatures were below average over most of the Antarctic coastline, in a band stretching to west of southern South America, south-east of South Africa and over several other areas.

0.85°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average for October

the warmest October on record, 0.40°C warmer than October 2019, the second warmest October

more anomalous in warmth than any single month of any year in the ERA5 dataset other than September 2023.

around 1.7°C warmer than estimates of the pre-industrial average for 1850-1900.

Each month since June 2023 has been the warmest on record for the respective month of the year. Indications for 2023 as a whole are discussed in the first highlight box.

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for October 2023 was 1.30°C higher than the 1991-2020 average, the fourth highest on record for October. The month was 0.62°C cooler over Europe than October 2022, the warmest October in the European record.

Comparing the average temperature for the calendar year to date (January to October) with the temperatures for the same periods of past years, the global average for 2023 is the highest on record, with an anomaly of 0.55°C relative to 1991-2020. This year so far is 1.43°C higher than the designated 1850-1900 pre-industrial level, using the annual-mean offset of 0.88°C (see sidebar). Currently, the warmest complete year on record is 2016, which had a January-October average anomaly of 0.45°C relative to 1991-2020.

The final two months of 2016 were relatively cool (reducing the annual average anomaly to 0.44°C), while the remainder of 2023 is expected to be relatively warm, as the current El Niño event continues, and relatively high sea-surface temperatures elsewhere persist. Global-mean temperatures for November and December 2023 in the range 0.3-0.7°C above 1991-2020 levels, for example, would result in annual values for 2023 in the range 0.51-0.58°C above the 1991-2020 average, equivalent to 1.39-1.46°C above 1850-1900.

A recent web article discusses this year’s record progression of sea-surface temperatures (SSTs) up to the end of August. Since then, the SST averaged over extra-polar waters has begun its usual seasonal decrease. It nevertheless continues to be substantially higher for the time of year than in any previous year in the data record. At 20.79°C, the October 2023 average was by far the highest on record for the month, 0.58°C above the 1991–2020 average for October, and 0.27°C higher than the previous record from October 2015 (20.52°C).

The current El Niño event in the tropical Pacific contributes to the warmth of 2023, although oceanic and atmospheric indices indicate that to date it has not been as strong an event as the El Niño experienced in 2015 and 1997. The tropical and sub-tropical North Atlantic, much of the western Indian and Pacific Ocean basins and far southern parts of the Atlantic, Indian and Pacific Oceans are other regions where SSTs in October 2023 were unusually high.

below average over parts of Australia, Antarctica, western North America, eastern South America, and central and north-eastern Asia.

Averaging over twelve-month periods smooths out shorter-term variations in regional- and global-average temperatures. Globally, the last 12-month period was 0.50°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average. This is higher than the 0.46°C average for the twelve-month periods ending in September 2016 and May and June 2020, the three previous warmest twelve-month periods in this data record. The warmest calendar year is 2016, with a temperature 0.44°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 was on a par with 2016, being cooler by less than 0.01°C, well below the spread among multiple datasets.

The average global temperature for the twelve months to October 2023 is about 1.38°C above the 1850-1900 level. The way the average for the 1991-2020 reference period is related to that for 1850-1900 is outlined in the sidebar. Allowing for the seasonal variation discussed in this article, October 2023 is estimated to have averaged around 1.7°C above the 1850-1900 level.

There is more variability in average European temperatures, but relatively dense observational coverage of the continent reduces uncertainty. The average for this latest twelve-month period is 1.08°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 is the warmest calendar year on record for Europe by a clear margin, with a temperature 1.19°C above the 1991-2020 average.

From October 2021 an updated approach is used to relate recent global temperatures to 1850-1900, a period taken to approximate the pre-industrial level. Following this approach, the value 0.88°C should be added to annual values relative to the 1991-2020 reference period. This estimate derives from the approach used in the WMO statements on “The state of the global climate” from the Preliminary Statement for 2021 onwards, which builds on the methods outlined in the IPCC AR6 WG1 report. Read more in About the data.

There is general agreement among datasets that the period since 2015 is much warmer globally than any previous period. There is also agreement that global temperature has risen at an average rate close to 0.2°C per decade since the late 1970s. There is nevertheless still some spread between the datasets for recent years, such as for 2021 and 2022, and the annual average temperature anomalies for these years from ERA5 are generally higher than those from the five other datasets considered. The differences range from 0.01 to 0.07°C for 2016-2022. The range is 0.00 to 0.06°C if air temperature over sea is replaced by sea-surface temperature for ERA5 and the other dataset for which sea-surface temperature was not used by design. The remaining differences depend partly on the extent to which datasets represent the relatively warm conditions that have predominated over the Arctic and Antarctic during these years. Differences elsewhere in estimates of sea-surface temperature and surface air temperature over land have been further factors.

The surface air temperature analysis homepage explains more about the production and reliability of the values presented here, but has yet to be updated to include the new information on dataset spread mentioned above.

Read more about long-term temperature changes for the globe, the Arctic and Europe in the Temperature indicator.

As a default graphics for both reference periods should be included on the webpage, with the 1991-2020 as the default out fo the two.

There may be exceptions, if so it is explicitly noted as a comment to that graphic.

October marked the sixth consecutive month that Antarctic sea ice extent remained at record low levels for the time of year, with a monthly value 11% below average. Sea ice concentrations were most below average in the northern Ross Sea and the South Atlantic sector, while above-average concentrations prevailed in the Bellingshausen Sea and the western Pacific sector.

Arctic sea ice extent reached its 7 th lowest value for October, at 12% below average. Sea ice concentrations were most below average in the Pacific sector of the Arctic Ocean while a stretch of above-average concentrations persisted between the Greenland Sea and the Laptev Sea.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in October 2023 was 6.9 million km 2, about 0.9 million km 2 (or 12%) below the 1991-2020 average for October. This value ranks 7 th lowest in the satellite data record, well above the lowest October of 27% below average recorded in 2020.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The map of sea ice concentration anomalies for October 2023 shows below-average concentrations prevailing over most of the Arctic Ocean. The most notable exception was a stretch of above-average concentrations between the Greenland Sea and the northern Laptev Sea, most of which was already present in July, August, and September. Sea ice concentrations were most below average in the Beaufort and East Siberian Seas.

For the sixth consecutive month and the eighth month in 2023 (after January, February, and May–September), Antarctic sea ice extent reached record low levels for the time of year. The average extent for October was 16.6 million km 2, 2.0 million km 2 (or 11%) below the 1991-2020 average  for October. This was the lowest extent for October by a large margin within the 45-year satellite dataset, well below the previous lowest extent of 5% below average recorded in October 1986.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The spatial distribution of sea ice concentration anomalies around Antarctica in October 2023 largely resembles the distribution seen in September. Concentrations continued to be most below average in the northern Ross Sea and in the South Atlantic sector. Below-average concentrations also prevailed in most of the ocean bordering the coast of East Antarctica. Conversely, sea ice concentrations were above average in the Bellingshausen and western Amundsen Seas as well as in parts of the western Pacific sector (120°–170°E).

The sea ice analysis page provides additional information about the nature, production and reliability of the data and information presented here.

The sea ice indicator page provides additional information about the long-term trends in both the Arctic and Antarctica.

September 2023 was the warmest September on record globally, with an average surface air temperature of 16.38°C, 0.93°C above the 1991-2020 average for September and 0.5°C above the temperature of the previous warmest September, in 2020. Its global temperature anomaly was more positive than any single month of any year in the ERA5 dataset. The month as a whole was around 1.75°C warmer than an estimate of the September average for 1850-1900, the designated pre-industrial reference period.

The average global temperature for the first nine months of 2023 (January–September) is 0.52°C higher than the corresponding 1991-2020 average, and 0.05°C higher than the nine-month average for 2016, currently the warmest calendar year on record. For the calendar year to date, January to September, the global mean temperature for 2023 is 1.40°C higher than the 1850-1900 preindustrial average.

September 2023 was also the warmest September on record for the European continent, at 2.51°C higher than the 1991-2020 average, and 1.1°C higher than 2020, the previous warmest September. The average sea surface temperature for September over 60°S–60°N reached 20.92°C, the highest on record for September and the second highest across all months, behind August 2023. El Niño conditions continued to develop over the equatorial eastern Pacific.

Most of Europe was much warmer in September 2023 than the 1991-2020 climatological average. Several countries in a band from France to Finland and north-western Russia experienced their warmest September on record, as seen in the data records from their weather stations and from the ERA5 dataset. Belgium and the United Kingdom were among the countries reporting unprecedented heatwave conditions early in the month. Temperatures at or slightly below the 1991-2020 average were limited to the periphery of the continent: Iceland, Svalbard and parts of Greece and the Iberian Peninsula.

Many regions outside Europe were also significantly warmer than average. Unusually warm conditions extended eastward from Europe to central Siberia and also prevailed further south across Asia, from the Middle East to China and Japan. Almost all of Africa had above-average temperatures, especially so in a band covering parts of Eritrea, Ethiopia, Sudan and South Sudan. Temperatures were also much higher than average over the southern USA and northern Mexico, and over central and eastern Canada. Countries in South America experiencing high early-spring temperatures included Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Paraguay and Peru. Western Australia experienced its warmest September on record, with the month ranking third warmest for Australia as a whole. Temperatures were also well above average over almost all of the Antarctic land mass.

Below-average temperatures only occurred over a small fraction of the land surface, including western parts of the USA, rain-affected Libya, southern Greenland, and the far south of South America and Africa.

Air temperatures were above average over large parts of the ocean, associated with the record sea-surface temperatures noted below in a highlight box. El Niño conditions continued to develop over the equatorial Pacific. Air temperatures were above average around much of Antarctica, where sea-ice cover continued to be far lower than normal. Regions of relatively mild temperatures extended northward from the Antarctic to the southern Indian Ocean and parts of the southern Atlantic and Pacific Oceans. Air temperatures were also higher than the 1991-2020 average over most of the tropics, the North Atlantic and the North Pacific, with notably warm temperatures east of Japan. Temperatures were below average over the ice-covered Weddell Sea and in a broad band stretching to west of southern South America. Smaller areas of below-average marine air temperature include ones to the west of southern Africa, north-east of Iceland, west of Greenland and south of Alaska.

0.93°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average for September

the warmest September on record, 0.50°C warmer than September 2020, the second warmest September

more anomalous in warmth than any single month of any year in the ERA5 dataset.

around 1.75°C warmer than estimates of the preindustrial average for 1850-1900.

Further global statistics relating to this exceptional September can be found in the highlight box. The month follows June,  July and August that were each the warmest on record for the respective month of the year. Indications for the year as a whole are discussed later.

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for September 2023 was 2.51°C higher than the 1991-2020 average, and the highest on record for September. The month was 1.09°C warmer over Europe than in September 2020, the previous warmest September in the European record.

September 2023 was the warmest September on record globally, with a global-mean surface air temperature of 16.38°C according to the ERA5 dataset. It followed the warmest two months on record, July and August 2023, when the global-mean temperature reached monthly records of 16.95°C and 16.82 respectively. The significant margin by which these two months exceeded the previous record of July 2019 (16.63°C) can be clearly seen in the figure above showing the global-mean temperatures for the 30 warmest months in the 1940–2023 period. September 2023 is the only September that appears in the figure. The second warmest September had a temperature of 15.88°C, which is below the lowest point of the temperature range shown in the figure.

The second figure compares the global-mean temperature for September 2023 from ERA5 with the temperatures from ERA5 and five other datasets for previous Septembers, dating back to 1850 in some cases. September 2023 has a temperature 1.75°C above the 1850-1900 average. The 0.5°C margin by which its temperature exceeds that of the previous warmest September is exceptional. In particular, the Septembers of other recent years with developing El Niño events, such as 1997 and 2015, have not been especially warm.

A recent web article discusses this year’s record progression of sea-surface temperatures (SSTs) up to the end of August. Since then, the SST averaged over extra-polar waters has begun its usual seasonal decrease. It nevertheless continues to be substantially higher than in any previous year in the data record. At 20.92°C, the September average SST over the 60°S–60°N domain was the highest on record for September and the second highest across all months, behind August 2023 (20.98°C).

below average over Australia, West Antarctica and parts of western North America, eastern South America, and central and north-eastern Asia.

Antarctic sea ice extent remained at a record low level for the time of year. Both the daily and monthly extents reached their lowest annual maxima in the satellite record in September, with the monthly extent 9% below average. Sea ice concentrations were the most below-average in the northern Ross Sea, South Atlantic and western Indian Ocean sectors, while above-average concentrations prevailed in the Bellingshausen–Amundsen Sea sector.

The daily Arctic sea ice extent reached its 6th lowest annual minimum while the monthly sea ice extent ranked 5th lowest, at 18% below average. Sea ice concentrations were most below average in the Pacific sector of the Arctic Ocean while a stretch of above-average concentrations persisted along the coast of northern Siberia.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in September 2023 reached its annual minimum at 4.8 million km 2, about 1.1 million km 2 (or 18%) below the 1991-2020 average  for September. This value ranks 5 th lowest in the satellite data record. The lowest September extent and lowest annual monthly minimum occurred in September 2012, at 32% below average.

The daily Arctic sea ice extent reached its 6 th lowest annual minimum in the satellite data record on 16 th September at 4.71 million km 2 according to data from EUMETSAT OSI SAF (the date may vary between sea ice datasets, especially as the daily extent hovered close to the minimum for several days around mid-September). The relatively low minimum came after several months of close-to-average, decreasing values in May–July followed by a rapid decrease during August. The all-time minimum in daily Arctic sea ice extent was reached in September 2012, at 3.78 million km 2.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The map of sea ice concentration anomalies for September 2023 shows below-average concentrations over most of the Arctic Ocean, except for a stretch of above-average concentrations between the northern coast of Franz Josef Land and the northern Laptev Sea and a more limited area in the Greenland Sea. Sea ice concentrations were most below average on the Pacific side of the Arctic Ocean, covering the Beaufort and East Siberian Seas, both of which were largely ice-free during September.

For the fifth consecutive month, Antarctic sea ice extent remained at record low levels for the time of year. The average extent for September was 17.4 million km 2, 1.6 million km 2 (or 9%) below the 1991-2020 average for September. This was by far the lowest extent for September and the lowest annual maximum within the 45-year satellite dataset, well below the previous lowest extents of about 4-5% below average recorded in September 1986 and 2017. September 2023 marked the seventh month in 2023 that Antarctic sea ice has reached a record low extent for the time of year (after January, February, and May–August). The highlight box provides further insight into the recent evolution of the daily sea ice extent that led to the record low monthly values.

The unprecedented low sea ice extents for the time of year observed around Antarctica during a large part of 2023 are clearly apparent in the daily time series shown above. The unusual behaviour of the sea ice extent began in the spring. After reaching an all-time minimum in February 2023 and remaining relatively close to the already low 2022 values during March and early April, the sea ice extent started falling below previous record minima for the time of year and has since remained well outside the range of previously observed values.

As in previous months, it is worth noting that despite the record-low values being reached for the time of year, the sea ice extent continued its expansion around Antarctica – albeit at a much slower pace than in previous years – until it reached a maximum of 17.6 million km2 on 12th September according to data from EUMETSAT OSI SAF (the date of the maximum can vary slightly depending on the dataset). This value was about 2.3 million km2 lower than the median maximum for 1991–2020, and about 1.0 million km2 lower than the maximum recorded a year ago in September 2022.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The spatial distribution of sea ice concentration anomalies around Antarctica in September 2023 largely resembles the distribution seen in August. Concentrations continued to be most below average in the northern and northwestern Ross Sea and in the South Atlantic and western Indian Ocean sectors. In contrast, as in previous months, concentrations were most above average over the Amundsen and Bellingshausen Seas. This month, the above-average concentrations also extended to the northern Weddell Sea, on the other side of the Antarctic Peninsula. Mixed concentration anomalies occurred in the western Pacific sector along the coast of East Antarctica.

The correspondence between sea ice concentration anomalies and surface temperature anomalies was less straightforward than in other months. Surface temperatures were well above average over the entire West Antarctic sector of the Southern Ocean (from the Ross Sea to the Bellingshausen Sea) but well below average over the entire Weddell Sea.

The sea ice analysis page provides additional information about the nature, production and reliability of the data and information presented here.

The sea ice indicator page provides additional information about the long-term trends in both the Arctic and Antarctica.

From the June 2021 summary onward, the time series of monthly sea ice extent anomalies are based on the EUMETSAT OSI SAF Sea Ice Index (see more here ).

From January 2021 onward, the main reference period used is 1991-2020. What this means is described in a web article. For a transition period, the graphics and data relating to the previous reference period (1981-2010) can be found by using the tab-function above each graphic. For key indicators, the 1981-2010 value is included when hovering over the 1991-2020 value.

August 2023 is the warmest August on record, and warmer than all other months except July 2023. The global-mean surface air temperature of 16.82°C for August 2023 was 0.71°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average for August, and 0.31°C warmer than the previous warmest August in 2016. The month is estimated to have been around 1.5°C warmer than the preindustrial average for 1850-1900. Heatwaves were experienced in multiple regions of the Northern Hemisphere, including southern Europe, the southern United States, and Japan. Well-above average temperatures occurred over Australia, several South American countries and around much of Antarctica. El Niño conditions continued to develop over the equatorial eastern Pacific. Marine air temperatures were well above average in several other regions. The global temperature anomaly for the first 8 months of 2023 (January–August) ranks second-warmest on record, only 0.01°C below 2016, currently the warmest year on record.

The boreal summer (June–August) 2023 was the warmest on record globally by a large margin, with an average temperature of 16.77°C, 0.66°C above average. Temperatures over land for the boreal summer of 2023 were predominantly above the 1991-2020 average. The European-average temperature for boreal summer was 19.63°C, which at 0.83°C above average, was the fifth warmest boreal summer season.

In August 2023, temperatures were mostly above their 1991-2020 climatological averages over most of Europe. Above-average temperatures were experienced in southern Europe, where a region from Portugal across France and into Italy had a heatwave. Milan recorded its hottest average daily temperature of 33°C in a record dating back to 1763. The warmth in southern Europe stretched into North Africa/Maghreb, with Morocco observing a temperature of greater than 50.4°C for the first time. Türkiye, eastern Europe, and an area over the Kara Sea to the south of Novaya Zemlya also had notable above-average temperatures. In contrast, an area centred on southern Scandinavia and including the Netherlands and southern UK had temperatures that were close to or below average.

Elsewhere, the majority of the Earth’s land surface was significantly warmer than average. The central and southern United States were affected by high temperatures, with many areas having near-record heat. For example, New Orleans tied its previous record high temperature of 102°F (38.9°C). Warmer-than-average temperatures also occurred over Alaska, northwest Canada, Hudson Bay, and Greenland. Northern and central Africa generally had above-average temperatures, and notable warm anomalies were seen over Eritrea, Ethiopia, and the Democratic Republic of Congo. In Asia, warmer-than-average temperatures were mostly experienced, and in particular Japan had heatwave conditions and multiple temperature records broken.

In the Southern Hemisphere, South America was largely affected by above-average temperatures. Brazil had temperatures near 42°C and at times 19 of its 26 states under heatwave conditions. Bolivia, Paraguay, and parts of Argentina also experienced warmer-than-average conditions. Australia was mostly above average and had its second warmest national area-average mean temperature on record for August (since 1910). Sydney reflected the warm conditions as it had its warmest winter day – of 27.5°C – in eight years.

Below-average temperatures occurred over only a small fraction of the land surface, and were largely within 1°C of the average. The Antarctic land mass was the main exception, with a mix of much above- and below-average temperatures, as is often the case there in wintertime. A part of the western United States and northeast Canada also experienced below-average temperatures.

Air temperatures were above average over large parts of the ocean, associated with record sea-surface temperatures. El Niño conditions continued to develop over the equatorial eastern Pacific. Air temperatures were unusually high around Antarctica, where sea-ice cover  continued to be far lower than normal. Regions of relatively mild temperatures extended northward from the Antarctic over much of the southern Atlantic, Indian and Pacific Oceans. Air temperatures were also higher than the 1991-2020 average over most of the tropics and North Pacific, with notably warm temperatures east of Japan. Temperatures were below average to the west and south-west of southern South America, off western Australia, to the south-west of the Mexico’s Baja California peninsula, north east of Iceland.

0.71°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average for August

the warmest August in this data record and 0.31°C warmer than August 2016, the second warmest month on record

around 1.5°C warmer than the preindustrial average for 1850-1900.

Further global statistics relating to this exceptional August can be found in the highlight box. The month follows a June and a July that were the warmest on record for the respective months of the year. Indications for the year as a whole are discussed later.

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for August 2023 was 1.36°C higher than the 1991-2020 average. The month was 0.36°C cooler over Europe than August 2022, the warmest August in the European record.

August 2023 was the second-warmest month on record globally, with a global-mean surface air temperature of 16.82°C according to the ERA5 dataset. It followed the warmest month on record, in July, when the global-mean temperature reached a monthly record of 16.95°C. The significant margin by which these two months exceeded the previous record of July 2019 (16.63°C) can be clearly seen in the figure above showing the global-mean temperatures for the 30 warmest months in the 1940–2023 period.

The second figure compares the global-mean temperature for August 2023 from ERA5 with the temperatures from ERA5 and five other datasets for previous Augusts, dating back to 1850 in some cases. August 2023 has a temperature 1.5°C above the 1850-1900 average. The margin by which its temperature exceeds that of the previous warmest August is unusually large. The warmth of August 1877 is also notable, for its time.

Temperatures over land for the boreal summer of 2023 were predominantly above the 1991-2020 average for the season and this was especially the case for Europe, where only part of western Russia had below-average temperatures. The Antarctic had a mixture of warmer-than-average and cooler-than-average conditions.

Regions where seasonal-mean temperatures were lower than average include Pakistan, parts of the northern United States, eastern Nunavut, central Brazil, and isolated pockets of Africa and Asia.

The seasonal-average temperatures over the ocean show much the same pattern of differences from 1991-2020 as already noted for August 2022.

The global-mean temperature for the boreal summer (June–August) 2023 was the warmest on record globally by a large margin. The average temperature of 16.77°C was 0.66°C above the 1991-2020 seasonal average.

The European-average temperature for the boreal summer was 19.63°C, which at 0.83°C above average, was the fifth warmest boreal summer season. This is about 0.5°C lower than that recorded for the warmest European summer, which occurred in 2022.

below average over Australia, West Antarctica, and parts of western North America, eastern South America, and central Asia.

Averaging over twelve-month periods smooths out shorter-term variations in regional- and global-average temperatures. Globally, the last 12-month period was 0.41°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average. This twelve-month average anomaly is below the 0.46°C average for the twelve-month periods ending in September 2016 and May and June 2020, the three warmest periods in this data record. The warmest calendar year is 2016, with a temperature of 0.44°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 was on a par with 2016, being cooler by less than 0.01°C, well below the spread among multiple datasets. The third and fourth warmest calendar years are 2019 and 2017, whose temperatures were 0.40°C and 0.34°C above average, respectively.

The average global temperature for the twelve months to July 2023 is about 1.3°C above the 1850-1900 level. The way the average for the 1991-2020 reference period is related to that for 1850-1900 is outlined in the sidebar. Allowing for seasonal variations as discussed in  this article, July and August 2023 are estimated to have averaged around 1.5°C above the 1850-1900 level.

Comparing averages for the calendar year to date, from January to August, the global mean for 2023 is the second highest on record, at 0.47°C relative to 1991-2020, compared with 0.45°C for 2020 and 0.48°C for 2016. The gap between 2023 and 2020 is expected to narrow in the coming months, as the latter months of 2016 were relatively cool (reducing the annual average to 0.44°C), while the remainder of 2023 is expected to be relatively warm as the current El Niño event develops.

From October 2021 an updated approach is used to relate recent global temperatures to 1850-1900, a period taken to approximate the pre-industrial level. Following this approach, the value 0.88°C should be added to the value relative to the 1991-2020 reference period.  This estimate derives from the approach used in the WMO statements on “The state of the global climate” from the Preliminary Statement for 2021 onwards, which builds on the methods outlined in the IPCC AR6 WG1 report. Read more in About the data.

There is more variability in average European temperatures, but relatively dense observational coverage of the continent reduces uncertainty. The average for this latest twelve-month period is 0.90°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 is the warmest calendar year on record for Europe by a clear margin, with a temperature of 1.19°C above the 1991-2020 average.

There is general agreement among datasets that the period since 2015 is much warmer globally than any previous period. There is also agreement that global temperature has risen at an average rate close to 0.2°C per decade since the late 1970s. There is nevertheless still some spread between the datasets for recent years, such as for 2021 and 2022, and the annual average temperature anomalies for these years from ERA5 are generally higher than those from the five other datasets considered. The differences range from 0.01 to 0.07°C for 2016-2022. The range is 0.00 to 0.06°C if air temperature over sea is replaced by sea-surface temperature for ERA5 and the other dataset for which sea-surface temperature was not used by design. The remaining differences depend partly on the extent to which datasets represent the relatively warm conditions that have predominated over the Arctic and Antarctic during these years. Differences elsewhere in estimates of sea-surface temperature and surface air temperature over land have been further factors.

Antarctic sea ice extent remained at a record low level for the time of year, with a monthly value 12% below average, by far the largest negative anomaly for August since satellite observations began. Sea ice concentrations were most below-average in the northern Ross Sea and in the South Atlantic and Indian Ocean sectors while above-average concentrations prevailed in the Bellingshausen–Amundsen Sea sector.

Arctic sea ice extent was further below average than in July, at 10% below average, but well above the record minimum from August 2012. While most of the central Arctic Ocean saw below-average sea ice concentrations, a stretch of above-average concentrations persisted between Svalbard and the Laptev Sea.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in August 2023 reached 6.1 million km 2, about 0.7 million km 2 (or 10%) below the 1991-2020 average for August. This value ranks 9 th lowest in the satellite data record. This anomaly is significantly larger than the one recorded in July (0.3 million km 2 or 3% below average), reflecting the decrease of the daily sea ice extent further away from the average, which was observed since the beginning of August. The extent anomaly recorded for August remains notably smaller in magnitude than the anomalies recorded in August 2007, 2012, 2019, and 2020. The lowest extent for August occurred in 2012, at 22% below average.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

As Arctic sea ice has been approaching its annual minimum extent (usually in September), the sea ice cover was mainly confined to the central Arctic Ocean during August. The spatial distribution of sea ice concentration anomalies was dominated by below-average concentrations, particularly in the East Siberian and Beaufort Seas. Conversely, a stretch of above-average concentrations, already present in July, was prominent between the eastern coast of Svalbard and the northern Laptev Sea.

As Antarctic sea ice extent has been approaching its annual maximum (usually in September), it remained at unprecedented low levels for the time of year. This has been the case consistently since May 2023. In August, it reached 16.0 million km 2 on average for the month as a whole, 2.2 million km 2 (or 12%) below the 1991-2020 average for August. This was by far the lowest extent for August within the 45-year satellite dataset, well below the previous lowest extents of about 4% below average recorded in August 1986, 2002, 2017, and 2022. August 2023 marks the sixth month in 2023 that Antarctic sea ice has reached a record low extent (January, February, and May-August). The highlight box provides further insight into the evolution of the daily sea ice extent that helps understand these monthly records.

The unprecedented low sea ice extents for the time of year observed around Antarctica in August 2023 are clearly apparent in the daily time series shown above. The unusual behaviour of the sea ice extent began in the spring. After reaching an all-time minimum in February 2023 and remaining relatively close to the already low 2022 values during March and early April, the sea ice extent started falling below previous record minima for the time of year and has since remained well outside the range of previously observed values.

The unprecedented nature of the evolution of the Antarctic sea ice extent in recent months is particularly striking in the daily anomalies. These anomalies reached their largest magnitude in early July and again in Mid-August, at about 2.7 million km 2 below average. They became notably smaller during the second half of August, reaching about 1.6 million km 2 on 31 st August. At the end of the month, the total sea ice extent around Antarctica (17.0 million km 2 ) was at a level more typically observed in mid-to-late July during the 1991–2020 period.

As in previous months, it is worth noting that despite the record-low values being reached for the time of year, the sea ice extent was still expanding around Antarctica, following an annual cycle that typically reaches a minimum in February and a maximum in September. However, since May 2023, this growth has been happening at a much slower pace than in previous years.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The spatial distribution of sea ice concentration anomalies around Antarctica in August 2023 shows that concentrations were most below average in the northern and northwestern Ross Sea, as they have been consistently since May. Concentrations were also below average in the South Atlantic (northern Weddell Sea) and Indian Ocean sectors, although not as prominently as in July. In contrast, concentrations remained above average in a broad sector stretching over the Bellingshausen and Amundsen Seas as well as (to a lesser extent) in the western Pacific sector along the coast of East Antarctica.

The below-average concentrations in the northern Ross Sea were associated with well above-average surface air temperatures, exceeding 10°C above average in some areas, the largest anomalies worldwide in August.

The global average temperature for July 2023 is confirmed to be the highest on record for any month. The month was 0.7°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average for July, and 0.3°C warmer than the previous warmest month, July 2019. The month is estimated to have been around 1.5°C warmer than the average for 1850-1900.

Heatwaves were experienced in multiple regions of the Northern Hemisphere, including southern Europe. Well-above average temperatures occurred over several South American countries and around much of Antarctica. El Niño conditions continued to develop over the equatorial eastern Pacific. Marine air temperatures were well above average in several other regions.

Temperatures for July 2023 were much higher than their 1991-2020 climatological averages over southern Europe. Heatwaves were experienced from Spain in the west to the Balkans in the east. Many new local temperature records were set, while other records were close to being broken. A temperature of 48°C was reported for a site in Sardinia and 47°C was reported from an observatory in Palermo, Sicily. Temperatures peaked at 46°C  in Greece. Additional information and discussion are presented in the box  below and in this blog article.

In contrast, much of northern Europe experienced temperatures that were close to or below average.

Northern and central Africa were generally hotter than average. Algeria and Tunisia experienced particularly anomalous conditions, with the World Meteorological Organization reporting daily maximum temperatures as high as 49°C. Temperatures were also especially high over Eritrea and north-western Ethiopia. Prolonged heatwaves occurred over the south-western USA and over northern Canada, Labrador and Newfoundland. In Phoenix, Arizona, only the last day of the month registered a daily maximum temperature lower than 110°F (43°C), while all-time temperature records higher than 37°C were recorded close to the Arctic Circle at two sites in the Canadian Northwest Territories. It was also much warmer than average over Greenland, where the estimated extent of surface ice melt was high. In Asia, Kazakhstan experienced heatwave conditions, China reported its highest ever measured temperature, 52.2°C, and Japan had its hottest July in a temperature record dating back to 1898. Temperatures were also much above average over parts of Siberia.

Mid-winter temperatures were well above average over northern Chile and Argentina, and over Uruguay and southern Brazil. Climate monitoring time series for an extensive set of observing stations in Argentina provide examples of both occasional very high daytime temperatures and of generally mild night-time conditions over the north-west of the country. Many months of above-average temperatures and below-average rainfall have contributed to severe water shortages in Uruguay.

Below-average temperatures occured over only a small fraction of the land surface, and were largely within 1°C of the average. The Antarctic land mass was the main exception, with a mix of much above- and below-average temperatures, as is often the case there in wintertime.

Air temperatures were above average over large parts of the ocean, associated with record sea-surface temperatures. Exceptionally high marine temperatures were observed over much of the North Atlantic in particular. El Niño conditions continued to develop over the equatorial eastern Pacific. Air temperatures were unusually high around Antarctica, where sea-ice cover  continued to be far lower than normal. Regions of relatively mild temperature extended northward from the Antarctic over much of the southern Atlantic, Indian and Pacific Oceans. Air temperatures were also higher than the 1991-2020 average over most of the tropics and North Pacific, with notably warm temperatures east of Japan. Temperatures were below average to the west and south-west of southern South America, off western Australia, to the south-west of the Mexico’s Baja California peninsula, north east of Iceland, and to a lesser extent over a number of other regions.

0.72 ° C warmer than the 1991-2020 average for July

warmer than July 2019, the second warmest month on record, by the unusually large margin of 0.33°C

around 1.5°C warmer than the average for 1850-1900.

Further global statistics relating to this exceptional July can be found here. The month follows a June that was the warmest on record for that month of the year. Indications for the year as a whole are discussed later.

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for July 2023 was 0.38°C  higher than the 1991-2020 average. The month was 1.34°C cooler over Europe than July 2010, the warmest month in the European record.

The heatwaves that occurred over southern Europe and other countries bordering the Mediterranean Sea in July 2023 were at their strongest in the middle weeks of the month. Figure 1 shows how high daily surface air temperatures were compared with other years, for averages over a region designated “Southern Europe”. The highest daily average, reached on 18 July, was marginally higher than the previous record value, which occurred on 31 July 2020. Peak values in 2015 and 2022 were more than 0.6°C lower. Secondary maxima were reached on 14 and 23 July in 2023. The peak in 2020 was short lived by comparison.

The lands around the Mediterranean are known 1 to be one of the fastest warming parts of the world in boreal summer: the ERA5 data for this Southern Europe region has a linear trend for July of 0.54°C/decade from 1979 to 2023, which is three times larger than the trend for global-average July temperatures. July 2023 is the warmest month in the ERA5 data record for the region, with an average temperature of 25.6°C. The next warmest month is July 2015, followed by July 2022. In both cases the average temperature for the month rounds to 25.3°C.

Another view of the heatwaves of recent years is provided by categorizing months according to estimated levels of heat stress. July 2023 had a high number of days over Southern Europe with each of the chosen categories of heat stress: moderate (9.7%), strong (14.8%), very strong (10.0%) and extreme (1.7%). Figure 2 illustrates this for the extreme category, showing percentages for each July from 1950 to 2023. July 2023 has the highest number of instances of this category of stress. The three years with the highest instances of the very strong category are 2022 (10.9%), 2015 (10.3%) and 2023 (10%).

Note: The land domain designated “Europe” that is routinely monitored in this series of monthly temperature bulletins has been sub-divided at 45°N to form a region designated to be “Southern Europe”. This region mostly comprises parts of Europe that border the Mediterranean, but also includes northern parts of Morocco, Algeria and Tunisia, most of Türkiye and part of Syria. The region almalgamates the “South-West Europe” and “South-East Europe” regions discussed regularly in the corresponding hydrological bulletins.

Heat stress indices represent the effect of the environment on the human body. One such index is the Universal Thermal Climate Index (UTCI), which takes into account temperature, humidity, wind speed, sunshine and heat emitted by the surroundings, and how the human body responds to different thermal environments. It can be used to infer weather-induced outdoor thermal stress in humans by classifying UTCI values into stress categories, covering from hot to cold conditions, with units of °C representing a ‘feels-like’ temperature. For summer, the focus is on maximum (daytime) daily UTCI. The heat-stress categories are “extreme” (UTCI > 46°C), “very strong” (38°C-46°C), “strong” (32°C-38°C) and “moderate” (26°C-32°C).

Antarctic sea ice extent continued to break records for the time of year, with a monthly value 15% below average, by far the lowest July extent since satellite observations began. As in June, the daily Antarctic sea ice extent remained substantially below previously observed values for the time of year throughout the month.

Sea ice concentrations were most below-average in the northern Weddell, eastern Bellingshausen, and northern Ross Seas while above-average concentrations persisted in a broad Amundsen Sea sector. Arctic sea ice extent was slightly below average, but well above the record minimum from July 2020. While most of the Arctic Ocean saw below-average sea ice concentrations, above-average concentrations prevailed north of the northern Siberian coast.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in July 2023 reached 8.9 million km 2, about 0.3 million km 2 (or 3%) below the 1991-2020 average  for July. This value ranks 15 th lowest in the satellite data record, virtually on a par with July 2018, and well above the lowest extent recorded in July 2020 (14% below average). This continues the series of small negative anomalies that has characterised Arctic sea ice extent since May, and more generally for the past two years.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The spatial distribution of sea ice concentration anomalies in the Arctic in July 2023 was dominated by below-average concentrations over most of the region. Concentrations were most below average in the East Siberian Sea. In addition, below-average concentrations prevailed in the central Arctic Ocean, Beaufort Sea, southern Laptev Sea, western Kara Sea, northern Barents Sea, and Hudson Bay. On the other hand, a prominent band stretching from the eastern coast of Svalbard to the New Siberian Islands (between the Laptev and East Siberian Seas) and over the eastern Kara Sea, saw above-average sea ice concentrations. Concentrations were also above average in the Chukchi Sea.

As in June, Antarctic sea ice extent continued to break record lows for the time of year in July 2023. It reached 14.0 million km 2 on average for the month as a whole, 2.4 million km 2 (15%) below the 1991-2020 average  for July. This was by far the lowest extent for July within the 45-year satellite dataset, with an anomaly more than twice as large as the previous July record from 2022 (7% below average). This is the fifth month in 2023 (after January, February, May, and June ) that Antarctic sea ice has reached a record-low monthly extent for the time of year. The highlight box below provides further insight into the evolution of the daily sea ice extent that has led to such a low value for July.

The unprecedented low sea ice extents for the time of year observed around Antarctica in July 2023 are clearly apparent in the daily time series shown above. The unusual behaviour of the sea ice extent began in the spring. After reaching an all-time minimum in February 2023 and remaining relatively close to the already low 2022 values during March and early April, it started falling below previous record minima for the time of year and has since remained well outside the range of previously observed values.

The unprecedented nature of the evolution of the Antarctic sea ice extent in recent months is particularly striking in the daily anomalies. These anomalies reached their largest magnitude in early July, at about 2.7 million km 2 below average, and were hovering around 2.3 million km 2 below average at the end of the month. On 31st July, the total sea ice extent around Antarctica (15.1 million km 2 ) was at a level typically reached a month earlier during the 1991–2020 period.

It is worth noting that despite the record-low values being reached for the time of year, the sea ice extent was still expanding around Antarctica, following an annual cycle that typically reaches a maximum in September. However, as in May and June 2023, this growth was happening at a much slower pace than in previous years.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The spatial distribution of sea ice concentration anomalies around Antarctica in July 2023 remained very similar to the one seen in June. Concentrations continued to be most below average in the northern Weddell Sea, eastern Bellingshausen Sea (west of the Antarctic Peninsula) and in the northern and northwestern Ross Sea (north of the Victoria Land sector of East Antarctica). Below-average concentrations also prevailed in general along the coast of East Antarctica. In contrast, concentrations remained above average in a broad sector stretching from the western Bellingshausen Sea to the Amundsen Sea.

The below-average concentrations in the Weddell Sea, eastern Bellingshausen Sea, and northwestern Ross Sea were associated with well above-average surface air temperatures, exceeding 10°C above average in the Ross Sea sector, the largest anomalies worldwide in July.

The month was the warmest June globally at just over 0.5°C above the 1991-2020 average exceeding June 2019 – the previous record – by a substantial margin. Record June temperatures were experienced across northwest Europe. Record-high sea surface temperature occurred over large swaths of the North Atlantic, leading to marine heatwave conditions around Ireland and the UK.

Parts of Canada, the United States, Mexico, Asia, and eastern Australia were significantly warmer than normal. It was cooler than normal over western Australia, the western United States, and western Russia.

June 2023 saw temperatures above the 1991-2020 climatological average over most of Europe. The highest anomalies were found in northwest Europe, where Ireland, the United Kingdom, Belgium, and the Netherlands had their warmest Junes on record. France and Scandinavia were also notably warmer than average. In contrast, the southern Balkans, Greece, Türkiye, and western Russia were colder than average. Mixed conditions occurred across Italy and Spain.

Most of Canada experienced above-average temperatures, with northern Ontario and northern Quebec particularly affected. Mexico and parts of the southern United States had well above-average temperatures which led to over 100 heat-related fatalities. Southern South America, southeast Africa, Iran, most of India, northeast Russia, and northern and eastern Australia saw above-average temperatures; there were nearly 100 fatalities reported in India due to the heat too. A band of warmer-than-average anomalies also extended from central to eastern Asia, where Beijing had its hottest June day on record. Conversely, colder-than-average temperatures occurred in the southwestern and eastern United States, Baja California, central South America, parts of northern Africa and south Asia, and across western Australia. Mixed, although pronounced anomalies, were found over Antarctica.

Above-average air temperatures were largely found over the oceans. Most notably, exceptionally high sea surface temperatures were observed in large sectors of the North Atlantic, including marine heatwave conditions around Ireland and the UK. El Niño conditions continued to develop over the equatorial eastern Pacific. Air temperatures were unusually high over the Weddell Sea, with regions of relatively mild temperature extending northward over much of the southern Atlantic, Indian and Pacific Oceans. Air temperatures were higher than the 1991-2020 average throughout the tropics and over most of the North Pacific. In contrast, temperatures were lower than average from southeastern South America to the Amundsen Sea, off southeastern Australia, southern Africa, and western North America.

•     0.53°C  warmer than the 1991-2020 average for June

•    substantially warmer than the 0.37°C anomaly in June 2019, the second warmest June on record

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for June 2023 was 0.74°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average. The month was about 1.1°C cooler over Europe than June 2019, the warmest June in the record.

below average over Australia, West Antarctica, and parts of western North America, eastern South America, and central Asia.

This twelve-month average anomaly is well below the 0.46°C average for the twelve-month periods ending in September 2016 and May and June 2020, the three warmest periods in this data record. The warmest calendar year is 2016, with a temperature of 0.44°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 was on a par with 2016, being cooler by less than 0.01°C, well below the spread among multiple datasets. The third and fourth warmest calendar years are 2019 and 2017, and their temperatures were 0.40°C and 0.34°C above average, respectively. 2022 was the fifth warmest on record at 0.30°C above average, but was only marginally warmer than the years 2015, 2018, and 2021.

The average global temperature for the twelve months to June 2023 is around 1.2°C above the 1850-1900 level. The way the average for the 1991-2020 reference period is related to that for 1850-1900 is outlined in the sidebar.

There is general agreement among datasets that the period since 2015 is much warmer globally than any previous period. There is also agreement that global temperature has risen at an average rate close to 0.2°C per decade since the late 1970s. There is nevertheless still some spread between the datasets for recent years, such as for 2020 and 2021, and the annual average temperature anomalies for these years from ERA5 are generally higher than those from the five other datasets considered. The differences range from 0.01 to 0.07°C for 2016-2022. The range is 0.00 to 0.06°C if air temperature over sea is replaced by sea-surface temperature for ERA5 and the other dataset for which sea-surface temperature was not used by design. The remaining differences depend partly on the extent to which datasets represent the relatively warm conditions that have predominated over the Arctic and Antarctic during these years. Differences elsewhere in estimates of sea-surface temperature and surface air temperature over land have been further factors.
