Antarctic sea ice reached its lowest extent for June since satellite observations began, at 17% below average, breaking the previous June record by a substantial margin. Throughout the month, the daily Antarctic sea ice extent remained at unprecedented low values for the time of year. Sea ice concentrations were most below-average in the northern Weddell, eastern Bellingshausen, and northern Ross Seas while above-average concentrations continued to prevail in a broad Amundsen Sea sector.

Arctic sea ice extent was slightly below average but well above the June values from the past eight years. Above-average sea ice concentrations occurred in the Beaufort, Chukchi, and Greenland Seas and below-average concentrations in the Barents Sea, western Kara Sea, and Hudson Bay.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in June 2023 reached 11.5 million km 2, about 0.1 million km 2 (or 0.8%) below the 1991-2020 average  for June. This is the highest extent recorded in June since 2013, well above the values from the past eight years, and coming after a near-average extent in May. The lowest extent for June occurred in 2016, at about 6% below average. The near-average  sea ice conditions overall in the Artic are in stark contrast with the record low extent observed around Antarctica.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The spatial distribution of sea ice concentration anomalies in the Arctic in June 2023 was characterised by a mix of positive and negative anomalies across the various sectors of the Arctic Ocean. Concentrations were above average in the Beaufort, Chukchi, and Greenland Seas. They were generally mixed in the seas bordering the northern coast of Siberia (East Siberian, Laptev, and Kara Seas) as well in Baffin Bay. Below-average concentrations prevailed in the northern Barents Sea and Hudson Bay and were most pronounced in the western Kara Sea.

Antarctic sea ice extent remained once again well below average in June 2023, reaching 11.5 million km 2 on average, 2.3 million km 2 (17%) below the 1991-2020 average for June. This was by far the lowest extent for June within the 45-year satellite dataset, with an anomaly almost twice as large as the previous June record from 2022 (9% below average). This is the fourth month in 2023 (after January, February, and May ) that Antarctic sea ice has reached a record-low monthly extent for the time of year. The highlight box below provides additional insight into the recent evolution of Antarctic sea ice extent leading to such a record-low value for June.

After reaching an all-time minimum in February 2023, the daily extent remained close to the values recorded a year earlier in 2022 during March and early April. In early May, however, it started falling below previous record minima for the time of year. The deviation below the range of previously observed values is particularly striking in the daily anomalies.

By the end of June, the daily extent was about 2.5 million km 2 below average, at 12.7 million km 2, a value typically reached a month earlier during the 1991–2020 period. While unprecedented for June, anomalies of similar magnitude have already occurred in December 2016 and were followed two months later, in February 2017, by an all-time minimum extent (this record was later broken in February 2022, and again in February 2023).

It is worth noting that despite the record-low values reached in June, the sea ice extent was still expanding around Antarctica, following an annual cycle that typically reaches a maximum in September. However, in May and June 2023, this growth was happening at a much slower pace than in previous years.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The spatial distribution of sea ice concentration anomalies around Antarctica in June 2023 resembles the distribution already seen in April and May, but with more pronounced negative anomalies, in line with the record-low June value for the total sea ice extent. Concentrations continued to be most below average in the northern Weddell Sea, eastern Bellingshausen Sea (west of the Antarctic Peninsula) and to the north and northwest of the Ross Sea (north of the Victoria Land sector of East Antarctica). Below-average concentrations also prevailed in general along the coast of East Antarctica. In contrast, concentrations were above average in a broad sector stretching from the western Bellingshausen Sea to the Amundsen Sea and the adjacent part of the Ross Sea too.

The below-average concentrations in the Weddell Sea, Bellingshausen Sea, and northeast of the Ross Sea were associated with well above-average surface air temperatures, up to 9°C above average in certain areas. In contrast, the Amundsen Sea sector saw well below-average surface air temperatures.

The pattern of sea ice concentration anomalies observed in June 2023 can be linked to atmospheric circulation anomalies. The latter were characterised by alternating positive and negative anomalies in the 500-hPa geopotential height field forming a typical “zonal wave number 3”. These conditions led (among others) to offshore winds over the Amundsen Sea sector (pushing the ice edge further north) and onshore winds over the Weddell Sea (pushing the ice edge further south).

The month was the joint second warmest May globally, less than 0.1°C cooler than the warmest May on record. Sea surface temperature and marine air temperature averaged over all ice-free seas were at a record high for May. Temperatures on average were close to normal over most of Europe.

Parts of Canada, Africa and South-East Asia were significantly warmer than normal. It was notably cooler than normal over Australia and from North-West India to southern Siberia. An El Niño signal continued to emerge over the tropical eastern Pacific.

Temperatures for May 2023 were not far from their 1991-2020 climatological average over most of Europe. The month was a little warmer than normal over the west of the continent and a little cooler than normal over central and eastern parts. It was substantially above normal only over the far north-east of the continent.

Canada and the northern USA experienced a very warm month, with temperatures reaching more than 10°C above average west of Hudson Bay. Numerous high-temperature records were broken and there were record wildfire emissions from British Columbia in the west to Nova Scotia in the east. Africa saw unusually high temperatures over South Sudan and the Central African Republic, and to the south over countries that included Botswana, Zambia and Zimbabwe. Several countries in South-East Asia experienced either their warmest ever recorded day or their warmest May day. The month was also warmer than usual over much of South America.

Conversely, May 2023 was a relatively cold month over Australia, where the country’s average daily-minimum temperature was the lowest since 1944. North-West India had its 8th coldest average temperature  in a record dating back to 1901. Temperatures were also well below average over a region to the north of India extending to Mongolia and southern Siberia, and over much of the Antarctic Plateau and Ross Ice Shelf.

Above-average air temperatures predominated over the oceans. El Niño conditions continued to develop over the equatorial eastern Pacific. Air temperatures were unusually high over the Weddell Sea and eastward around the Antarctic coast to the Ross Sea, with regions of relatively mild temperature extending northward over much of the southern Atlantic, Indian and Pacific Oceans. Air temperatures were higher than the 1991-2020 average throughout the tropics and over most of the North Pacific and North Atlantic, especially west of Europe and North Africa. Temperatures were lower than normal over the Arctic Ocean. Averages over all ice-free seas of both sea-surface and air temperature are higher for May 2023 than for any other May in the data record.

It has been widely reported that average sea surface temperatures have been at a record high in April and May 2023. This is based on averages over the seas from 60°S to 60°N of data from the US National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration. Alternative averages taken over all ice-free seas have been evaluated for the ERA5 dataset used to produce this bulletin. These reveal a somewhat different annual cycle, with slightly larger peak values in August than in April, rather than vice versa. The ERA5 data nevertheless indicate that sea surface temperatures in May were higher than in any previous May in the data record. This is shown by the lighter, wider bars in the figure, for the period since 1979 when data are at their most reliable.

The temperatures shown over sea in this monthly bulletin are not sea surface temperatures, but marine air temperatures, which are ERA5's estimates of the atmospheric temperature at a height of two metres above the ocean surface. The monthly averages of these marine air temperatures are shown by the narrower, darker bars in the figure. These average temperatures are typically a little more than 1°C lower than average sea surface temperatures: 1°C has been added to the air temperatures shown in the figure to enable them to be compared more directly with the sea surface temperatures. The variations from year to year and the warming trend are similar for the two types of temperature, but small differences can be seen in the figure. Marine air temperature reached a record high for May in 2023, but the difference from the next warmest year, 2020 for this variable, is smaller than the corresponding difference in sea surface temperature. The converse is the case when 2023 is compared with the warm years of 2015 and 2016.

ERA5 reanalyses show a large soil moisture anomaly northwest of the Caspian Sea. This anomaly is not justified by the precipitation pattern and is only partly reflected in the temperature and relative humidity anomalies. Investigations are in progress to address and assess this signal.

0.40°C  warmer than the 1991-2020 average for May

about 0.07°C cooler than May 2020, the warmest May on record

similar in temperature (within 0.02°C) to the Mays of 2016 and 2017.

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for May 2023 was nevertheless almost the same as the 1991-2020 average. The month was about 2.1°C cooler over Europe than May 2018, the warmest May in the record.

Temperatures for the boreal spring of 2023 were predominantly above the 1991-2020 average for the south west and far east of Europe. They were below average over parts of Norway and Sweden, and to a lesser extent over central and south-eastern Europe.

Seasonal average temperatures were much above average over north-eastern Canada, western Asia apart from the Middle East, eastern Asia, several parts of Africa, southern South America and East Antarctica. The season was appreciably colder than average over the western USA, including Alaska, and south central Canada, a region extending northward from central India, northern Australia and West Antarctica.

The seasonal-average temperatures over sea were similar in their pattern of differences from the 1991-2020 average to that seen for May 2023.

The seasonal average temperature for Europe from March to May 2023 was 0.36°C above the 1991-2020 climatological average for the season. Although the spring of 2023 was warmer than the previous three springs, twelve years from 1989 onwards had warmer springs than 2023, for Europe as a whole.

below average over Australia and West Antarctica, and to a lesser extent over several other land areas.

Averaging over twelve-month periods smooths out shorter-term variations in regional- and global-average temperatures. Globally, the last 12-month period was 0.33°C  warmer than the 1991-2020 average. This twelve-month average anomaly is well below the 0.46°C average for the twelve-month periods ending in September 2016 and May and June 2020, the three warmest periods in this data record. The warmest calendar year is 2016, with a temperature of 0.44°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 was on a par with 2016, being cooler by less than 0.01°C, well below the spread among multiple datasets. The third and fourth warmest calendar years are 2019 and 2017, and their temperatures were 0.40°C and 0.34°C above average, respectively. 2022 was the fifth warmest on record at 0.30°C above average, but was only marginally warmer than the years 2015, 2018, and 2021.

The average global temperature for the twelve months to May 2023 is around 1.2°C above the 1850-1900 level. The way the average for the 1991-2020 reference period is related to that for 1850-1900 is outlined in the sidebar.

From October 2021 an updated approach is used to relate recent global temperatures to 1850-1900, a period taken to approximate the pre-industrial level. Following this approach, the value 0.88°C should be added to the value relative to the 1991-2020 reference period.  This estimate derives from the approach used in the WMO statements on “The state of the global climate” from the Preliminary Statement for 2021 onwards, which builds on the methods outlined in the IPCC AR6 WG1 report. Read more in About the data.

There is more variability in average European temperatures, but relatively dense observational coverage of the continent reduces uncertainty. The average for this latest twelve-month period is 1.0°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 is the warmest calendar year on record for Europe by a clear margin, with a temperature of 1.2°C above the 1991-2020 average.

There is general agreement among datasets that the period since 2015 is much warmer globally than any previous period. There is also agreement that global temperature has risen at an average rate close to 0.2°C per decade since the late 1970s. There is nevertheless still some spread between the datasets for recent years, such as for 2020 and 2021, and the annual average temperature anomalies for these years from ERA5 are generally higher than those from the five other datasets considered. The differences range from 0.01 to 0.07°C for 2016-2022. The range is 0.00 to 0.06°C if air temperature over sea is replaced by sea-surface temperature for ERA5 and the other dataset for which sea-surface temperature was not used by design. The remaining differences depend partly on the extent to which datasets represent the relatively warm conditions that have predominated over the Arctic and Antarctic during these years. Differences elsewhere in estimates of sea-surface temperature and surface air temperature over land have been further factors.

For the third time in 2023, Antarctic sea ice extent reached a record low monthly value for the time of year within the satellite data record, at 17% below average. Sea ice concentrations were most below-average in the Weddell, Bellingshausen, and northern Ross Seas while above-average concentrations continued to prevail in the Amundsen Sea.

Arctic sea ice extent was very close to average and almost identical to the value recorded in May 2022. Sea ice concentration anomalies were dominated by above-average concentrations in the Greenland Sea and below-average concentrations in the Barents Sea.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in May 2023 reached 13.0 million km 2, only 0.02 million km 2 (or 0.2%) below the 1991-2020 average  for May. This was virtually identical to the value recorded a year earlier, in May 2022. The two near-average values recorded in 2022 and 2023 come after a series of larger negative anomalies between 2014 and 2021. The lowest extent for May occurred in 2016, at 7% below average.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The spatial distribution of sea ice concentration anomalies in the Arctic in May 2023 was characterised by a mix of positive and negative anomalies across the various ocean sectors. As in March and April, concentrations were well above average in the Greenland Sea. This feature can be linked to strong southward sea ice drift through the Fram Strait (between Greenland and Svalbard) during the month. In turn, this strong sea ice drift was favoured by winds associated with a deep centre of low pressure over the central Arctic. Above-average concentrations also prevailed in the Beaufort, Chukchi, and Kara Seas. Below-average concentrations dominated in the Barents Sea while Hudson Bay, the Labrador Sea, and the Laptev Sea saw mixed concentration anomalies.

Antarctic sea ice extent remained once again well below average in May 2023, reaching 8.8 million km 2 on average,  1.8 million km 2 (17%) below the 1991-2020 average  for May. This was the lowest extent for May within the 45-year satellite dataset, markedly lower than the previous record of 13% below average observed in 1980 and 2019. This is the third month in 2023 so far (after January  and February ) that is marked by record-low Antarctic sea ice extent for the time of year.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The spatial distribution of sea ice concentration anomalies around Antarctica in May 2023 bore similarities to the distribution seen in April. It was dominated by well below-average concentrations in the Weddell and Bellingshausen Seas as well as to the north and northwest of the Ross Sea (north of the Victoria Land sector of East Antarctica). The below-average concentrations in the Weddell Sea were associated with well above-average surface air temperatures. In contrast, concentrations were above average in a broad sector covering the Amundsen Sea and the adjacent part of the Ross Sea as well as in a small area off the coast of Queen Maud Land (east of the Weddell Sea).

The contrast in concentration anomalies between the Bellingshausen and Amundsen-Ross Sea Sectors was already prominent in April. It can be traced to strong cyclonic activity over the Bellingshausen and Amundsen Seas, leading to winds predominantly blowing from the north over the Bellingshausen Sea (pushing the ice edge further south) and predominantly blowing from the south over the Amundsen Sea (pushing the ice edge further north).

The month was the joint fourth warmest April globally. Temperatures were above average over southwest Europe, with Spain and Portugal recording their highest ever April temperatures. Colder-than-average temperatures were experienced in a band stretching from the United Kingdom to southeast Europe.

It was much warmer than average over parts of Africa, in central Asia surrounding the Caspian Sea, in southeast Asia and Japan, and across northern North America. Conversely, it was colder than average over Alaska, Mongolia, the Arabian Peninsula, India, and Australia. It was colder than average over Alaska, Mongolia, the Arabian Peninsula, India, and Australia.

In April 2023, there was a sharp contrast in air temperatures across Europe. In southwest Europe, temperatures were well above the 1991-2020 reference period, with Spain and Portugal recording their highest temperatures in April; this extreme heat also extended over Morocco where it, too, had its hottest April temperature. On 27th April, a temperature of 38.8°C was observed in Córdoba, Spain. The conditions over the Iberian Peninsula are discussed further in the Highlight Box in the Summary for Hydrological Variables. Above-average temperatures were also experienced across the northern parts of the Nordic countries and in western Russia. In contrast, the month was colder than normal in a band from the United Kingdom to southeast Europe. This included Italy, where Easter was unusually cold, Romania, and Türkiye.

Further afield, it was warmer than average in northwest, central, and southern Africa, in central Asia surrounding the Caspian Sea, and in southeast Asia and Japan. Northern Canada, the northeast United States, Argentina, Chile, and most of Antarctica were also warmer than average. Conversely, it was much colder than average over Alaska, where Nome recorded its lowest April temperature. Mongolia was also colder than average following a bitterly cold winter. Central Canada, the Arabian Peninsula, India, southeast South America, and most of Australia – especially western areas – were below average too.

Above-average air temperatures occurred over the majority of the ocean surface. The most notable warmer-than-average conditions were found across the Weddell Sea, the northern Pacific, and in the Humboldt Current in the equatorial eastern Pacific, as the close to three years of below average La Niña temperatures came to an end. These increased temperatures in the region are an early sign for a potential transition to El Niño conditions. Temperatures were considerably below average to the southwest of southern Chile, to the west of North America, over the northwest North Atlantic off North America, and off eastern Australia. A few other smaller marine regions also had below-average air temperatures.

about 0.2°C cooler than April 2016, the warmest April on record

similar in temperature (within 0.02°C)  to the Aprils of 2017 and 2018.

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for April 2023 was  0.23°C higher than the 1991-2020 average.  The month was about 1.7°C cooler over Europe than April 2018, the warmest April in the record.

below average over some land areas, including central and western Canada, parts of the western United States, eastern South America, most of Australia and parts of Africa and Antarctica.

Antarctic sea ice extent remained well below average, at -19%, ranking third lowest for April in the satellite data record. Sea ice concentrations were much below-average in all sectors of the Southern Ocean except in the Amundsen and the adjacent part of the Ross Sea.

Arctic sea ice extent was 3% below average, ranking 10th lowest for April in the satellite data record. Below-average sea ice concentrations prevailed in most sectors of the Arctic Ocean, except in the Greenland Sea where they were well above average, as in the previous month.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in April 2023 reached 14.0 million km 2, 0.4 million km 2 (or 3%) below the 1991-2020 average for April. This was close to the value recorded a year earlier, in April 2022, and marked the 10th consecutive year with a negative anomaly at this time of year. The April 2023 extent ranks 10th lowest for April in the satellite record, which starts in 1979, and is virtually tied with April 2004, 2007, and 2015. The lowest extent for April occurred in 2019, at 6% below average.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The spatial distribution of sea ice concentration anomalies in the Arctic in April 2023 remained relatively similar to those seen in March, although the magnitude of the anomalies (negative and positive) was generally smaller. Below-average concentrations prevailed in most marginal seas of the Arctic Ocean, including the Barents Sea, the Sea of Okhotsk, and the Labrador Sea. The Bering Sea saw mixed anomalies, negative in the south, positive in the north. The Greenland Sea continued to stand out with above-average concentrations. Sea ice concentrations were close to average along the eastern coast of Svalbard after being consistently below average since June 2022.

As in previous months, Antarctic sea ice extent remained well below average in April 2023, reaching 5.9 million km 2 on average, 1.4 million km 2 (19%) below the 1991-2020 average for April. This was the third-lowest extent for April within the 45-year satellite dataset, coming after record or near-record low extents in January, February, and March. It is noteworthy that the lowest extent for April occurred at the very beginning of the satellite data record, in 1980. The second-lowest extent for April occurred more recently, in 2017, and was followed by other large negative anomalies in 2018, 2019, and 2022.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

Sea ice concentration anomalies in April 2023 were dominated by below-average concentrations in most ocean sectors, including the Weddell and Bellingshausen Seas (on both sides of the Antarctic Peninsula) and most areas along the coast of East Antarctica. The main exceptions were the Amundsen Sea and the adjacent part of the Ross Sea, where above-average concentrations prevailed. The below-average concentrations in the Weddell Sea were associated with well above-average surface air temperatures in this ocean sector.

The contrast in concentration anomalies between the Bellingshausen and Amundsen-Ross Sea Sectors is a recurring pattern and was already prominent from August to November 2022. It is typically associated with strong cyclonic activity in the Amundsen Sea, leading to winds predominantly blowing from the north over the Bellingshausen Sea (pushing the ice edge further south) and predominantly blowing from the south over the Amundsen Sea (pushing the ice edge further north).

March of 2023 was jointly the second warmest March globally. While temperatures were above average over southern and central Europe, over most of northern Europe temperatures fell below average.

On a global scale, it was much warmer than average over a vast swathe of land covering North Africa, south-western Russia and most of Asia, where many new high temperatures records for March were set. Much above-average temperatures also occurred over north-eastern North America, Argentina and neighbouring countries, as well as across a large part of Australia and coastal Antarctica. Conversely, it was much colder than average over western and central North America.

Average air temperatures for March 2023 were above those of the 1991-2020 reference period over southern and central Europe, most so over south-western Russia. The month was colder than normal over most of northern Europe. Reykjavík, Iceland, had its coldest March for some decades, while   Tromsø in northern Norway experienced average daytime temperatures that were were at or below normal for all but the first three days of the month. In contrast, parts of Spain had a cold start to the month, but temperatures rose there to reach record or near-record values by the end of the month.

Further afield, it was much warmer than average over a vast swathe of land stretching from North Africa through central Asia to the Far East. Many new records for high temperature were set for the month of March, from Morocco to China and Japan. Much above-average temperatures, unprecedented for March in places, were also recorded over north-eastern North America, Argentina and neighbouring countries, a large part of Australia, and coastal Antarctica.

Conversely, it was much colder than average over central and western North America. Temperatures in Winnipeg, central Canada, remained below 0°C throughout the month, something reported to have been recorded only once before, in 1899. On the US West Coast, San Francisco had daily maximum temperatures in late March as low as any in records dating back to 1872.

Above-average air temperatures occurred over the majority of the ocean surface. In particular, warmer-than-average temperatures continued to develop over the equatorial eastern Pacific, as close to three years of below average La Niña temperatures came to an end. Temperatures were considerably below average to the west and southwest of southern Chile  and over the Greenland and Norwegian Seas. Particularly low temperatures over and to the north of the Denmark Strait can be linked with high sea-ice concentrations and the associated prevailing northerly flow. Several other marine regions also had below average air temperatures, but to a lesser degree or over a smaller area.

0.51°C  warmer than the 1991-2020 average for March

0.11°C cooler than March 2016, the warmest March on record

Similar in temperature (within 0.02°C)  to the Marches of 2017, 2019 and 2020.

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for March 2023 was  0.86°C higher than the 1991-2020 average,  but not one of the ten warmest Marches in the complete data record from 1940. The month was about 1.3°C cooler over Europe than March 2014, the warmest March in the record.

The European temperature anomaly map for February 2023 showed a large positive temperature anomaly in northern Italy. A similar but weaker feature is seen in March 2023. It is likely that these anomalies are overestimated in the ERA5 reanalysis. An internal investigation found that the issue is (in part) related to a mismatch between the actual elevation of surface temperature observations and the model orography, causing the observations to artificially increase the model temperature. This mismatch also leads to underestimated snow cover over the affected area which further amplifies the warming in the model. The resulting excessively large positive temperature anomalies in northern Italy affect ERA5 data from around 25th January 2023 onward. The issue is likely being exacerbated this year by the low snow conditions across the Alps. It is expected to resolve itself as the snow cover recedes. The ERA5 team continues to monitor the situation and will update this message if more information becomes available. It is unlikely that this issue will be corrected in the final version of ERA5 data. Finally, note that the problem is very localised and therefore does not invalidate the European temperature anomalies and rankings.

ERA5 reanalyses show a large soil moisture anomaly northwest of the Caspian Sea. This anomaly is not justified by the precipitation pattern and is only partly reflected in the temperature and relative humidity anomalies. Investigations are in progress to address and assess this signal.

Below average over some land areas, including central and western Canada, eastern South America, most of Australia and parts of Africa and Antarctica.

Averaging over twelve-month periods smooths out shorter-term variations in regional- and global-average temperatures. Globally, the last 12-month period was  0.31°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average. This twelve-month average anomaly is well below the 0.46°C average for the twelve-month periods ending in September 2016 and May and June 2020, the three warmest periods in this data record. The warmest calendar year is 2016, with a temperature of 0.44°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 was on a par with 2016, being cooler by less than 0.01°C, well below the spread among multiple datasets. The third and fourth warmest calendar years are 2019 and 2017, and their temperatures were 0.40°C and 0.34°C above average, respectively. 2022 was the fifth warmest on record at 0.30°C above average, but was only marginally warmer than the years 2015, 2018, and 2021.

The average global temperature for the twelve months to March 2023 is around 1.2°C above the 1850-1900 level. The way the average for the 1991-2020 reference period is related to that for 1850-1900 is outlined in the sidebar.

From October 2021 an updated approach is used to relate recent global temperatures to 1850-1900, a period taken to approximate the pre-industrial level. Following this approach, the value 0.88°C should be added to the value relative to the 1991-2020 reference period.  This estimate derives from the approach used in the WMO statements on “The state of the global climate” from the Preliminary Statement for 2021 onwards, which builds on the methods outlined in the IPCC AR6 WG1 report. Read more in About the data.

There is more variability in average European temperatures, but relatively dense observational coverage of the continent reduces uncertainty. The average for this latest twelve-month period is  1.00°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 is the warmest calendar year on record for Europe by a clear margin, with a temperature 1.2°C above the 1991-2020 average.

There is general agreement among datasets that the period since 2015  is much warmer globally than any previous period. There is also agreement that global temperature has risen at an average rate close to 0.2°C per decade since the late 1970s. There is nevertheless still some spread between the datasets for recent years, such as for 2020 and 2021, and the annual average temperature anomalies for these years from ERA5 are generally higher than those from the five other datasets considered. The differences range from 0.01 to 0.07°C for 2016-2022. The range is 0.00 to 0.06°C if air temperature over sea is replaced by sea-surface temperature for ERA5 and the other dataset for which sea-surface temperature was not used by design. The remaining differences depend partly on the extent to which datasets represent the relatively warm conditions that have predominated over the Arctic and Antarctic during these years. Differences elsewhere in estimates of sea-surface temperature and surface air temperature over land have been further factors.

The surface air temperature analysis homepage explains  more about the production and reliability of the values presented here.

Read more about longterm temperature changes for the globe, the Arctic and Europe in the Temperature indicator.

For key indicators, the 1981-2010 value is included when hovering over the 1991-2020 value.

Antarctic sea ice extent was the second lowest for March in the satellite data record, at 28% below average, following a record low extent in February. Sea ice concentrations were much below-average in all sectors of the Southern Ocean.

Arctic sea ice extent was 4% below average, ranking joint 4th lowest for March in the satellite data record, but also close to the three lowest extents. Contrasting with mostly below-average sea ice concentrations in other Arctic Ocean sectors, concentrations were well above average in the Greenland Sea.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in March 2023 reached 14.5 million km 2, 0.6 million km 2 (or 4%) below the 1991-2020 average for March. This value ranks 4th lowest for March in the satellite record, which starts in 1979, virtually tied with March 2006. It is also only slightly smaller in magnitude than the three lowest values (2015, 2017, and 2018). The daily sea ice extent reached its annual maximum in early March and has since begun decreasing with the onset of the melt season. Interestingly, both the total extent and the anomaly for March were virtually identical to what they were in February, reflecting very similar climatology for the two months.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

Sea ice concentration anomalies in the Arctic in March 2023 were dominated by below-average concentrations in most marginal seas of the Arctic Ocean, including the Barents Sea, the Svalbard region, the Bering Sea, the Sea of Okhotsk, and the Labrador Sea. The most notable exception was the Greenland Sea, which saw much above-average concentrations, something not seen in the previous month. In the same European Arctic sector, it is also worth noting that the negative concentration anomalies in the Barents Sea and near Svalbard, which had been a dominant feature in the previous months, were much less pronounced than in February. These differences between February and March 2023 can be linked to the atmospheric circulation that prevailed in March, characterised by a pattern of high pressure over Greenland and Baffin Bay and low pressure along the northern coast of Siberia. This pattern was associated with sustained southward winds over the Greenland Sea, leading to strong southward sea ice drift across the Fram Strait (between Greenland and Svalbard). The presence of above-average sea ice concentrations in the Greenland Sea also coincided with a band of much below-average surface air temperature anomalies extending northward towards the central Arctic Ocean.

In March 2023, the Antarctic sea ice extent reached 3.2 million km 2 on average, 1.2 million km 2 (28%) below the 1991-2020 average for March. This was the second-lowest extent for March within the 45-year satellite dataset, coming after a record low extent in February. The value for March 2023 was close to the value recorded in March 2022 (26% below average), marking the second year in a row with well below-average sea ice extent after the positive anomaly in February 2021. The lowest extent for March occurred in 2017, when it was 33% below average.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

Even though the sea ice cover began growing again after the February minimum, sea ice concentration anomalies around Antarctica in March 2023 showed relatively little change from February; they are remaining dominated by much below-average values in all sectors of the Southern Ocean, most notably in the Weddell, Bellingshausen, Amundsen, and Ross Sea. The bulk of the sea ice cover was still found in the Weddell Sea, with little sea ice present along East Antarctica and only sparse sea ice patches along West Antarctica.

The sea ice analysis page provides additional information about the nature, production and reliability of the data and information presented here.

The sea ice indicator page provides additional information about the long-term trends in both the Arctic and Antarctica.

The global average temperature for February 2023 was the fifth warmest globally. Most of Europe had above-average air temperatures, particularly northern Norway and Sweden, and the Svalbard region. Winter was the joint second warmest winter on record for Europe, with much above-average temperatures over eastern Europe and parts of northeastern Europe.

Above-average temperatures also occurred in the eastern United States, northern Russia and in Pakistan and India. Meanwhile, below-average temperatures were experienced across the Iberian Peninsula, Türkiye, the western United States, Canada, northeast Russia and northern Australia.

In February 2023, most areas of Europe had average air temperatures higher than those of the 1991-2020 reference period. The most notable warmer-than-average conditions were found in northern Norway and Sweden, across northwestern Russia in areas surrounding the Kara Sea, and in the Svalbard region. The United Kingdom also had its fifth warmest February in a record dating back to 1884. In contrast, colder-than-average monthly temperatures were found across the Iberian Peninsula, Türkiye, and parts of the Caucasus. Note that the area of northern Italy with large positive temperature anomalies is discussed further in the Highlight Box below.

Elsewhere, February 2023 had warmer-than-average temperatures across most of northwestern Eurasia, Pakistan, and India, with the latter having its warmest February in a record that extends back to 1901. Warm anomalies also occurred over central and northwest Africa and southern South America. Conversely, regions of colder-than-average temperatures were found across northern Canada, northeast Russia, northern South America, and in northeast and southern Africa. Furthermore, there were pronounced temperature contrasts across the United States and Australia. The United States had above-average and below-average temperatures in eastern and western areas, respectively, while Australia had warm anomalies in western areas and cold anomalies in northern areas.

Above-average marine air temperatures occurred over the northeast North Atlantic, most of the northern Pacific, the southwest South Atlantic, western South Pacific, and off South Africa. Temperatures were lower than average over the central tropical and northeast Pacific, as well as over a region off the coast of West Antarctica. The La Niña conditions continued to weaken. Temperatures were also below average across much of the Indian Ocean, off eastern Australia, and over the Labrador Sea.

0.29°C  warmer than the 1991-2020 average for February

0.40°C cooler than February 2016, the warmest February on record

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for February 2023 was  1.22°C higher than the 1991-2020 average.  The month was outside the top 10 warmest Februaries in the data record since 1979, about 2.75°C cooler than February 1990, the warmest February in the record.

The European temperature anomaly map for February 2023 shows large positive temperature anomalies in northern Italy. It is likely that these anomalies are overestimated in the ERA5 reanalysis. An internal investigation found that the issue is (in part) related to a mismatch between the actual elevation of surface temperature observations and the model orography, causing the observations to artificially increase the model temperature. This mismatch also leads to underestimated snow cover over the affected area which further amplifies the warming in the model. The resulting excessively large positive temperature anomalies in northern Italy affect ERA5 data from around 25th January 2023 onward. The issue is likely being exacerbated this year by the low snow conditions across the Alps. It is expected to resolve itself as the snow cover recedes during March and April. The ERA5 team is closely monitoring the situation and will update this message as more information becomes available. It is likely that this issue cannot be corrected in the final version of ERA5 data. Finally, note that the problem is very localised and therefore does not invalidate the European temperature anomalies and rankings.

ERA5 reanalyses show a large soil moisture anomaly northwest of the Caspian Sea. This anomaly is not justified by the precipitation pattern and is only partly reflected in the temperature and relative humidity anomalies. Investigations are in progress to address and assess this signal.

Across Europe, temperatures over land for the boreal winter of 2022/2023 were predominantly above the 1991-2020 average. They were much higher than average over eastern Europe and across the north of the Nordic countries. Temperatures were below average over parts of Norway, Sweden and western Russia, and over Iceland and southern Greenland.

Seasonal average temperatures across the United States reflected the pattern seen in February, with warm and cold conditions in the east and west, respectively. The season as a whole was also notably warmer than average over much of eastern Canada, Alaska, northern Africa, the Middle East, central Asia, southern South America, and parts of Antarctica. In contrast, the season was colder than average across northern South America, northeast Russia, and over northern and eastern Australia.

The seasonal-average temperatures over sea are similar to the pattern of differences from 1991-2020 as already seen for February 2023. However, the seasonal average shows the La Niña conditions more clearly, and a region of cold anomalies off the east coast of Brazil and warm anomalies over the Labrador Sea are found.

The average temperature for Europe from December 2022 to February 2023 was  1.44°C   above the 1991-2020 average for the season. This is the joint second warmest winter value on record for Europe. The winter of 2019/2020 was almost 1.4°C warmer, and this winter season was within 0.03°C of 2016 and 2007.

below average over some land areas, including a region of Canada, South America, Africa, Australia and Antarctica.

Read more in About the data.

There is more variability in average European temperatures, but relatively dense observational coverage of the continent reduces uncertainty. The average for this latest twelve-month period is  0.90°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 is the warmest calendar year on record for Europe by a clear margin, with a temperature 1.2°C above the 1991-2020 average.

Global and European monthly surface air temperature anomalies (ERA5, degrees C vs 1991-2020) covering the full series from 1979-01 through 2023-02: <ts></ts>

Antarctic sea ice reached its lowest monthly extent in the satellite data record, at 34% below the average for February, breaking the previous record of February 2017. Antarctic daily sea ice extent also reached an all-time minimum, surpassing the previous record set in February 2022.

Sea ice concentrations were much below-average in all sectors of the Southern Ocean. Arctic sea ice extent was 4% below average, ranking 2nd lowest for February in the satellite data record, jointly with February 2016 and 2017. Arctic sea ice concentrations were most below-average in the Barents Sea and Svalbard region.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in February 2023 reached 14.4 million km 2, 0.5 million km 2 (or 4%) below the 1991-2020 average for February. This value ranks joint 2nd lowest for February in the satellite record, which starts in 1979, on a par with February 2016 and February 2017. The lowest extent for February occurred in 2018 (6% below average).

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

Sea ice concentration anomalies in the Arctic in February 2023 were dominated by much below-average concentrations in the northern Barents Sea and around Svalbard and Franz Josef Land, a persistent feature since the autumn of 2022. February also saw below-average concentrations in the southern Barents Sea. Consistent with the low sea ice cover, this sector of the Arctic Ocean also experienced much above-average surface air temperatures in February 2023, with temperature more than 7°C above average in some areas. Below-average concentrations also prevailed along the coast of eastern Canada, around Newfoundland and Nova Scotia. Other Arctic sectors generally saw a mix of above- and below-average sea ice concentrations.

In February 2023, Antarctic sea ice extent reached 2.2 million km 2 on average, 1.1 million km 2 (34%) below the 1991-2020 average   for February. As was already the case in January, this was the lowest extent for February within the 45-year satellite dataset, significantly below the previous record of 2017 (28% below average). In addition, the Antarctic daily sea ice extent reached its lowest value on record in February 2023, breaking the 12-month old record from February 2022 (see Highlight Box).

While the new record minimum for February 2023 comes after several years with much below-average sea ice extents since 2017, it is noteworthy that Antarctic sea ice extent was very close to average in February 2020 and February 2021. This highlights the large year-to-year variability that has often characterised Antarctic sea ice in recent decades. Therefore, it remains to be seen if the current trends towards much below-average extent persists in the coming months and years.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

With sea ice extent being at a record low, little sea ice remained around the Antarctic continent in February, except in the western Weddell Sea. It is worth noting that sea ice is separate from the ice shelves (shown in light grey in the maps). Although both are floating ice, ice shelves are considerably thicker than sea ice and originate from glacier ice flowing into the ocean rather than from freezing ocean water. The record-low ice conditions in February 2023 refer only to the sea ice cover, not to the ice shelves. However, low sea ice conditions can have important implications for the stability of the ice shelves.

All sectors of the Southern Ocean saw much below-average sea ice concentrations. Anomalies along the coast of East Antarctica were generally confined to the immediate coastal areas or barely visible in sectors (e.g. the Atlantic sector) where very little sea ice persists in the climatology for February.

During the second half of February 2023, the Antarctic daily sea ice extent reached its annual minimum, setting a new record low within the 1979–2023 satellite dataset and surpassing the previous record set only 12 months earlier, in February 2022. This new record minimum is the result of several weeks at record low daily values for the time of year (since late December 2022). The record low daily sea ice extents seen in early 2023 are in sharp contrast with the conditions observed only two years earlier, in February 2021, when Antarctic sea ice extent was hovering at levels close to or above average. Following a marked shift in September 2021, Antarctic sea ice extent has since remained consistently well below average.

[1] In the time series of daily sea ice extent, the thick dark grey line represents the median daily sea ice extent during 1991‒2020 (climatological reference period); the darker grey-shaded envelope represents the difference between the 25th and 75th percentiles (or interquartile range) during 1991‒2020; the lighter grey-shaded envelope represents the difference between the 10th and 90th percentiles (or interdecile range) during 1991-2020; and the dashed grey lines represent the lowest and highest values recorded for each day of the year during 1979‒2022.

In addition to the sea ice extent time series for 2023, the figure also shows for comparison the time series for the previous two years (2021 and 2022) and for 2017, the year with the second-lowest monthly extent and third-lowest daily extent.

In Europe, January 2023 was the 3rd warmest January on record. Much of Europe had above-average air temperatures, including the Balkans and eastern Europe, where New Year's Day experienced record warmth; the Svalbard region also had prominent warm anomalies.

Above-average temperatures occurred in the eastern United States, Canada, Mexico and in a southeast-northwest band across western Russia. Below-average temperatures were experienced across Siberia, Afghanistan, Pakistan and neighbouring countries, as well as in Australia.

In January 2023, most of Europe experienced average air temperatures that were higher than the 1991-2020 reference period. In particular, the Balkans, eastern Europe, Finland, north-western Russia, and Svalbard region had the most notable warmer-than-average conditions. The month also began with record daily temperatures in Europe, as discussed further in the Highlight Box below. In contrast, colder-than-average monthly temperatures were found across the Iberian Peninsula, northern Africa, across Greenland and Iceland, in a region to the north of the Caspian Sea, and in northern Russia.

Elsewhere, January 2023 had some pronounced regions of above-average temperatures in the eastern United States, Canada, Mexico, and in a southeast-northwest band across western Russia. Southern South America, central, northern, and southernmost Africa, and parts of central Asia also experienced warmer-than-average conditions. Conversely, a broad region of well below-average temperatures was found in Siberia, where -62.1°C was recorded in Dzhalinda on 10th January. Afghanistan, Pakistan and neighbouring countries, and Australia also had below-average temperatures recorded in January, as did northern South America, part of southern Africa, most of Antarctica, and areas of the western United States.

Above-average marine air temperatures occurred over the Mediterranean, northwest North Atlantic, most of the northern Pacific, the southwest South Atlantic, western South Pacific, and off South Africa. Temperatures were lower than average over a large area covering the tropical and southern sub-tropical eastern Pacific, where La Niña conditions weakened but were still present, as well as over a region off the coast of West Antarctica. They were also below average across much of the Indian Ocean, over the South Atlantic off Brazil, off the western coastline of North America, and off eastern Australia.

0.25°C  warmer than the 1991-2020 average for January

0.33°C cooler than January 2020, the warmest January on record

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for January 2023 was 2.20°C  above the 1991-2020 average. The month was Europe’s third warmest January in the data record since 1979, about 0.45°C cooler than January 2020, the warmest January in the record.

below average over some land areas, including large regions in Canada, South America, Africa, Australia and Antarctica.

Averaging over twelve-month periods smooths out shorter-term variations in regional- and global-average temperatures. Globally, the last 12-month period was 0.30°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average. This twelve-month average anomaly is well below the 0.46°C average for the twelve-month periods ending in September 2016 and May and June 2020, the three warmest periods in this data record. The warmest calendar year is 2016, with a temperature of 0.44°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 was on a par with 2016, being cooler by less than 0.01°C, well below the spread among multiple datasets. The third and fourth warmest calendar years are 2019 and 2017, and their temperatures were 0.40°C and 0.34°C above average, respectively. 2022 was the fifth warmest on record at 0.30°C above average, but was only marginally warmer than the years 2015, 2018, and 2021.

The average global temperature for the twelve months to January 2023 is around 1.2°C above the 1850-1900 level. The way the average for the 1991-2020 reference period is related to that for 1850-1900 is outlined in the sidebar.

There is more variability in average European temperatures, but relatively dense observational coverage of the continent reduces uncertainty. The average for this latest twelve-month period is 0.99°C  above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 is the warmest calendar year on record for Europe by a clear margin, with a temperature of 1.2°C  above the 1991-2020 average. 2022 ranks as the second warmest year on record for Europe, a little warmer than 2014, 2015 and 2019, which had temperatures between 0.74 and 0.79°C above average.

From October 2021 an updated approach is used to relate recent global temperatures to 1850-1900, a period taken to approximate the pre-industrial level. Following this approach, the value 0.88°C should be added to the value relative to the 1991-2020 reference period. This estimate derives from the approach used in the WMO statements on “The state of the global climate” from the Preliminary Statement for 2021 onwards, which builds on the methods outlined in the IPCC AR6 WG1 report. Read more in About the data.

A south-westerly flow of air that had passed over relatively warm seas brought unusually high temperatures to much of Europe at the turn of the year. Accompanying föhn events contributed to the warmth in places. The average temperature across Europe peaked on New Year’s Day, with a value some 0.6°C higher than any previous daily January value in the full ERA5 data record back to 1940. Temperatures reached some 16°C higher than the 1991-2020 average over western Ukraine in the lee of the Carpathian mountains and 15°C above average over Poland, according to ERA5. The World Meteorological Organization reports that many national and local observational records were broken. A station in Warsaw, Poland, recorded a temperature of 18.9 °C, more than 5 °C higher than any temperature previously measured there in January. In contrast, temperatures were below average over southern Norway and central Sweden, by as much as 13°C.

We can go back just three years, to the winter of 2019/2020, for initial evidence as to how unusual this event was. That winter is the warmest on record for Europe by a substantial margin, with a January that was on average more than 0.4°C warmer than January 2023, as shown in this bulletin.

This figure showing the annual march of European-average temperatures exhibits no peak in temperature in January 2020 that comes close to the peak shown for 1 January 2023. The latter was a short-lived event, however: within a week the temperature had fallen to the median level for the period 1951-2018, before rising again. Moreover, the peaks in temperature recorded for 22 December 2019 and 2 February 2020 are commensurate with that of 1 January 2023. The temperature on 22 December 2019 was in fact 0.3°C higher than that on 1 January 2023, but this difference is smaller than the climatological drop in temperature from 22 December to 1 January. Conversely, 2 February 2022 was less than 0.2°C colder than 1 January 2023. This is of the order of the climatological difference in temperature between 1 January and 2 February. The latter is not known with precision because of the uncertainty introduced by limited sample size; the smoothed climatological curves shown in the figure should be regarded as indicative rather than precise.

Going further back in time requires that the rate of wintertime warming over Europe be taken into account. Warming in winter (and summer) is higher than in autumn and spring, at a little over 0.5°C per decade since 1979.  Maps of the trends can be found here. Prior to 1 January 2023 the warmest January day had been 10 January 2007, when the mean temperature was 5.0°C. As the most recent 16 years of climate warming equates to a 0.8°C temperature rise it may be concluded that the warm event of January 2023 is no more extreme, and perhaps slightly less extreme, than that of January 2007, if extremity is judged by the deviations from climatological values estimated separately for the two years in question. The maximum local temperature anomaly, relative to the 1991-2020 reference, is 13.5°C for 10 January 2007. This case was thus a little less extreme locally than 1 January 2023, but on the other hand did not exhibit a region of Europe that was as unusually cold as part of Scandinavia was on 1 January 2023.

The major cold winter events that have occurred over Europe from time to time must also be kept in mind. The most extreme of these have magnitudes that are about twice as large as the warm winter events discussed here. European-average temperatures are estimated by ERA5 to have fallen to -12°C or even -13°C in the coldest spells during the Januaries of 1942 and 1987, with anomalies reaching as low as -33°C locally.

There is general agreement among datasets that the period since 2015 is much warmer globally than any previous period. There is also agreement that global temperature has risen at an average rate close to 0.2°C per decade since the late 1970s. There is nevertheless still some spread between the datasets for recent years, such as for 2020 and 2021, and the annual average temperature anomalies for these years from ERA5 are generally higher than those from the five other datasets considered. The differences range from 0.02 to 0.08°C for 2016-2021. The range is 0.00 to 0.07°C if air temperature over sea is replaced by sea-surface temperature for ERA5 and the other dataset for which sea-surface temperature was not used by design. The remaining differences depend partly on the extent to which datasets represent the relatively warm conditions that have predominated over the Arctic and Antarctic during these years. Differences elsewhere in estimates of sea-surface temperature and surface air temperature over land have been further factors.

Antarctic sea ice extent reached its lowest value for January in the satellite dataset, at 31% below average, well below the previous record of January 2017. Below-average sea ice concentrations prevailed in all sectors of the Southern Ocean.

Arctic sea ice extent was 4% below average, ranking 3rd lowest for January in the satellite data record. Arctic sea ice concentrations were most below-average in the Barents Sea and Svalbard region.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in January 2023 reached 13.5 million km2, 0.6 million km2 (or 4%) below the 1991-2020 average for January. This value ranks 3rd lowest for January in the satellite record, which starts in 1979, well below the value recorded in January 2022 (-1% below average). This is a notable departure from the lower rankings seen throughout 2022. The lowest and second-lowest extents for January occurred in 2018 (-6%) and 2017 (-5%), respectively.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The map of sea ice concentration anomaly for January 2023 shows that, as in December, concentrations were most below-average in the northern Barents Sea and along the eastern coast of Svalbard. This sector of the Arctic Ocean experienced well above average surface air temperatures. The Kara Sea was fully ice-covered after a delayed freeze-up in December. Below-average concentrations also prevailed in the Labrador Sea. The southern parts of the Bering Sea and Sea of Okhotsk saw below-average concentrations while above-average concentrations prevailed in their northern part.

In January 2023, the Antarctic sea ice extent reached 3.8 million km2 on average, 1.7 million km2 (31%) below the 1991-2020 average for January. This is the lowest extent for January within the 44-year satellite dataset, significantly below the previous record of 2017 (-24%).  The low extent for January 2023 comes after several occurrences of record or near-record low extents over the course of 2022.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

With the austral summer well underway and as Antarctic sea ice is approaching its annual minimum extent (usually in February), the sea ice pack remains very fragmented. As in December, the map of sea ice concentration anomaly for January 2023 continues to show widespread below-average concentrations in virtually all sectors of the Southern Ocean. The largest patches with average or above-average sea ice concentrations were concentrated mainly in the Amundsen and Weddell Seas.

In Europe, 2022 finished up with December as the 7th warmest December on record. In December 2022, southern Europe had above-average air temperatures, while northern Europe experienced below-average air temperatures.

Globally, the 2022 calendar year ranks as the 5th warmest year on record. In December, above-average temperatures also occurred in eastern Canada, northern Africa, Siberia, and the Chukchi Peninsula. Meanwhile, below-average temperatures were experienced in western Canada, in northern parts of the contiguous United States, in central Eurasia and across eastern Australia.

In December 2022, European air temperatures, on average, were higher than for the 1991-2020 reference period. Spatially, however, a striking contrast was found, with the southern part of Europe experiencing the air temperatures that were higher than the 1991-2020 average. From the Iberian Peninsula and southern France across the Mediterranean into Italy and the Balkans, temperatures were well above average. Conversely, in northern Europe, air temperatures were below the long-term climatological values. The UK had cold conditions, as did Scandinavia and Iceland where Reykjavík recorded its lowest temperature since 1918. Conditions over much of Europe became extremely mild towards the end of the month.

Elsewhere, December 2022 was characterised by some pronounced regions of above-average temperatures in eastern Canada, northern Africa, Siberia, and the Chukchi Peninsula. Below-average temperatures were experienced in western Canada, in northern parts of the contiguous United States, in central Eurasia, and across eastern Australia. The cold spell that affected the eastern United States, however, is mostly not seen in the monthly maps which illustrate how individual weather events can be obscured in the monthly statistics.

Above-average marine air temperatures occurred over the Mediterranean, northern Pacific, central North Atlantic, most of the South Atlantic, western South Pacific, and over most of the ocean neighbouring Antarctica. Temperatures were lower than average over a large area covering the tropical and southern sub-tropical eastern Pacific, where La Niña conditions persisted, as well as over a region off the coast of West Antarctica. They were also below average in the northern North Atlantic, over the South Atlantic off Brazil, over parts of the western Pacific, off the western coastline of North America and over much of the Indian Ocean, west of Australia in particular.

0.27°C  warmer than the 1991-2020 average for December

0.27°C cooler than December 2019, the warmest December on record.

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for December 2022 was 0.89°C   above the 1991-2020 average. The month was Europe’s eighth warmest December in the data record since 1979, about 1.7°C cooler than December 2019, the warmest December in the record.

below average over some land areas, including large regions in Canada, South America, Africa, Australia and Antarctica.

Averaging over twelve-month periods smooths out shorter-term variations in regional- and global-average temperatures. Globally, 2022 was 0.30°C  warmer than the 1991-2020 average. This twelve-month average anomaly is well below the 0.46°C average for the twelve-month periods ending in September 2016 and May and June 2020, the three warmest periods in this data record. The warmest calendar year is 2016, with a temperature of 0.44°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 was on a par with 2016, being cooler by less than 0.01°C, well below the spread among multiple datasets. The third and fourth warmest calendar years are 2019 and 2017, and their temperatures were 0.40°C and 0.34°C above average, respectively. 2022 was the fifth warmest on record at 0.30°C above average, but was only marginally warmer than the years 2015, 2018, and 2021.

The average global temperature for the twelve months to December 2022 is around 1.2°C above the 1850-1900 level. The way the average for the 1991-2020 reference period is related to that for 1850-1900 is outlined in the sidebar.

There is more variability in average European temperatures, but relatively dense observational coverage of the continent reduces uncertainty. The average for this latest twelve-month period is 0.87°C  above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 is the warmest calendar year on record for Europe by a clear margin, with a temperature of 1.2°C  above the 1991-2020 average. 2022 ranks as the second warmest year on record for Europe, a little warmer than 2014, 2015 and 2019, which had temperatures between 0.74 and 0.79°C above average.

From October 2021 an updated approach is used to relate recent global temperatures to 1850-1900, a period taken to approximate the pre-industrial level. Following this approach, the value 0.88°C should be added to the value relative to the 1991-2020 reference period.  This estimate derives from the approach used in the WMO statements on “The state of the global climate” from the Preliminary Statement for 2021 onwards, which builds on the methods outlined in the IPCC AR6 WG1 report. Read more in About the data.

Based on data going back to 1950, record average surface air temperatures were observed for many parts of western Europe in 2022. This is seen by the dark red colours in the figure for surface percentiles for 2022. At a national level, this finding is confirmed in the ERA5 dataset, as presented in the ranking of near-surface air temperature for 2022 by country.

Provisionally, based on national data records, 2022 becomes the warmest year on record for France, Spain, and Italy, and despite some cooler conditions in December, for countries in northern Europe including Germany, the UK, and Ireland.

On the percentile map scale used here 'Warmest' means the warmest in the whole 1950-2022 dataset, 'Much warmer than average' means warmer than the 90th percentile of the monthly or seasonal temperature distribution during the 1991–2020 reference period; ‘Above average’ means warmer than the 66.6th percentile; ‘Near average’ means between the 33.3th and 66.6th percentiles; ‘Below average’ means colder than 33.3th percentile; ‘Much colder than average' means colder than the 10th percentile, 'Coldest' means the coldest in the whole 1950-2022 dataset''.

There is general agreement among datasets that the period since 2015  is much warmer globally than any previous period. There is also agreement that global temperature has risen at an average rate close to 0.2°C per decade since the late 1970s. There is nevertheless still some spread between the datasets for recent years, such as for 2020 and 2021, and the annual average temperature anomalies for these years from ERA5 are generally higher than those from the five other datasets considered. The differences range from 0.02 to 0.08°C for 2016-2021. The range is 0.00 to 0.07°C if air temperature over sea is replaced by sea-surface temperature for ERA5 and the other dataset for which sea-surface temperature was not used by design. The remaining differences depend partly on the extent to which datasets represent the relatively warm conditions that have predominated over the Arctic and Antarctic during these years. Differences elsewhere in estimates of sea-surface temperature and surface air temperature over land have been further factors.

The calculation of percentiles and rankings of the 2022 annual mean temperature is based on ERA5 2-meter temperature data for 1950-2022.

The ERA5 data for 1979-2022 come from the ERA5 monthly dataset available in the C3S Climate Data Store (CDS).

The ERA5 data for 1950-1978 are based on the final version of the ERA5 back-extension, which has been produced but has not been publicly released yet (only a preliminary version is available in the CDS).

The rankings are calculated using ERA5 data on a 0.25°×0.25° grid.

The country masks are based on shapefiles from Natural Earth (1:50m cultural v5.0.0). Corresponding gridded masks at a resolution similar to that of the ERA5 data are created using the regionmask python package.

The country masks are applied to the data to generate country-average area-weighted annual temperature time series for 1950-2022 for European countries, from which the rankings for 2022 are derived.

Only land grid points in the ERA5 land-sea mask are considered in the calculation.

Statistics are not calculated for small countries not resolved by the ERA5 land-sea mask or represented by only one grid point.

Data for Svalbard (not shown on the map) are included in the statistics for Norway.

In December, Arctic sea ice extent was 3% below average, ranking 8th lowest for December in the satellite record, in line with December values observed since 2015, but well above the minimum of 2016. Antarctic sea ice extent reached its joint second-lowest value for December in the 44-year satellite dataset, at 13% below average, following other record or near-record low values earlier in 2022. Below-average sea ice concentrations prevailed in all sectors of the Southern Ocean, including the Amundsen and Ross Seas, a marked contrast to previous months.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in December 2022 reached 12.2 million km2, 0.4 million km2 (or 3%) below the 1991-2020 average  for December. This value ranks 8th lowest for December in the satellite record, which starts in 1979, in line with the rankings found for October and November. The December 2022 extent remains relatively close to values within 3-5% below average frequently recorded since 2015, well above the minimum of 2016 (7% below average).

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The spatial distribution of sea ice concentration anomalies across the Arctic Ocean for December 2022 bears a lot of similarity with the distribution observed in November. Below-average concentrations primarily occurred in the northern Barents Sea (between Svalbard and Franz Josef Land), the western Kara Sea, and to a lesser extent the Chukchi Sea. Large fractions of the northern Barents Sea and western Kara Sea remained ice-free. Most other sectors of the Arctic Ocean saw average or slightly above-average concentrations, this despite large areas concurrently experiencing above-average surface air temperatures.

In December 2022, the Antarctic sea ice extent reached 9.6 million km2 on average, 1.4 million km2 (13%) below the 1991-2020 average   for December. This was the joint 2nd lowest extent for December within the 44-year satellite dataset, virtually identical to the value recorded for December 1979 (a relatively close value was also observed in December 1982). The low extent for December 2022 follows record or near-record low extents observed on several occasions in 2022 (e.g., February–March and June–August). It still remains well above the lowest December value recorded in 2016 (19% below average).

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

With the start of austral summer and as Antarctic sea ice is approaching its annual minimum extent (usually in February), the sea ice cover has become quite fragmented, with only few areas experiencing monthly concentrations above 90%. The map of sea ice concentration anomalies for December 2022 shows widespread below-average concentrations in virtually all sectors of the Southern Ocean, although often mixed with relatively small patches of above-average concentrations. One important feature that persisted from August to November was a broad area of above-average concentrations in the northern Amundsen-Ross Sea sector, off the coast of West Antarctica. In contrast, sea ice concentrations were mostly below average in this sector in December, especially in the Amundsen Sea, where coastal waters remained largely ice-free. Furthermore, as in previous months, sea ice concentrations in the Bellingshausen Sea were well below average, with most of the sector remaining free of ice. The position of the average ice edge for 1991-2020 indicates that such ice-free conditions in the Bellingshausen Sea are quite unusual.

On a global level, November was cooler globally than the Novembers of 2012 and 2015-2021, but warmer than all other Novembers. This month was nearly 0.2°C warmer globally than the 1991-2020 average for the month and just below 1.4°C warmer than average over Europe.

Europe experienced its third warmest autumn in 2022 at 1°C above normal. Temperatures were particularly high over the west of the continent.

As of November 2022, following a major change in hardware at ECMWF, the software used for the climate bulletin data processing chain has been updated. The update includes changes to the way the temperature time series are calculated, leading to differences of the order of 10 -4 for global and 10 -3 for European temperatures. This affects the non-significant digits in the time-series, but does not affect statements of any previous or current bulletin. To mark this change, the graphs produced from November 2022 onward are given a different version number. Read more here.

Temperatures for November 2022 were higher than the 1991-2020 average across most of Europe. They were well above average over the west, south-east and far north of the continent, and below average over part of European Russia. The Svalbard archipelago, Iceland and southern Norway saw the largest differences from long-term climatological values. Temperatures were also unusually mild over Greenland and the northern European seas.

Elsewhere November 2022 was characterized by some pronounced regions of below-average temperatures. These regions included the western USA and much of western Canada, parts of the Canadian and Siberian Arctic, and Antarctica.   Australia, observed the eighth-lowest countrywide average of daily maximum temperatures for November in an observational record dating back to 1910. Regions of above-average temperatures included Alaska and north-western Canada, the eastern seaboard of North America, northwestern Africa, southwestern and eastern Asia, central and southern Chile and Argentina, and New Zealand.

In addition to the mild temperatures over northen European seas, above-average marine air temperatures occurred most prominently over the western Mediterranean, northern Pacific, central North Atlantic, western South Pacific, and over most of the oceans neighbouring Antarctica. Temperatures were lower than average over a large area covering the tropical and southern sub-tropical eastern Pacific, where La Niña conditions persisted, as well as over a region off the coast of  West Antarctica. They were also below average east of Newfoundland, over parts of the western Pacific, off the western coastline of North America and over much of the Indian Ocean, west of Australia in particular.

0.16°C  warmer than the 1991-2020 average for November

close to 0.4°C cooler than November 2020, the warmest November on record.

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for November 2022 was  1.36°C  above the 1991-2020 average. The month was Europe’s fifth warmest November on record, about 0.4°C cooler than November 2015, the warmest November, and cooler also than the Novembers of 2020, 2009 and 2000.

Temperatures over land for the boreal autumn of 2022 were predominantly above the 1991-2020 average for the season. They were much higher than average over western Europe. The United Kingdom experienced its third warmest autumn in a data record extending back to 1884. Its warmer autumns were in 2006 and 2011. Temperatures were even more anomalous further south. Autumn temperatures for south-western France were higher than any in the ERA5 data record. The season was likewise warmer than usual over north-western Africa.

Seasonal average temperatures over Greenland were very much higher than the climatological average for the season, a reflection of exceptional temperatures over each of the last three months. The season as a whole was also notably warmer than average over much of Canada, the Middle East, eastern Asia, southern Chile and Argentina, and parts of Antarctica.

In the southern hemisphere, springtime temperatures were much below average over Australia in 2022. Other austral regions with markedly below-average seasonal temperatures include northern Chile and Argentina, Paraguay and southern Brazil, and much of Antarctica. Regions of below-average autumn temperature in the northern hemisphere were either of smaller area or had anomalies of smaller amplitude.

The seasonal-average temperatures over sea show much the same pattern of differences from 1991-2020 as already seen for November 2022, but the cold November anomalies east of Newfoundland, west of Australia, off the western coastline of North America and in the western Pacific are of smaller extent in the seasonal average.

The average temperature for Europe from September to November 2022 was 0.98 °C  above the 1991-2020 average for the season. This is the third warmest autumn value on record for Europe. The autumn of 2020 was almost 0.5°C warmer, and that of 2006 was warmer by less than 0.1°C.

below average over some land areas, including large regions in Canada, South America, Africa, Australia and Antarctica.
