Arctic sea ice extent was 3% below average, ranking 8th lowest for November in the satellite record and remaining well above the lowest November values recorded in 2016 and 2020. It was the fifth-lowest for November in the 44-year satellite data record, at 5% below average, close to November values recorded on four occasions since 2017.

As in previous months, sea ice concentrations around Antarctica displayed a marked contrast between much below-average concentrations in the Bellingshausen Sea and much above-average concentrations in the Amundsen and Ross Seas.

As of November 2022, following a major change in hardware at ECMWF, the software used for the climate bulletin data processing chain has been updated. To mark this change, all graphs   produced from November 2022 onward are given a different version number. Read more here.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in November 2022 reached 10.0 million km2, 0.3 million km2 (or 3%) below the 1991-2020 average   for November. This value ranks 8th lowest for November in the satellite record, which starts in 1979. The November 2022 extent remains well above the lowest values recorded over the past two decades, such as 13% below average in 2016 (lowest) and 10% below average in 2020 (second-lowest).

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The map of sea ice concentration anomalies for November 2022 shows below-average concentrations primarily in three areas: the northern Barents Sea (between Svalbard and Franz Josef Land), the western Kara Sea, and the Chukchi Sea, all of which remained largely ice-free. The absence of sea ice in the northern Barents Sea and western Kara Sea were associated with much above-average surface air temperature in these areas. Most other sectors of the Arctic Ocean saw average or slightly above-average concentrations, consistent with a mostly colder-than-average central Arctic.

In November 2022, Antarctic sea ice extent reached 15.6 million km2 on average, 0.8 million km2 (5%) below the 1991-2020 average for November. This was the 5th lowest extent for November within the 44-year satellite dataset. The value for November 2022 was also relatively close to the November values recorded in 2021 and during 2017–2019, and well above the minimum of 2016 (9% below average).

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The map of sea ice concentration anomalies around Antarctica for November 2022 shows concentrations most below-average in the eastern part of the Bellingshausen Sea (along the western side of the Antarctic Peninsula), in contrast with well above-average concentrations further west in the northern Amundsen-Ross Sea sector. This contrast between these two sectors of the Southern Ocean has persisted since August and has been favoured by strong cyclonic activity in the Amundsen Sea, leading to winds predominantly blowing from the north over the Bellingshausen Sea (pushing the ice edge further south) and predominantly blowing from the south over the Amundsen Sea (pushing the ice edge further north). Below-average concentrations prevailed in other ocean sectors in the northern Weddell Sea and along the coast of East Antarctica.

Europe had its warmest October in the record, with temperatures nearly 2°C above the 1991-2020 reference period. In western Europe a warm spell brought record daily temperatures and it was a record-warm October for Austria, Switzerland and France, as well as for large parts of Italy and Spain.

Beyond Europe, Canada experienced record warmth, and much warmer-than-average conditions also occurred in Greenland and Siberia. Meanwhile, the largest colder-than-average temperatures were found in Australia, far eastern Russia, and in parts of west Antarctica.

October 2022 saw temperatures above the 1991-2020 average across most of Europe, with certain parts of Europe having exceptional warmth on daily and monthly periods. The highlight box below provides further details. Only Iceland and Türkiye experienced temperatures slightly below average.

Based on data going back to 1979, record average surface air temperatures were observed for many parts of Austria, France, Germany, Italy, Spain, and Switzerland for October 2022. In particular, in western Europe, daily temperatures soared to record highs, with values exceeding 30°C in France and Spain, including the latest recorded date – on 16th October – of 30°C at Bordeaux. This resulted in the warmest ever October in France since records began; the average across France was 17.2°C. Austria and Switzerland also had their warmest Octobers on record, and Austria also reported its warmest October night nationally of 20.4°C. This is now recorded as the latest tropical night in Austria’s history, where a tropical night is defined as a day when temperatures do not fall below 20°C.

These warmer-than-average conditions resulted in a European-average temperature being nearly 2°C higher than the 1991-2020 October average. It was the southwesterly prevailing winds across western Europe that brought warmer-than-average air northwards.

Canada and western parts of the United States experienced above-average temperatures. In British Columbia, Canada, the temperatures were approximately 3°C warmer than average. Large parts of Greenland and Siberia had much above-average temperatures, although the Greenland anomalies were smaller than those witnessed in September. The Middle East, Iran, and the southern half of Africa also had warmer-than-average conditions. Antarctica saw regions of above- and below-average temperatures in the Atlantic/Indian and Pacific sectors, respectively.

Below-average temperatures occurred across most of Australia, and particularly in western Australia. The southeast United States, parts of Mexico, South America, northern Africa, pockets across Asia, and far eastern Russia also experienced colder-than-average conditions.

Near-surface air temperatures were mixed over the major ocean basins. Above-average temperatures occurred over the majority of the Atlantic Ocean, the Arctic Ocean, the northwestern Pacific, and in a band of the South Pacific stretching from northern Australia to southern South America. Marine air temperatures were lower than average over a large area covering the tropical and southern sub-tropical eastern Pacific, indicative of the continuing La Niña conditions, as well as in a region off the coast of West Antarctica. Mixed conditions were found across the Indian Ocean.

0.41°C  warmer than the 1991-2020 average

cooler by around 0.04°C than October 2019, the warmest October

it ranks within the three next warmest Octobers by differing only marginally from October 2015 and 2021 (by 0.01 and 0.03°C respectively).

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for October 2022 was 1.92°C   above the 1991-2020 average which is the warmest October in the record. This is about 0.5°C warmer than the next warmest October which occurred in 2020.

also below average over the Chukchi Sea, parts of the eastern North Pacific and a large part of the southeast Pacific.

Averaging over twelve-month periods smooths out shorter-term variations in regional- and global-average temperatures. Globally, the last twelve months were 0.32°C  warmer than the 1991-2020 average. This twelve-month average anomaly is well below the 0.46°C for the twelve-month periods ending in September 2016 and May and June 2020, the three warmest periods in this data record. The warmest calendar year is 2016, with a temperature of 0.44°C  above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 was on a par with 2016, being cooler by less than 0.01°C, well below the spread among multiple datasets. The third and fourth warmest calendar years are 2019 and 2017, and their temperatures were 0.40°C and 0.34°C above average, respectively. 2021 was the fifth warmest on record, but only marginally warmer than 2015 and 2018.

The average global temperature for the twelve months to October 2022 is around 1.2°C above the 1850-1900 level. The way the average for the 1991-2020 reference period is related to that for 1850-1900 is outlined in the sidebar.

From October 2021 an updated approach is used to relate recent global temperatures to 1850-1900, a period taken to approximate the pre-industrial level. Following this approach, the value 0.88°C should be added to the value relative to the 1991-2020 reference period.  This estimate derives from the approach used in the WMO statements on “The state of the global climate” from the Preliminary Statement for 2021 onwards, which builds on the methods outlined in the IPCC AR6 WG1 report. Read more in About the data.

There is more variability in average European temperatures, but relatively dense observational coverage of the continent reduces uncertainty. The average for this latest twelve-month period is 0.71°C   above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 is the warmest calendar year on record for Europe by a clear margin, with a temperature of 1.2°C above the 1991-2020 average.

There is general agreement among datasets that the period since 2015  is much warmer globally than any previous period. There is also agreement that global temperature has risen at an average rate close to 0.2°C per decade since the late 1970s. There is nevertheless still some spread between the datasets for recent years, such as for 2020 and 2021, and the annual average temperature anomalies for these years from ERA5 are generally higher than those from the five other datasets considered. The differences range from 0.02 to 0.08°C for 2016-2021. The range is 0.00 to 0.07°C if air temperature over sea is replaced by sea-surface temperature for ERA5 and the other dataset for which sea-surface temperature was not used by design. The remaining differences depend partly on the extent to which datasets represent the relatively warm conditions that have predominated over the Arctic and Antarctic during these years. Differences elsewhere in estimates of sea-surface temperature and surface air temperature over land have been further factors.

In October Arctic sea ice extent was 9% below average, ranking 8th lowest in the satellite record, well above the record October minimum from 2020. Two notable areas of above-average sea ice concentrations prevailed in the Siberian sector surrounded by well below-average concentrations.

In the Antarctic, the sea ice extent ranked 3rd lowest for October, jointly with October 2016, at 4% below average. As in August and September, sea ice concentrations remained well below average in the Bellingshausen Sea and well above average in the northern Amundsen and Ross Seas.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in October 2022 reached 7.1 million km2, 0.7 million km2 (or 9%) below the 1991-2020 average  for October. This value ranks 8th lowest for October in the satellite record, which starts in 1979. Although it continues a series of negative October anomalies observed since 2007, the value for October 2022 remains well above the record minimum from October 2020 (-27%).

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The map of sea ice concentration anomalies for October 2022 shows predominantly below-average concentrations across the various sectors of the Arctic Ocean. The most notable exceptions are two areas of above-average concentrations in the northern Laptev Sea and in the central Eastern Siberian Sea. It is noteworthy that the sea ice tongue in the central East Siberian Sea, connecting the central Artic with the eastern Siberian coast, was flanked to the east and west by two areas with well below-average concentrations. This feature has persisted since July and is the result of a combination of atmospheric and oceanic processes that have taken place over the past few months.

In October 2022, Antarctic sea ice extent reached 17.9 million km2 on average, 0.7 million km2 (4%) below the 1991-2020 average  for October. This value ranks joint 3rd lowest for October in the satellite data record, on a par with the value recorded for October 2016. The lowest October extent occurred in 1986, at about 6% below average.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The overall pattern of sea ice concentration anomalies around Antarctica for October 2022 continued to be dominated by below-average concentrations around the Antarctic Peninsula, in the Northern Weddell Sea, and further east along the coast of East Antarctica. Sea ice concentration were most below average in the Bellingshausen Sea, west of the Antarctic Peninsula, adjacent to a broad sector of above-average concentrations in the northern Amundsen and Ross Seas. This contrast in concentration anomalies between the Bellingshausen and Amundsen-Ross Sea Sectors was already prominent in August and September and is reflected in the map of surface air temperature anomalies. In October, it was further strengthened by strong  cyclonic activity  in the Amundsen Sea, leading to winds predominantly blowing from the north over the Bellingshausen Sea (pushing the ice edge further south) and predominantly blowing from the south over the Amundsen Sea (pushing the ice edge further north).

The European-average temperature for September of 2022 was nearly 0.4°C below the 1991-2020 period, with a band of below-average temperatures from central Europe to Russia. Furthermore, the global-average temperature was close to 0.3°C above the 1991-2020 period and the joint 4th warmest September with 2016.

Greenland had exceptional temperatures that reached more than 8°C above the monthly average in places, the warmest temperatures for September in the record.

In September 2022, the European-average temperature was below the 1991-2020 average. This was largely due to a region of below-average temperatures stretching from central Europe to Finland and Russia. Conversely, temperatures were generally above average in western Europe, with the Mediterranean region still reflecting the marine heatwave that occurred during the summer. The largest above-average temperatures in the wider European domain – and globally – were found in Greenland; see more in the highlight box below.

The western part of North America experienced above-average temperatures where daily September records were broken in Canada and the USA, with a record temperature of 41.7°C observed in Salt Lake City, Utah. From northern Africa across Eurasia and into China, there were warmer-than-average conditions. In Hong Kong, the highest recorded maximum temperature for September of 35.9°C was observed. Warm conditions were also seen in northern South America, southern Africa, and northern Australia. Antarctica saw regions of above and below average temperatures over and around the continent, as is often the case.

Below-average temperatures extended from eastern Europe across Siberia to the Sea of Okhotsk. An area of central South America, including southern Brazil, Paraguay, Uruguay, and northern Argentina had below-average temperatures. Most of Australia also experienced colder-than-average conditions.

Near-surface air temperatures were mixed over the major ocean basins. Above-average temperatures occurred over the North Atlantic Ocean to the east of Canada, in the western Mediterranean, in the northwestern Pacific, in a region of the South Pacific stretching from northern Australia to southern South America, and over the seas around parts of Antarctica. Marine air temperatures were lower than average over a large area covering the tropical and southern sub-tropical eastern Pacific, indicative of the continuing La Niña conditions, as well as in a region off the coast of West Antarctica. Mixed conditions were found across the South Atlantic and Indian Oceans.

Surface air temperatures over the centre of Greenland in September 2022 averaged more than 8°C higher than the 1991-2020 September average. The percentiles map shows that almost all of Greenland experienced average temperatures that were higher than any for September in the ERA5 temperature record from 1979 onwards. This was associated with prevailing winds on to Greenland from the south and south-west that had crossed regions that for the most part were also much warmer than average.

Although ERA5 temperatures are more uncertain over the Greenland plateau than over better observed parts of the world, the uncertainties are small compared with the extent to which climatogical average temperatures were exceeded in September 2022. Further evidence of extreme conditions comes from the US National Snow and Ice Data Center (NSIDC), which reports a temperature on the afternooon of 3 September from the Summit Station that was above 0°C, the first time this has been observed in the month of September since measurements began in 1989. Continued relatively high temperatures later in the month are evident from the estimates of unusual amounts of ice melt throughout September shown both by NSIDC and by Denmark’s Polar Portal.

Around mid-month of September 2022 the Arctic daily sea ice extent reached its 9 th lowest annual minimum, while the monthly average extent ranked 11 th lowest, remaining well above the lowest and second lowest extents observed for both minima in 2012 and 2020. Overall, Arctic sea ice was below average, however, there were two noteworthy areas of above average sea ice in the Siberian sector.

The Antarctic sea ice extent for September 2022 ranked outside the lowest five for any September month, at 3% below average. This is following several months of record and close-to-record low monthly extent.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in September 2022 reached 5.4 million km2, 0.7 million km 2 (or 11%) below the 1991-2020 average  for September. This is the month during which the annual minimum sea ice extent is generally reached in the Arctic (see more in box below). This value ranks 11 th lowest for September in the satellite record, which starts in 1979, and is close to the rankings for the last three months.

The 2022 annual daily minimum of Arctic sea ice extent was reached around mid-September and ranks 9th lowest for the OSI SAF and joint 10th lowest for the NSIDC satellite records. The curve of daily sea extent shows that September 2022, similarly to 2021, follows the lower end of the interdecile [1] range closely. Both this year’s daily and monthly minima for the Arctic are far from the lowest and second lowest on record, which occurred in 2012 and 2020 respectively.

[1] In the time series of daily sea ice extent, the thick dark grey line represents the median daily sea ice extent during 1991‒2020 (climatological reference period); the darker grey-shaded envelope represents the difference between the 25 th and 75 th percentiles (or interquartile range) during 1991‒2020; the lighter grey-shaded envelope represents the difference between the 10 th and 90 th percentiles (or interdecile range) during 1991-2020; and the dashed grey lines represent the lowest and highest values recorded for each day of the year during 1979‒2022.

In addition to the sea ice extent time series for 2022, the figure also shows for comparison the time series for the previous year (2021) and for 2020 and 2012, the years with the second-lowest and lowest daily Arctic sea ice extent at the time of the minimum.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

In contrast to previous months, the map of sea ice concentration anomalies for September 2022 shows predominantly negative anomalies across the various sectors of the Arctic Ocean. Only a few regions continue to show clear positive anomalies, the most noteworthy being two areas along the central and eastern Siberian coast, one east of Severnaya Zemlya and one in the central Siberian Sea.

In September 2022, Antarctic sea ice extent reached 18.5 million km 2 on average, 0.6 million km 2 (3%) below the 1991-2020 average  for September. Though it follows a series of record or close-to-record low extents since early 2022, the value for September ranks outside the lowest five and is similar to September extents observed on several other occasions since the start of satellite record in 1979. The lowest September extent was observed in 1986, with a value 5% below average.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The overall pattern of Antarctic sea ice concentration anomalies for September 2022 shows a clear resemblance to the patterns seen in August. Below-average concentrations continued to prevail over a broad sector of the Southern Ocean stretching from the Bellingshausen Sea, west of the Antarctic Peninsula, where the largest negative anomalies occurred, eastward to the Western Pacific sector (around 100°E). As in the previous month, the largest positive anomalies occurred in a broad sector in the northern Ross and Amundsen Seas. Compared to August, along the East Antarctic coast a longer stretch (around 0°E to 60°E). saw positive sea ice anomalies poleward of the sea ice edge.

The contrast in sea ice concentration anomalies between the Bellingshausen Sea and the Ross/Amundsen Sea is well reflected in the surface air temperature anomalies, with much warmer-than-average conditions over the former and much colder-than-average conditions over the latter, while the correspondence between the anomalies is less clear along the East Antarctic coast.

On a global level, August 2022 was the joint 3rd warmest August on record with the average temperature being 0.3°C higher than the 1991-2020 average for the month. This month was similar to the values for August 2017 and 2021 and within about 0.1°C of the higher values reached in August 2016 and 2019.

The average temperature over Europe in 2022 was the highest on record for both August and summer (June – August) by substantial margins of 0.8°C over 2018 for August and 0.4°C over 2021 for summer.

European temperatures were the highest above average in the east of the continent in August, however, temperatures in the south-west were still well above average where they had been high also in June and July. Heatwaves were prevalent in this part of Europe as well as over central and eastern China for all three summer months. Additionally, North America also experienced one of its warmest summers.

Temperatures for August 2022 were higher than the 1991-2020 average across most of Europe. They were most above average in the far east of the continent, in a band stretching from the Barents and Kara seas to the Caucasus. Temperatures were also generally high in the west, but were not as extreme as experienced earlier this summer and in August 2003 and 2021.

A large part of China experienced temperatures that were much higher than average. Heatwave conditions were especially severe in the drought-affected Sichuan Basin. Western regions of Canada and the USA also experienced unusually high temperatures.  Antarctica saw regions of markedly above and below average temperature interspersed over and around the continent, as is often the case.

Flood-hit Pakistan continued to have below-average temperatures, in stark contrast to the unusually hot conditions it experienced in the pre-monsoon period. To the north and east, a large area of below-average temperature stretched from the Central Asian Republics, through central Russia and Mongolia to north-western China.

Near-surface air temperatures were mixed over parts of all the major ocean basins. Above-average temperatures occurred most prominently over the Barents and Kara seas, the western Mediterranean, the northern Pacific and Atlantic, a region of the South Pacific stretching from Australia to southern South America, and over the seas around parts of Antarctica. Marine air temperatures were lower than average over a large area covering the tropical and southern sub-tropical eastern Pacific, indicative of continuing La Niña conditions, as well as in a region off the coast of  West Antarctica.

0.30°C  warmer than the 1991-2020 average for August and the joint 3rd warmest August in this data record

cooler by 0.11°C than August 2016 and by 0.06°C than August 2019

on a par with (less than 0.02°C cooler than) August 2021 and August 2017

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for August 2022 was by far the highest on record for August, at 1.72°C higher than the 1991-2020 average.  August 2022 was 0.8°C warmer than the previous warmest August over Europe, which occurred in 2018. It was 0.9°C warmer than the next warmest Augusts, those of 2010, 2003 and 2015. 2003 and 2010 had higher local August extremes than 2022, but unusually high temperatures were particularly widespread across the continent in 2022, whereas some large regions of Europe were colder than average in August 2003 and 2010.

Temperatures over land for the boreal summer of 2022 were predominantly above the 1991-2020 average for the season. They were much higher than average over Europe, especially in the south-west and far north-east. The summer that France experienced in 2022 was second only to 2003 in terms of average temperature, although the number of days were that were classified as being part of a heatwave was higher in 2022 than 2003. England had its joint hottest summer, on a par with 2018, in terms of mean temperature.

North America, north-western Africa, China, Japan and eastern Russia also experienced summers that were for the most part appreciably warmer than average. Most of the Antarctic had a winter that was less cold than usual.

Regions where seasonal-mean temperatures were lower than average include Pakistan and coastal regions of Oman and Yemen, most of eastern Australia, much of Greenland, and a band from central Siberia to north-eastern China.

The seasonal-average temperatures over sea show much the same pattern of differences from 1991-2020 as already noted for August 2022.

The average temperature for Europe from June to August 2022 was about 1.34°C   above the 1991-2020 average for the season. This is almost 0.4°C higher than that recorded for the previous warmest European summer, which occurred just one year earlier, in 2021. The next warmest summers of 2010 and 2018 were 0.5°C cooler, and 2003 was 0.6°C cooler.

A long-term perspective on summer temperatures over the extratropical northen hemisphere is provided by time series of ERA5 temperatures back to 1950, and by the monthly climatological datasets that reach back to at least 1880, but which do not at the time of writing include 2022. The latter datasets show little long-term change in average summer temperatures prior to a warm period in the 1940s and 50s. Temperatures then in general decrease, before starting to increase at a relatively substantial rate from around 1980. Regional averages for the last four decades show a general rise that is linked to climate change on which is superimposed a variability from year to year that tends to be larger for smaller regions and is present throughout the period shown.

Summer 2022 shows up clearly as the warmest on record for eastern China and Europe, the two northern hemisphere regions that show consistently large anomalies across their whole extent (see above).  In both cases the differences between 2022 and earlier years are much larger than the typical differences among alternative datasets for a particular year. For the larger North American region and for the land areas of the extratropical northern hemisphere as a whole, the temperatures for summer 2022 are similar to those of the warmest previous summers, 2012 in the case of North America and 2021 for the extratropical hemisphere.

The remaining differences depend partly on the extent to which datasets represent the relatively warm conditions that have predominated over the Arctic and Antarctic during these years. Antarctic sea ice extent reached its second-lowest value for August in the 44-year satellite data record, at 4% below average, tied with the value recorded in August 2002. Sea ice concentrations around Antarctica were characterised by a marked contrast between much below-average concentrations in the Bellingshausen Sea and much above-average concentrations in the Amundsen and Ross Seas.

Arctic sea ice extent was 5% below average, ranking 12th lowest for August in the satellite record and remaining well above the lowest August values recorded over the past two decades.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in August 2022 reached 6.5 million km 2, 0.4 million km 2 (or 5%) below the 1991-2020 average   for August. This value ranks 12th lowest for August in the satellite record, which starts in 1979, and is similar to the rankings for June and July. As Arctic sea ice is approaching its annual minimum extent, the August 2022 extent remains well above the lowest values recorded over the past two decades, such as 21% below average in 2012 (lowest) and 19% below average in 2020 (second-lowest).

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

As in the previous two months, the map of sea ice concentration anomalies for August 2022 continued to display a mix of positive and negative anomalies across the various sectors of the Arctic Ocean. The Eurasian side of the Arctic Ocean, from the Svalbard region to the East Siberian Sea, was dominated overall by below-average concentrations; these were most pronounced north of the New Siberian Islands (between the Laptev and East Siberian Seas). As in July, an area of below-average concentrations also occurred in the central Arctic Ocean, close to the North Pole. In contrast, above-average concentrations prevailed in parts of the Greenland, Laptev and East Siberian Seas. On the North American side of the Arctic, below-average concentrations dominated in the Beaufort and Chukchi Seas and within the Canadian Arctic Archipelago.

The map of surface air temperature anomalies for August 2022 shows above-average temperature over most of the Barents, Kara and Laptev Seas. These areas are mostly ice-free on average at this time of year. Therefore, the warmer-than-average conditions are likely more a reflection of warm air being advected from the south than of the absence of underlying sea ice.

In August 2022, Antarctic sea ice extent reached 17.5 million km2 on average, 0.8 million km2 (4%) below the 1991-2020 average  for August. The August 2022 extent is virtually tied with the value recorded in August 2002 as the second lowest within the 44-year satellite dataset. It follows a series of much below-average extents observed since February 2022, including record low values for the time of year in June and July 2022, making a clear contrast with the above-average value observed a year earlier. The lowest August extent occurred much earlier in the satellite record, in 1986, with a value 5% below average.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The overall pattern of Antarctic sea ice concentration anomalies for August 2022 bears some resemblance to the patterns seen in June and July. Below-average concentrations continued to prevail over a broad sector of the Southern Ocean stretching from the Bellingshausen Sea, west of the Antarctic Peninsula, where the largest negative anomalies occurred, eastward to the Western Pacific sector (around 100°E). One marked difference from the previous month is the broader sector of above-average concentrations in the northern Ross and Amundsen Seas. The contrast in sea ice concentration anomalies between the Bellingshausen Sea and the Ross/Amundsen Sea is well reflected in the surface air temperature anomalies, with much warmer-than-average conditions over the former and much colder-than-average conditions over the latter.

The sea ice analysis page provides additional information about the nature, production and reliability of the data and information presented here.

The sea ice indicator page provides additional information about the long-term trends in both the Arctic and Antarctica.

Globally, July 2022 was one of three warmest Julys on record, close to 0.4°C above the 1991-2020 reference period, with much above average temperatures over large parts of land masses in the northern hemisphere. Below average temperatures observed along the western Indian Ocean, from the Horn of Africa to southern India, over much of central Asia, as well as over most of Australia. This was the joint sixth warmest July for Europe; heatwave brings local and national records to the western and northern parts of the continent.

In July 2022, temperatures were above the 1991-2020 average across much of Europe, with temperatures being most above average in southwestern and western Europe because of an intense, and in some parts prolonged, heatwave around mid-month. Below-average temperatures dominated in a band from Iceland, across western and central Scandinavia via the Baltic countries continuing as far as the Caspian Sea. Temperatures were also generally below average in Georgia and over much of Türkiye.

During a heatwave from mid-month onward numerous July records for maximum temperature were broken in Portugal,   western France and Ireland. 40°C  temperatures were seen for the first time on record in England. Here the previous national temperature record of 38.7°C  was met or broken at over 40 stations, with a maximum of 40.3°C  registered in Lincolnshire. National all-time records for daily maximum temperatures were also broken in Wales and Scotland. The heat travelled further north and east bringing very high temperatures across other countries, including Germany and parts of Scandinavia, with local July and all-time records broken at several locations in Sweden. Read more in the article “ Europe continued to swelter in July.”

Daily temperature anomalies varied throughout July, with several occurences of below-average temperatures in many parts of Europe, intermixed with predominantly above-average temperatures in the southwest. The most above-average daily temperatures are concurrent with the heatwave, which gripped parts of the continent from around mid-month onward.

Temperatures over northern hemisphere land areas were mostly above average, with especially large positive anomalies over central and northernmost North America and in large parts of Asia. Conversely, three large regions with below-average temperatures stand out: easternmost Russia, a region bordering the western Indian Ocean, from the Horn of Africa in the west, to southern India in the east, which saw much above average precipitation, as well as a central region, encompassing parts of western Siberia and central Asia.

In the southern hemisphere the most above-average temperatures were recorded in central south America and southern Africa, while most of Australia saw below-average temperatures.

Near-surface air temperatures were mixed over parts of all the major ocean basins. Above-average temperatures occurred most prominently over the Mediterranean Sea, the northern Pacific and in the Seas adjacent to the Antarctic Peninsula, while below-average marine air temperatures were located in the tropical and southern sub-tropical eastern Pacific, indicative of continuing La Niña conditions. Below-average temperatures were also experienced in the western southern Indian Ocean.

0.38°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average for July

one of the three warmest Julys on record,  marginally cooler than July 2019, and  marginally warmer than July 2016. These margins are so small that a clear ranking is not possible.

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for July 2022 was.72°C above the 1991-2020 average which was the joint sixth warmest July, with July 2006, in this data record. It was, however, 1.0°C colder than the warmest July, which occurred in 2010.

Averaging over twelve-month periods smooths out shorter-term variations in regional- and global-average temperatures. Globally, the last twelve months were  0.33°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average. This twelve-month average anomaly is well below the 0.46°C average for the twelve-month periods ending in September 2016 and May and June 2020, the three warmest periods in this data record. The warmest calendar year is 2016, with a temperature of 0.44°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 was on a par with 2016, being cooler by less than 0.01°C, well below the spread among multiple datasets. The third and fourth warmest calendar years are 2019 and 2017, and their temperatures were 0.40°C and 0.34°C above average, respectively. 2021 was the fifth warmest on record, but only marginally warmer than the years 2015 and 2018.

The average global temperature for the twelve months to July 2022 is around 1.2°C above the 1850-1900 level. The way the average for the 1991-2020 reference period is related to that for 1850-1900 is outlined in the sidebar.

From October 2021 an updated approach is used to relate recent global temperatures to 1850-1900, a period taken to approximate the pre-industrial level. Following this approach, the value 0.88°C should be added to the value relative to the 1991-2020 reference period.  This estimate derives from the approach used in the WMO statements on “The state of the global climate” from the Preliminary Statement for 2021 onwards, which builds on the methods outlined in the IPCC AR6 WG1 report. Read more in About the data.

There is more variability in average European temperatures, but relatively dense observational coverage of the continent reduces uncertainty. The average for this latest twelve-month period is 0.42°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 is the warmest calendar year on record for Europe by a clear margin, with a temperature of 1.2°C above the 1991-2020 average.

The spread in global averages from various temperature datasets has been reported previously to have been relatively large over recent years. An updated version of the dataset principally responsible for this spread was released in mid-December 2020. This version agrees much better with other datasets for the latest few years. This version does however show a larger warming since 1850-1900 than its predecessor, which has an influence on the ‘pre-industrial’ estimate as mentioned above. Read more in the Temperature indicator.

There is general agreement among datasets that the period from 2015 to 2020 is much warmer globally than any previous such period. There is also agreement that the global temperature has risen at an average rate close to 0.2°C per decade since the late 1970s. There is nevertheless still some spread between the datasets for recent years, such as for 2020, and the annual average temperature anomalies for these years from ERA5 are generally higher than those from the five other datasets considered. The differences range from 0.02 to 0.08°C for 2016-2020. The range is 0.00 to 0.07°C if air temperature over sea is replaced by sea-surface temperature for ERA5 and the other dataset for which sea-surface temperature was not used by design. The remaining differences depend partly on the extent to which datasets represent the relatively warm conditions that have predominated over the Arctic and Antarctic during these years. Differences elsewhere in estimates of sea-surface temperature and surface air temperature over land have been further factors.

The global average temperature for June 2022 was about 0.32ºC higher than the 1991-2020 average which makes it the third warmest June on record. Europe as a whole had its second warmest June on record at about 1.6ºC above average. Extreme temperatures occurred from Spain across France and into Italy; above-average temperatures were also experienced in China, Japan, and the USA Colder than average temperatures were seen over Greenland and most of South America.

In June 2022, temperatures were above the 1991-2020 average across most of Europe, with southern parts of the continent from the Iberian Peninsula across France and into Italy most affected. Daily maximum temperatures in Spain, France, and Italy soared above 40°C and the extreme heat exacerbated the ongoing drought conditions in the Po river basin. Numerous June temperature records were broken across France and Spain, with Biarritz, France, and San Sebastián, Spain, being two prime examples. This heat also extended across North Africa, where Tunisia equalled its monthly temperature record. Also, at Banak in northern Norway, a daily maximum temperature of 32.5°C was recorded, which if confirmed, would be a new June record for the county in which it is situated. Despite these record-breaking events, the monthly mean temperature for Europe was not the absolute warmest on record. In contrast, colder-than-average temperatures were experienced in western Russia, central Turkey, western areas of the Iberian Peninsula, and in Ireland.

Above-average temperatures were also found across Siberia and large parts of Asia, where heatwaves in central and northern China led to increased electricity demand. Temperatures of greater than 35°C were recorded for five days in a row in Tokyo, Japan, which is a record. In North America, high temperatures occurred in Texas, with Houston having its hottest June on record; San Antonio suffered from this extreme heat too. The Middle East also saw above-average temperatures.  Temperatures were furthermore much higher than average over large parts of Antarctica and over the Weddell sea. Other regions of above-average temperature included northern Canada, central Africa, and western Australia.

June 2022 had much colder than average temperatures over Greenland, most of South America and especially Argentina, southern Africa, and across eastern Australia. Temperatures were also below the 1991-2020 average over several other land regions, including north-western Africa and small pockets of eastern Asia.

Near-surface air temperatures were mixed over parts of all the major ocean basins. Above-average temperatures occurred most prominently over the northeast Pacific and South Atlantic, while below-average marine air temperatures were located in the tropical and southern sub-tropical eastern Pacific, indicative of continuing La Niña conditions. Below-average temperatures were also experienced in the southern Indian Ocean to the south of Madagascar and Australia.

0.31°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average for June

more than 0.05°C cooler than the warmest Junes, which were in 2019 and 2020

The European average temperature for June 2022 was 1.57°C above the 1991-2020 average which makes the month the second warmest June in this data record. It was, however, 0.3°C colder than the warmest June, which occurred in 2019.

Antarctic sea ice extent reached its lowest value for June in the 44-year satellite data record, at 9% below average, but only marginally lower than the value for June 2019. The Southern Ocean saw widespread areas of below-average sea ice concentration from the Amundsen and Bellingshausen Seas to the northern Weddell Sea and the Indian Ocean sector. Arctic sea ice extent was 3% below average, ranking 12th lowest for June in the satellite record, although it was relatively close to seven other lower-ranking June extents recorded since 2006.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in June 2022 reached 11.2 million km 2, 0.3 million km 2 (or 3%) below the 1991-2020 average for June. This value ranks 12 th lowest for June in the satellite record, which starts in 1979, and comes after a near-average extent in May 2022. While ranking 12 th lowest, the magnitude of the June 2022 anomaly is only slightly smaller than the values recorded in seven other years (from 5 th to 11 th lowest) since 2006. The lowest June extent occurred in 2016, with a value about 6% below average.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The map of average sea ice concentration for June 2022 highlights the rapid seasonal retreat of the sea ice cover in most sectors of the Arctic Ocean since May. However, the patterns of sea ice concentration anomalies are markedly different from those seen in May. Concentrations were most below-average in the northern Barents Sea (between Svalbard and Franz-Josef Land), in the Kara and Laptev Seas further east, as well as in most of Hudson Bay. The large negative anomalies in the northern Barents Sea are in stark contrast to the positive anomalies that prevailed in May in the same area. The positive anomalies in May were due to strong sea ice drift from the north toward the area early in the month, which later ceased and did not occur in June. In Baffin Bay and the southern Beaufort Sea, on the other hand, above-average sea ice concentrations dominated.

In June 2022, Antarctic sea ice extent reached 12.6 million km 2 on average, 1.2 million km 2 (9%) below the 1991-2020 average   for June. This is the lowest extent for June in the 44-year satellite data record but is only marginally lower than the value for June 2019 (second-lowest). It is noteworthy that 2019 and 2022 were separated by near-average June values in 2020 and 2022, highlighting the large interannual variability that has often characterised the Antarctic sea ice data record since 1979. The June 2022 anomaly continues a series of relatively large negative anomalies observed every month since November 2021.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The map of sea ice concentration anomalies for June 2022 shows below-average concentrations in most sectors of the Southern Ocean, from the Amundsen and Bellingshausen Seas along West Antarctica, to the northern Weddell Sea further east, and in the Indian Ocean sector along the coast of East Antarctica. Sea ice concentrations were most above average in the northern Ross Sea and in the Western Pacific sector. The contrast in sea ice concentration anomalies between the Weddell Sea and the Ross Sea is reflected in the surface air temperature anomalies, with much warmer-than-average conditions over the Weddell Sea and much colder-than-average conditions over most of the Ross Sea.

Globally, May was the fifth warmest on record, joint with May 2018 and 2021. In southwestern Europe, temperatures were much above average, associated with a heatwave which broke national and local records for daily maximum and minimum temperatures. Temperatures were also much above average in a band stretching southward from western Siberia across Central Asia to northern India and Pakistan, over the Horn of Africa, southern USA and Mexico, as well as Antarctica. Below average temperatures dominated in easternmost Europe, over much of South America, and parts of the Arctic and western Canada.

For Europe as a whole, spring temperatures were just below the 1991-2020 average. There were large regional differences, with parts of western Europe seeing much above average temperatures, while the central and eastern parts were below average. For Pakistan and north-west India, located within a larger region of unusual warmth, the season was characterized by long-lived heatwave conditions and record-breaking averages of maximum and minimum temperatures.

Temperatures in May 2022 varied considerably across Europe relative to their average values for 1991-2020. Record-breaking high values were experienced over the south-west of the continent, which came under the influence of winds from the Sahara that was hotter than average in the far west. France experienced its warmest May in records dating back to 1900, with numerous local records broken.  In Spain the city of Jaén experienced both its hottest ever May day and the highest daily minimum temperature ever recorded in May in mainland Spain. Portugal reported a heatwave in the first half of the month and an exceptionally hot night later in the month. The United Kingdom had a less extreme average temperature, the fifth highest on record for May, but the highest recorded average daily minimum temperature for the month, associated with higher-than-average cloud cover. In contrast, it was much colder than average over a region comprising the far east of Europe and parts of western Asia, where winds predominantly from the north-west were atypical for May.

May 2022 was colder than the 1991-2020 average over western and north-eastern Canada, Greenland and much of the Arctic Ocean, and over several other land regions, including parts of northern and southern Africa, southern and eastern Asia, most of South America and parts of southern Australia and East Antarctica.

Air temperatures were substantially above average over parts of all the major ocean basins, particularly over regions in the eastern North and South Pacific, around Japan, between Australia and New Zealand, and along much of the coast of Antarctica. Below-average marine air temperatures were located in the tropical and southern sub-tropical eastern Pacific, indicative of continuing La Niña conditions. It was especially cool off the coasts of Ecuador and Peru.

0.26°C  warmer than the 1991-2020 average for May

0.2°C cooler than the warmest May, which was in 2020

cooler than the Mays of 2016, 2017 and 2019.

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for May 2022 was about. 29°C higher than the 1991-2020 average.  The month was 1.8°C colder than the warmest May, which occurred in 2018.

As discussed in the main text, much of south-west Europe was affected by an early and prolonged heatwave in May 2022, with many local records for daily maximum and minimum temperaures broken across the region. Time series of the average daily maximum and minimum temperatures for the month of May from 1979 to 2022 are presented above for south-west Europe, indicating the average daily maximum temperature was the highest on record in 2022, ahead of 2015, 2017 and 2020. The average daily minimum temperature was also unusually high in 2022, on a par with previous record values recorded for 2020 and 1999.

The monthly average temperatures that are the main subject of this bulletin are the averages over all hours of the day. Values are included in the downloadable data. The daily average is usually approximated well by averaging the daily maximum and minimum temperatures.

The ERA5 temperatures over four quadrants of Europe are routinely monitored by the Copernicus Climate Change Service, and time series of four-month averages are reported alongside corresponding time series for other variables in the monthly hydrological bulletin. The south-west quadrant used above is defined as land bounded by longitudes 25°W and 15°E and latitudes 34°N and 45°N.

Temperatures for the boreal spring of 2022 were predominantly above the 1991-2020 average for the season. They were particularly above average over a region comprising eastern Iran, Pakistan, North-West India, Afghanistan, Tibet and Qinghai, and over parts of the Antarctic. Temperatures were also well above average over north-western and eastern Siberia, southern North America, eastern Africa and northern Australia. Cooler than average conditions were experienced largely over those regions noted above to be relatively cool for May alone.

Most of central and eastern Europe experienced a colder spring than the average for 1991-2020. However, France was significantly warmer than average, which had its joint-third warmest spring on record, with 2022 on a par with 2007 and cooler than 2011 and 2020. The United Kingdom recorded its fifth warmest spring. Northern Scandinavia was also relatively warm.

The seasonal-average temperatures over the ocean show much the same pattern of differences from 1991-2020 as already noted for May 2022.

The much above average temperatures experienced over Pakistan and north-west India in the boreal spring of 2022 occurred at a time of high climatological pre-monsoon temperatures. The monthly reports of the Pakistan and India Meteorological Departments document the occurences of heatwaves and numerous high-temperature records, both for individual stations and for country or regional averages of station values.

Average temperatures for a region encompassing Pakistan and north-west India were also the highest on record for this season according to ERA5. This is seen in the time series for daily maximum and minimum temperature anomalies shown above, as well as in time series for monthly mean temperatures. 2010 and 2004 were the years with the two next highest values of average daily maximum temperature; 2010 and jointly 2016 and 2018 were the years with the two next highest values of average daily minimum temperature.

2022 was nevertheless not the most extreme year for Pakistan in terms of absolute highs recorded for April and May. The highest temperature of 49.0°C  recorded for April 2022 is lower than the widely reported peak temperatures of 50.0°C for April 2017 and 50.2°C for April 2018. Moreover, the highest ever reported temperature for Pakistan, 53.7 °C, was recorded at Turbat on 28 May 2017.  This has been ratified by WMO as the fourth highest recorded temperature anywhere in the world.

The highest temperatures over the region in the ERA5 dataset are 50°C for April 2017, 49°C for April 2018, 47°C for April 2022, 54°C for May 2017 and 51°C for May 2022. The ERA5 values are based on hourly analyses that do not resolve local effects on peak temperatures.

The average temperature for Europe from March to May 2022 was about  0.14°C   above the 1991-2020 average for the season. Spring 2022 was cooler than seventeen of the springs from the year 2000 onwards, for Europe as a whole. It was nevertheless  0.3°C warmer than the spring of 2021, and warmer than most springs prior the year 2000.

also below average over the Chukchi Sea, parts of the eastern North Pacific and several oceanic areas in the southern hemisphere.

Read more in About the data.

There is more variability in average European temperatures, but values are more certain due to the relatively dense observational coverage of the continent. The average for the twelve-month period is 0.48°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 is the warmest calendar year on record for Europe by a clear margin, with a temperature 1.2°C above the 1991-2020 average.

The spread in global averages from various temperature datasets has been reported previously to have been relatively large over recent years. An updated version of the dataset principally responsible for this spread was released in mid-December 2020. This version agrees much better with other datasets for the latest few years. This version does however show a larger warming since 1850-1900 than its predecessor, which has an influence on the ‘pre-industrial’ estimate as mentioned above. Read more in the Temperature indicator.

There is general agreement among datasets that the period from 2015 to 2020 is much warmer globally than any previous such period. There is also agreement that the global temperature has risen at an average rate close to 0.2°C per decade since the late 1970s. There is nevertheless still some spread between the datasets for recent years, such as for 2020, and the annual average temperature anomalies for these years from ERA5 are generally higher than those from the five other datasets considered. The differences range from 0.02 to 0.08°C for 2016-2020. The range is 0.00 to 0.07°C if air temperature over sea is replaced by sea-surface temperature for ERA5 and the other dataset for which sea-surface temperature was not used by design. The remaining differences depend partly on the extent to which datasets represent the relatively warm conditions that have predominated over the Arctic and Antarctic during these years. Differences elsewhere in estimates of sea-surface temperature and surface air temperature over land have been further factors.

The average surface air temperature analysis homepage explains more about the production and reliability of the values presented here, but has yet to be updated to include the new information on dataset spread mentioned above.

Southwest Europe is here defined as all land for the region 25°W-15°E, 34°N-45°N.

The information for Pakistan and northern India is based on national reporting, as well as averages of the ERA5 dataset over all land for the region 20-37°N, 61-78°E.

Antarctic sea ice extent for May was 8% below the 1991-2020 average, ranking 6th lowest in the 44-year satellite record. Around Antarctica, sea ice concentration anomalies were most below average in the Bellingshausen and Amundsen Seas, in contrast to above-average concentrations in the Ross Sea. Arctic sea ice extent was very close to its 1991-2020 average, with generally above-average concentrations in the Atlantic sector and below-average concentrations in the Pacific sector and along the northern coast of Siberia.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in May 2022 reached 13.0 million km 2, which is very close to the  average extent in May for the 1991-2020 period. The May extent continues a series of relatively small anomalies observed since July 2021, with values well above the record minima from the past decade. The lowest May extent occurred in 2016, with a value of about 7% below the 1991-2020 average.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The seasonal retreat of the sea ice cover continued in all sectors of the Arctic Ocean. The map of sea ice concentration anomalies for May 2022 bears some resemblance to that of the previous month, with sea ice concentrations generally above average in the Atlantic sector, particularly in the northern Labrador Sea and Baffin Bay, in the Greenland Sea, and in the northern Barents Sea. On the other hand, concentrations were below average along the northern coast of Siberia (from the southern Barents Sea to the Laptev Sea) and in the Pacific sector (Sea of Okhotsk and Bering Sea), except for a small patch of above-average concentrations along the Chukchi Peninsula.

In May 2022, Antarctic sea ice extent reached 9.8 million km 2 on average,.9 million km 2 (8%) below the 1991-2020 average   for May. The monthly extent for May ranked 6th lowest in the 44-year satellite record. This is in contrast with the small positive anomaly recorded a year earlier, in May 2021. The value for May 2022 was close to that recorded for May 2018, which fell within a 3-year period (2017-2019) of markedly below-average extents. The two lowest May extents occurred in 1981 and 2019, with values around 13% below the 1991-2020 average.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The seasonal sea ice growth continued in May in all sectors of the Southern Ocean, particularly in the Ross and Weddell Seas. The map of sea ice concentration anomalies shows that concentrations were most below average in the Bellingshausen and Amundsen Seas but were above average farther west in the Ross Sea. This contrast between the eastern and western parts of the West Antarctic sector is a common feature of Antarctic sea ice anomalies. A typical driver behind these patterns is greater-than-average cyclonic weather conditions over the South Pacific sector of the Southern Ocean, as was the case in May 2022. These conditions lead to more offshore winds over the Ross Sea (pushing the sea ice northwards) and more onshore winds over the Bellingshausen-Amundsen Seas (pushing the sea ice southwards). Finally, as in April, sea ice concentrations remained below average in the Atlantic sector, including in the northern Weddell Sea. The rest of the Southern Ocean, along the coast of East Antarctica, saw mixed sea ice concentration anomalies.

April 2022 was globally around the sixth warmest April since records began. April temperatures over much of Europe were close to average values for 1991-2020. Temperatures were unusually high from north-eastern Africa across the Middle East to central and southern Asia. Pre-monsoon temperatures were extremely high over Pakistan and northern India.

Average temperatures for April 2022 differed little from the 1991-2020 April average over northern, western and southern Europe. They were colder than average over central and parts of eastern Europe and warmer than average over the far south-east of the continent.

An extensive region of much-above-average temperatures stretched from north-eastern Africa across the Middle East to central and southern Asia. Pre-monsoon temperatures were exceptionally high over Pakistan and northern India, following record highs in March; the heatwave led to critical water and power shortages as well as damage to crops. Heatwave conditions were also experienced in Egypt and Sudan. High spring temperatures were reported in the Central Asian Republics. Temperatures were also much higher than average over Greenland, easternmost Russia, and the Ross and Weddell sectors of Antarctica. Other regions of above-average temperature include the southern and south-western USA, Mexico, parts of the Far East, and northern Australia.

April 2022 was much colder than average over central and western Canada and much of the ice-covered Arctic Ocean. Temperatures were also below the 1991-2020 average over several other land regions, including north-western Africa, South East Asia, southernmost  Africa, south-western Australia and East Antarctica.

Air temperatures were substantially above average over parts of all the major ocean basins. Below-average marine air temperatures were located in the tropical and southern sub-tropical eastern Pacific, indicative of continuing La Niña conditions.

more than 0.2°C cooler than the warmest Aprils, which were in 2016 and 2020

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for April 2022 was  0.42°C lower than the 1991-2020 average.  The month was 2.4°C colder than the warmest April, which occurred in 2018, but 0.4°C warmer than April 2021.

also below average over the Chukchi Sea, parts of the eastern North Pacific and several oceanic areas in the southern hemisphere.

Averaging over twelve-month periods smooths out shorter-term variations in regional- and global-average temperatures. Globally, the last twelve months were  0.32°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average. This twelve-month average anomaly is well below the 0.46°C average for the twelve-month periods ending in September 2016 and May and June 2020, the three warmest periods in this data record. The warmest calendar year is 2016, with a temperature of 0.44°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 was on a par with 2016, being cooler by less than 0.01°C, well below the spread among multiple datasets. The third and fourth warmest calendar years are 2019 and 2017, and their temperatures were 0.40°C and 0.34°C above average, respectively. 2021 was the fifth warmest on record, but only marginally warmer than the years 2015 and 2018.

The average global temperature for the twelve months to April 2022 is between 1.1 and 1.2°C above the 1850-1900 level. The way the average for the 1991-2020 reference period is related to that for 1850-1900 is outlined in the sidebar.

From October 2021 an updated approach is used to relate recent global temperatures to 1850-1900, a period taken to approximate the pre-industrial level. Following this approach, the value 0.88°C should be added to the value relative to the 1991-2020 reference period.  This estimate derives from the approach used in the WMO statements on “The state of the global climate” from the Preliminary Statement for 2021 onwards, which builds on the methods outlined in the IPCC AR6 WG1 report. Read more in About the data.

Antarctic sea ice extent for April was 13% below the 1991-2020 average, ranking 5th lowest, jointly with 2006 and 2018, in the 44-year satellite record. Around Antarctica, the Ross, Amundsen, and northern Weddell Seas saw large areas of below-average sea ice concentration. Arctic sea ice extent was 2% below the 1991-2020 average, ranking outside the ten lowest extents.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in April 2022 reached 14.1 million km 2, 0.3 million km 2 (or 2%) below the 1991-2020 average for April. This was outside the 10 lowest extents for April in the satellite record, which starts in 1979. The April extent continues a series of relatively small negative anomalies observed since July 2021, with values well above the record minima from the past decade. The lowest April extent occurred in 2019, with a value of about 6% below average.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

Although the seasonal decrease in total Arctic sea ice extent continued in April, the map of sea ice concentrations shows that for some regions such as the area to the northeast of Svalbard and parts of the eastern Barents Sea, average sea ice concentrations in April were above those of March. As a result, the eastern Atlantic sector saw larger areas with above-average or close-to-average concentrations in April than in March. In other sectors of the Arctic Ocean, the spatial pattern of sea ice concentration anomalies was similar to that of the previous month, though the amplitudes of the anomalies were generally smaller. For example, concentrations were less below average in the Labrador Sea and less above-average in the Sea of Okhotsk than in March.

In April 2022, Antarctic sea ice extent reached 6.3 million km 2 on average, 0.9 million km 2 (13%) below the 1991-2020 average   for March, beginning its annual growth after reaching its annual minimum in February. As in February, the monthly extent for March ranked 2nd lowest in the 44-year satellite record, a ranking consistent with that provided by the NSIDC. The lowest March extent occurred in 2017 and the third-lowest in 2019, with values 33% and 22% below the 1991-2020 average, respectively.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The seasonal growth of sea ice continued in the Antarctic region; between March and April 2022, Antarctic sea ice concentrations increased in all sectors, most clearly in the Ross and Amundsen Seas. However, the map of sea ice concentration anomalies shows that concentrations remained well below average in the Ross, Amundsen, and Bellingshausen Seas. Below-average concentrations also prevailed in the northern Weddell Sea and further east along the coast of East Antarctica up to around 90°E. Directly eastward from there, was one of only two main areas with above-average concentrations, the other was recorded yet further east along the East Antarctic coast, close to the  Ross Sea.

The global average temperature for March 2022 was about 0.4ºC higher than the 1991-2020 average for March which makes it the fifth warmest on record. Europe as a whole was about 0.4ºC cooler than average in March 2022, which is the third coldest in the last 10 years. There was a contrast in temperature anomalies across Europe, with warmer-than-average conditions in the north and colder-than-average conditions in the south; these cold conditions extended into north Africa and across into Russia.

In March 2022, there was a large range of temperature anomalies across Europe. Warmer-than-average conditions were found in northern Europe, where the warmest temperatures occurred in northern Norway. In contrast, colder-than-average conditions were experienced in southern Europe. In particular, Greece and Turkey had the coldest anomalies.

The cold anomaly in southeastern Europe extended into north Africa, across the Middle East and Caucasus, and into Russia. Colder-than-average conditions were also found in northwest and southern Africa, across a wide area of South America, in the Rocky Mountains of the United States, and in central Canada. An interesting curved line of slightly colder-than-average anomalies in western Australia related to the passage of tropical cyclone Anika.

Higher than usual temperatures occurred in a region from Iran across India and into China. Notably, India had its warmest March in records which stretch back 122 years. Warmer-than-average conditions were also seen in northeastern Russia, eastern Antarctica, the Arctic, northern Australia, central Africa, along the Pacific coast of North America, and in the eastern United States.

Air temperatures varied over the Atlantic Ocean, apart from a warmer-than-average region off the eastern United States and a colder-than-average area in the Labrador Sea and to the south of Greenland. Conditions were mixed across the Pacific Ocean, with above-average temperatures mostly over the mid-latitude North and South Pacific. Warmer-than-average sea surface temperatures to the east of Australia also caused a bleaching event in the Great Barrier Reef. Below-average temperatures occurred along the coast of western North America and to the west of South America. Tropical and sub-tropical regions over the eastern Pacific were also colder than average, which related to the continuing La Niña conditions. There was a mixture of warm and cold anomalies over the Indian Ocean and Southern Ocean.

0.39°C  warmer than the 1991-2020 average for March

close to 0.2°C cooler than the warmest March, which was in 2016

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for March 2022 was  0.43°C lower than the 1991-2020 average.  The month was the third coldest March in the last 10 years, but it was much less extreme – around 2.4°C warmer – than the -2.71°C anomaly in March 2013.

The Arctic  experienced warmer-than-average temperatures in March, with the largest positive anomalies found in the eastern Atlantic sector from Svalbard to Novaya Zemlya. Across the Arctic, the temperature was 2.09°C above the 1991-2020 average, which made it the fourth warmest on record and about 0.5°C cooler than the warmest March in 2017. The Antarctic also saw an intense warm spell, with air temperatures well above average, and in Vostok, for example, the temperature reached -17.7° C which is the warmest March temperature measured there in its 65-year recorded history.

To read more about long-term temperature change in the Arctic, see the Arctic tab in the “ Temperature indicator ”.

Antarctic sea ice extent for March was 26% below the 1991-2020 average, ranking 2nd lowest in the 44-year satellite record, with large areas of below average sea ice concentration in the Ross, Amundsen, and northern Weddell Seas. Arctic sea ice extent was 3% below the 1991-2020 average, continuing the pattern of below average but not extremely low extents observed since July 2021.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in March 2022 reached 14.7 million km 2, 0.4 million km 2 (or 3%) below the 1991-2020 average for March. This was the 8th lowest extent for March in the satellite record. The March extent continues a series of relatively small negative anomalies observed since July 2021, with values well above the record minima from the past two decades. The lowest March extent occurred in 2018, with a value of about 5% below average. The daily extent has now begun its annual decrease after reaching its annual maximum in early March. Note that, according to the US National Snow and Ice Data Center (NSIDC), this maximum was reached a little earlier, in late February.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The map of sea ice concentration anomalies for February 2022 shows a mix of positive and negative values across the Atlantic and Pacific sectors of the Arctic, which are largely reflected in the surface temperature anomalies along the sea ice edge. The northern Labrador Sea continued to see above-average concentrations while below-average concentrations prevailed in the Gulf of St. Lawrence and off the northern coast of Newfoundland. The eastern Atlantic sector was dominated by below-average concentrations in the Barents Sea, east of Svalbard, and in the Denmark Strait, with only a small area of above-average concentrations occurring in the northern Greenland Sea. In the Pacific sector, concentrations were well below-average in the Sea of Okhotsk but mostly above-average in the Bering Sea.

In March 2022, Antarctic sea ice extent reached 3.2 million km 2 on average, 1.2 million km 2 (26%) below the 1991-2020 average   for March, beginning its annual growth after reaching its annual minimum in February. As in February, the monthly extent for March ranked 2nd lowest in the 44-year satellite record, a ranking consistent with that provided by the NSIDC. The lowest March extent occurred in 2017 and the third-lowest in 2019, with values 33% and 22% below the 1991-2020 average, respectively.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The map of sea ice concentration anomalies for the Antarctic region for March 2022 shows much below-average concentrations in the Ross and Amundsen Seas, both of which remained largely ice-free during the month. Below-average concentrations also prevailed in the northern Weddell Sea. Small patches of above-average concentrations occurred only in a few places around the continent, particularly in the eastern Weddell Sea.

The global average temperature for February 2022 was about 0.2ºC higher than the 1991-2020 average for February. This was well below February values for 2016, 2017 and 2020, and broadly similar to values for five other Februarys between 1998 and 2019. Temperatures were much above average over western and northern Russia and parts of the Arctic Ocean, and much below average over large parts of North America and China. Europe as a whole was more than 2ºC warmer than average, yet 1.6°C cooler than the warmest February (February 1990) in the record dating back to 1979.

Boreal winter temperatures in 2021/22 were much above average over south-eastern Europe and western Asia, and much below average over a large part of Canada. Winter for Europe as a whole was almost 1ºC warmer than the 1991-2020 average

Temperatures for February 2022 were generally higher than the 1991-2020 average across almost all of Europe. They were most above average over Russia west of the Urals. The principal exceptions were the below-average temperatures experienced in parts of Iceland and Svalbard.

Higher than usual temperatures occurred over a wider region than Europe, encompassing north-western Africa, much of the Middle East, western central Asia, northern Siberia, the Arctic Ocean, Sakhalin and Kamchatka. Temperatures were also notably above average over southern Alaska, western Canada and most of Antarctica. Although the heatwave that was prominent in January over northern Argentina and neighbouring countries receded in February, consequences in terms of wildfires and air pollution continued. Temperatures were mixed over Australia as a whole, but record high values for February were recorded in the south west of the country.

Below-average temperatures were most pronounced over northern Alaska, central and eastern Canada and Greenland. China and several neighbouring countries also experienced temperatures that reached well below average in places. This was also the case over central and eastern parts of North Africa and over parts of the USA from Texas northward.

Air temperatures were mostly above average over the Atlantic Ocean, apart from a region in the north. Conditions varied across the Pacific Ocean, with above-average temperatures over much of the mid-latitude North and South Pacific, and below-average temperatures along the coast of western North America, to the west of South America and to the south and east of Japan. Tropical and sub-tropical regions over the eastern Pacific were also cooler than average, which related to the current weakening La Niña conditions. There was a mixture of warm and cold anomalies over the Indian Ocean and Southern Ocean.

0.23°C  warmer than the 1991-2020 average for February

between 0.27 and 0.45°C cooler than the three warmest Februaries

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for February 2022 was about 2.4°C higher than the 1991-2020 average.  The month was 1.6°C cooler than February 1990, the warmest February on record, and cooler than five other Februaries since then.

Boreal winter temperatures for 2021/22 were most above average over south-eastern Europe and western Asia, over parts of the Arctic, especially west of Greenland, and over Kamchatka and Sakhalin. Other regions of above-average temperature include much of Antarctica, the south-eastern USA, north-western Africa, southern South America and western Australia.  It was  colder than average over northern Alaska, a large part of Canada and the Tibetan Plateau, and to a lesser extent over parts of all continents.

The seasonal-average temperatures over the oceans show much the same pattern of differences from 1991-2020 as already noted for February 2022.

The average temperature for Europe from December 2021 to February 2022 was about  0.9°C   above the 1991-2020 average for the season. Winter 2021/22 was cooler than six of the European winters in this 43-year data record and on a par with three others. Winter 2019/20 stands out as the warmest European winter, with a temperature 2.8°C higher than the 1991-2020 average.
