Globally, the last twelve months were  0.29°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average. This twelve-month average anomaly is well below the 0.46°C average for the twelve-month periods ending in September 2016 and May and June 2020, the three warmest periods in this data record. The warmest calendar year is 2016, with a temperature of 0.44°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 was on a par with 2016, being cooler by less than 0.01°C, well below the spread among multiple datasets. The third and fourth warmest calendar years are 2019 and 2017, and their temperatures were 0.40°C and 0.34°C above average, respectively. 2021 was the fifth warmest on record, but only marginally warmer than the years 2015 and 2018.

The average global temperature for the twelve months to February 2022 is between 1.1 and 1.2°C above the 1850-1900 level. The way the average for the 1991-2020 reference period is related to that for 1850-1900 is outlined in the sidebar.

From October 2021 an updated approach is used to relate recent global temperatures to 1850-1900, a period taken to approximate the pre-industrial level. Following this approach, the value 0.88°C should be added to the value relative to the 1991-2020 reference period.  This estimate derives from the approach used in the WMO statements on “The state of the global climate” from the Preliminary Statement for 2021 onwards, which builds on the methods outlined in the IPCC AR6 WG1 report. Read more in About the data.

There is more variability in average European temperatures, but values are more certain due to the relatively dense observational coverage of the continent. The average for the twelve-month period is 0.40°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 is the warmest calendar year on record for Europe by a clear margin, with a temperature 1.2°C above the 1991-2020 average.

The spread in global averages from various temperature datasets has been reported previously to have been relatively large over recent years. An updated version of the dataset principally responsible for this spread was released in mid-December 2020. This version agrees much better with other datasets for the latest few years. This version does however show a larger warming since 1850-1900 than its predecessor, which has an influence on the ‘pre-industrial’ estimate as mentioned above. Read more in the Temperature indicator.

There is general agreement among datasets that the period from 2015 to 2020 is much warmer globally than any previous such period. There is also agreement that the global temperature has risen at an average rate close to 0.2°C per decade since the late 1970s. There is nevertheless still some spread between the datasets for recent years, such as for 2020, and the annual average temperature anomalies for these years from ERA5 are generally higher than those from the five other datasets considered. The differences range from 0.02 to 0.08°C for 2016-2020. The range is 0.00 to 0.07°C if air temperature over sea is replaced by sea-surface temperature for ERA5 and the other dataset for which sea-surface temperature was not used by design. The remaining differences depend partly on the extent to which datasets represent the relatively warm conditions that have predominated over the Arctic and Antarctic during these years. Differences elsewhere in estimates of sea-surface temperature and surface air temperature over land have been further factors.

Antarctic sea ice extent was 27% below the 1991-2020 average for February, ranking 2nd lowest in this 44-year data record, with large areas of below average sea ice concentration in the Ross, western Amundsen, and northern Weddell Seas. During February, daily sea ice extent around Antarctica reached its lowest value on record. Arctic sea ice extent was just 2% below the 1991-2020 average, continuing the pattern of below average but not extremely low extents seen since July 2021.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in February 2022 reached 14.7 million km 2, 0.3 million km 2 (or 2%) below the 1991-2020 average for February. The situation that has prevailed since July, with below average, but far from extreme average Arctic sea ice extent, thus continues. The smallest February extent occurred in 2018, with a value close to 6% below average, so significantly smaller than that for 2022.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The map of sea ice concentration anomalies for February 2022 shows large variation across both the Atlantic and Pacific sectors of the Arctic. While the Labrador Sea continued to see above average concentrations, the Gulf of St. Lawrence and the seas to the north of Newfoundland saw below average concentrations. In the eastern Atlantic sector, the most notable feature were below average concentrations between the Barents and Kara Seas, while the seas around Svalbard generally saw above average concentrations. In the Pacific sector, there was a sharp contrast between well below average concentrations in the Sea of Okhotsk and above average concentrations in the Bering Sea.

In February 2022, Antarctic sea ice extent reached 2.5 million km 2 on average,.9 million km 2 (27%) below the 1991-2020 average   for February. This value ranks 2nd lowest in this 44-year satellite record. The previous two lowest extents were recorded in 2017 and 2018, reaching 28% and 25%, respectively, below the 1991-2020 average. Note that the U.S. National Snow and Ice Data Center (NSIDC) places February 2022 as the lowest February value in their data record, followed by 2017 and 2018.

During the latter half of February, Antarctic daily sea ice extent reached a new record low, bypassing the previous minimum reached in 2017.

Mid-February to early March is the time of year when Antarctic sea ice extent typically reaches its minimum daily value. The OSI SAF sea ice dataset used here, indicates a regrowth towards the end of February, while NSIDC shows a further reduction during the remainder of the month. This means that it is still not possible to confirm whether 2022’s daily and monthly minima have been reached.

During the melt season, satellite retrieval of sea ice is more challenging than during the colder seasons, leading to larger differences between datasets than for other times of the year. This is one of the key reasons why the timing and the ranking of the minimum may differ between datasets, as they do for February 2022.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The map of sea ice concentration for the Antarctic region for February 2022, shows that sea ice had decreased compared to January 2022 in most sectors, and most pronounced in the Ross Sea, which on average for February 2022 was ice free, bar a small area in its northernmost extension. The map of sea ice concentration anomalies shows that negative anomalies dominated around Antarctica, with values being particularly large in the northern Weddell, Ross and western Amundsen Seas, as well as along the coast westward of the Ross Sea. The largest continuous region of positive anomalies can be found in the eastern Amundsen and western Bellingshausen Seas.

January 2022 saw, in general, above-average temperatures across Europe. Warmer-than-average conditions occurred across Germany, eastern Europe, Scandinavia, the northern UK and Ireland, while colder-than-average conditions occurred in France, northeastern Spain, Greece, and Turkey. Temperatures were above average in parts of central Asia and Russia and a notable heatwave affected South America, whereas colder-than-average temperatures occurred across most parts of Canada, North Africa, and India.

In January 2022, there were generally above-average temperatures across Europe. From Germany, into eastern Europe and Russia, and across Scandinavia and the northern UK and Ireland, the temperatures were above the 1991-2020 average. For example, in Oslo, Norway, the highest ever January temperature of 12.5°C was recorded. Conversely, across most of France and northeastern Spain, the temperatures were below average. Southern Greece and Turkey were also affected by colder-than-average conditions.

Elsewhere, pronounced above-average temperatures occurred in a south-to-north region from central Asia to northern Russia and on the Kamchatka Peninsula. A notable heatwave in South America led to Argentina and Brazil having temperatures exceeding 40°C and the setting of more than 75 temperature records in Argentina. Western Greenland and a part of Antarctica also had above-average temperatures. Although for the month as a whole, western Australia only had a modest warm temperature anomaly above the 1991-2020 average, Onslow, Australia, matched its hottest ever recorded temperature of 50.7°C. Conversely, it was colder-than-average across eastern Canada and the eastern United States. Large parts of North Africa, southern Africa, Brazil, India, and southern Australia also experienced colder-than-average temperatures.

Air temperatures were mostly above average over the Atlantic Ocean except for a region in the central North Atlantic. The conditions varied across the Pacific Ocean, with above-average temperatures in much of the mid-latitude North and South Pacific, and below-average temperatures along the coast of western North America, to the west of South America and to the southeast of Japan. Tropical and sub-tropical regions in the eastern Pacific were also below average, related to the current La Niña conditions. In the Indian Ocean and over the extratropical parts of the Southern Ocean, there was a mixture of warm and cold temperature anomalies.

January 2022 Antarctic sea ice extent was 19% below the 1991-2020 average, ranking 4th lowest in this data record, with large areas of below average sea ice concentration in the Ross, Amundsen, and Weddell Seas. Arctic sea ice extent was just 1% below the 1991-2020 average, the largest January extent since 2009, yet smaller than any January extent prior to 2005. Hudson Bay was ice-covered during January, after seeing very low sea ice concentrations in previous months.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in January 2022 reached 14.0 million km 2, 0.1 million km 2 (or 1%) below the 1991-2020 average for January. It is the largest extent observed since 2009. However, it remains smaller than all January extents prior to 2005 in this satellite data record. The smallest January extent occurred in 2018, with a value close to 6% below average.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

During January 2022, sea ice continued to expand mainly within and around Hudson Bay, where it had been well below average in previous months. The map of sea ice concentration anomalies shows only few regions with very large positive or negative anomalies, most notably, a large patch of above average sea ice concentrations is seen for January between Svalbard and Franz Joseph Land. On the Atlantic side, above average concentrations continued  to dominate in the Labrador Sea, while the Greenland, Barents, and Kara Seas were characterized by a mixture of above and below average values. On the Pacific side, the largest negative anomalies were found just south of the Bering strait, while in the Sea of Okhotsk the sea ice concentrations were above average along the coast, but below average along the sea ice edge.

In January 2022, Antarctic sea ice extent reached 4.4 million km 2 on average, 1.0 million km 2 (19%) below the 1991-2020 average   for January. This value ranks 4th lowest in this 43-year satellite record. Lower January extents were recorded in 1988, 2019, and 2017, reaching 24% below the 1991-2020 average at its lowest in 2017. Note that the U.S. National Snow and Ice Data Center (NSIDC) places January 2022 as the 2nd lowest in their data record.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The map of sea ice concentration anomalies for the Antarctic region for January 2022 shows a large reduction in sea ice compared to December, especially in the Atlantic and Pacific sectors. Overall, below-average concentrations dominated around the continent, though small regions of above-average concentrations can be found in nearly all sectors. Sea ice concentrations were most above average in the inner Ross and Amundsen Seas, as well as immediately to the west of the Antarctic Peninsula and across the outer Weddell Sea. The largest negative anomalies are found in parts of the Bellingshausen and outer Ross Seas.

December 2021 saw a notable temperature contrast across Europe, with warm conditions in the west and south and cold conditions centred in the northeast. Europe had its coldest December since 2012, although several countries experienced record-breaking warmth at the end of the month. North America saw a large temperature difference between a warmer-than-average USA and a colder-then-average central and western Canada.

In December 2021, there was a striking contrast in temperature across Europe. Temperatures above the 1991-2020 average occurred in western and most of southern Europe from the Iberian Peninsula to the Black Sea. For example, in the United Kingdom, these above-average temperatures were associated with the setting of new daily temperature records of up to 16.5°C on New Year’s Eve. Conversely, across Scandinavia and northeast Europe, temperatures were below average, with Sweden having its first colder-than-average December since 2012. A temperature of –20.9°C in St. Petersburg,  Russia, also broke a daily record set in 1893.

Elsewhere, a notable temperature difference existed in North America between a warmer-than-average United States and a colder-than-average region in central and western Canada. This is exemplified by Texas having its hottest December since 1889 and Lytton, British Columbia, recording a minimum daily temperature of –25.4°C. Northeast Canada and Greenland had considerably above-average temperatures, as did parts of central Africa, central Asia, and western Australia. Conversely, it was colder-than-average across northern Eurasia and most of the Arctic, South Africa, and over and to the north of the western Himalayas.

Air temperatures were mostly above average over much of the Atlantic Ocean. In the mid-latitude North and South Pacific, conditions were mixed, with above-average temperatures near the international dateline and below-average temperatures particularly in the Gulf of Alaska and off western North America. It was also warm over parts of the Indian Ocean and the tropical western Pacific Ocean. The tropical and sub-tropical eastern Pacific were below average,  related to La Niña conditions. Temperatures were mixed over the extratropical parts of the southern hemisphere ocean.

Despite the colder-than-average conditions, several parts of western Europe did experience record-breaking warmth at the end of the month.

The December 2021 Arctic sea ice extent was just 2% below the 1991-2020 average, ranking 12 th  lowest in the 43-year satellite data record. Below average sea ice concentrations were recorded in the Hudson Bay and the Labrador Sea, while above average concentrations generally prevailed in the rest of the Arctic Ocean, especially in the Pacific and eastern Atlantic sectors.

Antarctic sea ice extent was 9% below the 1991-2020 average, ranking 6 th  lowest in this data record. Below average concentrations dominated in most sectors of the Southern Ocean.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in December 2021 reached 12.4 million km 2, 0.2 million km 2 (or 2%) below the 1991-2020 average for December. This year’s December extent ranks 12 th lowest for December in the 43-year satellite data record. The value is well above the low December extents of the past six years, which include the lowest on record in 2016.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

Sea ice has continued to grow in all sectors of the Arctic during December 2021. The map of sea ice concentration anomalies for the month highlights a contrast between negative anomalies in the Hudson Bay and Labrador Sea and mostly above average concentrations in the rest of the Arctic Ocean. The largest positive anomalies were in the northern Barents Sea and in the Kara Sea on the Atlantic side, and in the Chukchi Sea and Bering Strait on the Pacific side. The much below average concentrations in the Hudson Bay and Labrador Sea were the result of a late freeze-up in the autumn following much above average surface temperatures in north-eastern Canada. This unusual warmth persisted in December and was likely sustained in part - over ocean areas - by the reduced sea ice cover.

In December 2021, Antarctic sea ice extent reached 10.0 million km 2 on average, 1.0 million km 2 (9%) below the 1991-2020 average   for December. This value ranks 6th lowest in the 43-year satellite record. It is similar in magnitude to the low extents recorded in 2017-2019. In the early 1980s, two years recorded somewhat lower values. The only year with a much lower December sea ice extent was 2016, with a record low of 20% below the 1991-2020 average.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The map of sea ice concentration anomalies for the Antarctic region for December 2021 shows a large reduction in sea ice compared to November, especially in the Atlantic and Pacific sectors. Overall, below average concentrations dominated around the continent, though regions of above average concentrations can be found in all sectors. The largest continuous stretch of below average concentrations was across the Weddell Sea, while the largest negative anomalies were seen just west of the Antarctic Peninsula in the eastern Bellingshausen Sea and along the coastal parts of the Ross and Amundsen Seas. These two regions were separated by one of the few large areas of above average concentrations, the others being found in the northern Ross Sea and a region in the Indian Ocean sector

Globally, November 2021 is estimated to be the fifth warmest November on record. The November 2021 global average temperature was almost 0.2ºC cooler than the record-breaking November of 2020, but very close to temperatures of the other warmer Novembers: 2015, 2016 and 2019. Temperature over Europe was close overall to its 1991-2020 average, for autumn as a whole as well as November, but Ireland, Great Britain and southern Scandinavia experienced record or near-record autumn warmth. Regions where temperatures were well above average for both November and the season include most of North America, especially north-eastern Canada, a large part of Siberia and most of Africa and the Middle East. The month and season were cooler than average over the far east of Russia and Alaska, and much of central Asia and Australia.

The overall European temperature for November 2021 was close to the 1991-2020 average for the month, but there were pronounced regional differences in temperature. The month was warmer than average in the east and southeast, and in a zone extending westward to Ireland. Temperatures reached more than 3°C above average over western Russia. It was cooler than average over the Iberian Peninsula and France, and over the northernmost parts of the continent.

Elsewhere, temperatures were considerably above average over almost all of Africa, the Middle East, much of Siberia and Mongolia, western and central parts of the USA and almost all of Canada, especially in the northeast. Conversely, is was particularly cold over Alaska, the western Himalayas and the Xinjiang region of China. Although the northeast of China had close to average temperatures for the month as a whole, a cold wave brought record snowfalls during the month. Australia experienced its coolest November since 1999.

Air temperatures were predominantly above average over much of the Atlantic Ocean,  and over many central and eastern parts of the mid-latitude North and South Pacific. It was also unusually warm over parts of the Indian Ocean and the tropical western Pacific Ocean. The tropical and sub-tropical eastern Pacific continued to be below average, with the reinvigoration of La Niña conditions. Temperatures were also below average over the Gulf of Alaska and some extratropical parts of the southern hemisphere ocean.

0.35°C  warmer than the 1991-2020 average for November

close to 0.2°C cooler than November 2020, the warmest November

only a little cooler (≤0.06ºC) than the Novembers of 2015, 2016 and 2019.

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for November 2021 was about 0.5°C above the 1991-2020 average, but cooler than twelve of the Novembers since 1991. It was the fourth in a sequence of relatively cool months.

The boreal autumn temperatures for 2021 were predominantly above average, with below-average values over those regions noted for November alone. Temperatures were especially above average over north-eastern Canada, and to a lesser extent the western and central USA, much of Siberia and over and around a large part of Antarctica.

Although European temperatures were on average close to those of the 1991-2020 reference period, it was relatively warm over Ireland, Great Britain and southern Scandinavia, and to a lesser extent parts of southern, central and eastern Europe. As the 1991-2020 period is already much warmer than the long-term average, it is the warm differences that are closer to being record-breaking than cool differences of a similar magnitude.  Autumn 2021 was the warmest on record for Northern Ireland. Autumn temperatures over Norway ranged from more than 2°C above the 1991-2020 average in the south to more than 1°C below average in the north. Denmark reported a relatively high but not record-breaking average Autumn temperature.

The seasonal-average temperatures over the oceans show very much the same pattern of differences from 1991-2020 as already noted for November 2021.

The average 2021 temperature for Europe from September to November was 0.16°C   above the 1991-2020 average for the season. Autumn 2021 was cooler than thirteen of the autumns from the year 2000 onwards, for Europe as a whole. In the last decade, only Autumn 2016 was cooler. The contrast with Autumn 2020, the warmest on record, is striking.

also below average over parts of the eastern North Pacific and several oceanic areas in the southern hemisphere.

Averaging over twelve-month periods smooths out shorter-term variations in regional- and global-average temperatures. Globally, the twelve-month period to November 2021 was 0.27°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average. This twelve-month average anomaly is well below the 0.46°C average for the twelve-month periods ending in September 2016 and May and June 2020, the three warmest periods in this data record. The warmest calendar year is 2016, with a temperature 0.44°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 was on a par with 2016, being cooler by less than 0.01°C, well below the spread among multiple datasets. The third warmest calendar year is 2019; its temperature was 0.40°C above average.

The average global temperature for the twelve months to November 2021 is between 1.1 and 1.2°C above the 1850-1900 level. The way the average for the 1991-2020 reference period is related to that for 1850-1900 is outlined in the sidebar.

From October 2021 an updated approach is used to relate recent global temperatures to 1850-1900, a period taken to approximate the pre-industrial level. Following this approach, the value 0.88°C should be added to the value relative to the 1991-2020 reference period.  This estimate derives from the approach used in the WMO statements on “The state of the global climate” from the Preliminary Statement for 2021 onwards, which builds on the methods outlined in the IPCC AR6 WG1 report. Read more in About the data.

There is more variability in average European temperatures, but values are more certain due to the relatively dense observational coverage of the continent. The average for the twelve-month period to November 2021 is 0.30°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 is the warmest calendar year on record for Europe by a clear margin, with a temperature 1.2°C above the 1991-2020 average.

The spread in global averages from various temperature datasets has been reported previously to have been relatively large over recent years. An updated version of the dataset principally responsible for this spread was released in mid-December 2020. This version agrees much better with other datasets for the latest few years. This version does however show a larger warming since 1850-1900 than its predecessor, which has an influence on the ‘pre-industrial’ estimate as mentioned above. Read more in the Temperature indicator.

There is general agreement among datasets that the period from 2015 to 2020 is much warmer globally than any previous such period. There is also agreement that the global temperature has risen at an average rate close to 0.2°C per decade since the late 1970s. There is nevertheless still some spread between the datasets for recent years, such as for 2020, and the annual average temperature anomalies for these years from ERA5 are generally higher than those from the five other datasets considered. The differences range from 0.02 to 0.08°C for 2016-2020. The range is 0.00 to 0.07°C if air temperature over sea is replaced by sea-surface temperature for ERA5 and the other dataset for which sea-surface temperature was not used by design. The remaining differences depend partly on the extent to which datasets represent the relatively warm conditions that have predominated over the Arctic and Antarctic during these years. Differences elsewhere in estimates of sea-surface temperature and surface air temperature over land have been further factors.  The average surface air temperature analysis homepage explains more about the production and reliability of the values presented here, but has yet to be updated to include the new information on dataset spread mentioned above.

From January 2021 onward, the main reference period used is 1991-2020. What this means is described in a web article. For a transition period, the graphics and data relating to the previous reference period (1981-2010) can be found by using the tab-function above each graphic. For key indicators, the 1981-2010 value is included when hovering over the 1991-2020 value.

The November 2021 Arctic sea ice extent was just 2% below the 1991-2020 average, ranking 12th lowest in the 43-year satellite data record. Sea ice concentrations were below average in the northern Canadian sector, where Hudson Bay remained ice-free, while they were well above average in the Chukchi and Bering Seas.

Antarctic sea ice extent was 5% below the 1991-2020 average, ranking 4th lowest in this data record, resuming a series of negative anomalies interrupted in 2020.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in November 2021 reached 10.1 million km2, 0.2 million km2 (or 2%) below the 1991-2020 average  for November. This year’s November extent ranks 12th lowest for November in the 43-year satellite data record. The value is well above the low extents of November 2016 (record low) and 2020.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

Sea ice has continued to grow in the Arctic during November. Compared to the situation in October, the November map shows sea ice covering all of the central Arctic and most peripheral seas. Hudson Bay, which on average during 1991-2020 was partially covered by ice in November, remained ice-free. However, the map of sea ice concentration anomalies shows a large contrast between different sectors on opposite sides of the Arctic. While the Canadian/Atlantic side of the Arctic saw sea ice concentrations generally below average, the Chukchi and Bering Seas saw well above average concentrations. Sea ice along most of Arctic Siberia’s coastal regions was also more widespread than on average, especially compared to the last few years.

In November 2021, Antarctic sea ice extent reached 15.5 million km2 on average, 0.8 million km2 (5%) below the 1991-2020 average   for November. This value ranks 4th lowest in the 43-year satellite record. It is similar in magnitude to the low extents recorded in 2017-2019. The only year with much lower November sea ice extent was 2016, with a record low of 9% below the 1991-2020 average.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The map of sea ice concentration anomalies for the Antarctic region for November 2021 shows quite large differences around Antarctica. Sea ice concentrations were below average at the sea ice edge around the Antarctic Peninsula and eastward along the Weddell Sea. In the eastern Weddell Sea, off the coast of Queen Maud Land, the Weddell Sea polynya (an area of open water within the icepack), which has also been reported in several recent years, reopened. Farther east, sea ice concentrations in the Indian Ocean sector were generally above average in the western part; they were below average, in the eastern part along the ice edge but above average closer to the coast. The Ross and Amundsen Seas saw a mix of above and below average concentrations.

The  sea ice analysis page  provides additional information about the nature, production and reliability of the data and information presented here.

The  sea ice indicator page  provides additional information about the long-term trends in both the Arctic and Antarctica.

Globally, temperatures in October were 0.42°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average, and the month is estimated to be the third warmest October and only marginally cooler than October 2015 and 2019. European temperatures were most above average in the north and most below average in the southeast. Much warmer-than-average conditions were experienced in northern Canada, northern Russia, and eastern Antarctica, while colder-than-average conditions occurred in southern Africa, southern Australia, and western Antarctica.

October 2021 had marked contrasting temperature anomalies across northern and southeastern Europe. It was warmer than the 1991-2020 average over many northern regions, with Norway  - at 1.9°C above the October average - having its eighth warmest October in 120 years. The United Kingdom also reported temperatures 1.4°C above the long-term average  temperature for October. Conversely, it was colder than the 1991-2020 average in southeastern Europe. For example, Greece and countries surrounding the Black Sea, had temperatures below average. France, Italy, and parts of Germany also experienced cooler- than-average temperatures.

Warmer-than-average temperatures occurred over eastern North America, and in particular over northern Canada, where daily maximum temperature records were set and parts of Nunavut experienced temperatures of 7°C above average. Northern Russia, eastern Antarctica, and the Tibetan Plateau also had above-average temperatures.

Colder-than-average temperatures extended in a latitudinal band from Europe across the Caspian Sea and into western China. The southwest United States, southern Africa, southern and western Australia, and western Antarctica also had cold conditions.

Air temperatures were predominantly above average over the North Atlantic Ocean and over many central parts of the mid-latitude North and South Pacific. The tropical and sub-tropical eastern Pacific Ocean continued to be below average, with the possibility for a reemergence of La Niña conditions; the Gulf of Alaska and the oceanic region off eastern Antarctica were also below average. Conditions were mixed over the South Atlantic and Indian oceans.

It followed a relatively cool August and September.

also below average over the North Atlantic west of Ireland and several oceanic areas in the southern hemisphere.

The October 2021 Arctic sea ice extent was 7% below average, ranking 9th lowest in the 43-year satellite data record, yet well above the very low extent of October 2020. Most coastal sectors of the Arctic Ocean saw below average sea ice concentrations, with the notable exception of the Chukchi Sea.

Antarctic sea ice saw its joint sixth lowest October extent in the satellite record. Around Antarctica, sea ice concentration anomalies displayed large variability, with clearly positive or negative values in four distinct sectors of the Southern Ocean.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in October 2021 reached 7.33 million km 2, 0.5 million km 2 (or 7%) below the 1991-2020 average for October. October marks the start of the growing season for Arctic sea ice and the absolute anomaly for October is similar to that recorded in September. This indicates that the growth rate of sea ice from September to October 2021 was close to its average value for the period 1991-2020. This year’s October extent ranks 9 th lowest for September in the 43-year satellite data, closely preceded by 2017 and followed by 2009. The value remains well above the record low extent of October 2020.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

Compared to the map for sea ice concentrations for September 2021, the map for October 2021 shows sea ice starting to extend southward from the central Arctic and in particular over the seas along the northeastern coast of Siberia, where sea ice is becoming more and more widespread, covering seas that were largely ice-free in September. Nevertheless, the map of sea ice concentration anomalies shows large negative values in all coastal sectors of the Arctic Ocean, especially in the Canadian Archipelago and in the northern Kara and Laptev Seas.  The only exception is for parts of the Chukchi and East Siberian Seas where sea ice concentrations were slightly above average, as was the case also in September. The pattern of sea ice concentration anomalies for the Arctic in October closely mirrors (with opposite signs) the pattern of temperature anomalies, except for the East Siberian Sea. The same close correspondence also holds for Antarctic sea ice anomalies (see below).

In October 2021, Antarctic sea ice extent reached 18.1 million km 2 on average, 0.5 million km 2 (3%) below the 1991-2020 average for October. This value ranks joint 6 th lowest (with 2018) in the 43-year satellite record. This ranking is in stark contrast to the September ranking, which was close to the median of all Septembers in the data record. This large difference can be explained by a rapid decline in sea ice extent since the annual maximum in late August/early September, with daily values at the end of October being amongst the four lowest in the data record.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The map of sea ice concentration anomalies for the Antarctic region for October 2021 shows a distinct pattern of above- and below average anomalies divided into four main sectors. Sea ice concentrations were below average in the eastern Bellingshausen Sea and the Atlantic sector, as well as along the eastern part of East Antarctica (approximately 75°E-160°E). The remaining two sectors, the Amundsen and Ross Seas, and the western Indian Ocean sector (0°-75°E) saw predominantly above average sea ice concentrations.

September 2021 was one of the four warmest Septembers globally, along with those of 2020, 2019 and 2016. September 2021 is estimated to be the second warmest of these months, after September 2020, but only limited significance can be attached to rankings as the four warmest years differ in global-average temperature by less than 0.08ºC. Europe had near-record warmth in places, but was cooler than average in the east, and close to average overall. Regions where temperatures were much above average include central South America, north-western Africa and southern and eastern China.

September 2021 was a month with contrasting temperature anomalies over western and eastern Europe.  It was much warmer than the 1991-2020 average over most western regions. The United Kingdom reported its second warmest September on record, based on the countrywide average temperature for the month, while France reported its highest ever daily maximum September temperature for the country as a whole. It was correspondingly cooler than the 1991-2020 average in the east, though not cool enough to be record-breaking in a long-term context. Helsinki, for example, had a cooler September in 2021 than in any year from 1997 to 2020, but eleven earlier Septembers in the period from 1961 to 1996 were cooler still.

Elsewhere, below average temperatures occurred in a region extending eastward from eastern Europe to Lake Baikal. High August temperatures over Greenland were followed in September by much-below average temperatures over all but the far north-east of the country. It was also notably cooler than average over easternmost Russia.

September was warmer than average over most other land areas. This was particularly so over central regions of the USA and Canada, northwestern Africa, an arc from Iran through central Asian countries to southern China, parts of Siberia, drought-hit central South America and much of the Antarctic.

Air temperatures continued to be below average over much of the tropical and sub-tropical eastern Pacific Ocean. They were predominantly above average over central and eastern parts of the mid-latitude North Pacific, but below average east of Japan. Temperatures were also well above average over a region of the extratropical South Pacific east of the dateline. It was warmer than average over almost all of the Atlantic and Indian Oceans. Conditions were mixed elsewhere over the oceans of the southern hemisphere.

Globally, the latest three Septembers are the warmest three in the data record. September 2021 was:

0.40°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average for September.

estimated to be the second warmest September, after September 2020, but only limited significance can be attached to the rankings of the four warmest Septembers (which include 2016 and 2019) as the four differ by less than 0.08°C in global average temperature.

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for September 2021 was the coolest September since 2013, being 0.2°C below the 1991-2020 average. It followed a relatively cool August.

also below average over the North Atlantic west of Ireland and several oceanic areas in the southern hemisphere.

Averaging over twelve-month periods smooths out shorter-term variations in regional- and global-average temperatures. Globally, the twelve-month period to September 2021 was 0.28°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average. This twelve-month average anomaly is well below the 0.46°C average for the twelve-month periods ending in September 2016 and May and June 2020, the three warmest periods in this data record. The warmest calendar year is 2016, with a temperature 0.44°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 was on a par with 2016, being cooler by less than 0.01°C, well below the spread among multiple datasets. The third warmest calendar year is 2019; its temperature was 0.40°C above average.

0.82°C should be added to the value relative to the 1991-2020 reference period to relate recent global temperatures to the pre-industrial level defined in the IPCC Special Report on “Global Warming of 1.5°C”. The average global temperature for the twelve months to September 2021 is 1.1°C above that level.

There is more variability in average European temperatures, but values are more certain due to the relatively dense observational coverage of the continent. The average for the twelve-month period to September 2021 is 0.5°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 is the warmest calendar year on record for Europe by a clear margin, with a temperature 1.2°C above the 1991-2020 average.

This version does however show a larger warming since 1850-1900 than its predecessor, which has an influence on the ‘pre-industrial’ estimate as mentioned above. Read more in the Temperature indicator.

In September 2021, the monthly average sea ice extent reached its annual minimum in the Artic and its annual maximum in the Antarctic.

Arctic sea ice extent was 8% below average, ranking 12 th lowest in the 43-year satellite data record and fourth highest value since 2007, well above the very low extents of 2012, 2019 and 2020. On a regional scale, sea ice extent reached a record minimum in the Greenland Sea but a 15-year high in the Beaufort-Chukchi Sea sector.

Antarctic sea ice extent was slightly below average, after six consecutive months of above average values. Sea ice concentrations were above average around the Antarctic Peninsula and in the northern Weddell Sea and below average in the two adjacent ocean sectors.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in September 2021 reached its annual minimum at 5.6 million km 2, 0.5 million km 2 (or 8%) below the 1991-2020 average for September. This year’s September extent ranks 12 th lowest for September in the 43-year satellite data record and fourth highest value since 2007 (virtually tied with 2013). The value remains well above the record minimum of 2012 and above the low extents observed in 2019 and 2020.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The map of Arctic sea ice concentration anomalies for September 2021 shows large negative anomalies dominating from the Greenland Sea eastward up to the East Siberian Sea. In the Kara and East Siberian Sea sector, negative concentration anomalies to the south gave way to positive anomalies to the north, in the more central part of the Artic. Positive anomalies also dominated in the Chukchi Sea. The area of above-average sea ice cover in the eastern Kara Sea, already observed in previous months, survived the summer melt and continued to hinder transit along the Northern Sea Route.

One notable feature of Arctic sea ice cover in September 2021 was a record-low extent in the Greenland Sea, an area also characterised by much above-average surface temperatures. In contrast, on the near opposite side of the Arctic Ocean, sea ice extent in the Beaufort-Chukchi Sea sector reached its highest value in 15 years (since 2006). It is noteworthy that these two areas of the Arctic Ocean often – but not always – display sea ice extent anomalies of opposite signs, denoting among other factors the influence of atmospheric circulation patterns. This contrast was particularly prominent in 2012, the year with the lowest sea ice minimum for the Arctic as a whole.

In September 2021, Antarctic sea ice extent reached 18.9 million km 2 on average, only 0.3 million km 2 above the August average value. This small month-on-month increase can be explained by the fact that the daily Antarctic sea ice extent reached its annual maximum in late August/early September and has been slowly decreasing since then. The September average extent was 0.1 million km 2 (1%) below the 1991-2020 average for September, ending a streak of positive anomalies observed since March 2021. The September value ranks 21 st lowest (or 23 rd highest) in the 43-year satellite record and as such is close to the median extent observed during the period.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The map of sea ice concentration anomalies for the Antarctic region for September 2021 shows below-average concentrations in the vicinity of the northern Antarctic Peninsula and in the northern Weddell Sea, and above-average concentrations in the two adjacent sectors of the Southern Ocean (Amundsen-Bellingshausen Sea sector to the west, western Indian Ocean sector to the east). The pattern of anomalies in other sectors is more complex and often characterised by a mix of positive and negative values.

Globally, August 2021 was, with August 2017, the joint third warmest on record at a little over 0.3°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average. For Europe, August 2021 was near the 1991-2020 average, but with contrasting conditions across the continent: record-breaking maximum temperatures in Mediterranean countries, warmer-than-average temperatures in the east and generally below-average temperatures in the north. The average June-August temperature in this dataset for Europe was close to 1°C above the 1991-2020 average making it the warmest summer, though the previous warmest summers of 2010 and 2018 were only about 0.1°C cooler.

The temperature in August 2021 was close to the 1991-2020 average over Europe as a whole. There were, however, significant temperature variations across the continent, with southern and easternmost Europe experiencing warmer than average conditions, while northern Europe had colder than average conditions. During the heatwave in the south of Europe, a temperature of 48.8C was recorded on 11 August 2021 in Sicily, Italy. This temperature – if verified by the WMO – will be the highest temperature ever recorded in Europe. The heatwave, which was related to anticyclonic weather conditions, affected other countries including Spain, and follows extreme heat earlier in August in Greece and Turkey which contributed to the environmental conditions favourable for the devastating wildfires in the region.

Elsewhere, warmer than average conditions occurred from the north of the Black Sea right across Siberia, with the most anomalous temperatures found to the north of the Caspian Sea. Greenland had a large warm anomaly, and warmer than average conditions were also experienced over northeastern North America. Temperatures over north Africa were higher than normal, which were partly associated with the heatwave in southern Europe discussed earlier. Colder than average temperatures occurred to the north of Hudson Bay in Canada, across northern Alaska and the adjacent Beaufort Sea, in northeastern Mexico, and in an east-to-west band across Mongolia and China to the Korean Peninsula.

In the southern hemisphere, temperatures were above average across most of Australia and the largest anomalies were found in the north of the country. Over much of South America, temperatures were also warmer than average. Temperatures were much above average over eastern Antarctica, but it was colder than average over most of western Antarctica. Eastern Brazil and central southern Africa also had colder than average temperatures.

Air temperatures continued to be below average over large parts of the tropical and sub-tropical eastern Pacific Ocean. The north Pacific generally experienced above-average temperatures as did the extratropical North Atlantic, with conditions in the former termed a ‘ marine heatwave ’. Temperatures were generally warmer than average in the Indian Ocean and in the western Weddell Sea and to its immediate north over the South Atlantic Ocean, while unusually cold temperatures occurred over the Southern Ocean to the south of Africa. Conditions were mixed elsewhere over the  southern hemisphere ocean.

0.31°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average for August

cooler by only 0.1°C than August 2016, the warmest August on record

cooler by only 0.05°C than August 2019, the second warmest August.

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for August 2021 was near average at only 0.02°C below the 1991-2020 average. This is the coolest anomaly since August 2014 when the European-average temperature was 0.11°C below its 1991-2020 level.

The boreal summer temperatures in 2021 broadly reflect those seen for August. In particular, above-average temperatures occurred across most of southern and eastern Europe, north Africa, the Middle East, and northern Siberia. In contrast to the general pattern, the summer temperatures in northern and western Europe did not correspond closely to August. The strong positive anomaly across western North America reflects the heatwave earlier in boreal summer. In the tropical and sub-tropical eastern Pacific Ocean, temperatures were below average.

Most of the extratropical ocean in the North Atlantic and north Pacific were warmer than average. Conditions in the South Atlantic were also generally warm, but the remaining ocean areas in the southern hemisphere were more mixed, as were conditions on the Antarctic continent.

The average June-August temperature for Europe was 0.96°C above the 1991-2020 average for the season which marks the warmest boreal summer in this data record. This anomaly in 2021 is only 0.1°C warmer than the two previous warmest summers in 2010 and 2018. The spread between datasets for some summers, notably 2018, indicates that other datasets, which are not yet available, could have a different ranking for these years. There is agreement between datasets that during the commonly observed period (1850 onwards) there were no summers warmer than these.

also below average over the North Atlantic west of Ireland and several oceanic areas in the southern hemisphere.

Averaging over twelve-month periods smooths out shorter-term variations in regional- and global-average temperatures. Globally, the twelve-month period to August 2021 was 0.28°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average. This twelve-month average anomaly is well below the 0.46°C average for the twelve-month periods ending in September 2016 and May and June 2020, the three warmest periods in this data record. The warmest calendar year is 2016, with a temperature 0.44°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 was on a par with 2016, being cooler by less than 0.01°C, well below the spread among multiple datasets. The third warmest calendar year is 2019; its temperature was 0.40°C above average.

0.82°C should be added to the value relative to the 1991-2020 reference period to relate recent global temperatures to the pre-industrial level defined in the IPCC Special Report on “Global Warming of 1.5°C”. The average temperature for the twelve months to August 2021 is 1.1°C above that level.

There is more variability in average European temperatures, but values are more certain due to the relatively dense observational coverage of the continent. The average for the twelve-month period to August 2021 is about 0.6°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 is the warmest calendar year on record for Europe by a clear margin, with a temperature 1.2°C above the 1991-2020 average.

The spread in global averages from various temperature datasets has been reported previously to have been relatively large over recent years. An updated version of the dataset principally responsible for this spread was released in mid-December 2020. This version agrees much better with other datasets for the latest few years. This version does however show a larger warming since 1850-1900 than its predecessor, which has an influence on the ‘pre-industrial’ estimate as mentioned above. Read more in the Temperature indicator.

There is general agreement among datasets that the period from 2015 to 2020 is much warmer globally than any previous such period. There is also agreement that the global temperature has risen at an average rate close to 0.2°C per decade since the late 1970s. There is nevertheless still some spread between the datasets for recent years, such as for 2020, and the annual average temperature anomalies for these years from ERA5 are generally higher than those from the five other datasets considered. The differences range from 0.02 to 0.08°C for 2016-2020. The range is 0.00 to 0.07°C if air temperature over sea is replaced by sea-surface temperature for ERA5 and the other dataset for which sea-surface temperature was not used by design. The remaining differences depend partly on the extent to which datasets represent the relatively warm conditions that have predominated over the Arctic and Antarctic during these years. Differences elsewhere in estimates of sea-surface temperature and surface air temperature over land have been further factors.

The average surface air temperature analysis homepage explains more about the production and reliability of the values presented here, but has yet to be updated to include the new information on dataset spread mentioned above.

From January 2021 onward, the main reference period used is 1991-2020. What this means is described in a web article. For a transition period, the graphics and data relating to the previous reference period (1981-2010) can be found by using the tab-function above each graphic. For key indicators, the 1981-2010 value is included when hovering over the 1991-2020 value.

August 2021 Arctic sea ice extent was below average, but well above the very low August extents of 2012 (lowest on record), 2019 and 2020. Arctic sea ice cover was most below average along north-eastern Greenland and along the Siberian sector, except in the eastern Kara Sea, which saw a persistent sea ice patch (preventing the Northern Sea Route from opening).

Antarctic sea ice extent was the seventh largest on record, with only three years in the satellite data record seeing much larger August extents.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in August 2021 was 6.3 million km 2, 0.6 million km 2 (or 8%) below the 1991-2020 average for August. This extent ranks 10 th lowest for August in the satellite data record; it is well above the low extents seen for the past two Augusts (2019 and 2020), as well as in August 2012, when the lowest extent on record occurred.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The map of sea ice concentration anomalies for the Arctic region for August 2021 shows that negative anomalies predominated in the north-western Greenland Sea and along most of the Siberian sector of the Arctic. However, one notable exception is, as in previous months, along the western Severnaya Zemlya archipelago, which continued to see above average sea ice cover, resulting in non-ice-free conditions for parts of the Northern Sea Route. Small positive sea ice anomalies were recorded north of Alaska.

In August 2021, Antarctic sea ice extent reached 18.6 million km 2 on average, 0.4 million km 2 (2%) above the average for August. This value ranks 7 th in the satellite record and is close to other relatively high values seen in 2000, 2006, and 2009; only three other years (2010, 2013 and 2014) have clearly higher values. The August 2021 value also marks the first positive August anomaly since 2016, coming after a series of positive monthly anomalies recorded earlier in 2021 (since March).

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The map of sea ice concentration anomalies for the Antarctic region for August 2021 shows positive anomalies dominating over most of the Southern Ocean, with the largest positive values along the sea ice edge westward of the Antarctic Peninsula, as well as in the Indian Ocean sector. On the other hand, small negative sea ice concentration anomalies prevailed in two smaller sectors, one around the northern tip of the Antarctic Peninsula, and one in the northern Ross Sea.

July 2021 joins July 2020 as the third warmest July on record globally, less than 0.1°C cooler than July 2019 and July 2016. It was the second warmest July on record for Europe. Heatwaves occurred from the Baltic to the eastern Mediterranean. Western North America continued to experience anomalously high temperatures; parts of the Far East were also well above average.

July 2021 was considerably warmer than average over most of northern and eastern Europe. Its overall temperature was most above the 1991-2020 average over and around the Baltic Sea.  Helsinki experienced its second warmest July in an observational record dating back to 1961. Only July 2010 was warmer. Lithuania reported a heatwave that exceeded the previous duration record in part of the country.  Heat-wave conditions were especially severe in south-eastern Europe at the end of the month. The all-time record for daily maximum temperature was broken in Northern Ireland, and temperatures were much above average over eastern Iceland and parts of eastern Greenland. Conversely, the month was a little colder than the 1991-2020 average over a band stretching from Portugal to Germany, and over parts of north-western Russia and much of Svalbard.

Warmer than average conditions extended eastward from Europe across most of Asia. Here the most anomalous temperatures were over Sakhalin Island, Sapporo, the neighbouring Russian mainland and the far north-east of China. It was also particularly warm over the Sea of Japan and the Pacific Ocean to the east. Exceptional warmth over the western USA and western and central Canada continued from June into July. Temperatures over North Africa were also higher than normal almost everywhere.

In the southern hemisphere, temperatures were unusually mild over Patagonia. Australia recorded its fourth warmest July on record overall and a record high average daily maximum temperature over the tropical north of the country. Temperatures were much above average over West Antarctica, but it was colder than average over most of the sea ice around East Antarctica.

There were several regions of below-average temperature in the Arctic and subarctic zone. Other regions of below-average temperature include the southern USA and northern Mexico, eastern Canada, the Horn of Africa, Yemen and Oman, southern Brazil and eastern Paraguay, and southern Africa.

Air temperatures continued to be below average over large parts of the tropical and sub-tropical eastern Pacific Ocean. Temperatures were also below average over a large part of the Indian Ocean. The North Pacific generally experienced above-average temperatures as did the extratropical North Atlantic. Temperatures were also unusually high over the South Atlantic Ocean to the west and south-west of Africa and over the Drake Passage, as they were in June. Conditions were mixed elsewhere over the ocean in the southern hemisphere.

0.33°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average for July

July is usually the warmest month of the year globally, although August can sometimes be warmer. July 2021 was warmer globally than any previous month in the data record other than July 2019 and July 2016.

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for July 2021 was 1.4°C above the 1991-2020 average. The only July on record that was warmer than this over Europe occurred in 2010, when western Russia experienced a severe heatwave and the European-average temperature was 1.7°C above its 1991-2020 level.

Arctic sea ice extent was the 2 nd lowest for July in the satellite record, virtually tied with July 2012 and July 2019 and behind the lowest value recorded in July 2020. Sea ice cover was most below average in the Laptev, East Siberian, and Greenland Seas and markedly above average in the eastern Kara Sea.

Antarctic sea ice extent was above average overall, ranking sixth highest for July in the satellite record. Sea ice concentrations were above average in most sectors of the Southern Ocean, except in the vicinity of the Antarctic Peninsula.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in July 2021 was 8.5 million km 2, 0.9 million km 2 (or 9%) below the 1991-2020 average for July. This anomaly ranks second lowest for July in the satellite data record, which starts in 1979, virtually tied with the values recorded in 2012 and 2019. The magnitude of the July 2021 anomaly was also visibly smaller than the one recorded in July 2020, which was the lowest on record.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The map of sea ice concentration anomalies for the Arctic region for July 2021 shows a predominance of negative anomalies from the north-eastern Canadian sector eastward to the Greenland Sea and along the Siberian Coast, while positive anomalies prevailed in the Beaufort and Chukchi Seas and between Svalbard and Franz Joseph Land. The largest negative anomalies are found in the Laptev and Siberian Seas, which were largely ice-free. One notable exception to the below-average sea ice conditions along northern Siberia is the eastern Kara Sea which - as in June - saw markedly above-average sea ice conditions. As a result, the Northern Sea Route was still not ice-free by the end of July. In the Greenland Sea, the negative sea ice concentration anomalies were concurrent with much warmer temperatures over most of eastern Greenland.

In July 2021, Antarctic sea ice extent reached 16.9 million km 2 on average, 0.4 million km 2 (3%) above the average for July. This is the sixth highest value in the satellite data record and marks the first positive July anomaly since 2015, coming after a series of positive monthly anomalies recorded earlier in 2021 (since March). While the top four highest July extents occurred during 2010-2015, it is noteworthy that the fifth highest dates back to 1979.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The map of sea ice concentration anomalies for the Antarctic region for July 2021 shows positive anomalies dominating over most of the Southern Ocean. The largest positive anomalies were mostly concentrated along the sea ice edge in the Indian Ocean sector, western Pacific sector and in a broad West Antarctic sector including the northern Amundsen Sea and a portion of the northern Ross Sea. On the other hand, negative sea ice concentration anomalies prevailed along the western coast of the Antarctic Peninsula and in the northern Weddell Sea.

June 2021 joins June 2018 as the fourth warmest June on record globally, after the Junes of 2016, 2019 and 2020. The month was the second warmest June on record for Europe. The warm region over Europe peaked in the north-east, and extended south-westward to North Africa and south-eastward as far as Iran and Pakistan. Heatwave conditions persisted over western North America, where many temperature records were broken. Arctic Siberia also experienced high temperatures. Antarctic temperatures were predominantly colder than usual.

June 2021 was considerably warmer than average over much of Europe. Temperatures were particularly high over Finland and western Russia: the June average was the highest ever at a site in Helsinki with measurements dating back to 1844, and Moscow experienced its warmest June day on record.  Heatwave conditions were also experienced in many other European countries. In contrast, temperatures for the month as a whole were close to or below average over the south-east of the continent, the Iberian Peninsula, western Ireland, Iceland and Svalbard.

The warm conditions over Europe were part of an arc of unusually high temperatures that extended from northwest Africa, through Europe and southeastwards to Iran, Afghanistan and western Pakistan. Two other regions in the northern hemisphere experienced extremely high summer temperatures: western and central North America and northern Siberia. For North America, record-breaking heatwave conditions were centred initially over the southwestern USA and then over the northwestern USA and south western Canada. The all-time record for daily-maximum temperature in Canada was broken three days in a row in British Columbia.  The accompanying hydrological bulletin shows that the regions concerned had unusually dry soils. Wildfires as well as heat posed threats to life.  High June temperatures over northern Siberia have been a feature of recent years, but were not as widespread in 2021 as in the preceding three years.

Relatively large anomalies in wintertime Antarctic temperatures are quite common, and June 2021 was no exception. Conditions were mainly colder than the 1991-2020 average. Elsewhere over land, the temperature map shows a mix of regions with temperatures more moderately above or below average.

Below average air temperatures were present over large parts of the tropical and sub-tropical eastern Pacific Ocean and over the North Atlantic to the west of Europe. The North Pacific generally experienced above-average temperatures as did the extratropical western North Atlantic.  Temperatures were also unusually high over the South Atlantic Ocean to the west of Africa, the Drake Passage and the Tasman Sea in the Pacific. Conditions were mixed elsewhere over the southern hemisphere oceans.

0.21°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average for June

cooler by about 0.15°C than the Junes of 2019 and 2020

warmer than any other June in the data record.

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for June 2021 was 1.5°C above the 1991-2020 average. The only June on record that was warmer than this over Europe occurred in 2019.

Corresponding averages over North America show that June 2021 was the warmest June on record for the continent.  Arctic Siberia experienced its joint-fourth warmest June, on a par with June 2012. The warmer Junes occurred in 2018, 2019 and 2020.

below average over the eastern equatorial Pacific, the North Atlantic west of Ireland and several oceanic areas in the southern hemisphere.

In June 2021, Arctic sea ice extent was below average, ranking 8th lowest in the satellite record, above the June values recorded in the previous five years. Sea ice cover was most below average in the Laptev Sea, owing to the presence of large and persistent polynyas. Antarctic sea ice extent was close to average overall, but with large regional differences. As in May, sea ice concentrations were most above average in the Bellingshausen-Amundsen Sea, Indian Ocean and western Pacific sectors, and most below average in the northern Weddell and northern Ross Seas.

The monthly average Arctic sea ice extent in June 2021 was 11.2 million km 2, 0.4 million km 2 (or 3%) below the 1991-2020 average for June. This value ranks as the 8 th lowest for June in the satellite data record, which starts in 1979, and is close to the values seen in 2017 and 2018.

Map of the Arctic with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The map of sea ice concentration anomalies for the Arctic region for June 2021 shows a mix of negative and positive anomalies in most sectors of the Arctic Ocean, although negative anomalies tend to dominate overall. Sea ice concentrations were most below average in the Laptev Sea, along the northern coast of Siberia. The low sea ice concentrations in this sector came primarily from large areas of open water within the sea ice pack, known as polynyas, particularly east of Severnaya Zemlya Island (in the eastern Laptev Sea) and farther east around the New Siberian Islands. This whole area is well known for the frequent occurrences of polynyas. The low sea ice cover in the Laptev Sea contrasted with the above average sea ice cover farther west over most of the Kara Sea.

In June 2021, Antarctic sea ice extent was very close to the 1991-2020 average, at 14.0 million km 2 on average, just 0.1 million km 2 (1%) above the average for June. This small positive anomaly comes after five years of negative anomalies in June, especially between 2017 and 2019.

Map of Antarctica and the Southern Ocean with geographic labels. Background image from Natural Earth.

The small positive value for the total Antarctic sea ice extent in June 2021 masks large regional differences in sea ice concentration anomalies between the different sectors of the Southern Ocean. The pattern of anomalies bears much resemblance to the one observed in May 2021, although the anomalies were not as pronounced. Sea ice concentrations were above average west of the Antarctic Peninsula - in the northern Bellingshausen and Amundsen Seas, as well as in most of the Indian Ocean and western Pacific sectors of the Southern Ocean. Below average ice concentrations prevailed in the northern Weddell Sea and northern Ross Sea.

The global average temperature for May 2021 was 0.26°C higher than the 1991-2020 average for May. However, for Europe as a whole, May 2021 was 0.46°C below the 1991-2020 average. Temperatures were much above average over western Greenland, northern Africa, the Middle East, and northern and western Russia. Below-average May temperatures on the other hand occurred over the southern and central United States, parts of northern Canada, south-central Africa, most of India, eastern Russia, and eastern Antarctica.

In May 2021, most of Europe experienced below-average temperatures with the centre of the temperature anomaly located over Germany. The month was characteristed by unseasonal cyclonic  weather conditions and this led to Germany having the coldest May since 2010 and maximum temperatures in the United Kingdom being some of the lowest recorded. There were, however, above-average temperatures to the east of the continent and in southern Spain, Greece, Turkey, and western Norway. For example, in Russia, temperatures exceeded 30°C to the north of the Arctic Circle.

Other regions of the world with notably below-average May temperatures included the southern and central United States and parts of northern Canada to the west of Hudson Bay, south-central Africa, eastern Russia and northern Mongolia, most of India, and eastern Antarctica. Conversely, temperatures were above average over western Greenland, north Africa, the Middle East, and northern Siberia. The anomalies from Africa to the Middle East relate to the circulation causing above-average temperatures to the east of Europe.

Below average air temperatures were present over most of the tropical and sub-tropical eastern Pacific Ocean and over the North Atlantic to the northwest of Europe. The northern Pacific Ocean generally experienced above-average temperatures as did extratropical regions of the North Atlantic. The conditions were more mixed over the ocean in the southern hemisphere, with an exception due to large above-average temperatures over the Weddell Sea associated with the below-average sea ice.

The global-mean temperature for May 2021 was higher than the average for 1991-2020, but less extreme than for most months in the last six years. May 2021 was:

0.26°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average for May

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for May 2021 was 0.46°C below the 1991-2020 average.

The boreal spring temperatures in 2021 broadly reflect those seen for May. In particular, below-average temperatures occurred across most of Europe, northern Canada, India, southern Africa, and in parts of Antartica. In the tropical and sub-tropical eastern Pacific Ocean, temperatures were also below average due to the La Niña conditions present during most of spring.

Air temperatures were above average over north Africa, the Middle East, and to the east of Europe. Greenland, northeastern North America. Antarctica in the vicinity of the Weddell Sea also experienced above-average temperatures. Most of the extratropical ocean in the North Atlantic and North Pacific were warmer than average. Conditions were more mixed over the ocean in the southern hemisphere.

The average March-May temperature for Europe was 0.45°C below the 1991-2020 average for the season, which marks the coldest boreal spring since 2013, and spring 2021 temperature anomalies were similar to those from 2004-2006. It is also the fourth coldest spring since 1998.

below average over the eastern equatorial Pacific, the North Atlantic west of Ireland and several oceanic areas in the southern hemisphere.

Averaging over twelve-month periods smooths out shorter-term variations in regional- and global-average temperatures. Globally, the twelve-month period to May 2021 was   0.29°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average. This twelve-month average is well below the 0.46°C average for the twelve-month periods ending in September 2016 and May and June 2020, the three warmest periods in this data record. The warmest calendar year is 2016, with a temperature 0.44°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 was on a par with 2016, being cooler by less than 0.01°C, well below the spread among multiple datasets. The third warmest calendar year is 2019; its temperature was 0.40°C above average.

0.82°C should be added to the value relative to the 1991-2020 reference period  to relate recent global temperatures to the pre-industrial level defined in the IPCC Special Report on “Global Warming of 1.5°C”. The average temperature for the twelve months to May 2021 is 1.11°C above that level.

There is more variability in average European temperatures, but values are more certain due to the relatively dense observational coverage of the continent. The average for the twelve-month period to May 2021 is about 0.5°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 is the warmest calendar year on record for Europe by a clear margin, with a temperature 1.2°C above the 1991-2020 average.

The spread in global averages from various temperature datasets has been reported previously to have been relatively large over recent years. An updated version of the dataset principally responsible for this spread was released in mid-December 2020. This version agrees much better with other datasets for the latest few years. This version does however show a larger warming since 1850-1900 than its predecessor, which has an influence on the ‘pre-industrial’ estimate as mentioned above. Read more in the Temperature indicator.

There is general agreement among datasets that the period from 2015 to 2020 is much warmer globally than any previous such period. There is also agreement that the global temperature has risen at an average rate close to 0.2°C per decade since the late 1970s. There is nevertheless still some spread between the datasets for recent years, such as for 2020, and the annual average temperature anomalies for these years from ERA5 are generally higher than those from the five other datasets considered. The differences range from 0.02 to 0.08°C for 2016-2020. The range is 0.00 to 0.07°C if air temperature over sea is replaced by sea-surface temperature for ERA5 and the other dataset for which sea-surface temperature was not used by design. The remaining differences depend partly on the extent to which datasets represent the relatively warm conditions that have predominated over the Arctic and Antarctic during these years. Differences elsewhere in estimates of sea-surface temperature and surface air temperature over land have been further factors.

The average surface air temperature analysis homepage explains more about the production and reliability of the values presented here, but has yet to be updated to include the new information on dataset spread mentioned above.

From January 2021 onward, the main reference period used is 1991-2020. What this means is described in a web article. For a transition period, the graphics and data relating to the previous reference period (1981-2010) can be found by using the tab-function above each graphic. For key indicators, the 1981-2010 value is included when hovering over the 1991-2020 value.

The global average temperature for April 2021 was about 0.2ºC higher than the 1991-2020 average for April. Globally, April 2021 was warmer than any April prior to 2010, but colder than 2010 and 2016-2020 Aprils. April 2021 was the coldest April since 2003 over Europe as a whole. Temperatures were much above average over parts of north-eastern Canada, north-western Russia and the Middle East.

Average temperatures for April 2021 varied considerably across Europe. They were below average in a broad swathe from  Iceland to the Mediterranean and Black Seas, but above average over the west of the Iberian Peninsula and over the far east of the continent. The month had begun with unusually mild conditions in many places, but temperatures plummeted over an extensive region in the first week of the month, reaching record lows over parts of western and central Europe, for example over Slovenia. France was among the countries suffering substantial frost damage to vines and fruit trees that had begun developing earlier than usual. This led its government to declare a “calamité agricole”. Winds over several countries were more persistently from the north than is typical for April, with the United Kingdom experiencing its lowest average minimum temperature for April since 1922 and the highest number of April frosts in a data record going back to 1960.

Other regions of the world with notably below-average April temperatures included Alaska and parts of northern Canada, much of eastern Siberia and China, a band running from north-west to south-east over Australia, and several parts of Antarctica, especially West Antarctica. Conversely, temperatures were much higher than average over much of north-eastern Canada and Greenland, western Siberia, parts of the Middle East and North Africa, Argentina and southern Chile, coastal Namibia and South Africa, and parts of East Antarctica.

Air temperatures were below average over most of the tropical and sub-tropical eastern Pacific Ocean, where the latest La Niña event continued to weaken. Other oceanic regions of the extratropical northern hemisphere were mainly warmer than average, the main exceptions being the north-eastern Atlantic and seas to the north-east of Greenland. Conditions were more mixed over the oceans of the tropics and southern hemisphere.

The global-mean temperature for April 2021 was higher than the average for 1991-2020, but less extreme than for most months in the last six years. April 2021 was:

0.19°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average for April

more than 0.3°C cooler than April 2016 and April 2020, the two warmest Aprils on record

European-average temperature anomalies are generally larger and more variable than global anomalies. The European-average temperature for April 2021 was 0.9°C below the 1991-2020 average, making the month the coldest April since 2003 for Europe as a whole.

below average over the eastern equatorial Pacific, the North Atlantic west of Ireland and several oceanic areas in the southern hemisphere.

Averaging over twelve-month periods smooths out shorter-term variations in regional- and global-average temperatures. Globally, the twelve-month period to April 2021 was 0.31°C warmer than the 1991-2020 average. This twelve-month average is well below the 0.46°C average for the twelve-month periods ending in September 2016 and May 2020, the two warmest such periods in this data record. The warmest calendar year is 2016, with a temperature 0.44°C above the 1991-2020 average. 2020 was on a par with 2016, being cooler by less than 0.01°C, well below the spread among multiple datasets. The third warmest calendar year is 2019; its temperature was 0.40°C above average.

0.82°C should be added to the value relative to the 1991-2020 reference period to relate recent global temperatures to the pre-industrial level defined in the IPCC Special Report on “Global Warming of 1.5°C”. The average temperature for the twelve months to April 2021 is 1.13°C above that level.

Read more in the Temperature indicator.
